# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Assessment Pattern Followed (KTU 2024 OEMUT835):**
> - **Part A:** Short-answer questions — 3 marks each (typically 1–2 sentences to short paragraph).
> - **Part B:** Long-answer questions — 14 marks each, with **internal choice** (Module-wise, as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern). Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.
> - All questions are tagged with simulated past-year tags aligned to KTU's question paper style.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Define fleet management. State any four of its primary objectives.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** Fleet management is the systematic administration of a company's vehicle fleet to maximize operational efficiency and minimize total cost of ownership throughout the vehicle lifecycle.
>
> **Four Primary Objectives:**
> 1. Reduction of total operating cost (TCO).
> 2. Improvement in vehicle utilization and availability.
> 3. Ensuring statutory and safety compliance.
> 4. Optimization of fuel and maintenance efficiency.
>
> *(+1 mark for the clean definition; +2 marks for listing objectives with brief justification.)*

---

### Question 2
**Distinguish between *Vehicle Utilization Rate* and *Vehicle Availability* with formulas.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Utilization Rate (UR)** is a measure of *productive use* of a vehicle, while **Availability (A)** is a measure of *operational readiness* of the vehicle.
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> UR &= \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{total}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
> A &= \dfrac{T_{total} - T_{maintenance} - T_{breakdown}}{T_{total}} \times 100
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> A vehicle can be 100% available (well-maintained) but only 30% utilized (no customers). Conversely, a poorly maintained vehicle can be highly utilized when running but unavailable during repairs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Set 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1]**

> **Q (a)** Explain in detail the *five major objectives* of fleet management. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q (b)** A delivery van is purchased for ₹18,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹3,00,000 after 8 years. The annual operating cost is ₹3,20,000. The vehicle covers 50,000 km per year. Assuming an interest rate of 9% p.a., calculate the *full Cost Per Kilometre* using the straight-line depreciation method. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The five major objectives of fleet management are:

**1. Economic Objectives — Cost Minimization:** The foremost objective is to minimize the *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* across the vehicle's life. This includes fuel, maintenance, labour, insurance, and administrative costs. Fuel alone constitutes 30–40% of fleet OPEX, so fuel efficiency is a key lever.

**2. Operational Objectives — Utilization:** The fleet manager aims to maximize the *productive use* of each vehicle, expressed through the **Utilization Rate (UR)**. Higher UR means more revenue per asset and faster ROI.

**3. Safety and Compliance Objectives:** Compliance with the *Motor Vehicles Act 1988, CMVR, AIS standards, and pollution norms (BS-VI)* is non-negotiable. Safety objectives also include driver training, accident reduction, and insurance optimization.

**4. Maintenance and Reliability Objectives:** The aim is to keep vehicles *available* for service through preventive and predictive maintenance. Metrics include **Availability (A), MTBF, and MTTR**.

**5. Strategic and Environmental Objectives:** Modern fleet management aligns with sustainability — reducing **carbon footprint (g CO₂/km)**, adopting **electric vehicles** under FAME-II, and improving **customer satisfaction** through better service levels.

*(+1.4 marks per objective, fully described.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Purchase price $P = ₹18,00,000$
- Salvage value $S = ₹3,00,000$
- Holding period $N = 8$ years
- Annual operating cost $TOC = ₹3,20,000$
- Annual distance $D = 50,000$ km
- Interest rate $r = 9\% = 0.09$

**Step 1 — Annualized depreciation (straight-line):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{depr,\,ann} &= \dfrac{P - S}{N} = \dfrac{18,00,000 - 3,00,000}{8} = \dfrac{15,00,000}{8} = 1,87,500
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Annualized interest (on average book value):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{interest,\,ann} &= \dfrac{P + S}{2} \times r = \dfrac{18,00,000 + 3,00,000}{2} \times 0.09 \\[4pt]
&= 10,50,000 \times 0.09 = 94,500
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total annualized capital cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{acq,ann} &= C_{depr,\,ann} + C_{interest,\,ann} = 1,87,500 + 94,500 = 2,82,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total annual cost (TAC):**

$$\begin{aligned}
TAC &= C_{acq,ann} + TOC = 2,82,000 + 3,20,000 = 6,02,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Full CPK:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CPK &= \dfrac{TAC}{D} = \dfrac{6,02,000}{50,000} = 12.04 \text{ ₹/km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The full Cost Per Kilometre is **₹12.04 per km**.

---

### Question B — Set 1 — Alternative (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1]**

> **Q (a)** Discuss the *importance* of fleet management in modern transport organizations. Map the benefits to **at least four key stakeholders**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q (b)** Define the following metrics and state one formula for each: *TCO, MTBF, MTTR, Empty Running Ratio, On-Time Delivery.* *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

Fleet management is critical in modern transport organizations because it converts a fleet from a **passive cost centre** into an **actively optimized profit enabler**. Its importance can be mapped to stakeholders as follows:

**1. For the Owner / Investor:** Fleet management provides visibility into **TCO** and **ROI**, enabling better procurement, financing, and resale decisions. It improves profitability and asset value.

**2. For the Operations Manager:** It provides **route optimization**, **dispatch efficiency**, and **utilization tracking**, all of which improve service levels and lower per-trip cost.

**3. For the Maintenance Team:** It brings in **preventive maintenance scheduling**, **predictive IoT diagnostics**, and **spare-parts inventory control**, increasing vehicle **Availability** and reducing **MTTR**.

**4. For the Customer:** It ensures **on-time delivery (OTD)**, **real-time tracking**, and **lower damage rates**, leading to higher satisfaction and contract retention.

**5. For Society and the Environment:** Modern fleet management supports **EV adoption**, **route optimization**, and **emission tracking**, contributing to sustainability goals and **India's Net-Zero 2070** commitment.

*(1.4 marks per stakeholder explanation; bonus credit for environmental angle.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. TCO — Total Cost of Ownership:** Sum of all costs over a vehicle's life.

$$\begin{aligned}
TCO = C_{acq} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{lab} + C_{ins} + C_{admin} - C_{salv}
\end{aligned}$$

**2. MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total\,operating}}{N_{failures}}
\end{aligned}$$

**3. MTTR — Mean Time To Repair:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{repairs}}
\end{aligned}$$

**4. Empty Running Ratio (ERR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
ERR = \dfrac{D_{empty}}{D_{total}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**5. On-Time Delivery (OTD):**

$$\begin{aligned}
OTD = \dfrac{N_{on\,time}}{N_{total\,shipments}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

*(+1.4 marks for each metric: 0.4 for definition, 0.6 for formula, 0.4 for units/significance.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Utilization with Availability (–2 marks):** These are *not* the same. Availability is a *maintenance* metric; Utilization is an *operational* metric. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the salvage term in TCO (–1 mark):** Students compute TCO as a one-way sum of costs and forget to *subtract* the salvage value. TCO is a *net* lifetime cost.
>
> 3. **Skipping the average book value in interest calculation (–2 marks):** When the KTU question gives an interest rate, the student must compute interest on the **average book value** $\frac{(P+S)}{2}$, not on the full purchase price.
>
> 4. **Writing generic objectives without linking to metrics (–1.5 marks):** Simply writing *"reduce cost"* scores less than writing *"reduce cost by improving fuel efficiency from X to Y km/L, lowering CPK from ₹A to ₹B"*. Quantify where possible.
>
> 5. **Omitting the strategic / environmental pillar (–1.5 marks):** Many students stop at the *operational* and *economic* pillars and forget the modern strategic objectives (EV, sustainability, customer service). KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes these.
>
> 6. **Mermaid/diagram not memorizable:** The five-pillar diagram and stakeholder matrix are *high-yield visual aids* — if a student draws a partial diagram in the exam, they earn *easy 2–3 marks* on a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 1 hour before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Fleet management = integrated administration of vehicles across their lifecycle to **maximize efficiency** and **minimize TCO**.
- [ ] **Five Pillars of Objectives:** Economic, Operational, Safety & Risk, Maintenance, Strategic.
- [ ] **TCO formula:** $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{lab} + C_{ins} + C_{admin} - C_{salv}$.
- [ ] **CPK formula:** $CPK = \dfrac{TAC}{D}$. For full CPK, include annualized capital recovery.
- [ ] **UR formula:** $UR = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ — *operational* metric.
- [ ] **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{T_{total} - T_{maintenance} - T_{breakdown}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ — *maintenance* metric.
- [ ] **MTBF & MTTR** are reliability and maintainability indicators respectively.
- [ ] **Stakeholder Mapping:** Owner → ROI; Operations → UR/OTD; Maintenance → A/MTBF; Customer → OTD; Regulator → Compliance; Environment → CO₂.
- [ ] **Modern relevance:** EV adoption, AIS-140 telematics, BS-VI compliance, Net-Zero 2070, FAME-II policy.
- [ ] **Key Indian laws:** Motor Vehicles Act 1988, CMVR, AIS standards, BS-VI norms, Goods & Services Tax (on transport).
- [ ] **Common trap:** Utilization ≠ Availability. Memorize the distinction.
- [ ] **O-M-O-S Chain:** Always answer in *Objective → Mechanism → Outcome → Strategic Benefit* sequence.
- [ ] **Pareto Principle:** 80% of fleet lifecycle cost is committed at *acquisition* — start fleet management at the *buying* stage.
- [ ] **Numerical skill:** Practice one numerical on CPK and one on UR/A before the exam.
- [ ] **Diagrams to memorize:** Five-pillar architecture, UR vs Availability time-state diagram, stakeholder-benefit matrix.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Objectives and Importance of Fleet Management**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — OEMUT835*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Fleet Management Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of Fleet Management Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fleet Management System (FMS)** is an integrated, multi-module organizational framework that combines hardware (telematics devices, sensors, GPS units), software platforms (cloud-based dashboards, analytics engines), human processes, and communication networks to efficiently plan, execute, monitor, and control a fleet of commercial vehicles — from acquisition to disposal.

According to the KTU 2024 OEMUT835 syllabus, the *Components of Fleet Management Systems* constitute the structural pillars that enable the **four primary fleet functions**:

1. **Acquisition & Disposal** of vehicles
2. **Operation & Utilization** of vehicles
3. **Maintenance & Reliability** management
4. **Administration, Risk & Compliance** control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A Fleet Management System is NOT just GPS tracking. It is a **holistic, decision-support ecosystem** that converts raw vehicle/driver data into actionable managerial intelligence across the entire vehicle life-cycle.

### 1.2 The Eight Core Components (KTU-Certified Framework)

| S.No | Component | Core Functional Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vehicle/Asset Management Module** | Inventory, utilization, life-cycle |
| 2 | **Driver Management Module** | HR, training, performance, payroll |
| 3 | **Fuel Management Module** | Consumption, theft control, costing |
| 4 | **Maintenance Management Module** | Preventive, predictive, breakdown |
| 5 | **Routing & Dispatch Module** | Optimization, scheduling, navigation |
| 6 | **Telematics & Tracking Module** | GPS, sensors, real-time data |
| 7 | **Safety, Risk & Compliance Module** | Accidents, audits, regulatory |
| 8 | **Reporting & Analytics Module** | KPIs, BI dashboards, MIS |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Airport Control Tower" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine an **airport control tower**. The tower does not fly the planes, but it sees *every* plane, knows *every* pilot, monitors *every* fuel tank, and ensures *every* landing is safe. A **Fleet Management System is the "control tower" of a transport company.** Each subsystem (radar = telematics, pilot roster = driver management, runway schedule = routing, fuel trucks = fuel module, hangar = maintenance) is one *component* of the FMS.

**Why is this analogy useful?** It helps a student instantly grasp that FMS is:
- **Centralized** (one tower watching many vehicles)
- **Real-time** (live updates like radar)
- **Multi-disciplinary** (mixes HR, engineering, finance, IT)
- **Safety-critical** (one bad decision affects dozens of lives and crores in assets)

---

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in FMS (Industry Benchmarks)

The following are universally accepted benchmark metrics across the fleet industry. Memorize the units and formulas — these appear in KTU numericals.

> **Bold Constants & Industry Standard Metrics:**
> - **Average Vehicle Utilization:** **75%–85%** is considered optimal
> - **Fuel Cost Share in Operating Cost:** **30%–60%** (depending on commodity)
> - **Preventive-to-Breakdown Maintenance Ratio Target:** **80:20**
> - **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR) Target:** **< 0.5 per million km**
> - **Telematics Data Refresh Rate:** **10–30 seconds** (real-time tracking)
> - **Vehicle Downtime Target:** **< 5% of total available time**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-Wise Deep Dive — "Why" and "How"

#### **Component 1: Vehicle / Asset Management Module**
- **What it manages:** Vehicle acquisition, registration, depreciation, insurance, resale/disposal.
- **Why it exists:** A transport company often has 50–5000 vehicles worth crores. Without a structured *vehicle master record*, asset value is lost and taxation becomes chaotic.
- **How it works:** Each vehicle is assigned a unique **VIN (Vehicle Identification Number)** linked to a digital master file containing purchase date, chassis number, RC book, fitness certificate expiry, permit validity, and depreciation class.

**Key Formulas Used in Asset Management:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Depreciation (Straight Line)} &= \frac{\text{Asset Cost} - \text{Scrap Value}}{\text{Useful Life (years)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Asset Utilization Index} &= \frac{\text{Vehicle-Days Actually Used}}{\text{Vehicle-Days Available}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Component 2: Driver Management Module**
- **What it manages:** Recruitment, license verification, duty hours, leave, training, performance scoring, salary.
- **Why it exists:** Drivers are the **largest variable cost** (≈ 25–35% of operating cost in road transport) and the **single biggest cause of accidents** (human error ≈ 80%).
- **How it works:** Uses **Driver Behaviour Scorecards** derived from telematics data (harsh braking, over-speeding, idling) and HR databases for licensing compliance.

**Key Formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Driver Cost per km} &= \frac{\text{Total Driver Wages} + \text{Allowances} + \text{Training Cost}}{\text{Total km Operated}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Component 3: Fuel Management Module**
- **What it manages:** Fuel purchase, dispensing, consumption tracking, theft detection, CO₂ reporting.
- **Why it exists:** Fuel is typically the **largest operating expense** for a fleet (often 30–60%). A 1% leakage in fuel control can wipe out the entire annual profit.
- **How it works:** Combines **fuel card data**, **tank-level sensors (ultrasonic)**, and **refuel-event analytics** to detect anomalies like sudden tank drops while parked.

**Key Formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Kilometre per Litre (kmpl)} &= \frac{\text{Distance Travelled (km)}}{\text{Fuel Consumed (litres)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel Cost per Tonne-km} &= \frac{\text{Total Fuel Cost}}{\text{Total Payload} \times \text{Total Distance}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Component 4: Maintenance Management Module**
- **What it manages:** Preventive schedules, breakdown repairs, spare inventory, workshop scheduling.
- **Why it exists:** A single in-transit breakdown on a long-haul route can cost **10× the actual repair cost** in towing, missed deliveries, and customer penalties.
- **How it works:** Uses either **time-based PM schedules** or modern **predictive maintenance** driven by IoT sensors monitoring engine temperature, oil pressure, brake-pad wear.

**Key Formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)} &= \frac{\sum \text{Repair Time (hours)}}{\text{Number of Breakdowns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Hours}}{\text{Number of Failures}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fleet Availability} &= \frac{\text{Operating Hours}}{\text{Operating Hours} + \text{Downtime Hours}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Component 5: Routing & Dispatch Module**
- **What it manages:** Trip planning, vehicle-to-load assignment, route optimization, real-time re-routing.
- **Why it exists:** Poor routing can increase fuel consumption by **15–25%** and delivery times by **30%**.
- **How it works:** Uses algorithms like **Dijkstra's shortest path**, **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** solvers, and live traffic feed integration (Google Maps API, HERE Maps).

#### **Component 6: Telematics & Tracking Module**
- **What it manages:** Real-time GPS location, speed, ignition, geo-fence breaches, mileage.
- **Why it exists:** It is the **sensory nervous system** of the entire FMS — without telematics, all other modules work on stale, manual data.
- **How it works:** A **GPS + GSM + Accelerometer** device fitted in the vehicle transmits data every 10–30 seconds to a cloud server.

#### **Component 7: Safety, Risk & Compliance Module**
- **What it manages:** Accident reporting, insurance claims, statutory compliance (PUC, Fitness, Permits, Driver Hours under MV Act 1988 §93).
- **Why it exists:** Non-compliance attracts heavy fines; accidents attract liability.
- **How it works:** Auto-alerts for document expiry, dash-cam footage review, automated accident notification.

#### **Component 8: Reporting & Analytics Module**
- **What it manages:** Dashboards, KPIs, exception reports, predictive insights.
- **Why it exists:** Data without analysis is noise. This component converts raw data into **management decisions**.
- **How it works:** Uses BI tools (Power BI, Tableau, custom MIS) and KPIs like **Cost per km, Revenue per vehicle, Empty km percentage**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Exam Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** These formulas are the *minimum* required for any numerical or descriptive question on Components of FMS. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| S.No | Formula Name | Equation (LaTeX form) | Unit | Typical Boundary Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kilometre per Litre | $\dfrac{D}{F}$ | km/litre | 2.5 – 6.0 for trucks |
| 2 | Cost per Kilometre | $\dfrac{TC}{D}$ | Rs/km | Rs 25 – Rs 60 (commercial) |
| 3 | Asset Utilization | $\dfrac{V_d^{used}}{V_d^{avail}} \times 100$ | percent | 75 – 85 percent optimum |
| 4 | Fleet Availability | $\dfrac{OH}{OH + DH} \times 100$ | percent | greater than 90 percent target |
| 5 | MTBF | $\dfrac{\Sigma OH}{N_f}$ | hours | greater than 5000 hrs target |
| 6 | MTTR | $\dfrac{\Sigma RH}{N_f}$ | hours | less than 8 hrs target |
| 7 | Empty Run percent | $\dfrac{D_{empty}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | less than 15 percent |
| 8 | AFR | $\dfrac{N_{acc} \times 10^6}{D_{total}}$ | per million km | less than 0.5 target |
| 9 | Payload Utilization | $\dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{rated}} \times 100$ | percent | 80 – 95 percent ideal |
| 10 | Fuel Cost per tonne-km | $\dfrac{FC}{PL \times D}$ | Rs/tonne-km | depends on commodity |

> *Note:* Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation for absolute value wherever needed; rendered here as `vert` symbol without breaking the markdown table delimiter.

The **Components of FMS** are not just academic modules — they are the *backbone of every logistics company you interact with*:

- **Amazon Logistics** uses routing + telematics + analytics components to deliver same-day.
- **Ola / Uber** fleets use driver + vehicle + safety components for asset-light operations.
- **Indian Railways (CONCOR)** uses vehicle + maintenance + reporting components for container logistics.
- **KSRTC** (Kerala State Road Transport) uses fuel + maintenance + compliance components to manage 6000+ buses.

Understanding these components prepares you for roles in **logistics, supply chain, transport engineering, and operations management**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates **worked derivations** of the most exam-relevant formulas from the FMS component framework. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly so you can replicate it during the KTU exam.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Kilometre per Litre (kmpl) from First Principles

**Scenario:** A truck travels 1,250 km on a long-haul trip and refuels 5 times for 65, 70, 55, 68, and 72 litres respectively. Find its kmpl.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables from the problem statement.**
Distance travelled $D$ and total fuel consumed $F$.

**Step 2 — Define the governing equation.**

$$
\text{kmpl} = \frac{D}{F}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all the fuel refills.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 65 + 70 + 55 + 68 + 72 \\
F &= 135 + 55 + 68 + 72 \\
F &= 190 + 68 + 72 \\
F &= 258 + 72 \\
F &= 330 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{kmpl} &= \frac{1250}{330} \\
\text{kmpl} &= 3.7878 \text{ km/litre}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round to practical precision.**

$$
\text{kmpl} \approx 3.79 \text{ km/litre}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A kmpl of 3.79 is typical for a fully loaded 16-tonne truck on Indian highways.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Asset Utilization Index

**Scenario:** A transport company owns 100 trucks. In the month of June (30 days), 20 trucks were under repair for an average of 5 days each. Calculate the Asset Utilization Index.

**Step 1 — Compute total available vehicle-days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d^{avail} &= N_{vehicles} \times N_{days} \\
V_d^{avail} &= 100 \times 30 = 3000 \text{ vehicle-days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total downtime vehicle-days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d^{down} &= 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ vehicle-days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total utilized vehicle-days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d^{used} &= V_d^{avail} - V_d^{down} \\
V_d^{used} &= 3000 - 100 = 2900 \text{ vehicle-days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the utilization formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Asset Utilization} &= \frac{V_d^{used}}{V_d^{avail}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2900}{3000} \times 100 \\
&= 96.67 \text{ percent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare against the benchmark.**

Since the optimal benchmark is **75%–85%**, this fleet is **over-utilized** — risking breakdowns. Recommendation: increase fleet size or reduce repair days.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cost per Kilometre

**Scenario:** A delivery van records the following monthly costs:
- Diesel: Rs 18,000
- Driver salary: Rs 12,000
- Maintenance: Rs 3,500
- Insurance: Rs 1,500
- Toll: Rs 2,000

Total distance travelled: 3,200 km. Find the cost per km.

**Step 1 — Sum all operating costs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= 18000 + 12000 + 3500 + 1500 + 2000 \\
TC &= 30000 + 3500 + 1500 + 2000 \\
TC &= 33500 + 1500 + 2000 \\
TC &= 35000 + 2000 \\
TC &= 37000 \text{ Rs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cost-per-km formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per km} &= \frac{TC}{D} \\
&= \frac{37000}{3200} \\
&= 11.5625 \text{ Rs/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical conclusion.**

A small delivery van costing about Rs 11.56/km is efficient; any value above Rs 15/km needs route optimization.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)

**Scenario:** A fleet of 200 trucks covered 8,400,000 km in a year and had 3 reportable accidents. Compute the AFR.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$
\text{AFR} = \frac{N_{acc} \times 10^6}{D_{total}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AFR} &= \frac{3 \times 10^6}{8400000} \\
&= \frac{3000000}{8400000} \\
&= 0.357 \text{ accidents per million km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Benchmark.**

Since the target is **AFR < 0.5**, this fleet is **within the safe zone**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component × Data Flow Matrix

This is the **KTU 2024 high-yield matrix** examiners expect students to reproduce.

| Component (Module) | Primary Input Data | Output KPI / Report | Critical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Management | VIN, RC, Fitness data | Asset register, Depreciation schedule | Document expiry less than 30 days triggers alert |
| Driver Management | License, duty hours, scorecards | Driver performance score, payroll | Score less than 60 percent triggers retraining |
| Fuel Management | Refuel logs, sensor readings | kmpl report, theft alerts | kmpl drop greater than 10 percent month-on-month flags issue |
| Maintenance Management | OBD data, workshop logs | PM schedule, breakdown analysis | MTBF less than 5000 hrs requires fleet audit |
| Routing & Dispatch | Order book, traffic feed | Optimized route plan | Empty Run greater than 15 percent needs re-allocation |
| Telematics & Tracking | GPS, accelerometer | Live map, geofence alerts | Harsh braking greater than 5 per 100 km triggers review |
| Safety & Compliance | Dashcam, statutory docs | Compliance dashboard | Any document expired greater than 1 day fines applicable |
| Reporting & Analytics | All module outputs | KPI dashboard | Cost per km variance greater than 10 percent monthly |
