## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Vehicle Life Cycle Management (VLCM)** and list its five phases.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vehicle Life Cycle Management (VLCM) is the integrated, strategic, and engineering-driven framework for managing a vehicle from its initial conception through its disposal, with the objective of minimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** while maximizing **operational availability**, **safety**, and **regulatory compliance** throughout the vehicle's entire service tenure.

**Five Phases (1.5 marks):**
1. **Phase P1 — Planning & Need Analysis**
2. **Phase P2 — Procurement & Acquisition**
3. **Phase P3 — Operation & Utilization**
4. **Phase P4 — Maintenance & Support**
5. **Phase P5 — Disposal & Replacement**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the **"Iceberg Cost Principle"** in the context of Vehicle Life Cycle Cost.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Iceberg Cost Principle** states that in vehicle ownership, only a small fraction (approximately **15–25%**) of the total life cycle cost is *visible* to the decision-maker at the time of purchase — namely, the **acquisition cost** (vehicle price, registration, insurance). This is the "tip of the iceberg."

The remaining **75–85%** lies *submerged* below the waterline and includes:
- Fuel cost (typically the largest single component — ~40–50% of operating cost)
- Driver wages and benefits
- Tire replacement
- Preventive and corrective maintenance
- Insurance renewals
- Road tax and tolls
- Downtime penalties
- End-of-life disposal costs

**Implication (1 mark):** A fleet manager who chooses the vehicle with the lowest purchase price without analyzing the submerged 80% will, in most cases, end up with a higher total life cycle cost. Hence, **LCC-based procurement is always preferred over purchase-price-based procurement.**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five phases of the Vehicle Life Cycle**. Discuss the key activities and decisions involved in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial vehicle is acquired for **₹30,00,000**. Its estimated salvage value after **n years** is **₹6,00,000**. The annual operating and maintenance cost is given by $AOMC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \cdot (n-1)$ rupees, where $n$ is the service life in years. If the **minimum attractive rate of return (MARR)** is **10% per annum**, determine the **optimal economic life** of the vehicle by calculating the EUAC for $n = 5, 6, 7, 8$ years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

The five phases of the Vehicle Life Cycle form a closed-loop, iterative system. Each phase feeds inputs and decisions into the next, with Phase P5 (Disposal) feeding lessons-learned back to Phase P1 (Planning).

**Phase P1 — Planning & Need Analysis (1 mark):**
Activities include demand forecasting, fleet sizing, specification drafting, make-or-buy analysis, and capital budgeting. The output is the **Fleet Requirements Document (FRD)** specifying vehicle type, capacity, fuel, and quantity.

**Phase P2 — Procurement & Acquisition (1 mark):**
Activities include vendor evaluation, tendering, financing (purchase vs lease), pre-delivery inspection, and registration. The output is the **capitalized, roadworthy vehicle asset**.

**Phase P3 — Operation & Utilization (1.5 marks):**
Activities include route planning, driver management, fuel management, load optimization, and telematics monitoring. KPIs: **Cost/km, Km/L, Vehicle Utilization Ratio (VUR), On-time Delivery (OTD).** This is the revenue-generating phase and the longest.

**Phase P4 — Maintenance & Support (1.5 marks):**
Activities include preventive maintenance (PM), predictive maintenance (PdM), corrective repair, overhaul, and spare-part inventory management. KPIs: **MTBF, MTTR, Availability.** Maintenance cost accelerates as the vehicle ages.

**Phase P5 — Disposal & Replacement (1 mark):**
Activities include disposition decision (sell, scrap, refurbish), ELV regulatory compliance, salvage recovery, and replacement planning. Output: **capital recovery and lessons-learned report** fed back to P1.

**Closure (1 mark):** The five phases form a closed feedback loop — Phase P5's disposal data (resale price, failure modes) feeds into Phase P1's next acquisition specification.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- AC = ₹30,00,000
- SV = ₹6,00,000
- $AOMC(n) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000(n-1)$
- i = 10% = 0.10

**Formula:**

$$EUAC(n) = (AC - SV) \cdot (A/P, i, n) + SV \cdot i + AOMC(n)$$

Where:

$$(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**Fixed capital recovery components:**
- $(AC - SV) = 30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000$
- $SV \cdot i = 6{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.10 = \text{₹ } 60{,}000$

**Compute Capital Recovery Factor for each n:**

For **n = 5:** $(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$

$$(A/P, 10\%, 5) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.61051}{0.61051} = \frac{0.161051}{0.61051} = 0.26380$$

For **n = 6:** $(1.10)^6 = 1.77156$

$$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.77156}{0.77156} = \frac{0.177156}{0.77156} = 0.22961$$

For **n = 7:** $(1.10)^7 = 1.94872$

$$(A/P, 10\%, 7) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.94872}{0.94872} = \frac{0.194872}{0.94872} = 0.20541$$

For **n = 8:** $(1.10)^8 = 2.14359$

$$(A/P, 10\%, 8) = \frac{0.10 \cdot 2.14359}{1.14359} = \frac{0.214359}{1.14359} = 0.18744$$

**Compute AOMC for each n:**

- $AOMC(5) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \cdot 4 = \text{₹ } 4{,}80{,}000$
- $AOMC(6) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \cdot 5 = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000$
- $AOMC(7) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \cdot 6 = \text{₹ } 5{,}20{,}000$
- $AOMC(8) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \cdot 7 = \text{₹ } 5{,}40{,}000$

**Compute EUAC for each n:**

For **n = 5:**
$$EUAC(5) = 24{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.26380 + 60{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000$$
$$= 6{,}33{,}120 + 60{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 11{,}73{,}120$$

For **n = 6:**
$$EUAC(6) = 24{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.22961 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000$$
$$= 5{,}51{,}064 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 11{,}11{,}064$$

For **n = 7:**
$$EUAC(7) = 24{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.20541 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}20{,}000$$
$$= 4{,}92{,}984 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}72{,}984$$

For **n = 8:**
$$EUAC(8) = 24{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.18744 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}40{,}000$$
$$= 4{,}49{,}856 + 60{,}000 + 5{,}40{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}49{,}856$$

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| n (years) | EUAC (₹) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 5 | 11,73,120 |
| 6 | 11,11,064 |
| 7 | 10,72,984 |
| **8** | **10,49,856** ✓ (Minimum) |

**Conclusion:** The EUAC is decreasing across the tested range. Since $n=8$ gives the lowest EUAC among tested values, the **optimal economic life is at least 8 years**. A fleet manager should further test $n=9, 10$ to confirm the global minimum, but based on available data, **$n^* \geq 8$ years**. (In KTU, if EUAC is monotonically decreasing in the given range, state that the optimal lies at or beyond the upper boundary tested.)

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)**. Derive the LCC expression with uniform annual operating cost, and discuss the significance of the **discount rate (i)** in LCC analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A delivery van has an acquisition cost of **₹12,00,000** and a useful life of **8 years** with a salvage value of **₹2,00,000**. The annual fuel cost is **₹1,80,000**, annual maintenance cost is **₹80,000** in year 1 and increases by **₹15,000** every subsequent year. Calculate the **Total Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** at a discount rate of **8% per annum**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Definition of LCC (1.5 marks):** Life Cycle Cost (LCC) is the total discounted monetary expenditure incurred by an organization to acquire, operate, maintain, and dispose of a vehicle over its entire economic life, minus the salvage value recovered at the end of service.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let the acquisition cost be $AC$ incurred at $t = 0$. Let the uniform annual operating + maintenance + fuel cost be $C_{om}$, incurred at the end of each year from $t = 1$ to $t = n$. Let the salvage value $SV$ be realized at the end of year $n$.

The present value at $t = 0$ of the annual operating cost series is:

$$PV_{OM} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_{om}}{(1+i)^t} = C_{om} \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

The present value of the salvage value (a future inflow, hence negative in cost) is:

$$PV_{SV} = \frac{SV}{(1+i)^n}$$

Therefore, the total LCC (as a positive cost, with SV as a deduction):

$$\boxed{LCC = AC + C_{om} \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} - \frac{SV}{(1+i)^n}}$$

**Significance of Discount Rate i (2.5 marks):**

1. **Time Value of Money:** Money spent or received in the future is worth less than the same amount today because of earning potential. The discount rate $i$ converts future cash flows to present value.
2. **Comparison of Alternatives:** Different vehicles have different life spans and cost patterns. Discounting enables apples-to-apples comparison.
3. **Inflation Adjustment:** Real discount rate $i_r = \dfrac{1+i_n}{1+f} - 1$, where $f$ is the inflation rate.
4. **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** The fleet manager's opportunity cost of capital. Higher $i$ penalizes long-life vehicles more heavily.
5. **Risk Premium:** A higher $i$ reflects higher uncertainty in future cost estimates.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- AC = ₹12,00,000
- SV = ₹2,00,000 at year 8
- $n = 8$ years
- $i = 8\% = 0.08$
- Annual fuel cost = ₹1,80,000 (uniform throughout)
- Annual maintenance cost: Year 1 = ₹80,000; increases by ₹15,000 per year (arithmetic gradient)

**Step 1 — Identify the cost series:**
- Uniform fuel cost component: $A = 1{,}80{,}000$ per year
- Maintenance cost: $M_1 = 80{,}000$ (year 1), with gradient $G = 15{,}000$

**Step 2 — Compute present value of fuel cost:**

$(1.08)^8 = 1.85093$

$$PWF(8\%, 8) = \frac{(1.08)^8 - 1}{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^8} = \frac{0.85093}{0.14807} = 5.7466$$

$$PV_{fuel} = 1{,}80{,}000 \cdot 5.7466 = \text{₹ } 10{,}34{,}388$$

**Step 3 — Compute present value of maintenance cost (Arithmetic Gradient):**

For an arithmetic gradient series with base $M_1$ at year 1 and increment $G$ each subsequent year, the present worth formula is:

$$PV_{gradient} = M_1 \cdot PWF(i, n) + G \cdot \frac{PWF(i, n) - n \cdot (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

Compute the gradient component:

$(1.08)^{-8} = \dfrac{1}{1.85093} = 0.54027$

$$PWF - n \cdot (1+i)^{-n} = 5.7466 - 8 \cdot 0.54027 = 5.7466 - 4.3222 = 1.4244$$

$$\frac{1.4244}{0.08} = 17.805$$

Now apply:

$$PV_{maint} = 80{,}000 \cdot 5.7466 + 15{,}000 \cdot 17.805$$

$$= 4{,}59{,}728 + 2{,}67{,}075 = \text{₹ } 7{,}26{,}803$$

**Step 4 — Compute present value of salvage value (deduction):**

$$PV_{SV} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.85093} = \text{₹ } 1{,}08{,}054$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total LCC:**

$$LCC = AC + PV_{fuel} + PV_{maint} - PV_{SV}$$

$$LCC = 12{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}34{,}388 + 7{,}26{,}803 - 1{,}08{,}054$$

$$\boxed{LCC = \text{₹ } 28{,}53{,}137}$$

**Interpretation:** Owning and operating this van for 8 years costs approximately ₹28.53 lakhs in today's money (at 8% discount rate).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to discount the salvage value** — Salvage is recovered at $t=n$, NOT at $t=0$. Always apply $(1+i)^{-n}$ to SV. (-2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Mixing up Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) and Sinking Fund Factor (SFF):** CRF converts present to annual; SFF converts future to annual. They are reciprocals only in special cases. (-1.5 marks)
> 
> 3. **Treating maintenance cost as uniform** when the problem states "increases by ₹X per year." Read the problem statement word-by-word. (-2 marks)
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the salvage value** entirely — a frequent mistake. SV is a negative cost (recovery), and forgetting it makes LCC inflated by 5–15%. (-1.5 marks)
> 
> 5. **Not stating units** in the final answer — examiners explicitly require "₹ lakhs" or "₹ per year" or "years". (-0.5 mark)
> 
> 6. **Wrong formula for arithmetic gradient:** The standard gradient series assumes $G$ is the increment between consecutive years, not the total cost. Read carefully.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ VLCM = **5 phases:** **P**lan, **P**rocure, **O**perate, **M**aintain, **D**ispose (mnemonic: **"PP-OMD"**).
- ✅ **LCC** includes AC + OC + MC + FC − SV, all discounted to present value at $t=0$.
- ✅ **EUAC = minimum** gives the **optimal economic life (n\*)** of the vehicle.
- ✅ **Iceberg Cost Principle:** Only ~20% of LCC is visible at purchase; ~80% is hidden.
- ✅ **Straight-Line Depreciation:** $D = (AC - SV)/n$ — uniform annual charge.
- ✅ **Declining Balance Depreciation:** $BV_t = AC \cdot (1-d)^t$ — accelerating charge.
- ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor:** $(A/P, i, n) = i(1+i)^n \big/ \big[(1+i)^n - 1\big]$ — converts present to annual.
- ✅ **Present Worth Factor:** $PWF = 1/(A/P, i, n)$ — converts annual to present.
- ✅ **Fuel cost** is the **single largest** operating-cost component (~40–50%).
- ✅ **Maintenance cost** rises **non-linearly (convex)** with vehicle age.
- ✅ **Salvage Value** is recovered at $t=n$ — always apply $(1+i)^{-n}$ discount.
- ✅ **MARR (i)** in India for fleet ops typically = **8–12%** real.
- ✅ **Vehicle Utilization Ratio** = Actual km / Available km × 100%.
- ✅ **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- ✅ India's **Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021** mandates fitness re-certification after **15 years** for commercial vehicles.
- ✅ Phase P5 **must feed back** to Phase P1 — VLCM is a **closed-loop** system, not linear.
- ✅ KTU numericals: **always state the formula, substitute step-by-step, show intermediate values, and write the final answer with units.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Extending Vehicle Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Extending Vehicle Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strategies for Extending Vehicle Life** refer to the systematic, engineered, and managerial methodologies employed by fleet operators to maximize the **Useful Service Life (USL)** of a vehicle while minimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** and maintaining peak operational availability. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, these strategies integrate **preventive maintenance scheduling**, **condition monitoring**, **predictive diagnostics**, **operator training**, and **lifecycle asset management** to defer the point of economic obsolescence.

Mathematically, the goal of any life-extension strategy is to shift the **Vehicle Life Curve** (decline in reliability/performance over time) rightward on the time axis, thereby increasing the area under the **Operational Availability curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Fleet managers are expected to demonstrate competency in identifying, evaluating, and applying life-extension strategies that align with the **Course Outcome (CO1)**: "Understand the fundamental principles of vehicle transport systems and fleet lifecycle management."

## 1.2 Key Performance Metrics

The following metrics are evaluated by KTU examiners when discussing life extension:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: Average operational time between consecutive breakdowns. Higher is better.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**: Average downtime for restoration. Lower is better.
- **Availability (A)**: The fraction of time a vehicle is operationally ready.
- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**: Sum of acquisition + operating + maintenance + disposal costs.
- **Useful Service Life (USL)**: Total operational time before economic retirement.
- **Depreciation Rate (D)**: The percentage of value lost per year.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Health Checkup" Model

Imagine a vehicle is a human body. If you never visit a doctor, ignore warning signs, eat poor fuel (junk food), and drive recklessly (sedentary lifestyle), your body will fail early. However, if you:

- Schedule regular health checkups (preventive maintenance),
- Run blood tests before symptoms appear (predictive diagnostics),
- Exercise gently and use good fuel (proper operation),
- Replace worn-out parts proactively (component renewal),

then your body operates for decades. Vehicle life extension follows the **exact same logic**: **proactive care, early detection, and disciplined operation** add years of productive service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** A truck is not a one-time purchase — it is a *long-term capital asset* whose profitability is directly proportional to the number of productive kilometres it generates before being scrapped. Every avoided breakdown adds revenue.

## 1.4 Categories of Life-Extension Strategies

The strategies can be classified into **four primary pillars** as recognized in KTU Module 1:

1. **Maintenance-Based Strategies** — Preventive, Predictive, and Condition-Based Maintenance.
2. **Operational Strategies** — Driver behavior, load management, route planning.
3. **Component Management Strategies** — Quality spares, fluid management, tire care.
4. **Administrative Strategies** — Documentation, warranty management, lifecycle tracking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1: Maintenance-Based Strategies

### 2.1.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM)
**Definition:** Time-based or mileage-based servicing performed at fixed intervals regardless of component condition.

- **Logic:** Replace components *before* they fail, since unscheduled downtime costs far more than scheduled servicing.
- **Examples:** Engine oil change every 10,000 km, brake pad inspection every 20,000 km, timing belt replacement at 100,000 km.
- **Why it works:** Most components follow a **bathtub curve** failure pattern — failure probability is highest early (infant mortality) and late (wear-out), but low in between. PM targets the wear-out phase.

### 2.1.2 Predictive Maintenance (PdM)
**Definition:** Maintenance triggered by *real-time condition data* from sensors, rather than fixed schedules.

- **Logic:** Use IoT sensors and analytics to predict failure *before* it occurs.
- **Techniques:** Oil analysis, vibration analysis, thermography, ultrasonic testing, telematics.
- **Advantage over PM:** Reduces unnecessary part replacement, increases component utilization.

### 2.1.3 Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
**Definition:** A subset of PdM where maintenance is performed only when monitored indicators show signs of degradation.

- **Example:** Replacing brake pads only when thickness falls below 3 mm (rather than at every 20,000 km).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to differentiate between PM, PdM, and CBM, students often confuse them. Remember: **PM = Calendar/Mileage based; CBM = Real-condition based; PdM = Sensor-data predicted based.** CBM is *reactive within a condition boundary*, while PdM is *proactive with forecasting*.

## 2.2 Pillar 2: Operational Strategies

### 2.2.1 Driver Behavior Management
- Aggressive acceleration, hard braking, and over-revving cause **up to 35% additional wear** on engine, brakes, and tires.
- **Eco-driving training** can reduce fuel consumption by 5–15% and extend component life.

### 2.2.2 Load Management
- Overloading stresses the chassis, suspension, transmission, and tires exponentially.
- The **Fourth Power Law of Fatigue** states that fatigue life is inversely proportional to the *fourth power* of stress amplitude — a small overload dramatically reduces life.

### 2.2.3 Route Planning & Idle Reduction
- Idling more than 10 seconds wastes more fuel than restarting the engine.
- Avoiding rough roads reduces suspension and tire wear.

## 2.3 Pillar 3: Component Management Strategies

- **Use OEM-specified fluids and filters** — cheap substitutes accelerate wear.
- **Tire rotation, alignment, and balancing** every 10,000 km extend tire life by 30%.
- **Battery care** — cleaning terminals, checking electrolyte, avoiding deep discharge.

## 2.4 Pillar 4: Administrative Strategies

- **Fleet Maintenance Records** — digital logbooks via Fleet Management Systems (FMS).
- **Warranty Management** — timely claims preserve capital.
- **Lifecycle Cost Tracking** — informs replacement decisions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Operational Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{down} + C_{disp}$ | Total Cost of Ownership | ₹ / USD |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{V_0 - V_n}{n \cdot V_0} \times 100$ | Depreciation Rate (% per year) | % per year |
| 4 | $C_{eq}(n) = \dfrac{C_{acq} - S_n + \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{maint,t}}{n}$ | Equivalent Annual Cost | ₹ / USD per year |
| 5 | $n^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{acq}}{g}$ | Optimal Replacement Age (constant gradient cost) | years |
| 6 | $\text{Fatigue Life} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sigma^4}$ | Fourth Power Fatigue Law | cycles |
| 7 | $\eta_{maint} = \dfrac{\text{Avoided Failures}}{\text{PM Actions}} \times 100$ | Maintenance Effectiveness | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, the **vertical bar symbol** has been replaced with `\vert` in line 4 of LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. The term $S_n$ denotes the **salvage value** at year $n$, while $C_{maint,t}$ is the **maintenance cost in year $t$**, and $g$ represents the **annual linear gradient** of operating cost increase.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

These strategies are deployed by every major commercial fleet operator worldwide:
- **Logistics companies** (Amazon, DHL, FedEx) use telematics-based PdM.
- **Public transport buses** in BMTC, KSRTC, and BEST follow strict PM schedules.
- **Mining fleets** use vibration-based CBM for haul trucks worth crores.
- **Ride-sharing platforms** (Uber, Ola) use OBD-II data to enforce driver behavior scoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Replacement Age (Minimum Cost Point)

The KTU examiner frequently tests the **Economic Replacement Model**, where a fleet manager must decide *when* to replace a vehicle so that the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** is minimized.

### Step 1 — Define Acquisition Cost
Let the purchase cost of the vehicle be $C_{acq}$ (₹). This is a one-time capital outflow at year zero.

### Step 2 — Define Maintenance Cost Growth
As the vehicle ages, maintenance costs typically grow in **arithmetic progression** due to wear-out of components.

$$C_{maint}(t) = g \cdot t$$

where $g$ is the **annual maintenance cost gradient** (₹ per year, per year). In other words, maintenance cost grows linearly with age $t$.

### Step 3 — Define Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)
The **Equivalent Annual Cost** spreads the total lifecycle cost evenly across the years of service. For a vehicle held for $n$ years with salvage value $S_n$:

$$C_{eq}(n) = \dfrac{C_{acq} - S_n + \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{maint,t}}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Maintenance Sum
The sum of an arithmetic series is given by $\sum_{t=1}^{n} t = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$. Substituting:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{maint,t} = g \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Assume Salvage Value is Small
For simplification, we initially assume $S_n \approx 0$ (vehicle is scrapped with negligible value). Then:

$$C_{eq}(n) = \dfrac{C_{acq} + g \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}}{n}$$

### Step 6 — Simplify the Expression
Dividing numerator by $n$:

$$C_{eq}(n) = \dfrac{C_{acq}}{n} + g \cdot \dfrac{(n+1)}{2}$$

### Step 7 — Differentiate with Respect to $n$
To find the **minimum**, we differentiate $C_{eq}(n)$ with respect to $n$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{d \cdot C_{eq}(n)}{dn} = -\dfrac{C_{acq}}{n^2} + \dfrac{g}{2} = 0$$

### Step 8 — Solve for $n^*$
Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{C_{acq}}{n^2} = \dfrac{g}{2}$$

$$n^2 = \dfrac{2 \cdot C_{acq}}{g}$$

$$\boxed{n^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{acq}}{g}}}$$

### Step 9 — Interpretation
- The **optimal replacement age** $n^*$ is the year at which the EAC is minimized.
- If $n < n^*$, the vehicle is replaced *too early* — capital cost dominates.
- If $n > n^*$, the vehicle is kept *too long* — accumulated maintenance cost dominates.
- **Strategic insight:** Proper PM and CBM *reduce* $g$ (slower cost growth), which in turn **increases** $n^*$, allowing longer service life.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
**Problem:** A fleet truck costs ₹20,00,000. Maintenance costs grow at ₹1,00,000 per year per year ($g = 100{,}000$). Salvage value is negligible. Find the optimal replacement age.

**Solution:**

$$n^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2{,}000{,}000}{100{,}000}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 \text{ years}$$

**Verification by tabulation:**

| $n$ (years) | $C_{eq}(n)$ (₹ lakhs) |
|-------------|------------------------|
| 5 | $4.00 + 3.00 = 7.00$ |
| 6 | $3.33 + 3.50 = 6.83$ |
| 7 | $2.86 + 4.00 = 6.86$ |
| 8 | $2.50 + 4.50 = 7.00$ |

The minimum is at $n = 6$–$7$ years, confirming $n^* \approx 6.32$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Strategy vs. Failure Type

| Failure Type | Best Strategy | Reasoning |
|--------------|---------------|-----------|
| Wear-out (piston ring, brake pad) | **Preventive Maintenance** | Failure is age/mileage predictable |
| Random electronic failure (ECU) | **Predictive Maintenance** | Time-based PM wastes money on non-wear parts |
| Tire blowout | **CBM + Driver Training** | Condition is visible; abrupt driver inputs accelerate wear |
| Engine overheating | **Telematics + Coolant Mgmt** | Sensor data warns *before* seizure |
| Transmission failure | **PM + Oil Analysis** | Lubricant degradation is a leading indicator |

## 3.3 Cost-Benefit Logic of Life Extension

Each life-extension action has a **Cost ($C_a$)** and an **Avoided Failure Cost ($C_{af}$)**. The action is justified when:

$$C_a < C_{af} \times P_{fail}$$

where $P_{fail}$ is the probability of failure *without* the action. This **Risk-Weighted Payoff** is the foundation of fleet maintenance economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Life-Extension Strategy — Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["VEHICLE ACQUIRED"] --> node2["OPERATIONAL PHASE BEGINS"]
    node2 --> node3{"MONITOR VEHICLE CONDITION"}
    node3 --> node4["TELEMATICS AND OBD DATA"]
    node3 --> node5["DRIVER BEHAVIOR SCORE"]
    node3 --> node6["FLUID AND TIRE STATUS"]
    node4 --> node7{"TRIGGER MAINTENANCE"}
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8["PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE PM"]
    node7 --> node9["PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE PDM"]
    node7 --> node10["CONDITION BASED MAINTENANCE CBM"]
    node8 --> node11["RECORD IN FMS"]
    node9 --> node11
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12{"USEFUL SERVICE LIFE REMAINING"}
    node12 --> node13["CONTINUE OPERATION"] --> node3
    node12 --> node14["DECOMMISSION OR RESELL"] --> node15["END OF LIFE"]
```

## 4.2 Maintenance Strategy Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START DECISION"] --> nodeB["IDENTIFY COMPONENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"IS COMPONENT WEAR PREDICTABLE"}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD["USE PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE"]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE{"ARE SENSORS AVAILABLE"}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF["USE PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE"]
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG["USE CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["SCHEDULE AT FIXED INTERVALS"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["MONITOR REAL TIME DATA"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["REPAIR AFTER FAILURE"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["LOG AND OPTIMIZE"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Lifecycle Cost Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["ACQUISITION COST HIGH AT YEAR 0"] --> nodeQ["OPERATING COST RISES GRADUALLY"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["MAINTENANCE COST RISES EXPONENTIALLY AFTER 60 PERCENT LIFE"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["DOWN TIME COST PEAKS NEAR END OF LIFE"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["DISPOSAL OR RESALE COST"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["LIFECYCLE ENDS REPLACEMENT DECISION"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fleet Maintenance System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeV1["DATA ACQUISITION LAYER OBD SENSORS GPS TIRE PRESSURE"] --> nodeV2["COMMUNICATION LAYER GSM 4G 5G CLOUD UPLINK"]
    nodeV2 --> nodeV3["ANALYTICS LAYER ML MODELS ANOMALY DETECTION"]
    nodeV3 --> nodeV4["DECISION LAYER MAINTENANCE TRIGGER WORK ORDER"]
    nodeV4 --> nodeV5["ACTION LAYER WORKSHOP EXECUTION SPARE PARTS"]
    nodeV5 --> nodeV6["FEEDBACK LAYER FMS RECORDS KPI DASHBOARD"]
    nodeV6 --> nodeV1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture shown is a **closed-loop feedback system** where data continuously refines the maintenance strategy. This is the modern Industry 4.0 approach to fleet life extension, moving beyond the older calendar-based PM model.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
Define **Preventive Maintenance (PM)**. How does it differ from **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Preventive Maintenance (PM) is a scheduled maintenance activity performed at fixed time or mileage intervals, irrespective of the component's current condition, to prevent unexpected failures. Examples include oil change every 10,000 km and timing belt replacement at 100,000 km. **(1 mark)**

Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM) is a maintenance strategy where actions are triggered only when monitored indicators (e.g., brake pad thickness, oil degradation, vibration levels) cross predefined thresholds. **(1 mark)**

The key difference is the **trigger**: PM is **calendar/mileage-driven**, while CBM is **real-condition-driven**. CBM reduces unnecessary part replacement, while PM ensures reliability even when monitoring is not feasible. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
State and explain the **Fourth Power Law of Fatigue** in the context of vehicle life extension.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Fourth Power Law of Fatigue states that the fatigue life ($N$) of a metal component is **inversely proportional to the fourth power of the cyclic stress amplitude** ($\sigma$). **(1 mark)**

$$N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sigma^4}$$

This means that a **25% increase in load** reduces fatigue life by approximately **$1.5^4 \approx 5$ times**. **(1 mark)**

In fleet management, this law justifies strict **load management** and **speed restrictions**, since even small overloads drastically accelerate structural fatigue in axles, springs, and chassis members. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four pillars of vehicle life-extension strategies**. Discuss **Preventive, Predictive, and Condition-Based Maintenance** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial truck was purchased for **₹15,00,000**. The annual maintenance cost is observed to grow by **₹80,000 per year** (gradient $g$). Salvage value is negligible. Determine the **optimal replacement age** and the **minimum equivalent annual cost**. Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
The four pillars of vehicle life extension are:

1. **Maintenance-Based Strategies** (4 marks)
   - **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Time/mileage-based servicing. Example: Oil change at 10,000 km intervals. (1 mark)
   - **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Sensor-data-based forecasting. Example: Vibration analysis on wheel bearings to detect inner race defects. (1 mark)
   - **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM):** Real-condition-based action. Example: Replacing brake pads only when thickness < 3 mm. (1 mark)
   - **Comparison:** PM is rigid but reliable; CBM is efficient; PdM is the most advanced but sensor-intensive. (1 mark)

2. **Operational Strategies** (1 mark)
   - Driver training, eco-driving, load management, idle reduction.

3. **Component Management Strategies** (1 mark)
   - OEM-quality fluids, filters, tires, and batteries.

4. **Administrative Strategies** (1 mark)
   - FMS records, warranty tracking, lifecycle cost analysis.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Given:** $C_{acq} = 15{,}00{,}000$, $g = 80{,}000$, $S_n \approx 0$

**Step 1 — Apply the Optimal Replacement Formula:**

$$n^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 15{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000}}$$

$$n^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000}} = \sqrt{37.5}$$

$$n^* \approx 6.12 \text{ years} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Minimum Equivalent Annual Cost:**

$$C_{eq}(n^*) = \dfrac{C_{acq}}{n^*} + g \cdot \dfrac{(n^* + 1)}{2}$$

$$C_{eq}(6.12) = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{6.12} + 80{,}000 \times \dfrac{7.12}{2}$$

$$C_{eq}(6.12) = 2{,}45{,}098 + 2{,}84{,}800$$

$$C_{eq}(6.12) \approx ₹5{,}29{,}898 \text{ per year} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Verification Table:**

| $n$ (years) | $C_{acq}/n$ | $g(n+1)/2$ | $C_{eq}(n)$ (₹) |
|-------------|-------------|------------|------------------|
| 5 | 3,00,000 | 2,40,000 | 5,40,000 |
| 6 | 2,50,000 | 2,80,000 | 5,30,000 |
| 7 | 2,14,286 | 3,20,000 | 5,34,286 |
| 8 | 1,87,500 | 3,60,000 | 5,47,500 |

The minimum occurs near $n = 6$, confirming $n^* \approx 6.12$ years. **(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** The fleet manager should plan to replace the truck at approximately **6 years** to minimize annual lifecycle cost. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **driver behavior, load management, and telematics** in extending vehicle life. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fleet operator compares two maintenance policies. Policy A (PM) costs ₹2,00,000/year and reduces breakdown probability from 0.20 to 0.05. Policy B (PdM) costs ₹3,50,000/year and reduces breakdown probability to 0.02. The average breakdown cost is ₹15,00,000. Which policy is more economical using **Expected Cost Analysis**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- **Driver Behavior (3 marks):** Aggressive driving (hard braking, rapid acceleration, over-revving) accelerates wear on brakes, clutch, tires, and engine. Studies show eco-driving can reduce component wear by up to 35% and fuel use by 5–15%. Training programs with telematics-linked scoring improve driver behavior significantly.
- **Load Management (2 marks):** Overloading stresses the chassis, axles, and suspension. By the Fourth Power Law, a 20% overload reduces fatigue life by $1.2^4 \approx 2.07$ times. Strict adherence to Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) limits is essential.
- **Telematics (2 marks):** Real-time GPS, OBD-II, and IoT sensors enable remote diagnostics, fuel monitoring, driver scoring, and route optimization. Telematics forms the data backbone of modern PdM and CBM strategies.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Step 1 — Expected Cost Formula:**

$$E(C) = C_{policy} + P_{fail} \times C_{breakdown}$$

**Step 2 — Policy A (PM):**

$$E(C)_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 0.05 \times 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$E(C)_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 75{,}000 = ₹2{,}75{,}000 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Policy B (PdM):**

$$E(C)_B = 3{,}50{,}000 + 0.02 \times 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$E(C)_B = 3{,}50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = ₹3{,}80{,}000 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Do-Nothing Baseline:**

$$E(C)_{none} = 0 + 0.20 \times 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}00{,}000 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

| Policy | Annual Expected Cost |
|--------|----------------------|
| No Maintenance | ₹3,00,000 |
| Policy A (PM) | ₹2,75,000 |
| Policy B (PdM) | ₹3,80,000 |

**Policy A (PM) is the most economical** with the lowest expected annual cost. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to include the **policy cost** in the expected cost calculation and only multiply breakdown probability by breakdown cost, leading to an **incorrect conclusion that PdM is always best.** Always compute $E(C) = C_{policy} + P_{fail} \times C_{breakdown}$ before comparing. Also, do not confuse the **PM cost** (recurring) with the **acquisition cost** (one-time) when tabulating the EAC.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PM is time-based; CBM is condition-based; PdM is data-predicted.** (Most tested distinction.)
- **MTBF ↑** and **MTTR ↓** = higher availability.
- **Fourth Power Law:** $\text{Life} \propto 1/\sigma^4$ — a 20% overload halves fatigue life.
- **Optimal replacement age** $n^* = \sqrt{2C_{acq}/g}$ balances capital and maintenance cost.
- **EAC formula:** $C_{eq}(n) = (C_{acq} - S_n + \sum C_{maint,t}) / n$.
- **Expected Cost Analysis:** $E(C) = C_{policy} + P_{fail} \cdot C_{breakdown}$.
- **Four Pillars:** Maintenance / Operational / Component / Administrative.
- **Telematics + IoT** form the modern data backbone for life extension.
- **Eco-driving** can extend component life by up to **35%** and cut fuel use by **5–15%**.
- **Salvage value $S_n$** is often assumed negligible in KTU problems — but always check the question.
- **Driver behavior, load management, and tire care** are *low-cost* life-extension levers with *high ROI*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disposal and Resale of Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Disposal and Resale of Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Fleet Management**, *vehicle disposal* refers to the systematic, planned withdrawal of a vehicle from active operational service after it has reached the end of its *economic life*, *useful life*, or *contractual life* (in the case of leased units). *Vehicle resale* is the monetised transfer of ownership of the disposed asset — either to a dealer, an employee, a third-party buyer, an auction house, or a scrap dealer — to recover the **residual / salvage value** of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam-Ready Definition:**
> *Vehicle disposal is the managerial decision-making process that determines **when** a fleet asset should be retired from service, **how** it should be retired, and **at what price** its residual value can be recovered, while ensuring regulatory compliance (RTO transfer, GST, hazardous-waste rules) and minimum loss of capital to the fleet owner.*

### Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Capital Cost ($C$)** | Purchase price of the vehicle (excluding registration). |
| **Salvage Value ($S_n$)** | Estimated resale value at the end of year $n$. |
| **Book Value ($BV_n$)** | Capital cost minus accumulated depreciation up to year $n$. |
| **Depreciation ($D$)** | Loss in value of the asset over time. |
| **Economic Life** | The age at which the *average annual cost* of ownership is minimum. |
| **Useful Life** | The period over which the asset is operationally productive. |
| **Write-Off** | Removing the asset from the books because its book value has fallen below a threshold. |
| **Residual Value** | The end-of-life market value of the asset. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Life $\neq$ Physical Life.** A vehicle may still *physically* run, but its **economic life** ends earlier because maintenance and fuel inefficiency costs rise faster than its depreciation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Old Two-Wheeler"

Imagine you bought a bicycle for ₹15,000 five years ago. After five years, it still *works*, but:
- The chain, brake pads and tyres cost ₹4,000 a year.
- You can sell it on OLX for maybe ₹2,500.

If you keep it another year, you spend ₹4,000 in repairs and your resale value drops further to ₹1,500 (a loss of ₹1,000). The cost of "keeping it one more year" = ₹5,000. But a *new* bicycle costs ₹15,000, and you can sell the old one for ₹2,500 — so the cost of "replacing it now" = ₹12,500.

Clearly, **repairing is cheaper than replacing** — so you *keep* it. But two years later, when maintenance hits ₹10,000 a year and resale value drops by ₹2,000 annually, suddenly *"the cost of keeping"* (₹12,000) > *"the cost of replacing"* (₹12,500). That crossover point is your **optimal disposal age**.

This crossover logic is exactly what professional fleet managers formalise using **Minimum Cost Analysis** — the mathematical heart of *Disposal & Resale of Vehicles*.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for **OEMUT835** evaluates this topic against:
> - **CO1 (Module 1)** — *Recall the fundamental concepts of fleet management including acquisition, operation, and disposal of vehicles.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse*.
> - **Typical weightage:** 14–20 % of the End-Semester paper (Module 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Disposal Decision Framework

A fleet manager's disposal decision rests on three pillars:

1. **When to dispose?** → *Replacement Analysis* (Economic Life determination)
2. **How to dispose?** → *Disposal Method Selection* (Trade-in / Auction / Scrap / etc.)
3. **At what price?** → *Depreciation & Valuation Models*

## 2.2 Pillar 1 — Optimal Replacement (Economic Life) Theory

The vehicle should be replaced at year $n$ where the **Average Annual Cost (AAC)** is minimum.

### 2.2.1 Total Cost of Ownership over $n$ Years

The total cost of owning a vehicle from year 1 to year $n$ is:

$$TC(n) = C + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k - S_n$$

where $M_k$ is the maintenance + operating cost in year $k$.

### 2.2.2 Average Annual Cost (AAC)

$$AAC(n) = \frac{TC(n)}{n} = \frac{C - S_n}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k$$

The first term represents *capital recovery per year*; the second term represents *average operating cost per year*.

### 2.2.3 Minimum-Cost Optimality Condition

The economic life is found where $AAC$ is minimised. A more practical **marginal (year-on-year) check** is:

$$\text{Replace at end of year } n \;\Longleftrightarrow\; M_{n+1} + (S_n - S_{n+1}) > C_{\text{new}} - S_n$$

In words: *Replace when the **cost of keeping one more year** (next year's maintenance plus loss in resale value) exceeds the **cost of buying a new vehicle now**.*

> [!NOTE]
> This condition is what every KTU numerical problem on **"optimum replacement age"** is built on. Memorise the formula and the inequality sign.

## 2.3 Pillar 2 — Methods of Computing Depreciation

Depreciation quantifies the asset's loss in value and directly impacts the resale price.

### 2.3.1 Straight Line Method (SLM)

$$D_n = \frac{C - S_N}{N}$$

where $D_n$ = annual depreciation, $N$ = useful life in years. Book value at end of year $n$:

$$BV_n = C - n \cdot D_n$$

### 2.3.2 Declining Balance Method (DBM)

A fixed *rate* $r$ is applied to the *diminishing book value* every year:

$$BV_n = C(1 - r)^n$$

For double-declining balance: $r = \frac{2}{N}$.

### 2.3.3 Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits Method (SYD)

$$D_n = \frac{(N - n + 1)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} i} \cdot (C - S_N) = \frac{2(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)} \cdot (C - S_N)$$

This method *front-loads* depreciation — heaviest in year 1, lightest in year $N$.

### 2.3.4 Sinking Fund Method

Annual depreciation = capital-recovery component + sinking-fund interest component. The book value is:

$$BV_n = C \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - (1+i)^n}{(1+i)^N - 1}$$

where $i$ is the annual interest rate.

## 2.4 Pillar 3 — Methods of Disposal

| # | Method | Best Suited For | Advantages | Limitations |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Trade-in to Dealer** | New-vehicle replacement | Quick, convenient, may reduce new-vehicle price | Lower recovery than direct sale |
| 2 | **Public Auction** | Mid-life vehicles with active resale market | Competitive bidding → best market price | Time-consuming, administrative cost |
| 3 | **Direct Sale to Staff** | Small fleets, goodwill gesture | Fast, no commission | May be undervalued, internal bias |
| 4 | **Scrap / Salvage** | End-of-life, accident-write-off vehicles | Zero further maintenance liability | Very low realisation |
| 5 | **Export Sale** | Specific vehicle categories (used commercial vehicles) | Higher realisation for older assets | Compliance with Export-Import policy |
| 6 | **Dismantling for Parts** | Vehicles with reusable aggregates (engine, gearbox, tyres) | Recovers part-level value, environmentally sound | Needs workshop + spares inventory |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table.** It is the *single most important* reference for any KTU 2024 numerical on disposal.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Straight Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{C - S_N}{N}$ | $C$ = cost, $S_N$ = salvage, $N$ = life |
| 2 | Book Value (SLM) | $BV_n = C - nD$ | $n$ = age in years |
| 3 | Declining Balance Book Value | $BV_n = C(1-r)^n$ | $r$ = depreciation rate |
| 4 | Sum-of-Years'-Digits | $D_n = \dfrac{2(N-n+1)}{N(N+1)}(C-S_N)$ | $N$ = useful life |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Book Value | $BV_n = C \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N-(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^N-1}$ | $i$ = interest rate |
| 6 | Total Cost over $n$ years | $TC(n) = C + \sum M_k - S_n$ | $M_k$ = year-$k$ maintenance |
| 7 | Average Annual Cost | $AAC(n) = \dfrac{C - S_n}{n} + \dfrac{1}{n}\sum M_k$ | $n$ = candidate age |
| 8 | Optimal Replacement | Replace if $M_{n+1} + S_n - S_{n+1} > C_{\text{new}} - S_n$ | year-on-year check |
| 9 | Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | used in equivalent annual cost |
| 10 | Loss on Disposal | $\text{Loss} = BV_n - \text{Sale Price}$ | compared to NBV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real fleet operations, the *Minimum-Cost / Economic-Life* formula is used to decide whether to keep a taxi, truck, or bus for 5 years or 7 years. The *Sum-of-Years'-Digits* method is used for tax-reporting by Indian companies (Schedule II of Companies Act, 2013). The *Declining-Balance* method suits vehicles with rapid early obsolescence (battery EVs, technology-heavy trucks).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Annual Cost Equation

### Step 1 — Define Total Cost Function

The total cost of holding the vehicle from year 1 through year $n$ includes the initial capital outlay, all intermediate maintenance costs, and is offset by the salvage realised at the end of year $n$.

$$\begin{aligned}
TC(n) &= \underbrace{C}_{\text{capital paid year 0}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k}_{\text{maintenance over n years}} - \underbrace{S_n}_{\text{salvage recovered at year n}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Average Annual Cost

Divide $TC(n)$ by $n$ (number of years of service):

$$\begin{aligned}
AAC(n) &= \frac{TC(n)}{n} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{C - S_n}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Marginal Replacement Rule (Discrete Form)

For two consecutive candidate ages $n$ and $n+1$, we **keep** the vehicle at age $n$ if $AAC(n) \le AAC(n+1)$. Expanding this inequality gives the **Marginal Decision Rule**:

$$\begin{aligned}
AAC(n) &\le AAC(n+1) \\[4pt]
\frac{C - S_n}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k &\le \frac{C - S_{n+1}}{n+1} + \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{k=1}^{n+1} M_k
\end{aligned}$$

After algebraic simplification (KTU students are *not* expected to redo this in the exam — but they must *understand* it), the rule reduces to:

$$\boxed{\;M_{n+1} + S_n - S_{n+1} \;\le\; C_{\text{new}} - S_n\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the box:**
> *Left side* = extra cost of **keeping** one more year (next year's maintenance + drop in resale value).
> *Right side* = extra cost of **replacing now** (price of new vehicle minus current salvage).
> **Replace** when the inequality flips.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — "Optimum Replacement Age of a Truck"

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question)

A transport company purchases a truck for **₹ 5,00,000**. The company estimates the following annual maintenance costs and resale values:

| Year ($n$) | Maintenance Cost $M_n$ (₹) | Resale Value $S_n$ (₹) |
| :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | 10,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 2 | 15,000 | 3,10,000 |
| 3 | 25,000 | 2,30,000 |
| 4 | 40,000 | 1,60,000 |
| 5 | 60,000 | 1,00,000 |

The price of a *new equivalent* truck is also **₹ 5,00,000**. Determine the **optimum replacement age** of the truck using the *Average Annual Cost* method.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Set Up the AAC Formula**

We use:
$$AAC(n) = \frac{C - S_n}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k$$

where $C = 5{,}00{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Compute for Year 1**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital recovery} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{1} = 1{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg maintenance} &= \frac{10{,}000}{1} = 10{,}000 \\[4pt]
AAC(1) &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}10{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for Year 2**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital recovery} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}10{,}000}{2} = 95{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cum. maint.} &= 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg maintenance} &= \frac{25{,}000}{2} = 12{,}500 \\[4pt]
AAC(2) &= 95{,}000 + 12{,}500 = \mathbf{1{,}07{,}500}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute for Year 3**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital recovery} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}30{,}000}{3} = 90{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cum. maint.} &= 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg maintenance} &= \frac{50{,}000}{3} \approx 16{,}667 \\[4pt]
AAC(3) &= 90{,}000 + 16{,}667 = \mathbf{1{,}06{,}667}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute for Year 4**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital recovery} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000}{4} = 85{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cum. maint.} &= 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 90{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg maintenance} &= \frac{90{,}000}{4} = 22{,}500 \\[4pt]
AAC(4) &= 85{,}000 + 22{,}500 = \mathbf{1{,}07{,}500}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute for Year 5**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital recovery} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{5} = 80{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cum. maint.} &= 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg maintenance} &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{5} = 30{,}000 \\[4pt]
AAC(5) &= 80{,}000 + 30{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}10{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Tabulate and Identify Minimum**

| Year $n$ | $\dfrac{C-S_n}{n}$ (₹) | Avg. Maint. (₹) | $AAC(n)$ (₹) |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | 1,00,000 | 10,000 | **1,10,000** |
| 2 | 95,000 | 12,500 | **1,07,500** |
| 3 | 90,000 | 16,667 | **1,06,667 ← MIN** |
| 4 | 85,000 | 22,500 | **1,07,500** |
| 5 | 80,000 | 30,000 | **1,10,000** |

**Step 8 — Conclude**

The **Average Annual Cost is minimum at year 3** (₹ 1,06,667).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** The truck should be **replaced after 3 years of service**. After year 3, the rising maintenance costs outweigh the benefits of continued capital recovery, and the AAC begins to climb.

## 3.3 Cross-Check Using the Marginal Rule

Let us verify with the *year-on-year* rule at $n = 3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of keeping one more year} &= M_4 + S_3 - S_4 = 40{,}000 + (2{,}30{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000) = 1{,}10{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cost of replacing now} &= C_{\text{new}} - S_3 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}30{,}000 = 2{,}70{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Since } 1{,}10{,}000 < 2{,}70{,}000 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Keep the truck for year 4.}
\end{aligned}$$

At $n = 4$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of keeping} &= M_5 + S_4 - S_5 = 60{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Cost of replacing} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Still } 1{,}20{,}000 < 3{,}40{,}000 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Keep for year 5.}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this problem the *marginal rule* suggests keeping up to year 5, while the *AAC method* suggests year 3. **The two methods can give slightly different answers** because the marginal rule is *local* (year-by-year), whereas the AAC rule is *global* (looks at the whole lifetime). In KTU exams, the **AAC method** is the standard answer unless the question specifically asks for the marginal check.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Which Method

| Criterion | Best Disposal Choice |
| :--- | :--- |
| Vehicle < 3 years old, good condition | **Direct sale / Trade-in** for best price |
| Vehicle 4–6 years old, high mileage | **Auction** to attract competitive bidders |
| Vehicle > 8 years old, unreliable | **Scrap / Salvage** to recover base metal value |
| Vehicle with rare aggregates (vintage, EV) | **Dismantling for parts** for high part-level recovery |
| Vehicle with pending loan | **Trade-in** to authorised dealer (handles NOC) |
| Vehicle with no resale market | **Export sale** to African / SAARC markets |
| Fleet size > 50 vehicles | **Public auction** (efficiency of scale) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the term "optimum replacement age" of a vehicle. State the factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The *optimum replacement age* of a vehicle is the age (in years or kilometres) at which the **Average Annual Cost (AAC)** of owning the vehicle is minimum, considering capital recovery, interest on capital, and operating/maintenance costs.

**Factors influencing it:**

1. **Maintenance and repair cost pattern** — rising non-linearly with age.
2. **Resale value pattern** — falling non-linearly with age.
3. **Utilisation pattern** — annual kilometres run.
4. **Technological obsolescence** — newer models may offer better fuel efficiency.
5. **Reliability and downtime** — older vehicles spend more time in the workshop.
6. **Operating conditions** — terrain, load, climate.

*(Mark Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 4 factors.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between "salvage value" and "book value" of a vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Salvage Value ($S_n$) | Book Value ($BV_n$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Meaning | Realisable market price at end of useful life | Accounting value remaining in books |
| Basis | Market estimate | Cost minus accumulated depreciation |
| Influenced by | Demand, condition, market | Depreciation method used |
| Time | Only at disposal | Every year of life |
| Use | Pricing the resale | Tax computation, financial statements |

*(Mark Allocation: 1.5 marks for each column with 2–3 distinguishing points.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme follows *Module Internal Choice* in the End-Semester Examination. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain any **three methods of computing depreciation** of a fleet vehicle with suitable formulas. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A mini-bus was purchased for **₹ 8,00,000**. The expected maintenance cost and resale values are given below. Determine the **optimum replacement age** using the *Average Annual Cost Method*. *(7 Marks)*

| Year ($n$) | Maintenance Cost $M_n$ (₹) | Resale Value $S_n$ (₹) |
| :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | 12,000 | 6,50,000 |
| 2 | 18,000 | 5,20,000 |
| 3 | 28,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 4 | 44,000 | 2,90,000 |
| 5 | 65,000 | 1,90,000 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Straight Line Method (SLM)**
$$D = \frac{C - S_N}{N}$$
Equal depreciation every year. Simple; suitable for assets with uniform wear. *Disadvantage:* ignores the fact that vehicles lose value faster in early years.

**2. Declining Balance Method (DBM)**
$$BV_n = C(1-r)^n$$
Depreciation is a fixed *percentage* of the *diminishing* book value. Suitable for vehicles with rapid early obsolescence (taxis, EVs). *Disadvantage:* book value never reaches zero unless a switch-over is made.

**3. Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits Method (SYD)**
$$D_n = \frac{2(N-n+1)}{N(N+1)} \cdot (C - S_N)$$
Front-loaded depreciation; high in year 1, low in year $N$. Matches the *actual* market depreciation curve better than SLM.

*(7 Marks: 2 marks per method for formula + 1 mark for advantages/disadvantages.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Apply:
$$AAC(n) = \frac{C - S_n}{n} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k, \quad C = 8{,}00{,}000$$

**Year 1:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cap. Recovery} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000}{1} = 1{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg. Maint.} &= 12{,}000 \\[4pt]
AAC(1) &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 12{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}62{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 2:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cap. Recovery} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}20{,}000}{2} = 1{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg. Maint.} &= \frac{12{,}000 + 18{,}000}{2} = 15{,}000 \\[4pt]
AAC(2) &= 1{,}40{,}000 + 15{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}55{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 3:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cap. Recovery} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{3} = 1{,}33{,}333 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg. Maint.} &= \frac{12{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 28{,}000}{3} = \frac{58{,}000}{3} \approx 19{,}333 \\[4pt]
AAC(3) &= 1{,}33{,}333 + 19{,}333 = \mathbf{1{,}52{,}666 \; \leftarrow \; MIN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 4:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cap. Recovery} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}90{,}000}{4} = 1{,}27{,}500 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg. Maint.} &= \frac{12{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 44{,}000}{4} = \frac{1{,}02{,}000}{4} = 25{,}500 \\[4pt]
AAC(4) &= 1{,}27{,}500 + 25{,}500 = \mathbf{1{,}53{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Year 5:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cap. Recovery} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}90{,}000}{5} = 1{,}22{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Avg. Maint.} &= \frac{12{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 44{,}000 + 65{,}000}{5} = \frac{1{,}67{,}000}{5} = 33{,}400 \\[4pt]
AAC(5) &= 1{,}22{,}000 + 33{,}400 = \mathbf{1{,}55{,}400}
\end{aligned}$$

| Year | AAC (₹) |
| :-: | :-: |
| 1 | 1,62,000 |
| 2 | 1,55,000 |
| **3** | **1,52,666 ← MIN** |
| 4 | 1,53,000 |
| 5 | 1,55,400 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The mini-bus should be **replaced after 3 years of service**.

*(7 Marks: 1 mark for formula, 5 marks for calculation, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** List and briefly explain the **various methods of disposal of vehicles** in a fleet. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **documentation, legal, and tax compliance steps** that must be completed before a fleet vehicle can be lawfully sold or scrapped in India. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major methods of disposal are:

1. **Trade-in to Authorised Dealer** — Fleet owner hands over the old vehicle as part-payment for a new vehicle. Quick, but resale value is typically 8–12 % lower than the open market price.

2. **Public Auction** — Vehicle is offered to multiple bidders. Open, transparent, and usually fetches a competitive price. Suited for medium-age vehicles.

3. **Direct Sale to Staff / Employees** — Offered at a *notional* price to company employees. Promotes goodwill, ensures transparency if rates are uniform.

4. **Scrap / Salvage** — Vehicle is sold by weight to scrap dealers for its base metal (steel, aluminium) value. Used for end-of-life, accident-write-off, or non-roadworthy vehicles.

5. **Export Sale** — Vehicles (often older commercial vehicles, buses) are exported to neighbouring countries (Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, African nations). Requires RTO NOC, customs clearance, and *one-time* export permit.

6. **Dismantling for Parts** — Salvageable aggregates (engine, gearbox, axles, body panels, tyres, batteries) are removed, refurbished, and sold as spare parts. Requires an organised dismantling yard and compliance with *Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016*.

*(7 Marks: 1 mark per method × 6 methods + 1 mark for comparison.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The legal-compliance checklist before any fleet vehicle disposal in India includes:

| # | Compliance Step | Authority / Form | Purpose |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Issue **Form 29** (Notice of Transfer of Ownership) | RTO | Records transfer intent |
| 2 | Issue **Form 30** (Application for Transfer) | RTO | Submitted by buyer |
| 3 | Submit **NOC from Financier** (if under hire-purchase / loan) | Bank / NBFC | Releases hypothecation |
| 4 | Cancel / transfer **Insurance Policy** (Form 51 or new policy) | Insurer (IRDA-regulated) | Avoids post-sale claims |
| 5 | Cancel **Fitness Certificate** (for transport vehicles) | RTO | Removes road-use eligibility |
| 6 | Issue **Pollution Under Control (PUC) Cancellation** | PUC Centre | Required for scrap |
| 7 | Update **Asset Register / Fixed Asset Schedule** | Company books | Tax & audit compliance |
| 8 | Issue **Tax Invoice** for sale | Seller | GST compliance |
| 9 | File **GST Return** with sale value | GST Network | Section 9 of CGST Act |
| 10 | Maintain records for **Income Tax** (Section 43(6) — block of assets) | Income Tax Dept. | Depreciation recapture |
| 11 | For scrap: deposit vehicle at **authorised scrappage centre** | MoRTH-registered centre | Ensures compliance with Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021 |
| 12 | For export: **Customs clearance + re-registration** | Customs + Embassy attestation | Cross-border legality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021 (India):** Commercial vehicles > 15 years and personal vehicles > 20 years *must* undergo fitness testing; failure leads to mandatory scrapping. This is a *frequently-asked* KTU question under Module 1.

*(7 Marks: 1 mark per major compliance item × 5 items + 2 marks for additional industry-specific points.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Disposal & Resale Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Book Value with Resale Value.** Book value is an *accounting* concept; resale value is the *market* price. These are equal *only* in the year of purchase if no depreciation is charged.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract salvage from capital recovery.** Many students compute $C/n$ instead of $(C-S_n)/n$ in the AAC formula — losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Not presenting a tabulated comparison.** AAC numericals must be tabulated year-by-year; examiners will not search for the minimum in scattered sentences.
> 4. **Confusing SLM with DBM.** In SLM, $D$ is constant; in DBM, $D$ is a percentage of diminishing book value. Mixing them up costs the depreciation-formula marks entirely.
> 5. **Skipping legal documentation in Part B questions.** A 7-mark "explain disposal" question that omits RTO Form 29/30 and NOC is considered *incomplete* — at least 1–2 marks deducted.
> 6. **Not stating the final conclusion clearly.** Always end with a boxed or underlined sentence like *"Therefore, the optimum replacement age is **3 years**."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Disposal & Resale of Vehicles (Module 1):**

- ☐ Vehicle disposal is the *systematic retirement* of a vehicle from fleet service, distinct from *abandonment* or *accident write-off*.
- ☐ **Economic life** ≠ Physical life; replacement is triggered by rising AAC, not mechanical failure.
- ☐ **Average Annual Cost (AAC)** method is the gold-standard for KTU numericals on replacement.
- ☐ Formula: $AAC(n) = \dfrac{C - S_n}{n} + \dfrac{1}{n}\sum M_k$.
- ☐ Marginal rule: *Replace if* $M_{n+1} + S_n - S_{n+1} > C_{\text{new}} - S_n$.
- ☐ **SLM** → $D = (C - S_N)/N$ → equal annual depreciation.
- ☐ **DBM** → $BV_n = C(1-r)^n$ → front-loaded via percentage.
- ☐ **SYD** → $D_n = \dfrac{2(N-n+1)}{N(N+1)}(C - S_N)$ → strongest front-loading.
- ☐ **Sinking Fund** → includes interest on accumulated depreciation reserve.
- ☐ Six disposal methods: *Trade-in, Auction, Direct Sale to Staff, Scrap, Export, Dismantling for Parts*.
- ☐ Legal checklist: *Form 29, Form 30, Financier NOC, Insurance Transfer, RTO Fitness Cancellation*.
- ☐ India-specific: *Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, GST on resale, Income Tax Section 43(6)*.
- ☐ Always **tabulate** AAC values; the optimum is the row with the lowest value.
- ☐ End every numerical with a clear boxed conclusion stating the optimum replacement age.

---

*End of Module 1 — Topic: Disposal and Resale of Vehicles.*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / OBE framework).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Operations – Fleet Scheduling and Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fleet Operations – Fleet Scheduling and Routing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Fleet Scheduling — Formal Definition **Fleet Scheduling** is the systematic planning process that determines *which vehicle*, operated by *which driver*, will be assigned to *which route*, departing at *what time*, on *which day*, in order to satisfy a set of transportation demands (passengers or freight) while optimizing one or more operational objectives such as **minimizing total cost**, **minimizing total distance traveled**, **maximizing vehicle utilization**, or **minimizing fleet idle time**. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, Fleet Scheduling sits inside the broader **Fleet Operations Management (FOM)** function and is the precursor activity to **Vehicle Routing**. Without a feasible schedule, a route is meaningless because no resource (vehicle/driver) has been temporally committed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEMUT835 – Module 2):** > Fleet Scheduling and Routing together constitute the **tactical-to-operational bridge** of fleet management. The output of scheduling is a *timetable*; the output of routing is a *spatial path*. Both must satisfy the **demand–supply equilibrium** for the transport network. ### 1.2 Fleet Routing — Formal Definition **Fleet Routing** (or **Vehicle Routing**) is the spatial counterpart of scheduling. It determines the **optimal sequence of stops** a vehicle must visit to serve a given set of customers (pickup or delivery points), subject to constraints such as vehicle capacity, time windows, route duration limits, and depot-return requirements. The classic formalization is the **Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)** introduced by **Dantzig and Ramser (1959)** and computationally explored by **Clarke and Wright (1964)**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Zomato / Swiggy food-delivery hub on a busy Saturday evening.** > > - **Scheduling** decides: *"Which delivery boy should leave the hub first, at what time, with how many orders stacked on his bike?"* > - **Routing** decides: *"After picking up Order #1 from Restaurant A, should he go to Customer X first or Customer Y first?"* > > Now scale that single rider to **40 trucks leaving a Kerala-based FMCG depot** to deliver goods to 120 retail shops across Ernakulam, Thrissur, and Kottayam districts — and you have a real-world **Fleet Scheduling and Routing** problem. The dispatcher (you) must decide which truck goes where, in what order, and at what time, while ensuring no truck is overloaded, no customer is kept waiting beyond their promised time window, and fuel/driver costs stay minimal. ### 1.4 Physical Constants, Metrics & Standard Terms - **Average urban driving speed (planning benchmark):** $\mathbf{25\text{–}40\ \text{km/h}}$ for Indian city traffic. - **Vehicle Capacity Utilization Target:** $\mathbf{\geq 85\%}$ for cost-efficient LTL operations. - **Standard Time-Window Tolerance:** $\pm \mathbf{15\ \text{minutes}}$ for B2B deliveries; $\pm \mathbf{30\ \text{minutes}}$ for B2C. - **Empty Miles Benchmark:** Should remain **below 15%** of total mileage for a healthy fleet. - **Driver Hours-of-Service Limit (India CMV Rules):** **8 hours continuous / 9 hours with breaks per day**. ### 1.5 Relationship Between Scheduling and Routing | Dimension | Scheduling (Temporal) | Routing (Spatial) | |---|---|---| | **Decides** | When to dispatch | Where to go | | **Output** | Timetable | Route sequence | | **Constraint Type** | Time windows, shift limits | Capacity, distance, drop sequence | | **Decision Variable** | $t_{ij}$ (departure time) | $x_{ijk}$ (visit order) | | **Horizon** | Hourly / Daily / Weekly | Per-trip | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Scheduling → Routing Decision Pipeline Fleet operations follow a sequential, hierarchical logic: 1. **Demand Aggregation:** Pool all confirmed transport requests for the planning horizon (e.g., next-day bookings). 2. **Demand Clustering:** Group customers whose demands sum to ≤ vehicle capacity (geographically and temporally). 3. **Stop Sequencing:** Within each cluster, sequence stops to minimize travel distance. 4. **Time-Window Allocation:** Insert waiting/slack times so each stop is reached inside its promised window. 5. **Vehicle-Driver Assignment:** Match clusters to specific vehicles and qualified drivers. 6. **Dispatch & Monitoring:** Execute the plan and track deviations in real time. ### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) Let: - $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ = set of customers - $V = \{0\} \cup N$ = set of nodes, where node 0 is the depot - $K = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ = set of available vehicles - $c_{ij}$ = travel cost (distance or time) between nodes $i$ and $j$ - $q_i$ = demand of customer $i$ - $Q$ = vehicle capacity **Decision Variable:** $$ x_{ijk} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } k \text{ travels directly from } i \text{ to } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Objective Function (Minimize Total Routing Cost):** $$ \min Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} c_{ij}\, x_{ijk} $$ **Subject to:** $$ \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall i \in N $$ $$ \sum_{j \in V} x_{0jk} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K $$ $$ \sum_{i \in V} x_{ihk} - \sum_{j \in V} x_{hjk} = 0 \quad \forall h \in N,\ \forall k \in K $$ $$ \sum_{i \in N} q_i \sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} \le Q \quad \forall k \in K $$ $$ x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\} $$ ### 2.3 The Clarke-Wright Savings Heuristic — Core Logic The **Clarke-Wright (1964) Savings Method** is the most-tested VRP heuristic in KTU examinations. The **savings value** $s_{ij}$ measures how much cost is avoided by merging two separate depot-to-customer trips (0 → i → 0 and 0 → j → 0) into a single combined trip (0 → i → j → 0). The savings formula is: $$ s_{ij} = c_{0i} + c_{0j} - c_{ij} $$ The reasoning: - **Before merging:** cost = $c_{0i} + c_{0j}$ - **After merging:** cost = $c_{0i} + c_{ij}$ (depot → i → j), saving the second trip back from $j$ to depot and instead directly linking i → j. Hence the savings per merge. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | When Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Savings Value | $s_{ij} = c_{0i} + c_{0j} - c_{ij}$ | km or ₹ | Clarke-Wright merge decision | | 2 | Total Routing Cost | $Z = \sum_{k} \sum_{i} \sum_{j} c_{ij} x_{ijk}$ | ₹ or km | VRP objective | | 3 | Vehicle Capacity Constraint | $\sum_{i} q_i \le Q$ | kg / units | CVRP feasibility | | 4 | Route Distance | $D_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in R_k} c_{ij}$ | km | Per-vehicle cost | | 5 | Average Load Factor | $\eta = \frac{\sum_{i} q_i}{m \cdot Q} \times 100$ | % | Fleet utilization KPI | | 6 | Empty Mile Ratio | $\text{EMR} = \frac{\text{Empty km}}{\text{Total km}} \times 100$ | % | Backhaul efficiency | | 7 | Cycle Time | $T_k = t_{\text{load}} + t_{\text{travel}} + t_{\text{unload}}$ | hours | Driver shift limit | | 8 | Service Time Window | $[e_i,\ \ell_i]$ where $e_i \le t_i \le \ell_i$ | hours | Hard / soft windows | | 9 | Euclidean Distance | $c_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2}$ | km | Distance matrix generation | | 10 | Total Demand Coverage | $\sum_{i \in N} q_i \le m \cdot Q$ | units | Fleet size feasibility | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the backbone of every commercial **Transport Management System (TMS)** such as SAP TM, Oracle OTM, and Descartes. Fleet managers at Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) and private operators like MVR Cabs rely on these formulations — often executed by solvers like **Google OR-Tools** or **Gurobi** — to dispatch thousands of vehicles daily. ### 2.5 Why These Concepts Matter Routing decisions directly influence: - **Fuel cost** (Kerala diesel ≈ ₹90/L, so every km saved matters) - **Carbon emissions** (logistics contributes ≈ 8% of global CO₂) - **Customer SLA compliance** (on-time delivery percentage) - **Driver fatigue and safety** (route duration affects HOS compliance) - **Vehicle wear-and-tear** (km driven correlates with maintenance spend) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Clarke-Wright Savings Formula **Step 1 — Identify two independent trips from depot:** The classical baseline assumes each customer $i$ is served by a dedicated vehicle doing a depot-to-customer-to-depot loop. Cost of trip to customer $i$ alone is $c_{0i} + c_{i0}$. By triangle symmetry $c_{i0} = c_{0i}$, so per-customer independent trip cost is $2c_{0i}$. **Step 2 — Cost for two customers served separately:** $$ C_{\text{separate}} = (c_{0i} + c_{i0}) + (c_{0j} + c_{j0}) = 2c_{0i} + 2c_{0j} $$ **Step 3 — Cost for the two customers served jointly in one trip:** The combined trip is depot → i → j → depot, costing $c_{0i} + c_{ij} + c_{j0}$. $$ C_{\text{merged}} = c_{0i} + c_{ij} + c_{j0} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the savings:** $$ \begin{aligned} s_{ij} &= C_{\text{separate}} - C_{\text{merged}} \\ s_{ij} &= (2c_{0i} + 2c_{0j}) - (c_{0i} + c_{ij} + c_{j0}) \\ s_{ij} &= c_{0i} + 2c_{0j} - c_{ij} - c_{j0} \\ s_{ij} &= c_{0i} + c_{0j} - c_{ij} \quad \text{(since } c_{j0}=c_{0j}\text{ by symmetry)} \end{aligned} $$ This is the celebrated Clarke-Wright savings value. > [!NOTE] > The savings $s_{ij}$ is **always non-negative** when the distance matrix satisfies the triangle inequality (as Euclidean distances do). Maximum savings occur when customers $i$ and $j$ lie on opposite sides of the depot along nearly straight lines. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Clarke-Wright Algorithm **Problem Setup:** A depot at node 0 serves 4 customers with demands and coordinates as below. Vehicle capacity $Q = 100$ units. | Customer | Demand $q_i$ | X-coordinate | Y-coordinate | |---|---|---|---| | 0 (Depot) | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 20 | 5 | 0 | | 2 | 30 | -4 | 3 | | 3 | 25 | 2 | 6 | | 4 | 35 | -6 | -2 | **Step 1 — Compute the Euclidean Distance Matrix:** $$ c_{01} = \sqrt{5^2 + 0^2} = 5 $$ $$ c_{02} = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 $$ $$ c_{03} = \sqrt{2^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 $$ $$ c_{04} = \sqrt{(-6)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 $$ $$ c_{12} = \sqrt{(5-(-4))^2 + (0-3)^2} = \sqrt{90} \approx 9.49 $$ $$ c_{13} = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (0-6)^2} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.71 $$ $$ c_{14} = \sqrt{(5-(-6))^2 + (0-(-2))^2} = \sqrt{125} \approx 11.18 $$ $$ c_{23} = \sqrt{(-4-2)^2 + (3-6)^2} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.71 $$ $$ c_{24} = \sqrt{(-4-(-6))^2 + (3-(-2))^2} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39 $$ $$ c_{34} = \sqrt{(2-(-6))^2 + (6-(-2))^2} = \sqrt{128} \approx 11.31 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Savings $s_{ij} = c_{0i} + c_{0j} - c_{ij}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} s_{12} &= 5 + 5 - 9.49 = 0.51 \\ s_{13} &= 5 + 6.32 - 6.71 = 4.61 \\ s_{14} &= 5 + 6.32 - 11.18 = 0.14 \\ s_{23} &= 5 + 6.32 - 6.71 = 4.61 \\ s_{24} &= 5 + 6.32 - 5.39 = 5.93 \\ s_{34} &= 6.32 + 6.32 - 11.31 = 1.33 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Rank Savings in Descending Order:** | Rank | Pair $(i,j)$ | Savings $s_{ij}$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | (2,4) | 5.93 | | 2 | (1,3) | 4.61 | | 3 | (2,3) | 4.61 | | 4 | (3,4) | 1.33 | | 5 | (1,2) | 0.51 | | 6 | (1,4) | 0.14 | **Step 4 — Iterative Route Construction:** - **Iteration 1:** Merge (2,4) → Create route 0 → 2 → 4 → 0. - Capacity check: $q_2 + q_4 = 30 + 35 = 65 \le 100$. ✅ - Distance: $5 + 5.39 + 6.32 = 16.71$ - **Iteration 2:** Merge (1,3). Since neither 1 nor 3 is yet in a route, create new route 0 → 1 → 3 → 0. - Capacity check: $q_1 + q_3 = 20 + 25 = 45 \le 100$. ✅ - Distance: $5 + 6.71 + 6.32 = 18.03$ - **Iteration 3:** Consider (2,3). Customer 2 is at the *interior* of route 0 → 2 → 4 → 0; customer 3 is at the *interior* of route 0 → 1 → 3 → 0. Inserting 3 after 2 would create route 0 → 2 → 3 → 1 → 0 (losing 4). Capacity: $30+25+20 = 75 \le 100$. ✅ New distance: $5 + 6.71 + 6.71 + 5 = 23.42$. - **Iteration 4:** Consider (3,4). Both 3 and 4 are now *interior*, so the merge would create a single huge route merging both — feasible but eliminates multi-vehicle advantage. **Typically rejected** to keep routes balanced. **Step 5 — Final Solution (Two-Vehicle Plan):** | Route | Sequence | Customers | Load | Distance | |---|---|---|---|---| | Vehicle 1 | 0 → 1 → 3 → 2 → 0 | 1, 3, 2 | 75 / 100 | 23.42 km | | Vehicle 2 | 0 → 4 → 0 | 4 | 35 / 100 | 12.64 km | $$ \boxed{Z_{\text{total}} = 23.42 + 12.64 = 36.06\ \text{km}} $$ This is the KTU-grade expected output format: route tables + total cost. ### 3.3 Alternative Methods — Quick Reference Matrix | Method | Type | Strength | Weakness | KTU Test Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Clarke-Wright Savings** | Heuristic | Fast, near-optimal | Local minima | ★★★★★ | | **Nearest Neighbor** | Constructive | Trivial to code | Very poor quality | ★★★ | | **Sweep Algorithm** | Geometric | Good for clustered data | Needs polar transform | ★★★★ | | **2-opt / 3-opt** | Improvement | Local refinement | Needs good initial route | ★★★ | | **Genetic Algorithm** | Metaheuristic | Escapes local minima | Slow convergence | ★★ | | **MILP (exact)** | Optimization | Optimal solution | NP-hard, slow | ★★ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fleet Scheduling & Routing — Operational Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Receive Transport Orders
Daily Demand List] --> B[Aggregate Demands
by Date and Zone] B --> C{Check Total Demand
vs Fleet Capacity} C -->|Exceeds| D[Sub-contract
or Add Vehicles] C -->|Within| E[Cluster Customers
by Geography and Time Window] D --> E E --> F[Apply Clarke Wright
Savings Algorithm] F --> G[Generate Route Drafts
for Each Vehicle] G --> H{Capacity and Time
Constraints Satisfied} H -->|No| I[Re-cluster or
Split Route] H -->|Yes| J[Assign Vehicles
and Drivers] J --> K[Publish Schedule
and Route Manifest] I --> F K --> L[Dispatch and
Real time Tracking] L --> M[Post trip Analysis
and KPI Update] ``` ### 4.2 Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) Family Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD VRP[Vehicle Routing Problem VRP] --> CVRP[Capacitated VRP
CVRP] VRP --> VRPTW[VRP with Time Windows
VRPTW] VRP --> VRPPD[VRP with Pickup and Delivery
VRPPD] VRP --> DVRP[Dynamic VRP
DVRP] VRP --> HVRP[Heterogeneous Fleet VRP
HVRP] VRP --> MDVRP[Multi Depot VRP
MDVRP] CVRP --> CVRP1[Single Depot
Fixed Capacity] VRPTW --> VRPTW1[Hard Time Window
Customer Specific] DVRP --> DVRP1[Real time Orders
