```mermaid
    nodeD -- Failure / Alarm --> nodeF[CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    nodeD -- Sensor Threshold Breach --> nodeG[CONDITION BASED MAINTENANCE]
    nodeE --> nodeH{PM Outcome Satisfactory?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Return Vehicle to Service]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeJ{Root Cause Identified?}
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK[Repair / Replace Component]
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeL[Detailed Diagnostic]
    nodeL --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeM{Quality Test Pass?}
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeM -- No --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeI[Return Vehicle to Revenue Service]
    nodeC --> nodeA
```

---

## 4.2 PM-vs-CM Functional Architecture Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **operational contrast** between the two strategies in terms of process flow, cost, and time behaviour.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PM_BLOCK [PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE DOMAIN]
        nodeP1[Calendar / km Trigger] --> nodeP2[Job Card Auto-Generated]
        nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Scheduled Parts Pull]
        nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Bay Allocated in Advance]
        nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Mechanic Performs PM Tasks]
        nodeP5 --> nodeP6[QC Check and Sign-off]
        nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Vehicle Returned to Service]
    end
    subgraph CM_BLOCK [CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE DOMAIN]
        nodeC1[Driver Reports Fault] --> nodeC2[Workshop Receives Vehicle]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Diagnostic Phase]
        nodeC3 --> nodeC4[Parts Availability Check]
        nodeC4 --> nodeC5[Emergency Procurement if Needed]
        nodeC5 --> nodeC6[Repair Execution]
        nodeC6 --> nodeC7[Road Test and Release]
    end
    PM_BLOCK -.->|Low Downtime Predictable Cost| nodeOUT[Fleet KPI Dashboard]
    CM_BLOCK -.->|High Downtime Variable Cost| nodeOUT
    nodeOUT --> nodeDEC{Decision: Increase PM or Upgrade CM Tools}
    nodeDEC --> PM_BLOCK
    nodeDEC --> CM_BLOCK
```

---

## 4.3 Cost-versus-Time Comparison Curve

The following diagram illustrates how total maintenance cost behaves as PM interval is varied. It visually justifies the existence of an **optimum PM interval**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[PM Interval T very small Frequent PM] --> nodeB[Total Cost High because PM Cost C_p is multiplied many times]
    nodeC[PM Interval T very large Infrequent PM] --> nodeD[Total Cost High because Failure Cost C_f dominates]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Optimum Point T Star]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Minimum Total Cost Rate Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowcharts map the **decision architecture** of a fleet maintenance system. In KTU examination answers, you may reproduce a similar simplified Mermaid-style diagram in your answer booklet using boxes and arrows — examiners award marks for **clarity of logic** and **labelling of decision branches**, not for graphic perfection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define preventive maintenance and corrective maintenance. State two advantages of preventive maintenance over corrective maintenance for a commercial vehicle fleet.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is a planned, scheduled maintenance activity carried out at predetermined intervals (time, mileage, or engine hours) **before** a failure occurs. Examples include oil change at 10,000 km, brake pad inspection every 20,000 km, and engine overhaul at 4,00,000 km.
>
> **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** is performed **after** a failure has taken place, with the aim of restoring the vehicle to a serviceable condition.
>
> **Two advantages of PM over CM:**
> 1. **Reduced unscheduled downtime** — PM is planned during off-peak hours, whereas CM is unpredictable and may strand the vehicle mid-route.
> 2. **Lower consequential damage** — A worn brake pad replaced at 25,000 km prevents disc scoring, avoiding the far costlier corrective replacement of the disc rotor.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark each for PM and CM definition, 1 mark for the two advantages. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the significance of the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) in vehicle maintenance management?**

**Model Answer:**

> **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is a measure of vehicle **reliability** — the average operational interval between two successive failures, expressed in hours or km. A higher MTBF indicates a more reliable vehicle.
>
> **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** is a measure of vehicle **maintainability** — the average time required to detect, diagnose, repair, and return a vehicle to service after a failure, expressed in hours. A lower MTTR indicates an efficient workshop.
>
> **Significance:** The two metrics together determine **Steady-State Availability** through the relation $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. Fleet managers use these to benchmark vehicles, set service contracts, and identify weak components.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways on this topic:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in MTBF/MTTR/CPKM answers — KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation steps** in Barlow–Hunter — writing only the final formula $T^*$ without showing the differentiation earns only 2–3 of the 7 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Inherent Availability" with "Achieved Availability"** — Inherent excludes logistics delay; Achieved includes all downtime. Many students interchange them.
> 4. **Not commenting on real-world interpretation** — KTU values application-oriented discussion. After every numerical problem, add a 2–3 line "practical implication" comment to fetch full marks.
> 5. **Writing CM-only or PM-only without comparison** — When asked "compare", always present a tabular comparison; examiners scan for it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Barlow–Hunter model** for determining the optimum interval of preventive maintenance. Derive the formula for optimum PM interval $T^*$ in terms of PM cost $C_p$, failure cost $C_f$, and hazard slope $\lambda_s$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bus operating in a city fleet reports the following data over one year:
* Total operating hours: 4,000 hours
* Number of failures: 8
* Total downtime (corrective): 320 hours
* Cost of preventive maintenance per cycle: ₹12,000
* Cost of corrective maintenance per failure: ₹35,000
* Hazard slope: $\lambda_s = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/h²

Calculate:
(i) MTBF and MTTR
(ii) Steady-State Availability
(iii) Optimum PM interval using Barlow–Hunter model
(iv) Cost per operating hour (CPH)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Barlow–Hunter Derivation [7 marks]:**

> Begin by stating the assumption: the component hazard rate increases linearly with time in the wear-out phase:
>
> $$\lambda(t) = \lambda_s \cdot t$$
>
> Define expected number of failures in interval $T$:
>
> $$E[N_f] = \int_0^T \lambda_s t \, dt = \dfrac{\lambda_s T^2}{2}$$
>
> Define total expected cost per cycle:
>
> $$E[C(T)] = C_p + C_f \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_s T^2}{2}$$
>
> Convert to cost rate (cost per unit time) to enable optimization:
>
> $$C_r(T) = \dfrac{E[C(T)]}{T} = \dfrac{C_p}{T} + \dfrac{C_f \lambda_s T}{2}$$
>
> Differentiate and equate to zero:
>
> $$\dfrac{dC_r}{dT} = -\dfrac{C_p}{T^2} + \dfrac{C_f \lambda_s}{2} = 0$$
>
> Solving:
>
> $$\boxed{T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2C_p}{C_f \lambda_s}}}$$
>
> Substitute back to get the minimum cost rate:
>
> $$C_r^* = \sqrt{2 C_p C_f \lambda_s}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** The optimum interval exists because PM cost decreases with longer $T$ (fewer PMs) while corrective cost increases (more failures). The optimum balances the two. **(1 mark for interpretation)**

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

(i) MTBF and MTTR:

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{4000 \text{ h}}{8} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{320 \text{ h}}{8} = 40 \text{ hours}$$

(ii) Steady-State Availability:

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{500}{500 + 40} = \dfrac{500}{540} = 0.9259 \approx 92.6\%$$

(iii) Optimum PM Interval:

$$T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12{,}000}{35{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{24{,}000}{0.07}} = \sqrt{3.4286 \times 10^5} = 585.5 \text{ hours}$$

(iv) Cost per Operating Hour (CPH):

Total annual cost $= 8 \times 12{,}000 + 8 \times 35{,}000 = 96{,}000 + 280{,}000 = 376{,}000$ ₹
*(assuming 8 PM cycles, since 4000 h ÷ 500 h per cycle = 8 cycles)*

$$\text{CPH} = \dfrac{376{,}000}{4000} = 94 \text{ ₹/hour}$$

**Practical comment:** The current effective PM cycle is 500 hours (MTBF), but the optimal PM cycle should be ~585 hours, indicating the bus may be **slightly over-maintained** — the fleet manager can extend PM intervals marginally to reduce cost without significantly increasing breakdown risk. However, given the 92.6% availability already achieved, the existing schedule is acceptable. **(½ to 1 mark for interpretation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Preventive Maintenance** and **Corrective Maintenance** strategies for a commercial vehicle fleet under the following heads: (i) Trigger, (ii) Cost pattern, (iii) Vehicle downtime, (iv) Spare parts inventory, (v) Skill requirement, (vi) Impact on safety, (vii) Suitability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a **bathtub curve**, explain how preventive maintenance influences vehicle reliability. Discuss the role of **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** as a hybrid approach.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Tabular Comparison [7 marks]:**

| Sl. | Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Corrective Maintenance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trigger** | Pre-scheduled (time/km/hrs) | Post-failure breakdown |
| 2 | **Cost Pattern** | Predictable, distributed, small-ticket | Lumpy, large-ticket, unpredictable |
| 3 | **Vehicle Downtime** | Planned, short, scheduled | Unplanned, long, often urgent |
| 4 | **Spare Parts Inventory** | Buffer stock planned, JIT possible | Emergency procurement, premium freight |
| 5 | **Skill Requirement** | Standard mechanics, OEM checklist | Diagnostic specialists, advanced tools |
| 6 | **Impact on Safety** | High — reduces catastrophic failures | Low — failure may occur in unsafe condition |
| 7 | **Suitability** | Safety-critical, high-utilisation fleets | Low-utilisation, non-critical, old vehicles nearing end-of-life |

*(Award 1 mark per row of comparison; 7 rows for 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Bathtub Curve and CBM [7 marks]:**

**Bathtub Curve Explanation:**
The bathtub curve plots hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time and has three distinct regions:

* **Region I — Infant Mortality:** High initial failure rate due to manufacturing defects, improper installation, or commissioning errors. Mitigated by **commissioning inspections** and **burn-in tests** before deployment.
* **Region II — Useful Life:** Low and constant failure rate dominated by random events. Mitigated by **routine inspections and minor adjustments** during PM.
* **Region III — Wear-Out:** Rising failure rate due to fatigue, corrosion, wear. Mitigated by **scheduled overhaul and component replacement at PM** — the precise rationale for hard-time replacement intervals.

**Influence of PM on reliability:** PM aims to **renew or replace** components **just before** they enter Region III, effectively keeping the entire fleet operating in Region II. This **truncates the hazard curve** and maintains a low, predictable failure rate.

**Role of Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM):**
CBM is a **hybrid** of PM and CM that uses real-time **condition monitoring** (oil analysis, vibration sensors, thermography, ultrasonic testing) to trigger maintenance **only when a degradation threshold is crossed**, regardless of calendar time. Advantages:

* Eliminates unnecessary PM (no over-maintenance).
* Prevents unexpected failures (no under-maintenance).
* Reduces inventory and labour cost.
* Ideal for high-value, safety-critical systems (engine, transmission, brake system in buses).

CBM represents the **modern best-practice** for large commercial fleets and is increasingly integrated with **IoT telematics** and **AI-based predictive analytics** in Industry 4.0 fleet operations.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is *scheduled, before failure*; **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** is *reactive, after failure*.
- **PM advantages:** predictable cost, low downtime, high safety, long vehicle life.
- **CM advantages:** low upfront cost, suitable for non-critical / low-utilisation assets, no over-maintenance.
- **MTBF** = reliability measure; **MTTR** = maintainability measure.
- **Availability formula** (most important): $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- **OEE** = $A \times P \times Q$ (Availability × Performance × Quality).
- **CPKM** = Total Maintenance Cost / Total km Operated.
- **PM Compliance Rate** is a key workshop discipline KPI; target > 95%.
- **Barlow–Hunter optimum PM interval:** $T^* = \sqrt{2C_p / (C_f \lambda_s)}$.
- **Bathtub curve** has 3 regions: infant mortality, useful life, wear-out. PM truncates Region III.
- **CBM (Condition-Based Maintenance)** is the modern hybrid using sensors and IoT.
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** uses AI/ML on CBM data to forecast failure before threshold breach.
- **Always include units** (hours, km, ₹/km) in every numerical answer.
- **Always add an interpretation line** after numerical solutions to fetch full marks.
- **Inherent Availability** excludes logistics delays; **Achieved Availability** includes them — never confuse.
- **High-yield KTU keywords** to use in answers: life-cycle cost (LCC), reliability-centered maintenance (RCM), mean time to maintain (MTTM), total productive maintenance (TPM), vehicle off-road (VOR).

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Vehicle Maintenance Management: Preventive vs. Corrective Maintenance**

*These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3 of OEMUT835, and the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate) typically tested in End Semester Examinations.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Scheduling and Record Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Scheduling and Record Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maintenance Scheduling** is the systematic, pre-planned arrangement of inspection, servicing, repair, and replacement activities for a vehicle or fleet at predetermined intervals — based on time, mileage, engine hours, or operating conditions — to ensure maximum operational availability, safety, reliability, and cost efficiency.

**Record Keeping** is the continuous, structured documentation of every maintenance activity, vehicle performance metric, operating cost, component life cycle, and service history, forming the informational backbone of any professional fleet management operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Maintenance scheduling is treated as a *strategic managerial function* — not merely a workshop activity. It bridges engineering (mechanical reliability) with management (economics, planning, documentation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Check-Up

Think of a fleet of trucks as the human body of a logistics company.

- **Preventive Maintenance** = Going for a routine health check-up every 6 months. You do it *before* you fall sick.
- **Corrective (Breakdown) Maintenance** = Rushing to the emergency room only when a heart attack strikes. It's expensive, painful, and disruptive.
- **Predictive Maintenance** = Wearing a smartwatch that monitors heart rate and alerts you *before* a problem occurs.
- **Record Keeping** = Your medical file with the doctor. Without it, even the best doctor cannot treat you effectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Insight:** A commercial vehicle off the road for one day can cost a fleet operator **₹15,000 – ₹40,000 in lost revenue** plus customer goodwill. Proactive maintenance scheduling minimizes this exposure.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PM** | Preventive Maintenance |
| **CM** | Corrective Maintenance |
| **PdM** | Predictive / Condition-Based Maintenance |
| **TPM** | Total Productive Maintenance |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair |
| **Availability** | Uptime ratio of a vehicle |
| **Job Card** | Document authorizing a specific maintenance task |
| **Work Order** | Formal instruction to the workshop |
| **Dossier** | Complete lifetime record of a vehicle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These terms appear repeatedly in KTU university exams. Memorize both the abbreviation and its full form.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Maintenance — The Master Framework

Maintenance is broadly classified into **two strategic categories** and **five operational types**.

### A. Strategic Categories
1. **Planned Maintenance** — Pre-scheduled, documented, and budgeted.
2. **Unplanned Maintenance** — Reactive; triggered by unexpected failure.

### B. Operational Types

1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Performed *before* failure occurs.
2. **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** — Performed *after* a failure occurs.
3. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** — Performed *based on condition monitoring*.
4. **Breakdown Maintenance** — Emergency repair (subset of CM).
5. **Scheduled Overhaul** — Major component rebuild at fixed life limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Maintenance Scheduling

Every mechanical system follows a **bathtub curve** of failure probability. Maintenance scheduling directly addresses the *wear-out phase* by replacing components just before their statistical failure point.

**The Engineering Rationale (Why we schedule):**
- **Reliability** — Vehicles start every morning without surprise.
- **Safety** — Brakes, steering, tires never fail mid-route.
- **Economics** — Planned downtime costs less than emergency tow charges.
- **Resale Value** — A well-documented service history increases resale price by **15–25%**.
- **Legal Compliance** — Motor Vehicles Department (Kerala RTO) fitness certificates require documented maintenance.

---

## 2.2 Types of Maintenance — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM)

Activities performed at **fixed intervals** regardless of current condition. It is the **backbone of fleet operations**.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Time-Based PM** — Every 30 days, 90 days, 180 days.
- **Mileage-Based PM** — Every 5,000 km, 10,000 km, 40,000 km.
- **Engine-Hour Based PM** — Every 250 hrs, 500 hrs, 1000 hrs (used in heavy equipment).
- **Combination PM** — Whichever interval is reached first.

**KTU High-Yield Example:**
> A truck has a PM schedule of "Every 10,000 km OR 3 months, whichever is earlier." This is a **Combination PM** — the most common in commercial fleets.

### 2.2.2 Corrective Maintenance (CM)

Repairs executed *after* a component fails. Subdivided into:
- **Deferred CM** — Vehicle is still operable; repair scheduled.
- **Emergency CM** — Roadside breakdown; immediate action required.
- **Remedial CM** — Recurrence prevention after a failure.

### 2.2.3 Predictive Maintenance (PdM)

Uses **condition monitoring tools** to predict failure before it occurs.

**Common Techniques:**
- Oil analysis (spectrography)
- Vibration analysis (for bearings)
- Thermography (for electrical systems)
- Ultrasonic testing
- Telematics-based engine diagnostics (OBD-II)

### 2.2.4 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

A **Japanese-origin philosophy** (Nakajima, 1988) where *every employee* — from driver to CEO — participates in equipment upkeep. Five pillars of TPM are heavily tested in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Pillars of TPM (Highly Tested):**
> 1. **Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance)** — Operators maintain their own machines.
> 2. **Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement)** — Targeted small-group improvements.
> 3. **Planned Maintenance** — Scheduled, zero-breakdown goal.
> 4. **Quality Maintenance** — Zero-defect output.
> 5. **Education & Training** — Skill upgrading.
> 6. **Office TPM** — Administrative efficiency.
> 7. **Safety, Health, Environment** — Safe workplace.

### 2.2.5 Scheduled Overhaul

Major rebuilding of engines, transmissions, axles at manufacturer-specified life limits (e.g., engine overhaul at **2,00,000 km**).

---

## 2.3 Maintenance Scheduling Methods (Operational)

| Method | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Time Interval** | Uniform fleet, predictable routes | Ignores actual usage |
| **Mileage-Based** | High-mileage long-haul trucks | Time-bound wear (rubber) ignored |
| **Engineer's Judgment** | Mixed fleet, varied duty | Subjective, non-replicable |
| **Condition-Based (Predictive)** | Critical equipment | Requires sensors, expertise |
| **Reliability-Centered (RCM)** | Safety-critical vehicles | High upfront analysis cost |
| **Manufacturer's Recommended** | New vehicles under warranty | May not match local conditions |
| **Operating-Condition Based** | Mining, military, off-road | Complex data collection |

---

## 2.4 Components of a Maintenance Schedule

A complete KTU-grade schedule must include:

1. **Vehicle Identification** — Registration number, chassis number, engine number.
2. **Task Description** — What is to be done.
3. **Interval Specification** — Time, km, or hours.
4. **Skill Level Required** — Helper, technician, engineer.
5. **Estimated Duration** — Downtime in hours.
6. **Parts & Materials List** — With part numbers.
7. **Tools & Equipment** — Special tools required.
8. **Cost Estimate** — Direct + indirect.
9. **Sign-off Authority** — Workshop supervisor.
10. **Acceptance Criteria** — Pass/fail benchmarks after the job.

---

## 2.5 Record Keeping — The Information Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A maintenance schedule without records is operationally **blind**. Records enable data-driven decisions.

### Categories of Records

**1. Vehicle Master Records**
- Registration certificate
- Insurance papers
- Fitness certificate
- Permit copies
- Original invoice

**2. Operational Records**
- Daily trip sheet (km, fuel, route, driver)
- Monthly utilization report
- Load carried vs. capacity

**3. Maintenance Records**
- Job cards (open and closed)
- Work orders
- Inspection checklists
- Service history logbook
- Warranty claim records

**4. Cost Records**
- Spare parts cost
- Lubricant cost
- Tyre cost
- Labor cost (in-house and outsourced)
- Third-party job invoices

**5. Component Life Records**
- Engine rebuild history
- Tyre rotation and replacement log
- Battery replacement history
- Brake pad/shoe life tracking

**6. Compliance Records**
- Pollution Under Control (PUC) certificates
- Fitness certificate renewals
- Insurance renewals
- Permit renewals

### Modern Digital Systems
- **CMMS** — Computerized Maintenance Management Software
- **FMS** — Fleet Management System
- **ERP-integrated** maintenance modules (SAP PM, Oracle)
- **Telematics** for real-time data (OBD-II, GPS-based)

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains the most exam-relevant quantitative relationships. All are mathematically verifiable.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Fraction of time vehicle is operational | Ratio (0–1) or % |
| 2 | $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Average time between two consecutive failures | Hours or km |
| 3 | $MTTR = \dfrac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{Number\ of\ Repairs}$ | Average downtime per failure | Hours |
| 4 | $Cost\ per\ km = \dfrac{Total\ Maintenance\ Cost}{Total\ km\ Run}$ | Cost efficiency metric | ₹/km |
| 5 | $Cost\ per\ hour = \dfrac{Total\ Maintenance\ Cost}{Operating\ Hours}$ | Time-based cost | ₹/hr |
| 6 | $Downtime\ \% = \dfrac{Downtime\ Hours}{Total\ Hours\ in\ Period} \times 100$ | Unavailability percentage | % |
| 7 | $PM\ Compliance\ \% = \dfrac{PM\ Jobs\ Done\ on\ Time}{Total\ PM\ Jobs\ Scheduled} \times 100$ | Schedule adherence | % |
| 8 | $Spare\ Cost\ Ratio = \dfrac{Spare\ Parts\ Cost}{Total\ Maintenance\ Cost} \times 100$ | Cost composition analysis | % |
| 9 | $Vehicle\ Utilization = \dfrac{Operating\ Hours}{Total\ Available\ Hours} \times 100$ | Fleet usage efficiency | % |
| 10 | $OEE = Availability \times Performance \times Quality$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For absolute value in formulas, write `\vert x \vert` — never use the `|` character, as it breaks markdown tables.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics Companies** (TCI, DHL, Blue Dart) — Save **8–12% of operating cost** through disciplined PM.
- **Public Transport (KSRTC)** — Uses mileage-based PM due to high km/day (400–600 km).
- **Taxi Aggregators (Ola, Uber)** — Use telematics + condition-based PM.
- **Defense Vehicles** — Use RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance) due to mission-criticality.
- **Mining Fleets (Volvo, Caterpillar)** — Combine engine-hour + condition-based.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating MTBF, MTTR, and Availability

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A delivery truck recorded the following failure data over **6 months (180 days)**, operating **10 hours/day** for **25 days/month**.
>
> | Month | Operating Hours | Failures | Total Repair Hours |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Jan | 250 | 2 | 6 |
> | Feb | 250 | 1 | 4 |
> | Mar | 240 | 3 | 9 |
> | Apr | 250 | 0 | 0 |
> | May | 250 | 2 | 8 |
> | Jun | 250 | 1 | 3 |
>
> **Calculate: (a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, (c) Availability, (d) Downtime %**

### Step 1 — Compute Aggregate Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Operating Hours} &= 250 + 250 + 240 + 250 + 250 + 250 \\
&= 1490\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Number of Failures} &= 2 + 1 + 3 + 0 + 2 + 1 \\
&= 9\ \text{failures}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Repair Hours (Downtime)} &= 6 + 4 + 9 + 0 + 8 + 3 \\
&= 30\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* We are simply summing the operational and repair data row-wise across the 6-month observation window to obtain the cumulative input values for the formulas.

### Step 2 — Calculate MTBF

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Hours}}{\text{Total Number of Failures}} \\
&= \frac{1490}{9} \\
&\approx 165.56\ \text{hours/failure}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* MTBF represents the average number of operating hours the truck successfully completes between two successive breakdowns. A higher value indicates better reliability.

### Step 3 — Calculate MTTR

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{\text{Total Repair Hours}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \\
&= \frac{30}{9} \\
&\approx 3.33\ \text{hours/repair}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* MTTR is the average workshop dwell-time per failure. Lower MTTR implies faster turnaround — a key KPI for the workshop manager.

### Step 4 — Calculate Availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
Availability &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
&= \frac{165.56}{165.56 + 3.33} \\
&= \frac{165.56}{168.89} \\
&\approx 0.9803 \\
&= 98.03\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:* Availability is the fraction of total calendar-equivalent time that the vehicle is operationally ready. A world-class fleet targets **≥ 95%**; we are above benchmark.

### Step 5 — Calculate Downtime %

$$
\begin{aligned}
Downtime\ \% &= \frac{30}{1490 + 30} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{30}{1520} \times 100 \\
&\approx 1.97\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Verification:* $Availability\ \% + Downtime\ \% = 98.03 + 1.97 = 100\ \%$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost-per-Kilometer Analysis for PM Decision

> **Problem:**
> A KSRTC bus runs **4,500 km/month**. The workshop proposes two PM alternatives.
>
> | Item | Option A (5,000 km interval) | Option B (10,000 km interval) |
> |---|---|---|
> | PM Cost per event | ₹4,500 | ₹7,800 |
> | Breakdown repairs per event | ₹1,200 | ₹4,000 |
> | Events per month | 0.9 | 0.45 |
>
> **Which option is more economical? Compute cost per km for both.**

### Option A — Frequent PM (5,000 km)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly PM Cost} &= 0.9 \times 4500 = 4050\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Monthly Breakdown Cost} &= 0.9 \times 1200 = 1080\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Total Monthly Cost} &= 4050 + 1080 = 5130\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Cost per km} &= \frac{5130}{4500} = ₹1.14 \text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Option B — Less Frequent PM (10,000 km)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly PM Cost} &= 0.45 \times 7800 = 3510\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Monthly Breakdown Cost} &= 0.45 \times 4000 = 1800\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Total Monthly Cost} &= 3510 + 1800 = 5310\ \text{₹} \\
\text{Cost per km} &= \frac{5310}{4500} = ₹1.18 \text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Decision

$$
\Delta\ Cost = 1.18 - 1.14 = ₹0.04 \text{/km}
$$

**Option A is more economical** — saving **₹180/month per bus**, scaling to **₹2,160/year per bus** or **₹21.6 lakhs/year for a 100-bus depot**.

*Textual insight:* This is the classic **"Maintenance Optimization Trade-off"** — too-frequent PM wastes money, too-infrequent PM invites breakdowns. The optimum is found at the *crossover point* of the two cost curves.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Maintenance Strategies

| Parameter | PM | CM | PdM | TPM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Time/km | Failure | Condition | Operator + System |
| **Cost (Short-term)** | Medium | Low | High | High initial |
| **Cost (Long-term)** | Low | Very High | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Downtime** | Planned, low | Unplanned, high | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Data Required** | Low | Low | Very high | Very high |
| **Skill Required** | Medium | Variable | High | Multi-skilled |
| **Suitability** | Stable fleets | Non-critical | Critical assets | Mature operations |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | High | Medium | High |

---

## 3.4 PM Interval Selection Logic (Derivation)

For a vehicle, the **optimum PM interval** $T^*$ can be approximated where the sum of **PM cost rate** + **Failure cost rate** is minimized.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(T) &= C_{PM}(T) + C_{failure}(T) \\
\text{where:}\quad C_{PM}(T) &= \frac{C_{p}}{T} \\
C_{failure}(T) &\approx \lambda(T) \cdot C_{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual explanation:*
- $C_{p}$ = Cost of one preventive maintenance event (₹).
- $T$ = PM interval (km or hours).
- $\frac{C_{p}}{T}$ = PM cost per km/hour (decreases as $T$ increases).
- $\lambda(T)$ = Failure rate, which *increases* with $T$ (older components fail more).
- $C_{f}$ = Average cost of a failure (₹).

**Optimal interval** occurs at:
$$
\frac{dC_{total}}{dT} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{C_{p}}{T^{2}} + \frac{d\lambda}{dT} \cdot C_{f} = 0
$$

Solving yields the engineering-economic optimum, beyond which breakdowns dominate costs.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU rarely asks for this derivation, but **understanding it earns full marks** in "Discuss" type 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Maintenance Scheduling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fleet Inventory and Vehicle Registration] --> B[Collect Manufacturer PM Schedule]
    B --> C[Adapt to Local Operating Conditions]
    C --> D[Define PM Intervals Time, km, Engine hours]
    D --> E[Issue Annual PM Calendar]
    E --> F[Daily Inspection Checklist]
    E --> G[Weekly PM Tasks]
    E --> H[Monthly PM Tasks]
    E --> I[Quarterly PM Tasks]
    E --> J[Annual Overhaul Tasks]
    F --> K{Defect Found?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Generate Job Card]
    K -- No --> M[Release Vehicle]
    L --> N[Work Order to Workshop]
    N --> O[Repair or Service]
    O --> P[Quality Inspection]
    P --> Q[Update Vehicle History Record]
    Q --> R[Cost Entry in Records]
    R --> S[Generate Performance KPI Report]
    S --> T[Management Review and Schedule Refinement]
    T --> E
```

*Block explanation:* This diagram represents the **closed-loop maintenance cycle** used by professional fleet operators. Data from past performance (R) feeds back (T) into the next scheduling cycle (E), enabling continuous improvement — a core ISO 9001 and TPM principle.

---

## 4.2 Record Keeping Information Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Trip Sheet] --> D[Central Fleet Database]
    B[Fuel Receipts] --> D
    C[Job Card Open] --> D
    C1[Job Card Closed] --> D
    D --> E[Cost Reports]
    D --> F[Compliance Reports]
    D --> G[Vehicle History Dossier]
    D --> H[KPI Dashboard]
    E --> I[Management Decision]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Budget Allocation]
    I --> K[Schedule Adjustment]
    I --> L[Vehicle Replacement Decision]
```

*Block explanation:* Every operational input (trip sheet, fuel, job card) flows into a **central database**, which produces structured outputs (cost, compliance, history, KPIs). These outputs drive **strategic managerial decisions** (budget, schedule, replacement).

---

## 4.3 TPM Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TPM[Total Productive Maintenance TPM] --> P1[Autonomous Maintenance Jishu Hozen]
    TPM --> P2[Planned Maintenance]
    TPM --> P3[Focused Improvement Kobetsu Kaizen]
    TPM --> P4[Quality Maintenance]
    TPM --> P5[Education and Training]
    TPM --> P6[Office TPM]
    TPM --> P7[Safety Health Environment]
    P1 --> OUT[Zero Breakdown, Zero Defect, Zero Accident]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT
    P7 --> OUT
```

*Block explanation:* All seven pillars of TPM converge toward the unified target of **zero loss** — a foundational concept heavily tested in KTU Module 2.

---

## 4.4 Maintenance Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Asset or Vehicle to be Maintained] --> Q1{Critical to Operation?}
    Q1 -- No --> LOW[Run to Failure Corrective]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Failure Consequences Severe?}
    Q2 -- No --> PM[Use Preventive Maintenance]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Condition Monitoring Feasible?}
    Q3 -- No --> RCM[Use Reliability Centered Maintenance]
    Q3 -- Yes --> PDM[Use Predictive Maintenance]
    LOW --> REC[Document and Reassess Annually]
    PM --> REC
    RCM --> REC
    PDM --> REC
```

*Block explanation:* This decision tree helps fleet managers select an appropriate maintenance strategy based on **criticality** and **failure consequence severity** — exactly the logic taught in Reliability Engineering and tested in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Fixed time/km interval | Real-time condition data |
| **Tools Needed** | Standard workshop tools | Sensors, analyzers, telematics |
| **Cost** | Moderate, predictable | Higher initial, lower long-term |
| **Downtime** | Planned | Minimal, just-in-time |
| **Example** | Engine oil change every 10,000 km | Replacing bearing when vibration spectrum crosses alarm threshold |
| **Data Dependency** | Low | High |

> *Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for each correct comparative point, 1 mark for example. Total 3.*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: What is a Job Card? List any four essential fields it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Job Card** is a workshop document that authorizes, records, and tracks a specific maintenance activity on a specific vehicle.

**Four Essential Fields:**
1. **Vehicle identification** (Registration No., Chassis No.)
2. **Date and time of opening the job**
3. **Complaint / Task description** (what is to be done)
4. **Mechanic name and estimated time**

*(Optional additional fields: parts used, supervisor sign-off, closing time, cost.)*

> *Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks each for 4 fields = 3 marks total.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **five pillars of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** with a focus on **Autonomous Maintenance** and **Planned Maintenance**. Discuss how TPM is implemented in a commercial vehicle fleet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A truck's failure data for 4 months is as follows: Operating hours = 1000, 1050, 1100, 950. Failures per month = 3, 2, 4, 1. Total repair hours = 9, 6, 12, 3. Calculate **MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and Downtime %**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to (a): TPM in a Fleet Context**

The **five pillars of TPM** (with two additional supporting pillars in modern practice) are:

**1. Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance)**
- Operators (drivers) perform daily checks: oil level, coolant, tire pressure, lights, leaks.
- Eliminates the "I assumed someone else did it" gap.
- Standard practice: **Driver Pre-Inspection Checklist** signed before each shift.

**2. Planned Maintenance**
- Schedules all PM, inspections, and overhauls in advance.
- Uses CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System).
- Goal: **Zero breakdown, planned downtime only**.

**3. Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement)**
- Cross-functional teams tackle specific loss sources (e.g., recurring brake failure).
- Uses **Why-Why analysis** and **Pareto charts**.

**4. Quality Maintenance**
- "Do it right the first time." Zero-defect target.
- Applies to maintenance jobs as well as driving.

**5. Education and Training**
- Continuous skill upgradation of mechanics, drivers, supervisors.
- Includes **5S, KAIZEN, root cause analysis** training.

*(Optional extra pillars: Office TPM, Safety/Health/Environment.)*

**Implementation in a Commercial Fleet:**
- Stage 1: 5S workplace organization in workshop.
- Stage 2: Autonomous maintenance by drivers.
- Stage 3: Planned maintenance system rollout.
- Stage 4: Training and skill development.
- Stage 5: Continuous improvement via Kobetsu Kaizen.

> *Examiner's note: 4 marks for pillars, 1.5 marks for fleet implementation example, 1.5 marks for autonomous/planned emphasis.*

---

### **Solution to (b): MTBF, MTTR, Availability, Downtime**

**Step 1 — Aggregations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Operating Hours} &= 1000 + 1050 + 1100 + 950 = 4100\ \text{hrs} \\
\text{Total Failures} &= 3 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 10 \\
\text{Total Repair Hours} &= 9 + 6 + 12 + 3 = 30\ \text{hrs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — MTBF**

$$
MTBF = \frac{4100}{10} = 410\ \text{hrs/failure}
$$

**Step 3 — MTTR**

$$
MTTR = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0\ \text{hrs/repair}
$$

**Step 4 — Availability**

$$
Availability = \frac{410}{410 + 3.0} = \frac{410}{413} = 0.9927 = 99.27\ \%
$$

**Step 5 — Downtime %**

$$
Downtime\ \% = \frac{30}{4100 + 30} \times 100 = \frac{30}{4130} \times 100 = 0.73\ \%
$$

**Verification:** $99.27 + 0.73 = 100.00\ \%$ ✓

> *Examiner's note: 1.5 marks each for MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and Downtime. Full marks only with units and correct percentage conversion.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`

**(a)** What is **Maintenance Scheduling**? Discuss in detail the **different methods of maintenance scheduling** used in fleet operations, with their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **importance of record keeping in fleet management**. Describe the **types of records** maintained and the **modern computerized systems** used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to (a): Maintenance Scheduling — Methods**

**Definition:** Maintenance scheduling is the pre-planned calendarization of inspection, servicing, and overhaul tasks at predetermined intervals to ensure vehicle availability, safety, and cost efficiency.

**Methods:**

1. **Fixed Time Interval** — Tasks done at fixed dates (e.g., every 1st of month).
   - *Advantage:* Simple to plan.
   - *Limitation:* Ignores actual usage.

2. **Mileage-Based** — Tasks done at fixed km (e.g., 10,000 km service).
   - *Advantage:* Reflects wear accurately.
   - *Limitation:* Ignores time-based degradation (rubber, coolant).

3. **Engine-Hour Based** — Used in equipment and high-idle vehicles.
   - *Advantage:* Most accurate for engines.
   - *Limitation:* Requires hour-meters.

4. **Condition-Based (Predictive)** — Based on sensor data.
   - *Advantage:* Avoids unnecessary maintenance.
   - *Limitation:* Capital intensive.

5. **Reliability-Centered (RCM)** — Logic-tree approach.
   - *Advantage:* Optimizes cost vs. safety.
   - *Limitation:* Initial analysis is expensive.

6. **Operating-Condition Based** — Modified schedule for harsh environments.
   - *Advantage:* Realistic.
   - *Limitation:* Requires data.

7. **Manufacturer's Recommended** — As per service manual.
   - *Advantage:* Validated design.
   - *Limitation:* May not match local conditions.

> *Examiner's note: 2 marks for definition, 4 marks for methods (0.5 mark each minimum), 1 mark for advantages/limitations table.*

---

### **Solution to (b): Record Keeping in Fleet Management**

**Importance:**
- **Decision support** — Replace, retain, or refurbish decisions.
- **Legal compliance** — Audit trail for RTO, insurance, warranty.
- **Cost control** — Identifies high-cost vehicles (cost per km).
- **Resale value** — Documented history commands premium.
- **Warranty claims** — Required by OEMs.
- **Driver accountability** — Trip-sheet cross-checks.

**Types of Records:**
1. Vehicle master records (RC, insurance, fitness, permits).
2. Operational records (trip sheets, fuel logs).
3. Maintenance records (job cards, work orders, inspection checklists).
4. Cost records (parts, lubricants, labor, third-party).
5. Component life records (engine, tyre, battery history).
6. Compliance records (PUC, fitness renewals).

**Modern Computerized Systems:**
- **CMMS** — Computerized Maintenance Management Software (e.g., Fiix, UpKeep).
- **FMS** — Fleet Management Systems (e.g., Trimble, Geotab).
- **ERP Modules** — SAP PM, Oracle EAM.
- **Telematics Platforms** — OBD-II + GPS integrated.
- **Cloud-based Mobile Apps** — Driver self-reporting.

> *Examiner's note: 2 marks importance, 3 marks types, 2 marks systems. Sub-parts must have distinct headings.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in MTBF/MTTR answers — leads to **-0.5 to -1 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Confusing TPM pillars with 5S** — they are related but TPM is the parent system.
> 3. **Writing "Job Card" and "Work Order" interchangeably** — they are *not the same*; a work order is a higher-level authorization.
> 4. **Skipping the formula for availability** — students often write the result without showing $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
> 5. **Ignoring record-keeping section** — a high-weightage area in KTU Module 2.
> 6. **Writing 'Preventive' without specifying the interval type** — examiner expects "time-based" or "mileage-based".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maintenance Scheduling & Record Keeping**

- **Maintenance scheduling** is a *proactive* managerial function; it is **not the same as** breakdown repair.
- **Two strategic categories:** Planned vs. Unplanned. **Five operational types:** PM, CM, PdM, TPM, Overhaul.
- **PM triggers:** Time, km, engine hours, or combination (whichever comes first).
- **TPM is Japanese-origin** (Nakajima) with **7 pillars** — know all names and meanings.
- **Job Card ≠ Work Order.** Job card = shop-floor level. Work order = administrative authorization.
- **MTBF** is a reliability metric; **MTTR** is a maintainability metric. Both feed into **Availability**.
- **Availability formula:** $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — write it explicitly, every time.
- **Record keeping has 6 categories:** Master, Operational, Maintenance, Cost, Component life, Compliance.
- **Modern tools:** CMMS, FMS, ERP, Telematics. Mention at least one in any "Discuss" answer.
- **Cost per km** is the **most important fleet KPI** — used in replacement decisions.
- **PM Compliance %** measures schedule adherence — a workshop manager's key metric.
- **Bathtub curve** justifies PM in the wear-out phase (last 1/3 of component life).
- **Kerala-specific RTO rules:** Fitness certificate, permit renewal, PUC are mandatory compliance records.
- **"Zero breakdown"** is the TPM target — it is a *goal*, not a guarantee.
- Always mention **units** (km, hrs, ₹) in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict.
- **Spare cost ratio** typically should be 40–60% of total maintenance cost in a healthy fleet.
- **Driver trip sheet** is the *starting point* of all operational records — without it, no cost data exists.
- **Preventive vs. Predictive:** PM is calendar-based, PdM is condition-based. *Don't mix them up.*
- **"Run to failure"** is acceptable only for **non-critical, low-cost** components (e.g., a cabin bulb).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Telematics in Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Telematics in Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Telematics in Maintenance** refers to the integrated application of telecommunications, vehicular electronics, sensor technology, GPS, and information processing systems to remotely monitor, diagnose, predict, and manage the maintenance needs of vehicles within a fleet. It enables the continuous acquisition of real-time vehicle operational data (engine parameters, mileage, fuel levels, fault codes, driver behavior, and component health) and transmits this information over wireless networks (GSM/4G/5G/Satellite) to a centralized fleet management platform where intelligent maintenance decisions are algorithmically generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEMUT835 Module 2):** Telematics-based maintenance is the convergence of *Information Technology*, *Communications*, and *Vehicle Engineering* that transforms traditional time-based or mileage-based maintenance into a **Condition-Based / Predictive Maintenance (CBM/PdM)** paradigm.

> [!NOTE]
> The term "Telematics" itself is a portmanteau of **Telecommunications** and **Informatics**, originally coined in the late 1970s to describe long-distance information transmission. In fleet engineering, it has evolved to specifically mean the in-vehicle data acquisition and transmission ecosystem.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor Inside the Car"

Imagine you own a pet dog. Instead of waiting for the dog to fall sick and rushing to the vet (which is **reactive maintenance**), you strap a smartwatch on the dog that continuously monitors its heart rate, body temperature, sleep cycle, and activity level. The watch sends this data to your phone. When the heart rate spikes or temperature rises abnormally, **the app alerts you BEFORE the dog becomes visibly sick** — allowing you to take preventive action. This is exactly how telematics works for vehicles.

In this analogy:
- **Smartwatch sensors** = On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) sensors in the vehicle
- **Phone app alerts** = Fleet Manager's Dashboard receiving data
- **Vet visit** = Workshop maintenance
- **Heart rate spike** = Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) or abnormal sensor reading

The key insight is that telematics turns vehicle maintenance from a **blind, schedule-based chore** into an **intelligent, data-driven, predictive science**.

---

### 1.3 Core Physical / Operational Metrics Highlighted

| Metric (Standard) | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **OBD-II** | On-Board Diagnostics second-generation port (mandatory emission monitoring) |
| **DTC** | Diagnostic Trouble Code — a standardized fault identifier |
| **CAN Bus** | Controller Area Network — vehicle's internal data backbone |
| **GNSS/GPS** | Global Navigation Satellite System for location and mileage tracking |
| **M2M** | Machine-to-Machine communication (vehicle-to-server) |

> [!NOTE]
> Every modern commercial vehicle produced after **2008 in India (BS-III/IV onwards)** is OBD-II compliant, making telematics integration a regulatory and operational necessity for fleet operators.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of a Telematics Maintenance System

A telematics-driven maintenance system is composed of **five logical layers**, each performing a distinct function:

#### Layer 1 — Data Acquisition Layer (In-Vehicle Sensors)
- **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** monitors RPM, air-fuel ratio, coolant temperature, oil pressure.
- **Transmission Control Unit (TCU):** tracks gear shifts, clutch wear, fluid temperature.
- **Brake system sensors:** ABS module data, brake pad wear indicators.
- **Battery Management System (BMS):** voltage, state-of-charge (SoC), state-of-health (SoH).
- **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS):** real-time pressure and temperature per wheel.

#### Layer 2 — Data Processing Layer (In-Vehicle Telematics Unit / IVTU)
- **OBD-II Dongle** or **factory-fitted Telematics Control Unit (TCU)**.
- Reads raw sensor data via the **CAN bus protocol** (ISO 11898 standard).
- Packs data into standardized message frames (e.g., SAE J1939 for heavy-duty vehicles).
- Executes **edge analytics** — local filtering to reduce data transmission load.

#### Layer 3 — Communication Layer (Wireless Transmission)
- Cellular networks: **2G / 4G LTE / 5G** (low-latency for safety-critical alerts).
- **Satellite communication** for remote / off-grid fleets (mining, maritime).
- **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth** for workshop-local diagnostic uploads.
- Protocols: MQTT, HTTP/HTTPS, TCP/IP over TLS-encrypted channels.

#### Layer 4 — Cloud / Server Processing Layer
- Aggregates petabytes of telemetry into **time-series databases**.
- Runs **machine learning models** for failure prediction (e.g., Random Forest, LSTM neural networks).
- Generates **work orders**, maintenance tickets, and parts-replenishment alerts.
- Performs **fault code classification** (ISO 15031-6 standard for DTCs).

#### Layer 5 — Presentation Layer (Fleet Manager Dashboard)
- Mobile + desktop application.
- Visualizes KPIs: downtime, MTTR, MTBF, fuel efficiency, vehicle health score.
- Sends **push notifications** for critical faults.
- Integrates with **ERP / SAP** for spare-part procurement.

---

### 2.2 Why Telematics Transforms Maintenance — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuous sensor polling (every 1–10 sec) | Captures transient faults invisible to manual inspection |
| 2 | CAN bus aggregation | A single gateway reads ALL ECUs — reduces hardware cost |
| 3 | Wireless transmission | Enables off-site monitoring of 1000+ vehicles |
| 4 | Cloud ML analysis | Identifies **latent patterns** before failure occurs |
| 5 | Auto-generated work orders | Eliminates human error in scheduling |
| 6 | Driver behavior feedback loop | Reduces maintenance cost by up to 25% |

---

### 2.3 The Maintenance Paradigm Shift — Pre vs Post Telematics

| Dimension | Traditional Maintenance | Telematics-Driven Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Time or mileage interval | **Real-time condition** |
| Inspection approach | Manual, periodic | **Continuous, automated** |
| Fault detection | After breakdown | **Pre-failure warning** |
| Spare part planning | Buffer stock | **Just-in-time (JIT) replenishment** |
| Cost per km | Higher (over-maintenance) | **Optimized** |
| Data source | Workshop logbook | **Sensor stream** |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **Availability** — fraction of time vehicle is operational | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | **Mean Time Between Failures** | Hours |
| $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | **Mean Time To Repair** | Hours |
| $\text{Cost}_{\text{saved}} = (C_{\text{breakdown}} - C_{\text{predictive}}) \times N_{v}$ | Savings from predictive vs. reactive | Currency |
| $\text{DT} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{dispatch}} + t_{\text{repair}}$ | Total vehicle **Downtime** components | Hours |
| $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | % |
| $\text{SOH} = \dfrac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | Battery **State of Health** | Percentage |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure **rate** | Failures/hour |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | Dimensionless |
| $\text{RUL} = \text{MTBF} - t_{\text{operated}}$ | Remaining Useful Life estimate | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exam:** When writing formulas, the symbols MTBF, MTTR, and OEE are **universally accepted abbreviations** and need NOT be expanded further. However, **availability 'A' is NOT a constant** — it is a derived ratio dependent on the specific vehicle and operating environment.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial fleet operations (logistics companies like DHL, FedEx, or Indian operators like Rivigo and Delhivery), telematics has demonstrated:
- **30–50% reduction** in unplanned breakdowns.
- **15–20% decrease** in total maintenance cost per kilometer.
- **40% improvement** in workshop scheduling efficiency.
- Compliance with **AIS-140** standard (mandatory in India for public transport vehicles since 2018).

In mining and construction fleets, telematics integrated with **payload sensors** and **engine load sensors** enables **load-based maintenance scheduling** — replacing outdated hour-meter-based service intervals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vehicle Availability Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A delivery truck operates for **480 hours** in a month, experiences **3 failures**, and each failure requires **8 hours** on average to repair. Compute the **Availability (A)** and interpret the result.

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{operating}} &= 480 \text{ hours} \\
N_{\text{failures}} &= 3 \\
\text{MTTR}_{\text{avg}} &= 8 \text{ hours/repair}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Downtime due to repairs**

Total downtime is the aggregate time the vehicle was unavailable in the workshop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{downtime}} &= N_{\text{failures}} \times \text{MTTR}_{\text{avg}} \\
&= 3 \times 8 \\
&= 24 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**

MTBF represents the average operational duration between successive failures:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{operating}}}{N_{\text{failures}}} \\
&= \dfrac{480}{3} \\
&= 160 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Availability Formula**

Availability is the proportion of total time that the asset is operationally ready:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \dfrac{160}{160 + 8} \\
&= \dfrac{160}{168} \\
&\approx 0.9524 \text{ or } 95.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for KTU Exam**

The truck is operationally available **95.24%** of the time. For a fleet of **20 such trucks**, the total monthly availability-hours would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours}_{\text{available}} &= 20 \times 720 \times 0.9524 \\
&= 20 \times 720 \times 0.9524 \\
&\approx 13715 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

The remaining **~4.76% downtime** (≈ 685 hours fleet-wide) represents the maintenance overhead — a **telematics-based predictive strategy aims to increase this availability towards 99%+**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Benefit Analysis of Predictive vs Reactive Maintenance

**Problem Statement:** A fleet operator manages **50 buses**. Each breakdown costs ₹ **15,000** in towing, lost revenue, and goodwill loss. A telematics system costs ₹ **2,000/month per bus** and reduces breakdowns by **60%**. Determine the **monthly savings** and **net benefit**.

**Step 1: Baseline breakdown cost (without telematics)**

Assume each bus experiences **1 breakdown per month** on average (a conservative baseline for an aged fleet):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{baseline}} &= N_{v} \times N_{\text{breakdowns/vehicle}} \times C_{\text{breakdown}} \\
&= 50 \times 1 \times 15000 \\
&= 750000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Telematics implementation cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{telematics}} &= N_{v} \times C_{\text{unit}} \\
&= 50 \times 2000 \\
&= 100000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Reduced breakdown cost (after telematics)**

With a 60% reduction in breakdowns, only 40% of original breakdowns occur:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{reduced}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times (1 - R) \\
&= 750000 \times (1 - 0.60) \\
&= 750000 \times 0.40 \\
&= 300000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net monthly savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{net}} &= (C_{\text{baseline}} - C_{\text{reduced}}) - C_{\text{telematics}} \\
&= (750000 - 300000) - 100000 \\
&= 450000 - 100000 \\
&= 350000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Annualized Benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= 350000 \times 12 \\
&= 4200000 \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The fleet saves ₹ **42 lakh per year** by deploying telematics — a strong ROI argument that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark problems.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping DTCs to Maintenance Actions

This matrix links **standard OBD-II Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)** to their physical cause and the appropriate telematics-triggered maintenance action.

| DTC Code | System Affected | Physical Cause | Telematics-Triggered Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0300** | Engine | Random/multiple cylinder misfire | Schedule ignition system check within 48 hours |
| **P0420** | Emission | Catalyst system efficiency below threshold | Plan O2 sensor / catalytic converter inspection |
| **P0171** | Fuel/Air | System too lean (Bank 1) | Flag vacuum leak / fuel pressure diagnostic |
| **P0700** | Transmission | Transmission Control System malfunction | Immediate workshop visit; cease long-distance trips |
| **C1234** | ABS | Wheel speed sensor malfunction | Brake system diagnostic within 24 hours |
| **B1318** | Battery | Battery voltage low | Schedule battery test + alternator check |
| **U0100** | Network | Lost communication with ECM | Inspect CAN bus wiring / connectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The first letter of a DTC denotes the system family — **P** (Powertrain), **C** (Chassis), **B** (Body), **U** (Network/Communication). This is a high-frequency 3-mark short-answer question.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Predictive Failure Probability (Reliability Function)

**Step 1: Define the exponential reliability model**

For components exhibiting a constant failure rate (memoryless property), reliability is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the failure rate per hour.

**Step 2: Determine $\lambda$ from MTBF**

For a fleet where MTBF = 160 hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{160} \\
&= 0.00625 \text{ failures/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute probability of failure within next 50 hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(t) &= 1 - R(t) \\
&= 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= 1 - e^{-(0.00625)(50)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.3125} \\
&= 1 - 0.7316 \\
&= 0.2684 \text{ or } 26.84\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

There is a **~26.84% probability** that this component fails within the next **50 operating hours**. A telematics system using this probability threshold (typically set at 25–30% by fleet managers) will automatically schedule a **preventive inspection**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Telematics Architecture for Vehicle Maintenance

This block diagram illustrates the **complete data flow** from in-vehicle sensors to the fleet manager's decision-making interface:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Engine ECU] --> C1[CAN Bus Aggregator]
    A2[Transmission TCU] --> C1
    A3[Brake ABS Module] --> C1
    A4[Battery BMS Sensor] --> C1
    A5[Tyre TPMS Sensor] --> C1
    A6[Fuel Level Sender] --> C1
    C1 --> B1[Telematics Control Unit TCU]
    B1 --> B2[Edge Analytics Filter]
    B2 --> D1{Communication Gateway}
    D1 -->|4G LTE| E1[Cloud Server]
    D1 -->|Satellite| E1
    E1 --> F1[Time Series Database]
    F1 --> F2[ML Failure Prediction Engine]
    F1 --> F3[DTC Decoder ISO 15031]
    F2 --> G1[Work Order Generator]
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Fleet Manager Dashboard]
    G1 --> H2[Workshop Scheduling System]
    G1 --> H3[Spare Parts ERP]
    H1 --> I1[Push Notification Mobile App]
    H2 --> J1[Mechanic on Duty]
    H3 --> K1[Vendor Procurement]
```

### 4.2 Maintenance Decision Logic Flowchart

This diagram shows the **logical decision tree** executed by the telematics cloud engine to determine maintenance action:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Vehicle Telemetry Stream Received] --> S2{Is DTC Active?}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Log Data + Continue Monitoring]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4{Is DTC Critical?}
    S4 -->|Yes Red Code| S5[Immediate Workshop Alert]
    S4 -->|No Amber Code| S6{Condition Threshold Exceeded?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Schedule Maintenance within 48 hrs]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Log + Driver Advisory Notification]
    S3 --> S9[Next Polling Cycle 10 sec]
    S5 --> S10[Vehicle Grounded Until Repair]
    S7 --> S11[Work Order Created]
    S8 --> S9
    S11 --> S12[Parts Availability Check]
    S12 --> S13[Mechanic Assigned]
```

### 4.3 Vehicle Health Score Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Engine Health Parameter] --> Q1[Weighted Sum Aggregator]
    P2[Brake Wear Index] --> Q1
    P3[Battery SOH Percentage] --> Q1
    P4[Transmission Fluid Status] --> Q1
    P5[Emission Compliance Score] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Overall Vehicle Health Score 0 to 100]
    R1 --> R2{Score Threshold}
    R2 -->|Above 80| R3[Green Status Routine Check]
    R2 -->|Between 50 and 80| R4[Amber Status Plan Service]
    R2 -->|Below 50| R5[Red Status Immediate Attention]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid:** In the Vehicle Health Score flowchart, the **weighted sum aggregator** assigns differential importance to each subsystem. For example, engine and brake systems typically carry **30% weight each**, while emission compliance carries **20%**. KTU exam questions may require students to compute the weighted score for a given scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Telematics** in the context of fleet maintenance. List any four components of an in-vehicle telematics system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Telematics in fleet maintenance** is the integrated use of telecommunications, sensors, GPS, and information technology to remotely monitor, diagnose, and manage vehicle health and maintenance schedules in real time.

**Four components of an in-vehicle telematics system:**
1. **GPS/GNSS receiver** — for location and mileage tracking.
2. **OBD-II port / ECU interface** — to read engine and emission data.
3. **Wireless communication modem (GSM/4G/5G)** — for data transmission.
4. **Sensors (TPMS, temperature, accelerometer)** — for condition monitoring.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for components.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Preventive Maintenance** and **Predictive Maintenance**. How does telematics enable predictive maintenance?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Time or mileage | Real-time sensor data |
| Trigger | Scheduled interval | Anomaly / threshold breach |
| Cost | Moderate (over-maintenance possible) | Optimized |

**Telematics enables predictive maintenance** by continuously collecting vehicle sensor data (engine temp, oil pressure, vibration), transmitting it to a cloud platform, and using machine-learning algorithms to detect **early warning signs of component degradation** BEFORE failure occurs. This converts maintenance from a calendar-driven activity to a **condition-driven, intelligent intervention**.

**Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for table + 1.5 marks for telematics role.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives below follow this structure.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of a telematics-based vehicle maintenance system**. Describe the function of each layer. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A logistics company operates **40 trucks**, each averaging **2 breakdowns per month** with an average repair cost of **₹18,000 per breakdown**. The company is evaluating a telematics solution at **₹2,500 per truck per month**, projected to **reduce breakdowns by 65%**. Calculate the **annual net savings** and comment on the investment decision. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture [7 Marks]**

The telematics-based maintenance system has **five functional layers**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer (1.5 marks):** Sensors and ECUs in the vehicle — engine RPM, coolant temp, oil pressure, ABS, TPMS — collect raw operational data.

2. **Data Processing Layer (1.5 marks):** The Telematics Control Unit (TCU) reads sensor data via the **CAN bus**, applies edge analytics to filter noise, and packages data for transmission.

3. **Communication Layer (1.5 marks):** Data is transmitted over **4G/5G cellular** or **satellite** networks using encrypted MQTT/HTTPS protocols.

4. **Cloud Processing Layer (1.5 marks):** Server-side infrastructure runs **DTC decoders**, **ML failure prediction models**, and **work order generators**.

5. **Presentation Layer (1 mark):** The fleet manager accesses dashboards, receives push notifications, and integrates with ERP for spare part procurement.

*(For diagram, refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.)*

---

**Part (b) — Cost-Benefit Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Baseline breakdown cost per month (without telematics)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{baseline}} &= 40 \times 2 \times 18000 \\
&= 1440000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Telematics monthly cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{telematics}} &= 40 \times 2500 \\
&= 100000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Breakdown cost after telematics deployment (65% reduction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{reduced}} &= 1440000 \times (1 - 0.65) \\
&= 1440000 \times 0.35 \\
&= 504000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Monthly savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{monthly}} &= (1440000 - 504000) - 100000 \\
&= 936000 - 100000 \\
&= 836000 \text{ INR/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Annual net savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= 836000 \times 12 \\
&= 10032000 \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Investment Decision Comment (1 mark)**

The telematics deployment yields an annual net benefit of **₹1.0032 crore** against an annual investment of **₹12 lakh**, giving a **Return on Investment (ROI) of ~736%**. **The investment is strongly recommended** — payback period is approximately **1.7 months**, making this a financially compelling decision.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **OBD-II and Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)** in telematics-based maintenance. Explain the **DTC naming convention** with at least three examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**. For a fleet of **25 vehicles** that operated for a total of **10,000 hours** last quarter, experienced **15 failures**, with total downtime of **180 hours**, compute the **Availability** and **Reliability at t = 100 hours** assuming exponential distribution. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — OBD-II and DTCs [7 Marks]**

The **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, Generation II)** is a standardized vehicle self-diagnostic system mandated for all petrol vehicles sold in India after 2010 (BS-IV) and all diesel vehicles post-2013 (BS-IV). It continuously monitors the performance of engine, transmission, emission, and auxiliary systems. When a parameter deviates from its calibrated range, the ECU stores a **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** in its non-volatile memory.

In telematics, the **OBD-II port (or hardwired TCU)** reads these DTCs in real time and transmits them to the cloud. The cloud platform decodes the DTC, correlates it with the vehicle's operating context (load, speed, ambient temp), and triggers an appropriate **maintenance workflow** — ranging from a driver advisory (amber alert) to immediate workshop intervention (red alert).

**DTC Naming Convention (ISO 15031-6):**

A DTC is a **5-character alphanumeric code** structured as:
- **1st character (Letter):** System family
  - **P** = Powertrain (engine, transmission)
  - **C** = Chassis (brakes, steering, suspension)
  - **B** = Body (airbags, lighting, wipers)
  - **U** = Network/Communication (CAN bus faults)
- **2nd character (Digit):** Code type (0 = Generic/Sae, 1 = Manufacturer-specific)
- **3rd character (Digit):** Subsystem (1 = emission, 2 = fuel/air, 3 = ignition, etc.)
- **4th & 5th characters:** Specific fault index (00–99)

**Three Examples:**

| DTC | Meaning | Maintenance Action |
|---|---|---|
| **P0301** | Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected | Inspect spark plug, ignition coil, fuel injector |
| **P0420** | Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold | Test O2 sensor, replace catalytic converter |
| **C0040** | Brake Pressure Sensor A Circuit Fault | Inspect brake lines, replace sensor |

*(Mark split: 3 marks OBD-II explanation + 2 marks DTC convention + 2 marks examples)*

---

**Part (b) — MTBF, MTTR, Availability, Reliability [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Compute MTBF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\
&= \dfrac{10000}{15} \\
&\approx 666.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MTTR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \\
&= \dfrac{180}{15} \\
&= 12 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \dfrac{666.67}{666.67 + 12} \\
&= \dfrac{666.67}{678.67} \\
&\approx 0.9823 \text{ or } 98.23\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Failure Rate $\lambda$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{666.67} \\
&\approx 0.0015 \text{ failures/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Reliability at t = 100 hours (Exponential Model)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(100) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
&= e^{-(0.0015)(100)} \\
&= e^{-0.15} \\
&\approx 0.8607 \text{ or } 86.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** There is an **86.07% probability** that the vehicle will operate without failure for the next 100 hours, and the fleet is **98.23% operationally available**. *(2 marks for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTR** — MTBF is **between** failures (operating time), MTTR is the **repair time** (downtime).
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract** `MTTR` from total time when computing MTBF — if the problem gives total calendar time, you MUST separate operating vs downtime.
> 3. **Writing Reliability = Availability** — these are different metrics. **Availability** is an *operational state* measure; **Reliability** is a *probability of failure-free operation* over a duration.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — always specify hours, km, or ₹ in numerical answers.
> 5. **In DTC questions**, students often forget the **first letter** (P/C/B/U) — losing 1 full mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use of Telematics in Maintenance**

- ✅ Telematics = **Telecommunications + Informatics** applied to vehicles.
- ✅ Core purpose: transform maintenance from **reactive → preventive → predictive**.
- ✅ System layers: **Sensor → TCU → Communication → Cloud → Dashboard**.
- ✅ **OBD-II** is the standardized diagnostic interface since 2010 (India).
- ✅ **DTC structure:** 1 letter (P/C/B/U) + 4 digits.
- ✅ **CAN bus** (ISO 11898) is the vehicle's internal data highway.
- ✅ Key formulas:
  - $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$
  - $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
- ✅ Telematics reduces unplanned breakdowns by **30–60%** and maintenance cost by **15–25%**.
- ✅ **AIS-140** is the Indian government mandate for telematics in public transport (since 2018).
- ✅ ROI on telematics is typically achieved within **2–6 months** for medium-sized fleets.
- ✅ **Predictive maintenance** is NOT the same as preventive — predictive uses **real-time condition data**, preventive uses **fixed schedules**.
- ✅ Exam tip: always draw the **5-layer architecture block diagram** for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ Always mention **specific DTC examples** (P0300, P0420, U0100) — examiners reward application-level answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Management – Fuel Procurement and Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Management – Fuel Procurement and Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fuel Management** in the context of automotive fleet operations is defined as the systematic, strategic, and procedural planning, procurement, storage, distribution, and accounting of vehicular fuels (petrol, diesel, CNG, LPG, electric energy equivalents, and emerging biofuels) across an organization's transport assets. It is a critical sub-system of **Fleet Operations Management** aimed at minimizing the *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* while ensuring uninterrupted mobility, regulatory compliance, and operational safety.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMUT835)** curriculum framework, Fuel Management is sub-divided into two principal pillars:

1. **Fuel Procurement**: The strategic acquisition of fuel from external sources (Oil Marketing Companies – OMCs, third-party vendors, or direct refineries) under negotiated commercial terms, including pricing models, delivery schedules, and quality specifications.
2. **Fuel Storage**: The on-premise infrastructure and procedural control required to safely hold, dispense, and account for fuel reserves at fleet depots, hubs, and transit yards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Fuel typically accounts for **30%–60%** of the total operating cost of a commercial transport fleet. Hence, mismanagement of fuel procurement and storage directly erodes the EBITDA of fleet operators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a large **hotel kitchen**. You don't buy tomatoes one at a time from the street vendor every morning. Instead, you:

- **Source in Bulk** (procurement) — sign annual contracts with farmers for predictable pricing.
- **Store Properly** (storage) — keep them in cold rooms so they don't rot.
- **Track Usage** (accountability) — note down every kilogram used per dish.

Fuel management is exactly the same, except the "tomatoes" are highly flammable, environmentally hazardous, and priced at international parity rates that fluctuate daily. The "kitchen" is your depot, and the "dishes" are your trucks, buses, or delivery vans.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

The following industry-standard constants and units govern fuel management calculations:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Diesel Density | **0.832 kg/L** at **15°C** | Conversion between volume and mass |
| Petrol Density | **0.745 kg/L** at **15°C** | Conversion between volume and mass |
| 1 US Gallon | **3.7854 L** | Cross-border fuel auditing |
| 1 Barrel (bbl) | **159 L** | International crude procurement |
| Storage Tank Standard | **IS 10987 / API 650** | KTU reference for tank design |
| Flash Point of Diesel | **> 55°C** | Fire safety classification |
| Flash Point of Petrol | **< -20°C** | Highly flammable storage class |

> [!NOTE]
> The densities above are **ISO 91.1-1986** standard reference values. Actual density varies with temperature; hence all bulk transactions are **volume-corrected to 15°C** before invoicing — a concept known as **Volume Correction Factor (VCF)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars — Decomposed

#### 2.1.1 Pillar A: Fuel Procurement

Fuel procurement is the **front-end strategic function**. It encompasses:

**Step 1 — Demand Forecasting**
- Historical consumption analysis (last 12–24 months)
- Seasonal adjustments (monsoon, harvest, festival logistics spikes)
- Fleet expansion projections
- Macroeconomic indicators (USD/INR parity for imported crude)

**Step 2 — Vendor / Source Selection**
- **OMC Direct** (Indian Oil, Bharat Petroleum, Hindustan Petroleum) — most reliable for bulk.
- **Third-Party Dealers** — flexible credit terms but margin overhead.
- **Direct Refinery Tie-ups** — for fleets > **500 KL/month** consumption.
- **Spot Market / E-auction** — for opportunistic pricing.

**Step 3 — Pricing Models**
- **Rack Price Model**: Daily notified price + dealer commission.
- **Contracted Price Model**: Fixed price for 3/6/12 months — hedges volatility.
- **Indexed Pricing**: Tied to **Platts Singapore** or **Brent crude** benchmarks.

**Step 4 — Delivery Logistics**
- Tanker truck capacity: **6 KL / 10 KL / 12 KL / 20 KL** standard bulk carriers.
- Minimum lead time: **48 hours** for OMC bulk delivery.
