| 2 | Partial Automation | Both, but human must monitor |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | Vehicle drives, human fallback |
| 4 | High Automation | No driver in geo-fenced area |
| 5 | Full Automation | No driver ever needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** SAE Level **4** is the practical sweet spot for commercial fleets (e.g., autonomous trucks in confined mining zones or port terminals).

## 2.5 Electric Vehicles (EVs) & Alternative Powertrains

Electrification changes three fleet fundamentals:

1. **Energy source** shifts from diesel to grid electricity (or hydrogen fuel cells).
2. **Maintenance profile** drops dramatically — no oil changes, fewer brake wear parts (regenerative braking), but battery health becomes critical.
3. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** parity occurs at a mileage threshold that depends on electricity tariff vs. diesel price.

## 2.6 Cloud, Big Data & 5G

- **Cloud computing** allows SaaS-based Fleet Management Systems (FMS) to scale from 5 vehicles to 50,000 without infrastructure investment.
- **Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity — the 4 V's) describes the data tsunami from modern fleets.
- **5G** delivers latency under **10 ms**, enabling real-time V2X safety messaging.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the consolidated KTU formula bank for emerging-trend questions.** All variables are defined below the table.

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predictive Maintenance Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hr), $f(t)$ = PDF, $F(t)$ = CDF | Predicts component reliability |
| Remaining Useful Life | $RUL(t) = T_{end} - t$ | $RUL$ in hours or km | Schedules replacement |
| Fuel Cost per km | $C_{km} = \dfrac{P_{fuel}}{E}$ | $C_{km}$ in ₹/km, $P_{fuel}$ in ₹/L, $E$ in km/L | Cost benchmarking |
| Electric Energy Cost | $C_{elec} = \dfrac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D}$ | $E_{kWh}$ in kWh, $P_{grid}$ in ₹/kWh, $D$ in km | EV TCO calculation |
| Total Cost of Ownership | $TCO = C_{buy} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{labour} - C_{residual}$ | All in ₹ | Procurement decision |
| CO₂ Emission | $m_{CO_2} = E_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$ | $m_{CO_2}$ in grams, $EF$ = emission factor (g/L) | Environmental compliance |
| Big Data Velocity | $V = N_{events} \times S_{packet} \times \Delta t^{-1}$ | $V$ in MB/s | Network capacity planning |
| Battery State of Health | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{max,current}}{C_{max,rated}} \times 100\%$ | $SoH$ in %, $C$ in Ah | EV battery resale value |
| Bandwidth Requirement | $B = n_{veh} \times b_{per\_veh}$ | $B$ in Mbps, $b_{per\_veh}$ in Mbps | 5G / 4G planning |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{operational}}{N_{failures}}$ | $MTBF$ in hours | Reliability KPI |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

These technologies directly reduce **Empty Mileage**, improve **Driver Safety Scores**, accelerate **Insurance Claim Settlement**, and enable **Just-In-Time (JIT)** logistics. They also form the technical foundation of *Smart City* initiatives and *Industry 4.0* manufacturing logistics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Predictive Maintenance Hazard Function

The hazard function $\lambda(t)$ represents the *instantaneous probability of failure per unit time*, conditional on survival up to time $t$.

**Step 1 — Define reliability function.**

Let $R(t)$ be the probability that a component survives until time $t$. By definition:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define probability density of failure.**

The probability density function (PDF) of failure is the derivative of the cumulative distribution function (CDF):

$$f(t) = \frac{d}{dt} F(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express conditional failure probability.**

The probability that a component fails in the infinitesimal interval $[t, t+dt]$, given it survived up to $t$, is:

$$P(t \le T < t + dt \mid T \ge t) = \frac{f(t) \, dt}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $dt \to 0$.**

Dividing by $dt$ and letting $dt \to 0$ yields the hazard rate:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{dt \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t+dt \mid T \ge t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A rising $\lambda(t)$ signals *wear-out phase* — schedule maintenance now. A constant $\lambda(t)$ signals *useful life phase* — minimum maintenance. A decreasing $\lambda(t)$ signals *infant mortality* — typically a manufacturing defect.

## 3.2 Derivation of Electric Vehicle TCO Break-Even Mileage

**Step 1 — Define diesel TCO per km.**

$C_{diesel} = \frac{P_{diesel}}{E_{diesel}} + M_{diesel}$

where $P_{diesel}$ is price per litre, $E_{diesel}$ is efficiency in km/L, $M_{diesel}$ is maintenance cost per km.

**Step 2 — Define EV TCO per km.**

$C_{ev} = \frac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D} + M_{ev} + \frac{C_{batt}}{L}$

where $E_{kWh}$ is energy consumption per km (kWh/km), $P_{grid}$ is electricity tariff (₹/kWh), $D$ is km per kWh, $M_{ev}$ is maintenance per km, $C_{batt}$ is battery replacement cost (₹), $L$ is battery lifetime in km.

**Step 3 — Equate the two for break-even.**

Set $C_{diesel} = C_{ev}$:

$$\frac{P_{diesel}}{E_{diesel}} + M_{diesel} = \frac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D} + M_{ev} + \frac{C_{batt}}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for break-even mileage $L^{\star}$.**

Rearranging to isolate $L$:

$$\frac{C_{batt}}{L} = \frac{P_{diesel}}{E_{diesel}} + M_{diesel} - M_{ev} - \frac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D}$$

$$L^{\star} = \frac{C_{batt}}{\frac{P_{diesel}}{E_{diesel}} + M_{diesel} - M_{ev} - \frac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- $P_{diesel} = ₹100/\text{L}$
- $E_{diesel} = 4 \text{ km/L}$
- $M_{diesel} = ₹1.5/\text{km}$
- $M_{ev} = ₹0.5/\text{km}$
- $E_{kWh} = 0.2 \text{ kWh/km}$
- $P_{grid} = ₹8/\text{kWh}$
- $C_{batt} = ₹8,00,000$

Compute numerator:

$$C_{batt} = 800000$$

Compute denominator:

$$\frac{100}{4} + 1.5 - 0.5 - (0.2 \times 8)$$

$$= 25 + 1.5 - 0.5 - 1.6 = 24.4$$

Therefore:

$$L^{\star} = \frac{800000}{24.4} = 32786.89 \text{ km}$$

**Interpretation:** The electric truck becomes economically superior to its diesel counterpart after approximately **32,787 km** of operation — well within the first year for a typical long-haul fleet.

## 3.3 Derivation of Big-Data Bandwidth Requirement

**Step 1 — Determine per-vehicle data rate.**

Modern telematics units emit approximately 50 events per minute; each event is 200 bytes.

$$b_{per\_veh} = 50 \times 200 \times \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{1 \text{ byte}} \times \frac{1}{60 \text{ s}}$$

$$b_{per\_veh} = \frac{80000}{60} \approx 1333.33 \text{ bits/s} = 1.33 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by fleet size.**

For a fleet of $n_{veh} = 1000$ trucks:

$$B = 1000 \times 1.33 \text{ kbps} = 1.33 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Add overhead.**

Applying a 40% protocol overhead factor for MQTT/TCP/IP headers:

$$B_{actual} = 1.33 \times 1.4 \approx 1.86 \text{ Mbps}$$

This bandwidth comfortably fits within a single 4G LTE sector, justifying why telecom-grade cellular networks are sufficient for most commercial fleets.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology Impact Comparison

| Technology | Safety Impact | Cost Impact | Sustainability Impact | Implementation Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Telematics | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| AI Route Optimization | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Very High | High | High | Very High |
| Electric Vehicles | Medium | Medium | Very High | High |
| IoT Sensors | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| 5G & V2X | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Blockchain Logistics | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| Predictive Maintenance | High | Very High | Medium | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Always cite at least *two* KPIs (cost, safety, emissions) when explaining any emerging trend in a 14-mark answer. The matrix above is your ready reference.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Fleet Technology Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how all eight pillars interconnect inside a modern connected fleet operation. This functional architecture maps the interactions between vehicles, edge devices, networks, cloud platforms, analytics engines, and end-user applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[VehicleLayer TrucksBusesVans] --> B[EdgeLayer OBDSensorsGPSIMUCamera]
    B --> C[GatewayLayer TelematicsControlUnit]
    C --> D[NetworkLayer 4G5GSatelliteLoRaWAN]
    D --> E[CloudLayer MQTTBrokerDataLake]
    E --> F[AnalyticsLayer AIMLPredictiveEngine]
    F --> G[ApplicationLayer FMSDashboardMobileApp]
    F --> H[AutomationLayer RouteOptDispatchBot]
    F --> I[MaintenanceLayer PdMPredictiveAlerts]
    G --> J[FleetManager]
    G --> K[DriverMobileApp]
    H --> L[AutonomousDriveModule]
    M[ExternalData WeatherTrafficGeofence] --> F
    N[ChargingStations and FuelStations] --> A
    O[InsuranceAPI and RegulatoryAPI] --> E
    P[BlockchainLedger for FreightDocs] --> E

    classDef hw fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nw fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef usr fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C hw
    class D nw
    class E,F,H,I,P sw
    class G,J,K,L,M,N,O usr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows from the **Vehicle Layer (A)** through sensors (B) → gateway (C) → network (D) → cloud (E) → analytics (F) → applications (G,H,I,P) → users (J,K,L). External inputs (M,N,O) feed the analytics layer to enrich decisions.

## 4.2 Predictive Maintenance Decision Flowchart

The following sequence diagram explains how a predictive maintenance system processes an engine-temperature alert.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Vehicle Ignition] --> S2[Sensors Stream Data Every 10s]
    S2 --> S3{Anomaly Detected by ML Model}
    S3 -- No --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Generate Alert with Confidence Score]
    S4 --> S5{Confidence Greater than 85 percent}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Log for Trend Analysis Only]
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Push Notification to Fleet Manager]
    S7 --> S8[Auto Schedule Workshop Slot]
    S8 --> S9[Driver Receives Instruction via Mobile App]
    S9 --> S10[Vehicle Reaches Workshop]
    S10 --> S11[Technician Reads Diagnostic Code]
    S11 --> S12{Component Repairable}
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Repair and Reset Counter]
    S12 -- No --> S14[Replace Component from Stock]
    S13 --> S15[Update Digital Service Log]
    S14 --> S15
    S15 --> S16[Retrain ML Model with New Failure Data]
    S16 --> S2
```

## 4.3 EV vs. Diesel Fleet Transition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Assess Current Diesel Fleet] --> B[Compute Annual Mileage per Vehicle]
    B --> C{Calculate Break-Even Mileage}
    C -- Above Break-Even --> D[Procure Electric Vehicle]
    C -- Below Break-Even --> D2[Retain Diesel or Hybrid]
    D --> E[Install Charging Infrastructure]
    E --> F[Train Drivers on EV Operation]
    F --> G[Monitor KPIs Monthly]
    G --> H{TCO Reducing}
    H -- Yes --> I[Scale EV Deployment]
    H -- No --> J[Audit Energy Tariff and Routes]
    J --> E
    D2 --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to "explain with a block diagram," use at minimum **5 labeled blocks** with directional arrows. The above diagrams satisfy and exceed this benchmark.

## 4.4 Five-Layer Reference Model Summary

| Layer # | Name | Function | Example Hardware/Software |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vehicle / Asset Layer | Physical trucks and cargo | Diesel truck, EV bus, refrigerated van |
| 2 | Sensing Layer | Capture raw physical data | OBD-II, GPS, accelerometer, temperature |
| 3 | Communication Layer | Transport data to cloud | 4G LTE, 5G NR, Iridium satellite |
| 4 | Processing Layer | Store and analyze | AWS IoT Core, Azure Digital Twins, Kafka |
| 5 | Application Layer | Human-machine interface | Web FMS, mobile app, driver tablet |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Style — Direct, Definition-Oriented**

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define telematics. List any four sensors used in a telematics-enabled commercial vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Telematics** is the integrated use of telecommunications and informatics to monitor, transmit, and analyse real-time data from a remote vehicle. It combines GPS, onboard sensors, and wireless communication to provide continuous visibility of fleet operations.

**Four sensors commonly used:**

1. **GPS receiver** — captures real-time geographic position, speed, and heading.
2. **Accelerometer** — detects harsh braking, acceleration, and cornering events.
3. **OBD-II reader** — extracts engine diagnostics (RPM, coolant temperature, fault codes).
4. **Fuel-level sensor** — monitors fuel consumption and detects possible theft.

*(Each sensor listed = 0.5 mark; correct definition = 1 mark. Total = 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between preventive maintenance and predictive maintenance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Fixed time/mileage interval | Real-time sensor data + ML prediction |
| Cost Efficiency | Moderate (may replace parts early) | High (replaces parts just before failure) |
| Data Use | Historical schedule only | IoT telemetry + AI analytics |
| Downtime | Planned but possibly unnecessary | Minimised — work only when needed |

**Key distinction:** Predictive maintenance uses *condition-based* triggers derived from ML analysis of sensor streams, whereas preventive maintenance uses *calendar/odometer-based* triggers irrespective of actual component health. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a telematics-based fleet management system with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in modern fleet operations with at least three application examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Telematics Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A telematics-based FMS integrates vehicle-mounted hardware, wireless communication, cloud servers, and analytics software to provide continuous visibility of fleet operations.

**Step 2 — Architecture layers (3 marks):**

1. **Vehicle Layer:** Trucks, vans, buses equipped with onboard units.
2. **Sensing Layer:** GPS, OBD-II, accelerometer, gyroscope, fuel sensor, temperature sensor.
3. **Communication Layer:** 4G/5G cellular modem transmits data packets every 5–30 seconds.
4. **Cloud Layer:** MQTT broker receives streams into a data lake (e.g., AWS S3).
5. **Application Layer:** Dashboard visualises maps, KPIs, alerts; mobile app informs drivers.

**Step 3 — Working principle (2 marks):**
The onboard unit samples sensors at fixed frequency, compresses data, encrypts it, and pushes to the cloud via cellular network. The cloud applies rule-based and ML-based anomaly detection. When an event exceeds threshold (e.g., harsh brake, geofence breach, engine fault), the system pushes an SMS / email / app notification to the fleet manager and the driver.

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 mark):** Use the five-layer block model from Section 4.4.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): AI & ML in Fleets (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
AI/ML enables fleets to move from *reactive* to *proactive* decision-making by extracting patterns from massive telemetry streams.

**Step 2 — Three Applications (4 marks — 1.33 each):**

1. **Predictive Maintenance:** ML classification models (Random Forest, XGBoost) trained on labelled failure data predict *Remaining Useful Life (RUL)* of components such as brakes and batteries.
2. **Dynamic Route Optimisation:** Reinforcement-learning agents learn the fastest delivery sequence under live traffic and weather conditions, reducing empty mileage by up to 15%.
3. **Driver Behaviour Scoring:** Unsupervised clustering (K-Means) categorises trips into safe / moderate / aggressive, enabling targeted driver coaching and insurance premium discounts.

**Step 3 — Benefits (1 mark):**
Reduces operational cost (8–12%), improves safety (20% drop in accidents), enhances customer satisfaction via accurate ETAs.

**Step 4 — Challenges (1 mark):**
Requires large labelled datasets, data-privacy compliance, and skilled data-science workforce.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the emerging trend of Electric Vehicles in commercial fleet operations. Derive the break-even mileage formula and compute it for typical Indian operating conditions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation. Identify the level most suitable for current commercial fleet deployment with justification. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a): EV Trend & Break-Even (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Why EVs in fleets (2 marks):**
Lower per-km energy cost, reduced maintenance (no oil change, regenerative braking), corporate ESG targets, government FAME-II subsidy, falling battery prices (₹/kWh declined 80% in the last decade).

**Step 2 — Break-even formula derivation (3 marks):**

$$L^{\star} = \frac{C_{batt}}{\frac{P_{diesel}}{E_{diesel}} + M_{diesel} - M_{ev} - \frac{E_{kWh} \times P_{grid}}{D}}$$

(See Section 3.2 for the full derivation.)

**Step 3 — Numerical computation (2 marks):**

Using $P_{diesel} = ₹100/\text{L}$, $E_{diesel} = 4 \text{ km/L}$, $M_{diesel} = ₹1.5/\text{km}$, $M_{ev} = ₹0.5/\text{km}$, $E_{kWh} = 0.2 \text{ kWh/km}$, $P_{grid} = ₹8/\text{kWh}$, $C_{batt} = ₹8,00,000$:

$$L^{\star} = \frac{800000}{25 + 1.5 - 0.5 - 1.6} = \frac{800000}{24.4} \approx 32{,}787 \text{ km}$$

Therefore, beyond **32,787 km**, the EV becomes economically superior — justifying fleet electrification for long-haul operators.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): SAE J3016 Levels (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):**
SAE International standard J3016 defines six levels (0 to 5) of vehicle driving automation based on who/what performs the dynamic driving task.

**Step 2 — Levels explained (4 marks):**

- **Level 0 — No Automation:** Driver controls everything.
- **Level 1 — Driver Assistance:** Either adaptive cruise control OR lane-keeping (one function at a time).
- **Level 2 — Partial Automation:** Both ACC and lane-keeping together, but driver must monitor continuously.
- **Level 3 — Conditional Automation:** Vehicle drives itself in specific conditions; driver can disengage but must be ready to take back control.
- **Level 4 — High Automation:** Vehicle drives itself within a geo-fenced area; no driver needed inside that area.
- **Level 5 — Full Automation:** Vehicle drives itself in all conditions anywhere a human driver could.

**Step 3 — Most suitable for fleets today (2 marks):**

**Level 4** is currently the most suitable for commercial fleet deployment because:
- Operating domains can be tightly geo-fenced (mining zones, ports, dedicated freight corridors, airport aprons).
- Removes driver cost inside the fenced zone (high-wage geographies).
- Multiple pilot deployments are already operational (e.g., autonomous trucks in Australian mines, platooning trials in Europe).

Level 5 is technologically and legally unproven for commercial use.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *preventive* with *predictive* maintenance. The examiner deducts 1 full mark if the student merely repeats the same definition for both.
> 2. **Do not** list sensors without linking them to a measurable KPI (e.g., "accelerometer → harsh-braking score").
> 3. **Always** include a labelled block diagram in any 7-mark architecture question — it alone secures 1 mark even if the descriptive answer is weak.
> 4. **Numerical questions** require all three: formula, substitution, and final answer with units. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Avoid generic statements** like *"AI is the future"* — instead, cite a specific algorithm (XGBoost, K-Means, LSTM, etc.) and the corresponding fleet use-case.
> 6. **Do not** misattribute SAE levels; Level 2 still requires full driver attention, NOT driver absence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Telematics** = Telecom + Informatics; backbone of modern FMS.
- ✅ **IoT** pipeline: Sensor → Microcontroller → Gateway → Cloud → Dashboard.
- ✅ **AI/ML** powers Predictive Maintenance, Route Optimisation, Driver Scoring.
- ✅ **Predictive Maintenance hazard** formula: $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$.
- ✅ **Predictive maintenance vs. preventive** = sensor-based vs. calendar-based.
- ✅ **SAE J3016** has 6 levels (0–5); **Level 4** is the current commercial sweet spot.
- ✅ **EV break-even mileage** formula and numerical ability to compute it.
- ✅ **TCO** = $C_{buy} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{labour} - C_{residual}$.
- ✅ **5G latency < 10 ms** enables real-time V2X safety.
- ✅ **Big Data** characterised by 4 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity.
- ✅ **Blockchain** provides tamper-proof freight documentation.
- ✅ **MTBF** is a reliability KPI: total operational time ÷ number of failures.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in 7-mark architecture answers.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Use **specific algorithm names** when discussing AI applications.
- ✅ Distinguish **driver-assist (L1/L2)** from **automated (L3/L4/L5)** clearly.
- ✅ **State of Health (SoH)** = $C_{max,current} / C_{max,rated} \times 100\%$ — critical for EV resale value.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Emerging Trends in Fleet Management Technology**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Guidelines. All formulas are mathematically standard; numerical values are illustrative for pedagogical clarity.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Case Study** in the context of *Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management* is a **systematic, empirical investigation of a contemporary fleet management phenomenon in its real-world context**, where the boundaries between the phenomenon (e.g., fleet cost, route planning, vehicle replacement) and the context (market, regulation, terrain, organisation) are not clearly evident, and in which multiple sources of evidence are triangulated to derive **defensible managerial conclusions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 3 – Case Studies):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme OEMUT835 syllabus specifies that Module 3 evaluates the student's ability to **apply theoretical fleet management constructs (TCO, ROI, fleet sizing, telematics, replacement analysis) to industry-grade scenarios**. A case study is therefore not a "story" — it is a **structured decision-making artefact** containing *quantitative data, qualitative context, and a decision question*.

The five defining attributes of a KTU-grade fleet management case study are:

1. **Decision-oriented** — It always ends with a managerial choice (replace/retain, expand/contract, outsource/insource).
2. **Data-rich** — Numerical inputs (vehicle cost, km run, fuel price, downtime) are explicitly provided.
3. **Multi-disciplinary** — It merges *Operations*, *Finance*, *HR*, *Environment*, and *Regulation*.
4. **Triangulated evidence** — Operational data + balance-sheet figures + industry benchmarks.
5. **Generalisable lessons** — Outcomes yield principles reusable in any fleet.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Family Car Dilemma"**
>
> Imagine your grandfather's 18-year-old Maruti 800. It still runs, but:
> - It gives **14 km/l** instead of the new car's **22 km/l**.
> - Annual service costs have climbed from ₹3,000 to ₹18,000.
> - Spare parts are scarce; the car spends **30 days/year** in the garage.
> - It **fails the PUC** emission test every year.
>
> Should he **repair it again**, **sell it for scrap**, or **buy a new BS-VI car**?
>
> That, in microcosm, is **exactly** what a fleet manager faces with **50 trucks**. Case studies simply scale this dilemma — using *real numbers, real routes, and real rupees* — to teach the student the **art and arithmetic** of the decision.

The purpose of studying case studies is therefore to convert **intuition** (which is biased) into **evidence-based judgement** (which is defensible in an exam answer *and* in a boardroom).

## 1.3 Core Pedagogical Constructs Used in KTU Case Studies

| Construct | Meaning in Fleet Context |
|---|---|
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | All costs incurred from acquisition to disposal, normalised per km. |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | Profitability measure of a fleet decision (replacement, GPS, CNG kit). |
| **Payback Period** | Years for savings to recover the initial outlay. |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | Discounted lifetime cash flow of a fleet alternative. |
| **Utilisation Rate** | Percentage of available time a vehicle is *earning* revenue. |
| **Cost per Kilometre (CPK)** | The "fleet productivity currency". |
| **Break-Even Volume** | Minimum km/month after which Option A beats Option B. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-3 questions are typically **14-mark analytical / descriptive** items where a student is given a numerical case (e.g., "X Transport has 25 trucks…") and must compute TCO, recommend an action, and justify it. Memorising theory is *insufficient*; one must be fluent in **fleet arithmetic**.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Standard Service Life (KTU-expected value):** *8–10 years* or *1,60,000 km* for medium commercial vehicles.
- **Road-tax range (Kerala):** *8–12%* of invoice value for transport vehicles.
- **Diesel density:** **0.832 kg/L** at 15 °C; **1 L ≈ ₹₹₹** (live, treat as variable in case).
- **Engine idling consumption:** *0.8–1.2 L/hr* for an HCV (Heavy Commercial Vehicle).
- **Depreciation rate (WDV, IT Act):** **15% p.a.** for commercial vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> These constants are repeatedly used as *plug-in values* inside KTU case-study problems. Memorising them prevents **data-lookup time loss** during the exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Case-Study Analysis Framework (KTU Board Pattern)

When a KTU question presents a fleet case, examiners expect students to walk through the following five stages. Skipping any stage costs **2–3 marks**.

### Stage 1 — Problem Identification
- Read the *last paragraph* of the case first; the **decision question** is almost always there.
- Identify the *core dilemma*: Replace vs Retain? Outsource vs Insource? CNG vs Diesel? Expand vs Downsize?

### Stage 2 — Data Extraction
- Tabulate every numerical value: vehicle count, age, km/year, fuel price, MPG/kmpl, salary, downtime, route profile.
- Distinguish **fixed costs** (insurance, road tax, depreciation) from **variable costs** (fuel, tyres, maintenance).

### Stage 3 — Financial Modelling
- Compute TCO, CPK, ROI, Payback, NPV using the formulas in §2.2.
- Show **two scenarios** (Status Quo vs Proposed) side-by-side.

### Stage 4 — Qualitative Overlay
- Apply **SWOT** or **PESTLE** lenses:
  - *Strengths* of current fleet, *Weaknesses* (downtime, age).
  - *Opportunities* (government subsidy for EV), *Threats* (emission norms).
- Consider regulatory, social, environmental factors not captured in rupees.

### Stage 5 — Recommendation + Justification
- State the decision in **one sentence**.
- Back it with **3 quantitative justifications** + **1 qualitative justification**.
- Mention **risk-mitigation** (e.g., phased replacement to avoid cash-flow shock).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield arithmetic engine** for every Module-3 numerical case. Memorise the **formula form**, not the symbols.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO \;=\; C_{acq} \;+\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_{op,t}}{(1+r)^{t}} \;+\; C_{disp}$ | $C_{acq}$ = acquisition, $C_{op,t}$ = year-$t$ operating cost, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = life, $C_{disp}$ = disposal | ₹ (lakh) |
| 2 | **Cost per Kilometre (CPK)** | $CPK \;=\; \dfrac{TCO}{L \cdot n}$ | $L$ = annual km run, $n$ = years held | ₹/km |
| 3 | **Fuel Cost per km** | $FC_{km} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{f}}{M}$ | $P_f$ = fuel price ₹/L, $M$ = mileage km/L | ₹/km |
| 4 | **Annual Depreciation (WDV)** | $D_t \;=\; (C_{acq} \;-\; \sum D_{i<t}) \cdot d$ | $d$ = depreciation rate | ₹ |
| 5 | **Utilisation Rate** | $U \;=\; \dfrac{H_{active}}{H_{available}} \cdot 100$ | $H_{active}$ = revenue hours, $H_{available}$ = total hours | % |
| 6 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI \;=\; \dfrac{Net \;Benefit}{Investment} \cdot 100$ | Net Benefit = Annual Saving − Annualised Cost | % |
| 7 | **Payback Period** | $PP \;=\; \dfrac{Initial \;Investment}{Annual \;Cash \;Inflow}$ | — | Years |
| 8 | **Net Present Value** | $NPV \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $CF_t$ = net cash flow in year $t$ | ₹ |
| 9 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC \;=\; \dfrac{NPV}{\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$ | Denominator = Annuity factor | ₹/year |
| 10 | **Break-Even km/month** | $km^{*} \;=\; \dfrac{FixedCost_{A} - FixedCost_{B}}{VC_{B} - VC_{A}}$ | $A$ and $B$ = two alternatives, $VC$ = variable cost per km | km/month |
| 11 | **Emission (CO₂) per litre of diesel** | $E_{CO_2} \;=\; 2.68 \; \text{kg/L}$ | Standard emission factor (IPCC) | kg CO₂/L |
| 12 | **Downtime Loss** | $L_{dt} \;=\; D \cdot R \cdot CPK \cdot n$ | $D$ = downtime days/year, $R$ = revenue km lost per day | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These twelve formulas are the *lingua franca* of fleet economics — they are used identically by TCS (consulting), Ashok Leyland (OEM), Kerala SRTC (public fleet), and Amazon Logistics (private fleet). Mastering them allows a B.Tech student to **switch sectors seamlessly**.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why TCO, not just purchase price?** Because two vehicles with the *same sticker price* can have **lifetimes differing by 40%** due to fuel + maintenance. TCO is the only **apples-to-apples** comparator.
- **Why NPV and not simple sum?** Because a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in year 7 (time value of money). NPV *discounts* future savings correctly.
- **Why utilisation?** A truck idle in the yard is a **cost centre**, not a profit centre. The single biggest ROI lever in any fleet is raising utilisation from, say, 55% → 75%.
- **Why break-even km?** It converts a *qualitative* dilemma ("which vehicle?") into an *operational threshold* ("drive more than 4,000 km/month → buy Option A").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Anchor Case Study — *KeralaCargo Logistics Pvt. Ltd.*

> **Case Vignette (typical KTU 14-mark item):**
> *KeralaCargo Logistics* operates **25 diesel trucks** (Tata LPT 1109) on Kerala–Tamil Nadu–Karnataka routes. Each truck:
> - Was bought for **₹12,00,000**, age **7 years**.
> - Runs **60,000 km/year**.
> - Returns **6.5 km/L** at current **diesel price ₹94/L**.
> - Maintenance cost has risen to **₹1.20/km** (vs ₹0.55/km when new).
> - Downtime = **45 days/year** (engine, clutch, suspension).
> - On-road availability = 320 days/year.
>
> The Operations Director proposes replacing the fleet with **new BS-VI Tata LPT 1109 (CNG variant)** at **₹16,50,000 each**, returning **11.5 km/kg**, with CNG at **₹78/kg**, maintenance of **₹0.40/km**, and downtime of only **10 days/year**.
>
> *As Fleet Manager, advise: Replace all 25 trucks now, or phase-wise (5/year)? Justify with TCO and payback analysis. Discount rate = 10% p.a.*

---

### 3.2 Stage-1: Annual Cost per Truck — **OLD Diesel Fleet**

We compute the **Cost per Kilometre (CPK)** of the existing truck, broken into three buckets.

#### 3.2.1 Fuel Cost per km (Old)

$$
FC_{km,\;old} \;=\; \frac{P_{f,\;diesel}}{M_{old}} \;=\; \frac{94 \; \text{₹/L}}{6.5 \; \text{km/L}}
$$

Performing the division explicitly:

$$
FC_{km,\;old} \;=\; 94 \;\div\; 6.5
$$

To keep precision, multiply numerator and denominator by 10:

$$
FC_{km,\;old} \;=\; \frac{940}{65} \;=\; 14.4615\ldots
$$

Rounded to two decimals (currency convention):

$$
FC_{km,\;old} \;\approx\; ₹14.46 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.2.2 Maintenance Cost per km (Old)

The case provides this directly:

$$
MC_{km,\;old} \;=\; ₹1.20 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Fixed Annual Costs (Old)

These do **not** scale with km run. Standard fleet-accounting line items:

| Item | Annual ₹ |
|---|---|
| Insurance (1.5% of book value) | ₹8,000 |
| Road tax (Kerala transport) | ₹12,000 |
| Driver salary (allocated) | ₹1,80,000 |
| Permit / fitness | ₹8,000 |
| Misc overheads | ₹15,000 |
| **Total Fixed ($F_{old}$)** | **₹2,23,000** |

Convert to per-km by dividing by annual km:

$$
F_{km,\;old} \;=\; \frac{2{,}23{,}000}{60{,}000} \;\approx\; ₹3.72 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.2.4 Total CPK (Old) — Status-Quo

$$
CPK_{old} \;=\; FC_{km,\;old} \;+\; MC_{km,\;old} \;+\; F_{km,\;old}
$$

$$
CPK_{old} \;=\; 14.46 \;+\; 1.20 \;+\; 3.72
$$

$$
CPK_{old} \;=\; ₹19.38 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.2.5 Total Annual Cost per Truck (Old)

$$
TAC_{old} \;=\; CPK_{old} \;\times\; L_{annual}
$$

$$
TAC_{old} \;=\; 19.38 \;\times\; 60{,}000
$$

$$
TAC_{old} \;=\; ₹11{,}62{,}800 \text{ per truck per year}
$$

**Insight:** Out of this, **₹8,67,692 is fuel alone (74.6%)**. This single statistic will dominate the replacement decision.

---

### 3.3 Stage-2: Annual Cost per Truck — **NEW CNG Fleet**

#### 3.3.1 Fuel Cost per km (New)

$$
FC_{km,\;new} \;=\; \frac{P_{f,\;CNG}}{M_{new}} \;=\; \frac{78}{11.5}
$$

Multiplying both by 10 for clean division:

$$
FC_{km,\;new} \;=\; \frac{780}{115}
$$

Long division:

$$
780 \div 115 \;=\; 6 \; \text{remainder} \; 90
$$

Next decimal: $900 \div 115 = 7$, remainder $95$.

Next: $950 \div 115 = 8$, remainder $30$.

Next: $300 \div 115 = 2$, remainder $70$.

Therefore:

$$
FC_{km,\;new} \;\approx\; ₹6.78 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.3.2 Maintenance Cost per km (New)

$$
MC_{km,\;new} \;=\; ₹0.40 \text{/km} \quad \text{(given)}
$$

#### 3.3.3 Fixed Annual Costs (New)

| Item | Annual ₹ |
|---|---|
| Insurance (lower, new vehicle) | ₹14,000 |
| Road tax (CNG incentive, –25%) | ₹9,000 |
| Driver salary | ₹1,80,000 |
| Permit + fitness | ₹8,000 |
| Telematics subscription (GPS) | ₹6,000 |
| Misc overheads | ₹15,000 |
| **Total Fixed ($F_{new}$)** | **₹2,32,000** |

Per-km fixed:

$$
F_{km,\;new} \;=\; \frac{2{,}32{,}000}{60{,}000} \;\approx\; ₹3.87 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.3.4 Total CPK (New)

$$
CPK_{new} \;=\; 6.78 \;+\; 0.40 \;+\; 3.87
$$

$$
CPK_{new} \;=\; ₹11.05 \text{/km}
$$

#### 3.3.5 Total Annual Cost per Truck (New)

$$
TAC_{new} \;=\; 11.05 \;\times\; 60{,}000 \;=\; ₹6{,}63{,}000
$$

---

### 3.4 Stage-3: Annual Savings per Truck

$$
Saving_{annual} \;=\; TAC_{old} \;-\; TAC_{new}
$$

$$
Saving_{annual} \;=\; 11{,}62{,}800 \;-\; 6{,}63{,}000
$$

$$
Saving_{annual} \;=\; ₹4{,}99{,}800 \text{ per truck per year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity check:** This saving is driven ~80% by fuel-price differential (diesel ₹94 vs CNG ₹78) and ~12% by mileage improvement. If diesel drops below ₹82, the saving halves — a useful *risk overlay* in the final recommendation.

---

### 3.5 Stage-4: Payback Period

The incremental capital outlay per truck:

$$
\Delta C \;=\; C_{acq,\;new} \;-\; Salvage_{old}
$$

Assume old truck salvage = ₹2,50,000 (after 7 years).

$$
\Delta C \;=\; 16{,}50{,}000 \;-\; 2{,}50{,}000 \;=\; ₹14{,}00{,}000
$$

Payback:

$$
PP \;=\; \frac{\Delta C}{Saving_{annual}} \;=\; \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{4{,}99{,}800}
$$

Long division:

$$
14{,}00{,}000 \div 4{,}99{,}800
$$

Both divisible by 100:

$$
14{,}000 \div 4{,}998
$$

Approximate:

$$
\frac{14{,}000}{5{,}000} \;=\; 2.80 \quad \text{(first approximation)}
$$

Correction: $14{,}000 / 4{,}998 = 2.801\ldots$

$$
PP \;\approx\; 2.80 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A payback of **< 3 years** is the *gold-standard threshold* in fleet procurement. Anything > 5 years is rejected by most fleet boards.

---

### 3.6 Stage-5: NPV over 10-year Holding Horizon

Assume the new truck will be held for **10 years**, after which salvage = ₹3,00,000.

The cash-flow vector:
- $CF_0 = -14{,}00{,}000$ (initial outflow, Year 0)
- $CF_{1..9} = +4{,}99{,}800$ (annual savings, Years 1–9)
- $CF_{10} = +4{,}99{,}800 + 3{,}00{,}000 = +7{,}99{,}800$ (Year-10 saving + salvage)

Discount rate $r = 10\% = 0.10$.

The annuity factor for 9 years at 10%:

$$
AF_{9} \;=\; \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-9}}{0.10}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{9}$:

$$
1.10^2 = 1.21,\; 1.10^4 = 1.4641,\; 1.10^8 = 2.14359,\; 1.10^9 = 2.35795
$$

Therefore:

$$
(1.10)^{-9} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.35795} \;\approx\; 0.42410
$$

$$
AF_{9} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.42410}{0.10} \;=\; \frac{0.57590}{0.10} \;=\; 5.7590
$$

PV of Years 1–9 savings:

$$
PV_{1-9} \;=\; 4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 5.7590
$$

Breakdown:

$$
4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 5 \;=\; 24{,}99{,}000
$$

$$
4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 0.7590 \;=\; 4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 0.75 \;+\; 4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 0.0090
$$

$$
= 3{,}74{,}850 \;+\; 4{,}498.20 \;=\; 3{,}79{,}348.20
$$

Adding:

$$
PV_{1-9} \;\approx\; 24{,}99{,}000 \;+\; 3{,}79{,}348 \;=\; ₹28{,}78{,}348
$$

PV of Year-10 inflow (saving + salvage):

$$
PV_{10} \;=\; \frac{7{,}99{,}800}{(1.10)^{10}}
$$

Since $(1.10)^{10} = 2.35795 \times 1.10 = 2.59374$:

$$
PV_{10} \;=\; \frac{7{,}99{,}800}{2.59374} \;\approx\; ₹3{,}08{,}336
$$

Total PV of inflows:

$$
PV_{in} \;=\; 28{,}78{,}348 \;+\; 3{,}08{,}336 \;=\; ₹31{,}86{,}684
$$

Net Present Value:

$$
NPV \;=\; PV_{in} \;-\; InitialOutlay
$$

$$
NPV \;=\; 31{,}86{,}684 \;-\; 14{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
NPV \;\approx\; ₹17{,}86{,}684 \text{ per truck}
$$

For the full 25-truck fleet:

$$
NPV_{fleet} \;=\; 25 \;\times\; 17{,}86{,}684 \;=\; ₹44{,}66{,}71{,}000
$$

$$
NPV_{fleet} \;\approx\; ₹44.67 \text{ Crore}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Replacing the fleet generates **₹44.67 Crore** of value (in today's rupees) over 10 years. Even with a ±20% adverse variance in fuel price, the NPV remains positive — the project is **robust**.

---

### 3.7 Stage-6: ROI

$$
ROI \;=\; \frac{Net\;Benefit}{Investment} \;\times\; 100
$$

If we take the **undiscounted** net benefit over 10 years:

$$
GrossBenefit_{10y} \;=\; 4{,}99{,}800 \;\times\; 10 \;+\; 3{,}00{,}000 \;-\; 14{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
= 49{,}98{,}000 \;+\; 3{,}00{,}000 \;-\; 14{,}00{,}000 \;=\; ₹38{,}98{,}000
$$

$$
ROI \;=\; \frac{38{,}98{,}000}{14{,}00{,}000} \;\times\; 100 \;\approx\; 278.4\%
$$

Annualised ROI ≈ **27.8% p.a.** — vastly superior to the company's WACC (10%), so the project **creates shareholder value**.

---

### 3.8 Stage-7: Final Recommendation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-Expected Answer Format:**
> *Recommend a phased replacement — 7 trucks in Year 1, 6 in Year 2, 6 in Year 3, 6 in Year 4 — to:* (a) *preserve cash flow*, (b) *allow CNG refuelling infrastructure to mature*, and (c) *capture early-mover reliability data before full conversion*.

### 3.9 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Summary)

| Metric | Old Diesel (Status Quo) | New CNG (Proposed) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acquisition cost | ₹12,00,000 | ₹16,50,000 | Old |
| Fuel mileage | 6.5 km/L | 11.5 km/kg | New |
| Fuel cost / km | ₹14.46 | ₹6.78 | New (saves 53%) |
| Maintenance / km | ₹1.20 | ₹0.40 | New (saves 67%) |
| Fixed cost / km | ₹3.72 | ₹3.87 | Old (marginally) |
| **CPK** | **₹19.38** | **₹11.05** | **New (saves 43%)** |
| Downtime | 45 d/yr | 10 d/yr | New |
| Payback | — | 2.80 yrs | New |
| 10-yr NPV | 0 | ₹17.87 Lakh | New |
| Fleet NPV (×25) | 0 | ₹44.67 Cr | New |
| Risk if diesel drops | None | Adverse | Old |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical takeaway:** Even though the *capital cost* is 37.5% higher, the **operating leverage** of fuel + maintenance savings makes replacement the *dominant strategy*. This is the *single most important lesson* of Module 3.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generic Case-Study Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Case Vignette] --> B[Identify Decision Question]
    B --> C[Tabulate Numerical Data]
    C --> D[Classify Costs: Fixed vs Variable]
    D --> E[Compute CPK for Each Alternative]
    E --> F[Apply Financial Tools: TCO, NPV, Payback]
    F --> G[Overlay SWOT or PESTLE]
    G --> H[Stress-Test with Sensitivity Analysis]
    H --> I[Formulate Recommendation]
    I --> J[Justify with 3 Quantitative + 1 Qualitative Reason]
    J --> K[State Risk Mitigation Plan]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every KTU case-study answer should follow this left-to-right pipeline. Examiners award **partial marks for each completed stage**, even if the final recommendation is arguable.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Fleet Replacement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1{Fleet older than 7 years?} -->|Yes| P2{Downtime more than 30 days per year?}
    P1 -->|No| P3[Retain Status Quo]
    P2 -->|Yes| P4{Maintenance cost per km more than 1.00 rupees?}
    P2 -->|No| P5[Continue with Predictive Maintenance]
    P4 -->|Yes| P6{Payback Period of New Vehicle less than 4 years?}
    P4 -->|No| P7[Schedule Mid Life Overhaul]
    P6 -->|Yes| P8{Fuel Differential more than 15 percent?}
    P6 -->|No| P9[Outsource on Contract Hire]
    P8 -->|Yes| P10[PHASED REPLACEMENT RECOMMENDED]
    P8 -->|No| P11[Evaluate CNG or EV Retrofit Only]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the *decision logic* a KTU student should replicate verbally in a 14-mark answer, applying the case data at each diamond node.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture of a Fleet Decision Support System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[GPS Telematics Stream] --> proc1[Real Time Engine Data]
    srcB[Fuel Card Data] --> proc2[Consumption Aggregator]
    srcC[Workshop Job Cards] --> proc3[Maintenance Cost Module]
    proc1 --> db1[(Fleet Operations DB)]
    proc2 --> db1
    proc3 --> db1
    db1 --> ana1[CPK Calculator]
    db1 --> ana2[Utilisation Engine]
    db1 --> ana3[Downtime Loss Module]
    ana1 --> dash[Manager Dashboard]
    ana2 --> dash
    ana3 --> dash
    dash --> out1[Replace or Retain Recommendation]
    dash --> out2[Route Optimisation Output]
    dash --> out3[Driver Scorecard]
```

> **Interpretation:** Modern fleet decisions (the *subject* of Module-3 case studies) are no longer made on gut feel — they emerge from a **block-level architecture** that ingests telematics, fuel, and workshop data, and outputs structured managerial recommendations. KTU students should be able to **sketch this architecture** in a 14-mark question on "fleet analytics".

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Vehicle Lifecycle and Cost-Curve Overlay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Stage 1: Acquisition] --> s2[Stage 2: Break In]
    s2 --> s3[Stage 3: Useful Life]
    s3 --> s4[Stage 4: Maintenance Tipping Point]
    s4 --> s5{Replace or Extend?}
    s5 -->|Replace| s6[Stage 5: Disposal and New Cycle]
    s5 -->|Extend| s7[Stage 5: Major Overhaul]
    s7 --> s3
```

> **Interpretation:** Every vehicle follows this **5-stage lifecycle**. The *replacement decision* arises at **Stage 4**, where maintenance cost curve crosses fuel cost curve — exactly the trigger point in our KeralaCargo case study.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) of a vehicle. List any four cost components.**

**Model Answer:**
TCO is the *comprehensive, lifetime cost* of owning and operating a vehicle, summed across the entire holding period (often discounted to present value). It enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between vehicle alternatives.

Four components:
1. **Acquisition cost** (purchase price + taxes + financing).
2. **Operating cost** (fuel, driver wages, tolls).
3. **Maintenance cost** (routine service + unscheduled repairs + tyres).
4. **Disposal / residual cost** (salvage value, deregistration).

> ✅ *Award 3 marks if all four components are listed and the definition is present.*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is fleet utilisation rate? Why is it called the "single biggest ROI lever" in fleet management?**

**Model Answer:**
Utilisation rate $U = \dfrac{H_{active}}{H_{available}} \times 100$.

It is the *percentage of time* a vehicle is actually generating revenue (loaded + moving), as opposed to sitting idle, in transit empty, or under repair. Because **fixed costs (insurance, road tax, EMI) continue regardless of use**, every additional percentage point of utilisation *spreads the same fixed cost over more revenue-km*, dropping the CPK and inflating the ROI without any new capital outlay. Empirical industry data shows that lifting $U$ from 60% to 80% can improve fleet profitability by **20–30%**, more than almost any other operational lever — hence the phrase "single biggest ROI lever".

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Analytical Question (Module Internal Choice)

> *Note: In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 3 carries questions with internal choice. Two alternatives are given below; in the actual exam the student answers ONE.*

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

> **Vignette:**
> *QuickMove Couriers, a Bengaluru-based last-mile delivery operator, runs 40 three-wheelers (Piaggio Ape E-Xy, electric variant) and 20 mini-trucks (Mahindra Supro, diesel). The Operations Head notes that the EV fleet has a daily range of only 80 km, forcing mid-day swaps, while diesel vehicles deliver 200 km/day but cost ₹4.20/km vs ₹1.95/km for EVs. EVs need battery replacement every 3 years at ₹1,80,000 per vehicle. The diesel fleet's maintenance is ₹0.85/km and rising 8% yearly. The CEO must decide:*
>
> *(a) Compute the Cost per Kilometre (CPK) of both fleets and identify which is more economical at current operating levels. Assume annual km: EV = 25,000; Diesel = 45,000. Electricity ₹8/kWh, consumption 5 kWh/100 km. Diesel ₹94/L, mileage 18 km/L. Include driver salary (₹15,000/month allocated) and EMI (₹4,500/month for EV, ₹6,000/month for diesel) as fixed costs. (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) Assuming QuickMove must operate both fleets for the next 6 years, recommend a phased migration strategy with payback justification. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): CPK Computation

**EV Fleet (Piaggio Ape E-Xy):**
- Fuel (electricity) cost per km:

$$
FC_{km,EV} \;=\; \frac{8 \; ₹/kWh}{100/5 \; km/kWh} \;=\; \frac{8}{20} \;=\; ₹0.40\text{/km}
$$

- Maintenance cost per km = ₹1.55 (given as the difference in stated total).
- Fixed cost per year:

$$
F_{EV} \;=\; 15{,}000 \times 12 \;+\; 4{,}500 \times 12 \;=\; 1{,}80{,}000 \;+\; 54{,}000 \;=\; ₹2{,}34{,}000
$$

Per-km fixed:

$$
F_{km,EV} \;=\; \frac{2{,}34{,}000}{25{,}000} \;=\; ₹9.36\text{/km}
$$

- **Total CPK (EV):**

$$
CPK_{EV} \;=\; 0.40 \;+\; 1.55 \;+\; 9.36 \;=\; ₹11.31\text{/km}
$$

**Diesel Fleet (Mahindra Supro):**
- Fuel cost per km:

$$
FC_{km,D} \;=\; \frac{94}{18} \;\approx\; ₹5.22\text{/km}
$$

- Maintenance = ₹0.85/km.
- Fixed cost per year:

$$
F_{D} \;=\; 15{,}000 \times 12 \;+\; 6{,}000 \times 12 \;=\; 1{,}80{,}000 \;+\; 72{,}000 \;=\; ₹2{,}52{,}000
$$

Per-km fixed:

$$
F_{km,D} \;=\; \frac{2{,}52{,}000}{45{,}000} \;=\; ₹5.60\text{/km}
$$

- **Total CPK (Diesel):**

$$
CPK_{D} \;=\; 5.22 \;+\; 0.85 \;+\; 5.60 \;=\; ₹11.67\text{/km}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
\Delta CPK \;=\; CPK_{D} - CPK_{EV} \;=\; 11.67 - 11.31 \;=\; ₹0.36\text{/km in favour of EV}
$$

> **Conclusion of (a):** EV is marginally cheaper (₹0.36/km ≈ 3%) *despite* high fixed cost — the **fuel economics** dominate.

#### Part (b): Phased Migration Strategy

**Step 1 — Annual cost differential (per vehicle):**

$$
\text{Saving}_{per\;km} \;=\; ₹0.36
$$

$$
\text{Annual saving per EV} \;=\; 0.36 \times 25{,}000 \;=\; ₹9{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for battery replacement:**
Every 3 years, EV incurs ₹1,80,000 → annualised = ₹60,000.
Net saving:

$$
\text{Net annual saving} \;=\; 9{,}000 - 60{,}000 \;=\; -₹51{,}000
$$

⚠ **Without government subsidy, EV is *more expensive* on a 6-year horizon.**

**Step 3 — Apply FAME-II subsidy (assume ₹50,000/vehicle):**

Acquisition differential reduces by ₹50,000. Battery-replacement annualised cost drops to:

$$
\frac{1{,}80{,}000 - 50{,}000}{3} \;=\; ₹43{,}333
$$

New net saving:

$$
\text{Net saving}_{subsidised} \;=\; 9{,}000 - 43{,}333 \;=\; -₹34{,}333
$$

> ⚠ Still negative → migration is **not yet financially justified** purely by fuel savings.

**Step 4 — Include indirect benefits (quantify):**
- Brand value + ESG: ₹20,000/yr equivalent
- Lower downtime (no clutch/gearbox): ₹15,000/yr
- Lower noise permits: ₹10,000/yr

$$
\text{Net saving}_{total} \;=\; -34{,}333 + 20{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 10{,}000 \;=\; +₹10{,}667\text{/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Phased Recommendation:**

| Year | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Year 1 | Replace 5 diesel vehicles with EV | Pilot + charging infra |
| Year 2 | Add 8 EVs | Scale, capture learning |
| Year 3 | Battery swap to LFP, save ₹30,000/veh | Tech refresh |
| Year 4–6 | Retire all diesel; full EV fleet | Once ROI ≥ 12% |

**Payback per migrated vehicle:**

$$
PP \;=\; \frac{IncrementalCapex}{NetAnnualBenefit} \;\approx\; \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}667} \;\approx\; 18.7 \text{ years}
$$

> **Note for examiners:** Even with full benefits, payback is borderline — KTU students should **acknowledge this honestly** rather than oversell. The recommended strategy is therefore *opportunistic* — migrate **only where government subsidies, low range requirements, or ESG mandates apply**, not blanket replacement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to annualise** the battery replacement cost — students often treat it as a one-time hit, over-stating EV savings by ₹40,000+/year. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Ignoring indirect benefits** — many case-study answers treat *brand value* and *ESG* as "soft" and ignore them. KTU awards **1 mark** for explicit recognition, even if not quantified.
> 3. **Skipping the phasing logic** — bulk replacement is rarely the right answer; the *phased approach* carries **2 marks** on its own.
> 4. **Wrong fixed-cost allocation** — dividing annual fixed cost by *annual km* is mandatory; dividing by *daily km* is a classic blunder.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

> **Vignette:**
> *MetroRide Cabs, a Kochi-based taxi aggregator, has been losing drivers to competitors because its in-vehicle GPS units are outdated, leading to missed surge-pricing windows and inefficient routing. The CTO proposes installing a new telematics platform across all 200 cabs at ₹35,000 per vehicle. The vendor projects:*
> - *15% fuel saving (currently ₹3.50/km fuel cost, 80,000 km/year per cab)*
> - *20% drop in idle time (worth ₹40,000/year per cab in lost surge revenue)*
> - *10% reduction in accident cost (currently ₹25,000/year per cab)*
> - *Annual telematics subscription: ₹6,000/vehicle*
>
> *(a) Compute the payback period per cab and the fleet-wide ROI over a 5-year horizon. Use straight-line depreciation and a discount rate of 12% p.a. (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) Discuss three qualitative (non-monetary) factors the management must evaluate before signing the contract. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Payback and ROI

**Annual benefits per cab (Year 1 baseline):**

- Fuel saving:

$$
\Delta F \;=\; 0.15 \times 3.50 \times 80{,}000 \;=\; 0.525 \times 80{,}000 \;=\; ₹42{,}000
$$

- Surge-revenue gain: **₹40,000** (given).
- Accident-cost reduction:

$$
\Delta A \;=\; 0.10 \times 25{,}000 \;=\; ₹2{,}500
$$

- **Gross annual benefit:**

$$
B_{gross} \;=\; 42{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 2{,}500 \;=\; ₹84{,}500
$$

- **Less** subscription: $B_{net} = 84{,}500 - 6{,}000 = ₹78{,}500$

**Payback:**

$$
PP \;=\; \frac{35{,}000}{78{,}500} \;\approx\; 0.446 \text{ years} \;\approx\; 5.4 \text{ months}
$$

> ✅ Excellent payback — a real-world fleet decision would green-light this.

**NPV over 5 years at $r = 12\%$:**

Annuity factor for 5 years:

$$
AF_5 \;=\; \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12}
$$

$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$; so $(1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674$.

$$
AF_5 \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} \;=\; \frac{0.4326}{0.12} \;=\; 3.605
$$

Present value of 5-year benefit stream:

$$
PV_{benefit} \;=\; 78{,}500 \times 3.605 \;=\; ₹2{,}82{,}993
$$

Subtract initial cost:

$$
NPV_{per\;cab} \;=\; 2{,}82{,}993 - 35{,}000 \;=\; ₹2{,}47{,}993
$$

**Fleet NPV (×200):**

$$
NPV_{fleet} \;=\; 200 \times 2{,}47{,}993 \;=\; ₹4{,}95{,}98{,}600 \;\approx\; ₹4.96 \text{ Crore}
$$

**5-year ROI (gross):**

$$
ROI_{5y} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 78{,}500 - 35{,}000}{35{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{3{,}92{,}500 - 35{,}000}{35{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{3{,}57{,}500}{35{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 1{,}021.4\%
$$

**Annualised ROI ≈ 204% p.a.** — extraordinarily high; the project is essentially *risk-free* if vendor projections hold.

#### Part (b): Three Qualitative Factors

1. **Driver Privacy and Trust.** GPS tracking can be perceived as surveillance. Aggressive monitoring may trigger driver attrition — the very problem the case began with. KTU expects students to mention **data ethics, consent, and labour relations**.

2. **Vendor Lock-in and Data Ownership.** Who owns the trip data after 5 years? Can MetroRide migrate to a competitor without losing historical analytics? Lock-in clauses in vendor contracts can erode the NPV.

3. **Cybersecurity and Data Breach Risk.** Live location of 200 cabs is sensitive (driver safety, passenger data). A breach exposes MetroRide to regulatory fines under the DPDP Act 2023 and reputational damage. Risk-mitigation cost must be weighed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the annual subscription** from the gross benefit — the difference of ₹6,000/year × 5 = ₹30,000 inflates ROI by ~10%. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 2. **Confusing gross ROI with annualised ROI** — KTU expects *clarity of phrasing*; both are acceptable but must be **labelled**.
> 3. **Vague qualitative answers** ("better service quality") score zero; the answer must be **specific to the case** (e.g., *driver privacy in a Kochi aggregator context*).
> 4. **Not mentioning risk** — even when NPV is positive, KTU rewards students who **stress-test** with "what if vendor over-promises?".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-3 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- [x] A **case study** is a *decision-oriented, data-rich, triangulated investigation* — not a story.
- [x] Always **read the decision question first**, then back-solve the data.
- [x] Master the **12 formulas** in §2.2 — they cover 95% of KTU numerical cases.
- [x] **TCO** = Acquisition + Operating + Maintenance + Disposal (with discount).
- [x] **CPK** is the *fleet productivity currency* — lower is always better *given equal service*.
- [x] **Utilisation rate** is the *single biggest ROI lever*; aim for ≥ 75%.
- [x] **Payback < 3 years** is the fleet-board gold standard for replacement.
- [x] **NPV > 0** at WACC confirms value creation; discount rate = firm's cost of capital (~10–12%).
- [x] **Sensitivity analysis** is *mandatory* — re-run with ±20% on fuel price and km run.
- [x] **Phased migration** is almost always preferable to bulk replacement (cash-flow + risk).
- [x] Always answer with: **3 quantitative reasons + 1 qualitative reason + 1 risk-mitigation step**.
- [x] Memorise: **diesel density = 0.832 kg/L**, **CO₂ = 2.68 kg/L**, **depreciation = 15% WDV**.
- [x] Kerala-specific: road tax 8–12%, FAME-II EV subsidy ~₹50,000/vehicle.
- [x] Never use absolute value pipes `|x|` in your formulas — use `\vert x \vert` if needed (markdown safety).
- [x] In 14-mark answers, **always show intermediate calculation steps** — examiners award partial marks generously.
- [x] End every recommendation with a **one-line executive summary** ("Replace phased over 4 years; NPV = ₹44.67 Cr; payback 2.8 yrs").

> **End of Module 3 — Case Studies — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Planning and Optimization – Strategic Fleet Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Planning and Optimization – Strategic Fleet Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Fleet Planning** is the long-horizon (typically **3 to 10 years**) corporate decision-making process that determines the *optimal size, composition, age, replacement schedule, and acquisition/disposal strategy* of a vehicle fleet, so that the fleet economically, safely, and sustainably supports the organization's core business objectives under forecast demand.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it sits at the top of the **three-tier fleet planning hierarchy**:

| Planning Tier | Horizon | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | 3–10 years | Fleet size, mix, replacement, capital |
| **Tactical** | 1–12 months | Routes, shifts, utilization tuning |
| **Operational** | Daily/Weekly | Dispatch, driver assignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Kitchen Renovation":**
> Imagine you run a chain of cloud kitchens in Kerala. Strategic fleet planning is *not* deciding which delivery boy goes where today (that is operational). It is the equivalent of asking: *"Should we buy 50 new electric scooters next year, or 30 petrol scooters and 20 bicycles, given that fuel prices are rising and the city is building more cycling lanes?"* It is a **capital-and-policy decision** taken once every few years, using forecasts, cost data, and business goals — not daily ops.

### Key Strategic Levers (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Fleet Sizing (Right-Sizing):** Matching vehicle count to forecast demand without chronic over- or under-capacity.
- **Fleet Composition (Mix):** Choice of vehicle class — LCV, MCV, HCV, EVs, CNG, Diesel.
- **Replacement Policy:** When to retire an existing vehicle vs. retain it.
- **Procurement Strategy:** Outright purchase, **leasing**, **rental**, or **fleet management outsourcing (FMO)**.
- **Standardization:** Reducing variety of makes/models to lower spares inventory and training cost.
- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** The lifetime cost lens — acquisition + fuel + maintenance + driver + insurance + disposal.
- **Alignment with ESG/Net-Zero:** Sustainability-driven fleet transition (e.g., EV adoption roadmap).

> [!TIP]
> A KTU favourite: examiners love to ask *"Differentiate Strategic from Operational Fleet Planning."* Memorize the horizon, scope, and the *type of money* involved (Capital vs. Recurring).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Fleet Planning Process (5-Phase Model)

> [!NOTE]
> The industry-standard process (also accepted under KTU's NEP-2020 outcome-based framework) has **five phases**. Examiners frequently test this as a 7-mark structured answer.

**Phase 1 — Demand Forecasting & Business Alignment**
- Estimate future transport demand (tonne-km, passenger-km, deliveries/day).
- Inputs: sales forecast, market expansion, regulatory changes (BS-VII, EV mandates).
- Tools: Time-series forecasting, regression, scenario analysis.

**Phase 2 — Fleet Sizing & Right-Sizing**
- Determine **Number of Vehicles (N)** required:

$$N = \frac{D \cdot (1 + R)}{C \cdot U \cdot A}$$

Where: $D$ = Annual demand, $R$ = Reserve margin (0.10–0.20), $C$ = Capacity per vehicle, $U$ = Utilization, $A$ = Availability (1 − Downtime).

**Phase 3 — Fleet Mix & Vehicle Selection**
- Choose vehicle class, fuel type, payload, technology.
- Use **Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)** or **Weighted Scoring Model**.
- Criteria: TCO, payload, range, brand service network, resale value, emissions.

**Phase 4 — Replacement & Life-Cycle Analysis**
- For each existing asset: compute **Minimum Cost Life (MCL)** and **Economic Service Life (ESL)**.
- Compare **Defender (keep old vehicle)** vs **Challenger (buy new)**.

**Phase 5 — Capital Planning, Funding & Policy**
- Prepare capital budget, financing plan (own vs. lease), and disposal/resale policy.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why right-size?** Over-fleet increases **fixed cost** (depreciation, insurance, drivers) and **capital lock-up**. Under-fleet causes service failure and lost revenue.
- **Why standardize?** A workshop that services only one engine type has **lower training cost** and **higher mechanic productivity**.
- **Why replacement analysis?** A truck at 8 years may have low book value but **exponentially rising maintenance cost** — the "bathtub curve" inverted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` characters inside the table cells — use `\mid` for absolute value/conditional.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fleet Size (Deterministic) | $N = \dfrac{D \cdot (1+R)}{C \cdot U \cdot A}$ | $D$ demand, $R$ reserve, $C$ capacity, $U$ util., $A$ avail. | vehicles |
| 2 | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) over life $n$ | $TCO = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{op,t}}{(1+i)^{t}} - \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | $C_0$ acquisition, $C_{op,t}$ opex, $S$ salvage, $i$ MARR | ₹ / ₹/yr |
| 3 | Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) | $AEC = TCO \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | ₹ / year |
| 4 | Declining-Balance Depreciation | $B_t = C_0 (1 - d)^{t}$ | $d$ depreciation rate, $B_t$ book value at $t$ | ₹ |
| 5 | Minimum Cost Life (approx.) | $n^{*} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_0}{G \cdot (i+1) - \Delta M}}$ | $G$ gradient of opex, $\Delta M$ jump in opex at major overhaul | years |
| 6 | Defender vs. Challenger (MAPI) | $\min\left[\, AEC_{defender} \;,\; AEC_{challenger} + L_{adv} \,\right]$ | $L_{adv}$ = advantage of new (lower opex, productivity) | ₹ / year |
| 7 | Payback Period | $PB = \dfrac{\text{Incremental Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Simple PB | years |
| 8 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | $CF_t$ cash flow, $i$ MARR | ₹ |
| 9 | Benefit–Cost Ratio | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \text{Discounted Benefits}}{\sum \text{Discounted Costs}}$ | — | ratio |
| 10 | IRR (internal rate of return) | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Solve by iteration | % |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** When asked for "minimum cost life", most students write the gradient formula but forget to subtract the **salvage decline**. Always include salvage in the analysis.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strategic fleet planning is the **single highest-leverage decision** in transport operations — it locks in **fuel type, brand, capacity, and technology** for a decade. A wrong decision in 2024 (e.g., buying diesel buses when state policy mandates EV by 2026) results in **stranded assets** worth crores. Hence every fleet KPI — utilization, downtime, cost/km — traces back to a strategic decision made years earlier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) for Fleet Replacement

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Convert a multi-year, uneven cash flow into a single "level annual cost" that is comparable across vehicles of different lives.

**Setup:**
A vehicle costs $C_0$ today, has operating cost $C_t$ in year $t$, and salvage $S$ at end of year $n$. MARR is $i$ (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).

**Step 1 — Compute Present Worth of all future costs:**

$$PW = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^{t}} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

*Text row:* "Add purchase now, discount each opex year, subtract the discounted salvage (since it is a *recovery*, an inflow treated as negative cost)."

**Step 2 — Recognize that comparing PW across different $n$ is meaningless** (a 5-yr life and 10-yr life have different $n$).

**Step 3 — Convert PW into an equivalent uniform annual series $A$** using the capital-recovery identity:

$$A = PW \cdot \left[ \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. Derivation:

$$\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}\right] &= PW \\
\text{Taking reciprocals:} \quad A &= PW \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} = PW \cdot CRF
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $PW$ with the full expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AEC = \left( C_0 - \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}} \right) \cdot CRF \; + \; C_{op}
\end{aligned}$$

(assuming constant opex $C_{op}$ per year; otherwise sum discounted $C_t$ first.)

**Step 5 — Choose $n$ that minimizes $AEC$:** the **Minimum Cost Life / Economic Service Life**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Cost Life (Closed-Form Approximation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions:** Operating cost $C_t$ grows linearly with age at rate $G$ (₹/yr²); vehicle is replaced at end of year $n$ with another identical one; infinite replacement horizon.

**Step 1 — Annual cost in year $t$** (excluding capital recovery):

$$C_t = C_{op,0} + G \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — The present cost of buying at year 0 and replacing every $n$ years indefinitely** is a geometric series of PWs. The equivalent annual cost simplifies (via first-order Taylor expansion of $(1+i)^{-n} \approx 1 - i n$ for small $in$) to:

$$AEC(n) \approx \frac{C_0}{n} + C_{op,0} + \frac{G \cdot n}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{i+1 \text{ (correction)}}$$

More rigorously, after including the depreciation in $(1+i)^{-n}$ terms, the classical result is:

$$n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_0}{G} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n^{*}}}}}$$

**Step 3 — First-order approximation for $i \to 0$:**

$$n^{*} \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_0}{G}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- $C_0 = ₹20,00,000$, $G = ₹1,50,000$/yr², $i = 10\%$

$$\begin{aligned}
n^{*} &\approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20,00,000}{1,50,000}} \\
&= \sqrt{26.67} \approx 5.16 \;\text{years} \;\Rightarrow\; n^{*} = 5 \text{ years (whole)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification by computing AEC for n = 4, 5, 6:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AEC(4) &= 5,00,000 + 1,50,000 \cdot \tfrac{4}{2} = 8,00,000 \\
AEC(5) &= 4,00,000 + 1,50,000 \cdot \tfrac{5}{2} = 7,75,000 \;\; \textbf{(lowest)}\\
AEC(6) &= 3,33,333 + 1,50,000 \cdot \tfrac{6}{2} = 7,83,333
\end{aligned}$$

> Result confirms: **n* = 5 years** is the economic service life.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defender vs. Challenger (MAPI Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **14-mark KTU favourite**. The decision rule is: keep the old vehicle if and only if its next-year marginal cost (Defender) is **less than** the AEC of a new vehicle plus the *advantage* loss of the old one (Challenger).

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Defender wins (keep)} & \text{if } MC_{def,n+1} < AEC_{chall} - L_{adv} \\ \text{Challenger wins (replace)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- $MC_{def,n+1}$ = marginal cost of operating the defender for one more year.
- $AEC_{chall}$ = annual equivalent cost of the new vehicle over its ESL.
- $L_{adv}$ = productivity / downtime / fuel advantage the challenger offers per year.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Phase Strategic Fleet Planning Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Phase 1: Demand Forecasting and Business Alignment"] --> P2["Phase 2: Fleet Sizing and Right Sizing"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 3: Fleet Mix and Vehicle Selection"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4: Replacement and Life Cycle Analysis"]
    P4 --> P5["Phase 5: Capital Planning Funding and Policy"]
    P5 --> P6["Approved Strategic Fleet Plan 3 to 10 Years"]
    P1 --> FB1["Inputs Sales Forecast Market Expansion Regulation"]
    P2 --> FB2["Capacity Utilization Reserve Margin Availability"]
    P3 --> FB3["MCDA TCO Brand Network Resale Emissions"]
    P4 --> FB4["AEC NPV MAPI Defender Challenger"]
    P5 --> FB5["Buy Lease Rent FMO Disposal Policy"]
```

### 4.2 Defender vs Challenger Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Asset at Age N years"] --> B{"Compute MC of Defender for Year N+1"}
    B --> C{"Compute AEC of Challenger plus Ladv"}
    C --> D{"Is MC def less than AEC chall minus Ladv"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Decision: KEEP Defender for One More Year"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Decision: REPLACE with Challenger"]
    E --> G["Re-evaluate Next Year"]
    F --> H["Procure Dispose Trade-in Lease"]
```

### 4.3 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAP["CAPITAL COSTS ONE TIME"]
        CAP1["Vehicle Purchase Price"]
        CAP2["Tax Registration Fitments"]
    end
    subgraph OPEX["OPERATING COSTS RECURRING"]
        OP1["Fuel or Energy"]
        OP2["Driver Wages and Benefits"]
        OP3["Tyres and Lubricants"]
        OP4["Maintenance and Repairs"]
        OP5["Insurance and Permits"]
        OP6["Tolls and Parking"]
    end
    subgraph ADMIN["ADMIN AND OVERHEAD"]
        ADM1["Depreciation"]
        ADM2["Financing Interest"]
        ADM3["Admin and Telematics"]
    end
    subgraph END["END OF LIFE"]
        END1["Salvage or Resale Value"]
        END2["Dismantling Cost"]
    end
    CAP --> SUM["TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP TCO"]
    OPEX --> SUM
    ADMIN --> SUM
    END --> SUM
    SUM --> KPI["KPI: Cost per km and Cost per tonne-km"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these in the exam:** A 7-mark question on "Explain the strategic fleet planning process" can be answered by **labelling the first diagram's five phases** in sequence plus a 2-line description per box. A 7-mark question on "Replacement decision" can use the second diagram as a labelled sketch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Strategic Fleet Planning. State any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Strategic Fleet Planning is the long-term (3–10 year) corporate decision-making process for determining the optimal **size, composition, age, replacement schedule, and acquisition/disposal strategy** of a vehicle fleet, aligned with the organization's business objectives and regulatory environment.

**Two objectives:**
1. **Right-sizing the fleet** to meet forecast demand without idle capacity or service failure.
2. **Minimizing the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** over the life of each asset while ensuring service quality and compliance.

*(Acceptable: alignment with ESG, standardization, capital efficiency, risk mitigation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Strategic and Operational Fleet Planning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Strategic Planning | Operational Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Time horizon** | 3–10 years | Daily / Weekly |
| **Focus** | Fleet size, mix, replacement, capital | Dispatch, routing, driver allocation |
| **Decision-maker** | Top management / Board | Fleet manager / dispatcher |
| **Cost type** | Capital expenditure (CapEx) | Recurring / variable cost |
| **Data inputs** | Demand forecast, business plan, regulation | Real-time GPS, orders, traffic |
| **Output** | Approved Strategic Fleet Plan, capital budget | Daily trip sheet, route plan |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five phases of Strategic Fleet Planning** with a neat block diagram. Why is *right-sizing* considered the most critical step?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A logistics company in Kochi is evaluating whether to replace a 6-year-old diesel truck. The defender (old truck) would incur an estimated marginal cost of **₹2,80,000 next year**, after which it will be sold for **₹1,50,000**. A new comparable diesel truck (challenger) costs **₹18,00,000**, has an economic life of **8 years**, salvage **₹2,00,000**, and an Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) of **₹3,90,000 per year** (including fuel, maintenance, insurance, driver). The new truck offers a productivity advantage (lower downtime, higher payload utilization) worth **₹40,000 per year**. MARR = 10%. **Should the company replace the truck? Use MAPI logic.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Phases of Strategic Fleet Planning:**
*(Draw a labelled block diagram as in Section 4.1. The five phases are:*
1. *Demand Forecasting and Business Alignment*
2. *Fleet Sizing and Right-Sizing*
3. *Fleet Mix and Vehicle Selection*
4. *Replacement and Life-Cycle Analysis*
5. *Capital Planning, Funding and Policy.)*

*Right-sizing is the most critical step because:* an oversized fleet locks capital and inflates fixed cost; an undersized fleet causes service failure and lost revenue. Once size is fixed, mix, replacement, and capital decisions cascade from it. Right-sizing also determines the *make-or-buy* boundary (own vs. lease).

**(b) Replacement Decision using MAPI Logic:**

**Step 1 — Identify the three required values:**
- Marginal cost of defender (next year): $MC_{def} = ₹2,80,000$
- AEC of challenger: $AEC_{chall} = ₹3,90,000$
- Productivity advantage of challenger: $L_{adv} = ₹40,000$

**Step 2 — Compute the *adjusted* AEC of the challenger** (subtract its advantage to find what it "really costs" relative to the defender):

$$\begin{aligned}
AEC_{chall,adj} &= AEC_{chall} - L_{adv} \\
&= 3,90,000 - 40,000 \\
&= ₹3,50,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the MAPI decision rule:**

Compare $MC_{def}$ with $AEC_{chall,adj}$:

$$MC_{def} = ₹2,80,000 \;<\; ₹3,50,000 = AEC_{chall,adj}$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since the marginal cost of keeping the defender is *less* than the adjusted AEC of the challenger, **KEEP the defender for one more year** (Defender wins).

**Step 5 — Note on salvage:** The defender's resale value of ₹1,50,000 (realized only if sold now) is *not* added to the marginal cost — the MAPI rule already accounts for next-year operating cost only. The resale is realized at the time of the decision; the 2,80,000 figure is the *avoidable* cost of operating the truck for one more year.

**Final Answer:** **Do NOT replace. Retain the defender for one more year. Re-evaluate next year.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> *Common 1–2 mark losses:*
> 1. **Forgetting the $L_{adv}$ adjustment** in part (b) — students compare raw $AEC_{chall}$ (₹3,90,000) with $MC_{def}$ (₹2,80,000) and *still* wrongly conclude "keep" without justifying *why* the advantage was not credited to the challenger.
> 2. **Adding the salvage value as a benefit** when computing next-year marginal cost — it is a one-time inflow *now*, not next year.
> 3. **Confusing "Defender wins" with "indefinite keep"** — the MAPI rule is a *one-year* decision. The plan must explicitly say "re-evaluate at end of year 7."

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** of a fleet. List and briefly explain the **five major cost components** of TCO with one example each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company is choosing between two trucks for a 7-year strategic fleet investment. MARR = 12%.

| Parameter | Truck X (Diesel) | Truck Y (CNG) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase Cost | ₹15,00,000 | ₹17,50,000 |
| Annual Operating Cost (constant) | ₹3,20,000 | ₹2,40,000 |
| Salvage Value (end of year 7) | ₹2,00,000 | ₹2,50,000 |

**Compute the Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) of each truck using the Capital Recovery Factor and recommend the better choice.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):**
TCO is the **lifetime cost of owning, operating, and disposing** of a vehicle — from acquisition to end-of-life — expressed in present-value or annual-equivalent terms. It enables like-for-like comparison of vehicles with different price tags, fuel types, and lives.

**Five Major Cost Components:**

1. **Acquisition / Capital Cost** — purchase price, taxes, fitments, financing. *Example: ₹18 lakh for a new HCV.*
2. **Fuel / Energy Cost** — diesel, petrol, CNG, electricity. *Example: ₹4,00,000/yr at 12 km/l and 80,000 km/yr.*
3. **Maintenance & Repair Cost** — preventive, breakdown, tyres, lubricants. *Example: ₹1.20/km × 80,000 km = ₹96,000.*
4. **Driver & Overhead Cost** — wages, insurance, permits, tolls, telematics. *Example: ₹25,000/month driver salary.*
5. **End-of-Life Cost / Salvage** — resale value (offset) or dismantling cost. *Example: ₹2,00,000 resale after 7 years.*

**(b) AEC Computation:**

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor at i = 12%, n = 7:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.12 \times (1.12)^{7}}{(1.12)^{7} - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.12 \times 2.2107}{2.2107 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.2653}{1.2107} \\
&\approx 0.2191
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Capital recovery (annualized purchase minus salvage):**

For Truck X:

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{X} &= (15,00,000 - 2,00,000) \times 0.2191 \\
&= 13,00,000 \times 0.2191 \\
&\approx ₹2,84,830
\end{aligned}$$

For Truck Y:

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{Y} &= (17,50,000 - 2,50,000) \times 0.2191 \\
&= 15,00,000 \times 0.2191 \\
&\approx ₹3,28,650
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Add annual operating cost to obtain AEC:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AEC_{X} &= 2,84,830 + 3,20,000 = ₹6,04,830 \\
AEC_{Y} &= 3,28,650 + 2,40,000 = ₹5,68,650
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and recommendation:**

$$AEC_{Y} = ₹5,68,650 < AEC_{X} = ₹6,04,830$$

Difference = ₹36,180 per year in favour of **Truck Y (CNG)**.

**Recommendation:** Choose **Truck Y (CNG)**. Despite higher purchase cost, its lower annual operating cost and higher salvage value yield a **lower annual equivalent cost by ~6%**, which compounded over 7 years is a substantial saving. The recommendation is robust provided CNG fuel infrastructure is reliably available on the company's routes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> *Common 1–3 mark losses:*
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract salvage** before applying CRF — this inflates AEC by 30–40% and reverses the decision.
> 2. **Using simple division by $n$** instead of the capital-recovery factor — this is *wrong* unless $i = 0$. Examiners will deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Rounding $(1.12)^7$ to 2.2 instead of 2.2107** — at 12%, the rounding error is ~0.5% and can flip a close decision; show 4 decimal places.
> 4. **Ignoring qualitative factors** (CNG pump availability, emission norms) in the recommendation — KTU expects a one-sentence *qualitative caveat* in the final answer for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin these 12 points on your revision wall:**
