- **Definition:** Strategic fleet planning = long-term (3–10 yr) decision on *size, mix, age, replacement, capital*.
- **Three tiers:** Strategic (long), Tactical (mid), Operational (daily). State the horizon in the exam.
- **Five phases:** Forecast → Right-Size → Mix → Replacement → Capital Plan.
- **Right-sizing formula:** $N = D(1+R) / (C \cdot U \cdot A)$. Always state definitions of every variable.
- **TCO** has 5 buckets: Capital, Fuel, Maintenance, Driver/Overhead, End-of-Life.
- **Economic Service Life (ESL)** is the $n$ that **minimizes AEC**, not the calendar life.
- **AEC Formula:** $AEC = (C_0 - S/(1+i)^n) \cdot CRF + C_{op}$, where $CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$.
- **Minimum Cost Life approximation:** $n^{*} \approx \sqrt{2C_0 / G}$.
- **MAPI Replacement Rule:** Keep defender if $MC_{def,n+1} < AEC_{chall} - L_{adv}$; otherwise replace.
- **NPV / BCR / IRR** are alternate capital-budgeting tools; AEC is the *fleet-favourite* because lives differ.
- **Standardization** = lower spares, lower training, higher mechanic productivity — a *silent* cost saver.
- **Today's strategic levers:** EV roadmap, BS-VII/VIII norms, FAME-II subsidies, ESG reporting, telematics-driven utilization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Optimization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Optimization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleet Optimization Techniques** are a coordinated set of analytical, operational, and strategic methodologies employed to maximize the **economic efficiency**, **operational productivity**, and **service reliability** of a transportation fleet while minimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**, **empty running**, **fuel consumption**, and **environmental footprint**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for OEMUT835, Fleet Optimization Techniques integrate principles from **Operations Research (OR)**, **Industrial Engineering**, **Logistics Management**, and **Automotive Engineering** to solve four core categories of fleet problems:

1. **Strategic Fleet Sizing** — determining the *right number* of vehicles.
2. **Tactical Vehicle Routing & Scheduling** — determining the *right path and timing*.
3. **Operational Load Matching** — determining the *right utilization*.
4. **Asset Replacement & Maintenance Planning** — determining the *right lifecycle*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Family Car on a Weekend Errand Run**

Imagine you run a household with **three cars** and a list of **ten errands** scattered across the city on a Saturday. You could:
- Send all three cars separately (wasteful, lots of fuel, some cars half-empty).
- Pool errands cleverly so one car does 4 errands in a loop, another does 3, etc. (efficient).
- Notice one car is too old and breaks down — should you repair or replace it?
- Realize one car always returns empty — maybe *rent it out* or *discard it*.

**Fleet Optimization Techniques do exactly this at industrial scale.** A logistics manager with 50 trucks and 200 daily delivery points uses mathematical models (instead of intuition) to:
- Decide how many trucks are *needed* (not how many are *owned*).
- Assign deliveries to trucks in the *shortest total distance*.
- Decide *when* a truck should be retired before its maintenance cost exceeds its value.
- Schedule *preventive maintenance* so breakdowns don't cripple the schedule.

The objective is to convert a **naïve fleet** (high cost, low utilization) into a **lean fleet** (low cost, high utilization) without compromising service quality.

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Fleet Optimization (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Fleet Optimization (Recall this in the exam):**
>
> 1. **SIZE OPTIMIZATION** — Right number of vehicles for a given demand.
> 2. **ROUTE OPTIMIZATION** — Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) & Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
> 3. **LOAD OPTIMIZATION** — Maximizing the **Load Factor (LF)** to reduce empty running.
> 4. **REPLACEMENT & MAINTENANCE OPTIMIZATION** — Economic life analysis & preventive scheduling.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Fleet Management

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics (Bold = Memorize):**
>
> - **TCO** = Total Cost of Ownership (₹/vehicle/km or ₹/vehicle/year).
> - **Load Factor (LF)** = (Actual Payload / Rated Payload) × 100%.
> - **Empty Running Percentage (ERP)** = (Empty Distance / Total Distance) × 100%.
> - **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)** = (Working Hours / Available Hours) × 100%.
> - **Cost per Kilometer (CPK)** = Total Operating Cost / Total Kilometers Run.
> - **Fuel Efficiency (FE)** = km/litre or km/kg (for CNG).
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — for reliability.
> - **Optimal Replacement Age (n\*)** — economic life in years.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Fleet Size Optimization (Strategic Level)

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

Fleet sizing determines the *minimum number of vehicles* needed to satisfy a forecasted transport demand without shortages. Too many vehicles → high fixed cost; too few → lost revenue and poor service.

### 2.1.2 Analytical Logic

The fleet size problem is modelled as a balancing act:

$$
N_{\text{required}} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{demand}} \times S_{\text{factor}}}{C_{\text{vehicle}} \times H_{\text{available}} \times U_{\text{rate}}} \right\rceil
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{demand}}$ = Total ton-kilometres of freight to be moved per period.
- $S_{\text{factor}}$ = Seasonal / surge factor (typically **1.10 to 1.25**).
- $C_{\text{vehicle}}$ = Rated capacity of one vehicle (tonnes).
- $H_{\text{available}}$ = Available working hours per vehicle per period.
- $U_{\text{rate}}$ = Vehicle Utilization Rate (decimal, e.g., 0.85).

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

A cement company in Kerala transporting **500 tonnes/day** over an average lead of **60 km** with **10-tonne tippers** operating **18 hr/day at 85% utilization** can directly compute fleet size using this equation, avoiding the common mistake of over-purchasing trucks which then sit idle (incurring **fixed cost without revenue**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Fleet sizing decisions are *strategic* (long-term) and *capital-intensive*. Once committed, they are expensive to reverse — hence the need for accurate demand forecasting.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Vehicle Routing & Scheduling (Tactical Level)

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is the central optimization challenge in fleet operations. It is a generalization of the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VRP Objective Function (Standard Mathematical Formulation):**
>
> Minimize the **total distance (or cost)** subject to:
> - Each customer visited exactly once.
> - Each vehicle starts and ends at the depot.
> - Vehicle capacity not exceeded.
> - Total route time ≤ driver shift limit.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let $C_{ij}$ be the cost of travel from node $i$ to node $j$, and $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$ be 1 if vehicle $k$ travels arc $i \rightarrow j$.

$$
\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{m} C_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}
$$

Subject to:
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall \, i = 1,2,\ldots,n
$$

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ijk} - \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{jik} = 0 \quad \forall \, i, k
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q_k \quad \forall \, k
$$

Where $q_i$ = demand at node $i$, $Q_k$ = capacity of vehicle $k$, $y_{ik}$ = 1 if customer $i$ is served by vehicle $k$.

### 2.2.3 Classical Heuristic — Clarke & Wright Savings Algorithm

The **Savings Method** (1964) is the most widely taught KTU heuristic:

$$
S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $d_{0i}$ = distance from depot to customer $i$.
- $d_{0j}$ = distance from depot to customer $j$.
- $d_{ij}$ = direct distance between customers $i$ and $j$.
- $S_{ij}$ = **Savings** obtained by combining customers $i$ and $j$ on a single route instead of separate depot round trips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Larger $S_{ij}$ → Higher priority for merging.** The algorithm sorts savings in descending order and merges routes greedily subject to capacity constraints.

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Load Optimization (Operational Level)

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

Load Optimization aims to maximize the **Load Factor (LF)** and minimize **Empty Running**.

$$
LF = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ERP = \frac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

The **Backhauling Strategy** is a key technique: instead of returning empty, the vehicle picks up a return load from the destination region.

### 2.3.2 Real-World Utility

A truck plying **Kochi → Bangalore** carrying electronics (full load) traditionally returns **empty**. By partnering with agricultural cooperatives in Karnataka for the return leg, ERP drops from **~50% to <15%**, slashing cost/km by **30–40%**.

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Replacement & Maintenance Optimization

### 2.4.1 Optimal Replacement Age — Minimum Average Cost Method

A vehicle's operating cost per year **increases** with age (due to wear), while its **depreciation per year decreases**. The **economic life (n\*)** is where the **average cost per year is minimized**.

$$
\text{Average Cost per Year } \bar{C}(n) = \frac{C_{ac}(n) + S(n)}{n}
$$

Where:
- $C_{ac}(n)$ = Cumulative operating cost over $n$ years.
- $S(n)$ = Salvage value at end of year $n$.

Equivalently:
$$
\bar{C}(n) = \text{Depreciation Cost/year} + \text{Operating Cost/year}
$$

The optimal $n^\*$ satisfies:
$$
C_{op}(n^\* - 1) < \Delta C < C_{op}(n^\*)
$$

Where $C_{op}(n)$ is the marginal operating cost in year $n$.

### 2.4.2 Preventive Maintenance Optimization

The optimal maintenance interval minimizes the sum of **preventive cost + expected breakdown cost**.

$$
TC(n) = C_{pm} + \frac{C_{bd}}{P(n)} \cdot F(n)
$$

Where $P(n)$ is the probability of breakdown within interval $n$, and $F(n)$ is a weighting factor.

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table for KTU University Exams.**

| Sl. No. | Formula | Variables & Meaning | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $N_{\text{req}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{\text{demand}} \cdot S_f}{C_v \cdot H_{\text{avail}} \cdot U_r} \right\rceil$ | $T_{\text{demand}}$ = Total demand; $S_f$ = Surge factor; $C_v$ = Vehicle capacity; $H_{\text{avail}}$ = Hours available; $U_r$ = Utilization rate | Tonnes, hrs |
| 2 | $LF = \dfrac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | $W_{\text{actual}}$ = Actual load; $W_{\text{rated}}$ = Rated capacity | % |
| 3 | $ERP = \dfrac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $D_{\text{empty}}$ = Empty distance; $D_{\text{total}}$ = Total distance | % |
| 4 | $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | Clarke-Wright savings combining customers $i$ and $j$ | km |
| 5 | $\bar{C}(n) = \dfrac{C_{ac}(n) - S(n)}{n} + C_{op}(n)$ | Average annual cost; $C_{ac}$ = cumulative cost; $S$ = salvage; $n$ = age | ₹/year |
| 6 | $CPK = \dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}}}{D_{\text{total}}}$ | Cost per kilometer; $C_{\text{fixed}}$ = annual fixed cost; $C_{\text{variable}}$ = fuel + maintenance | ₹/km |
| 7 | $TCO = C_{\text{capex}} + C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{driver}} + C_{\text{insurance}} - S_{\text{salvage}}$ | Total cost of ownership over vehicle life | ₹ |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot O}{H \cdot P}}$ | EOQ analogue for spare parts inventory | Units |

## 2.6 Why These Techniques Matter in Industry

- A **5% reduction** in ERP for a 100-truck fleet running **80,000 km/year** saves approximately **₹1.6 crore/year** at ₹5/km.
- A **10% improvement** in Load Factor directly reduces **fixed cost per tonne-km**.
- Optimal replacement decisions prevent the **"sunk cost trap"** — keeping old vehicles that consume more in maintenance than they save.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Fleet Size for Given Demand

### Problem Statement
A logistics company in Ernakulam must transport **1,800 tonnes/day** of seafood using refrigerated trucks of **10-tonne** rated capacity. Each truck operates **2 trips/day**, works **25 days/month**, and has a utilization rate of **0.85**. A seasonal surge of **15%** occurs during Onam. Determine the **minimum fleet size**.

### Given Data
- $T_{\text{demand}} = 1800$ tonnes/day.
- $C_v = 10$ tonnes.
- Trips per day per truck = 2.
- Surge factor $S_f = 1.15$.
- $U_r = 0.85$.

### Step 1 — Compute Effective Daily Capacity per Truck

$$
C_{\text{eff}} = C_v \times \text{Trips/day} \times U_r
$$

$$
C_{\text{eff}} = 10 \times 2 \times 0.85 = 17 \text{ tonnes/day per truck}
$$

**Explanation:** A 10-tonne truck doing 2 trips/day would normally carry 20 tonnes, but due to loading inefficiencies and trip cancellations, only 85% of rated capacity is realized.

### Step 2 — Apply the Peak Demand (Surge Factor)

$$
T_{\text{peak}} = T_{\text{demand}} \times S_f = 1800 \times 1.15 = 2070 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Explanation:** During Onam, demand rises by 15%; the fleet must handle this peak without shortages.

### Step 3 — Compute Minimum Number of Trucks

$$
N_{\text{req}} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{peak}}}{C_{\text{eff}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2070}{17} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\frac{2070}{17} = 121.76
$$

$$
N_{\text{req}} = 122 \text{ trucks}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification
Total capacity with 122 trucks = $122 \times 17 = 2074$ tonnes/day ≥ 2070 tonnes/day ✓.

**Conclusion:** The fleet must own **at least 122 trucks** to meet peak demand without shortages.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clarke & Wright Savings Algorithm (Numerical Walkthrough)

### Problem Setup
A depot (node 0) must serve **4 customers**. Distance matrix (km):

| From \ To | 0 (Depot) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **1** | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 |
| **2** | 15 | 12 | 0 | 9 | 14 |
| **3** | 20 | 18 | 9 | 0 | 7 |
| **4** | 25 | 22 | 14 | 7 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Initial Routes (Each Customer Separately)
Without optimization: $0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 0$ + $0 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 0$ + $0 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 0$ + $0 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 0$.

Total initial distance:
$$
D_0 = 2(10 + 15 + 20 + 25) = 2(70) = 140 \text{ km}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute All Pairwise Savings $S_{ij}$

Using $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12} &= 10 + 15 - 12 = 13 \text{ km} \\
S_{13} &= 10 + 20 - 18 = 12 \text{ km} \\
S_{14} &= 10 + 25 - 22 = 13 \text{ km} \\
S_{23} &= 15 + 20 - 9 = 26 \text{ km} \\
S_{24} &= 15 + 25 - 14 = 26 \text{ km} \\
S_{34} &= 20 + 25 - 7 = 38 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The savings $S_{ij}$ represents the km saved by combining customers $i$ and $j$ on a single route instead of two separate trips.

### Step 3 — Rank Savings in Descending Order

| Rank | Pair $(i,j)$ | Saving (km) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | (3,4) | 38 |
| 2 | (2,3) | 26 |
| 3 | (2,4) | 26 |
| 4 | (1,2) | 13 |
| 5 | (1,4) | 13 |
| 6 | (1,3) | 12 |

### Step 4 — Merge Routes Greedily

**Rank 1: Merge (3,4).** New route: $0 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 0$. Distance = $20 + 7 + 25 = 52$ km.

**Rank 2: Try Merge (2,3).** Customer 2 is not yet on a route; customer 3 is at the *end* of route $\{3,4\}$ — valid insertion at the *beginning*: New route $0 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 0$. Distance = $15 + 9 + 7 + 25 = 56$ km.

**Rank 3: Try Merge (2,4).** Customer 2 is already inside route $\{2,3,4\}$ — cannot be inserted at an *end node*. Skip.

**Rank 4: Try Merge (1,2).** Customer 1 is not yet on a route; customer 2 is at the *beginning* of route $\{2,3,4\}$ — valid insertion: New route $0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 0$. Distance = $10 + 12 + 9 + 7 + 25 = 63$ km.

**Rank 5 & 6: All customers already on the single merged route. Stop.**

### Step 5 — Compare with Initial Solution

- **Initial Total Distance:** 140 km.
- **Optimized Total Distance:** 63 km.
- **Distance Saved:** $140 - 63 = 77$ km.
- **Percentage Saving:** $(77/140) \times 100 = 55\%$.

**Conclusion:** The Clarke-Wright Savings algorithm reduced total travel distance by **55%**, allowing the same demand to be served with significantly fewer vehicle-kilometres and hence lower fuel, driver, and maintenance cost.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Replacement Age (Minimum Average Cost)

### Problem Statement
A truck has the following costs:

| Year ($n$) | Cumulative Operating Cost $C_{ac}(n)$ (₹ lakh) | Salvage Value $S(n)$ (₹ lakh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1.5 | 8.0 |
| 2 | 3.5 | 6.5 |
| 3 | 6.0 | 5.0 |
| 4 | 9.5 | 3.5 |
| 5 | 14.5 | 2.0 |

Purchase price = ₹10 lakh. Determine the **optimal replacement age**.

### Step 1 — Compute Average Annual Cost $\bar{C}(n)$

$$
\bar{C}(n) = \frac{\text{Purchase Price} - S(n) + C_{ac}(n)}{n}
$$

**Year 1:**
$$
\bar{C}(1) = \frac{10 - 8.0 + 1.5}{1} = \frac{3.5}{1} = 3.5 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
\bar{C}(2) = \frac{10 - 6.5 + 3.5}{2} = \frac{7.0}{2} = 3.5 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
\bar{C}(3) = \frac{10 - 5.0 + 6.0}{3} = \frac{11.0}{3} = 3.667 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 4:**
$$
\bar{C}(4) = \frac{10 - 3.5 + 9.5}{4} = \frac{16.0}{4} = 4.0 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 5:**
$$
\bar{C}(5) = \frac{10 - 2.0 + 14.5}{5} = \frac{22.5}{5} = 4.5 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

### Step 2 — Tabulate and Identify Minimum

| Year | Average Cost (₹ lakh/year) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **3.5** |
| 2 | **3.5** |
| 3 | 3.667 |
| 4 | 4.000 |
| 5 | 4.500 |

### Step 3 — Decision

The minimum average cost is ₹3.5 lakh/year, occurring at **Year 1 and Year 2 (tie)**. The economic optimal replacement age is therefore **$n^\* = 2$ years**.

**Explanation:** Holding the truck beyond 2 years increases the average annual cost (depreciation savings no longer compensate for rising maintenance). At $n=2$, the *marginal* operating cost in year 2 is $C_{ac}(2) - C_{ac}(1) = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0$ lakh, which exactly equals the depreciation saved (₹1.5 lakh) plus residual benefit — confirming the trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Optimization Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Exam:**

| Problem Type | Best Technique | Output | Complexity |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| How many vehicles? | Queuing / Fleet Sizing Equation | $N_{\text{req}}$ | Low |
| Which route? | Clarke-Wright Savings / VRP | Route plan | Medium |
| When to replace? | Minimum Average Cost Method | $n^\*$ | Medium |
| When to maintain? | PM Optimization (Reliability-centred) | Maintenance schedule | High |
| What load to carry? | Load Factor Maximization | Optimum mix | Low |
| Reduce empty running? | Backhauling / Trip Consolidation | Reduced ERP | Medium |
| Driver allocation? | Crew Scheduling (Set Partitioning) | Duty roster | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Overall Fleet Optimization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fleet Optimization Problem] --> B{Problem Type}
    B -->|Strategic| C1[Fleet Sizing]
    B -->|Tactical| C2[Route Planning]
    B -->|Operational| C3[Load Management]
    B -->|Lifecycle| C4[Replacement & Maintenance]
    C1 --> D1[Demand Forecast Model]
    C1 --> D2[Capacity Equation]
    D1 --> E1[Required Vehicle Count]
    D2 --> E1
    C2 --> F1[Clarke Wright Savings]
    C2 --> F2[VRP Heuristic]
    F1 --> E2[Optimized Route Plan]
    F2 --> E2
    C3 --> G1[Load Factor Analysis]
    C3 --> G2[Backhauling Strategy]
    G1 --> E3[Higher Utilization]
    G2 --> E3
    C4 --> H1[Average Cost Method]
    C4 --> H2[Preventive Maintenance Model]
    H1 --> E4[Optimal Replacement Age]
    H2 --> E5[Maintenance Schedule]
    E1 --> Z[OPTIMIZED FLEET]
    E2 --> Z
    E3 --> Z
    E4 --> Z
    E5 --> Z
    Z --> K[Reduced TCO and Higher Service Level]
```

**Explanation:** This block-level functional architecture shows how the four pillars of fleet optimization (Sizing, Routing, Loading, Replacement) feed into a unified optimized fleet outcome. Each pillar has its own input models and produces a specific tactical or strategic output.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Read Distance Matrix and Demands] --> P2[Initialize One Route Per Customer]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Savings Sij for All Customer Pairs]
    P3 --> P4[Sort Savings in Descending Order]
    P4 --> P5{Pick Highest Saving Pair}
    P5 --> P6{Both Customers at Route Ends?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Merge Routes]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Skip This Pair]
    P7 --> P9{Capacity Violated?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P8
    P9 -->|No| P10[Update Route Distance]
    P8 --> P11{All Pairs Processed?}
    P10 --> P11
    P11 -->|No| P5
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Output Final Routes and Total Distance]
```

**Explanation:** The flow visualizes the iterative greedy logic of Clarke-Wright — pairing customers with the highest savings first, validating geometric (end-node) and capacity constraints, and iterating until all feasible merges are exhausted.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Minimum Average Cost Replacement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Year 1 Data] --> D2[Year 2 Data]
    D2 --> D3[Year 3 Data]
    D3 --> D4[Year 4 Data]
    D4 --> D5[Year 5 Data]
    D1 --> S1[Compute Avg Cost Year 1]
    D2 --> S2[Compute Avg Cost Year 2]
    D3 --> S3[Compute Avg Cost Year 3]
    D4 --> S4[Compute Avg Cost Year 4]
    D5 --> S5[Compute Avg Cost Year 5]
    S1 --> CMP{Minimum Average Cost Year}
    S2 --> CMP
    S3 --> CMP
    S4 --> CMP
    S5 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Identified| OUT[Optimal Replacement Age n Star]
```

**Explanation:** This represents the cyclical computation of average cost across vehicle ages, converging on the year where the average cost reaches its minimum — the economic replacement point.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAPEX[Capital Expenditure - Purchase Price] --> TCO1
    FUEL[Fuel and Lubricant Cost] --> TCO1
    MAINT[Maintenance and Repair Cost] --> TCO1
    DRV[Driver Wages and Overheads] --> TCO1
    INS[Insurance and Taxes] --> TCO1
    TIRE[Tyre and Battery Cost] --> TCO1
    DOWN[Downtime and Opportunity Loss] --> TCO1
    TCO1[Total Operating Cost Aggregator] --> SUB[Subtract Salvage Value at End of Life]
    SUB --> TCOFIN[Net Total Cost of Ownership TCO]
    TCOFIN --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|TCO Lower than Alternative| BUY[Proceed with Vehicle Purchase]
    DEC -->|TCO Higher than Alternative| ALT[Choose Alternative Vehicle or Outsource]
```

**Explanation:** The TCO aggregator captures all cost streams, subtracts residual salvage value, and compares against alternatives — supporting make-vs-buy, lease-vs-buy, and fleet renewal decisions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Fleet Optimization Techniques. List the four pillars of fleet optimization.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Fleet Optimization Techniques refer to the set of analytical and operational methods used to maximize the productivity and minimize the total cost of a transportation fleet while ensuring service reliability.
>
> **The four pillars are:**
> 1. **Size Optimization** – determining the right number of vehicles.
> 2. **Route Optimization** – determining the best routes using VRP/TSP methods.
> 3. **Load Optimization** – maximizing the load factor and minimizing empty running.
> 4. **Replacement & Maintenance Optimization** – deciding the optimal economic life and maintenance schedule of vehicles.

*(Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for listing and explaining the four pillars.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
*Define Load Factor and Empty Running Percentage. State their significance in fleet management.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Load Factor (LF)** is the ratio of actual payload carried to the rated payload capacity of the vehicle, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$
> LF = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{rated}}} \times 100
> $$
>
> **Empty Running Percentage (ERP)** is the proportion of total distance travelled without any load (empty) to the total distance travelled.
>
> $$
> ERP = \frac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100
> $$
>
> **Significance:** A low load factor increases the *fixed cost per tonne-km*, reducing profitability. High empty running wastes fuel, driver time, and vehicle life. Both metrics are **direct indicators of fleet inefficiency** and are primary targets for optimization interventions such as backhauling, trip consolidation, and load matching.

*(Award 1 mark each for LF and ERP definitions; 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm for vehicle routing. Describe its algorithmic steps clearly. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A depot has to serve **5 customers** with the distance matrix given below. Apply the Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm to determine the optimized routes and compute the total distance saved compared to serving each customer separately. *(7 Marks)*

| From \ To | Depot | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Depot** | 0 | 8 | 14 | 18 | 22 | 28 |
| **C1** | 8 | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **C2** | 14 | 10 | 0 | 8 | 12 | 18 |
| **C3** | 18 | 15 | 8 | 0 | 6 | 10 |
| **C4** | 22 | 20 | 12 | 6 | 0 | 7 |
| **C5** | 28 | 25 | 18 | 10 | 7 | 0 |

Assume vehicle capacity is sufficient to combine all customers in one route if savings permit.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 Marks):**

The Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm is a **greedy heuristic** for solving the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). It works on the principle that combining two customers on a single route is cheaper than serving them with separate depot round trips. The savings in distance achieved by merging customers $i$ and $j$ on the same route is given by:

$$
S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}
$$

**Algorithmic Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Treat each customer as a separate route from the depot (i.e., $0 \rightarrow i \rightarrow 0$ for every $i$).
2. **Savings Computation:** Calculate all pairwise savings $S_{ij}$ using the formula above.
3. **Sorting:** Rank all savings in descending order (highest savings first).
4. **Iterative Merging:** For each pair in sorted order, attempt to merge the routes containing customers $i$ and $j$ subject to:
   - Both $i$ and $j$ must lie at the *end nodes* of their respective routes (geometric feasibility).
   - The combined route must respect vehicle capacity constraints (and any time/distance limits).
5. **Termination:** Stop when all customers are merged into a single route (or no more feasible merges exist).
6. **Output:** Final route plan and total distance.

The algorithm yields a substantial reduction in total travel distance, often **20–40%** in real distribution networks.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Savings $S_{ij}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12} &= 8 + 14 - 10 = 12 \text{ km} \\
S_{13} &= 8 + 18 - 15 = 11 \text{ km} \\
S_{14} &= 8 + 22 - 20 = 10 \text{ km} \\
S_{15} &= 8 + 28 - 25 = 11 \text{ km} \\
S_{23} &= 14 + 18 - 8 = 24 \text{ km} \\
S_{24} &= 14 + 22 - 12 = 24 \text{ km} \\
S_{25} &= 14 + 28 - 18 = 24 \text{ km} \\
S_{34} &= 18 + 22 - 6 = 34 \text{ km} \\
S_{35} &= 18 + 28 - 10 = 36 \text{ km} \\
S_{45} &= 22 + 28 - 7 = 43 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sort in Descending Order:**

| Rank | Pair | Saving (km) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | (4,5) | 43 |
| 2 | (3,5) | 36 |
| 3 | (3,4) | 34 |
| 4 | (2,3) | 24 |
| 5 | (2,4) | 24 |
| 6 | (2,5) | 24 |
| 7 | (1,2) | 12 |
| 8 | (1,3) | 11 |
| 9 | (1,5) | 11 |
| 10 | (1,4) | 10 |

**Step 3: Greedy Merging (Capacity Sufficient):**

- Merge (4,5): Route becomes $0 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 0$.
- Merge (3,5): Customer 3 free; Customer 5 at end of $\{4,5\}$ route. Insert: $0 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 0$.
- Merge (3,4): Both already on same route. Skip.
- Merge (2,3): Customer 2 free; Customer 3 at start of $\{3,4,5\}$ route. Insert: $0 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 0$.
- Merge (1,2): Customer 1 free; Customer 2 at start. Insert: $0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 0$.

All customers are on one route. Stop.

**Step 4: Compute Optimized Route Distance:**

$$
D_{\text{opt}} = 8 + 10 + 8 + 6 + 7 + 28 = 67 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Initial (Separate) Total Distance:**

$$
D_{\text{init}} = 2(8 + 14 + 18 + 22 + 28) = 2(90) = 180 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 6: Distance Saved:**

$$
\text{Distance Saved} = 180 - 67 = 113 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{113}{180} \times 100 = 62.78\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm reduced total travel distance by **113 km (62.78%)**, demonstrating the substantial operational benefit of optimized routing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** in fleet management. List its major cost components and explain how TCO analysis supports fleet optimization decisions. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A transport company has the following cost data for a truck purchased at **₹15 lakh**:

| Year | Cumulative Operating Cost (₹ lakh) | Salvage Value (₹ lakh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1.8 | 12.0 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 9.5 |
| 3 | 7.0 | 7.0 |
| 4 | 11.0 | 4.5 |
| 5 | 16.5 | 2.0 |

Using the **Minimum Average Cost Method**, determine the **optimal replacement age** of the truck. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — TCO Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** is the *comprehensive* lifetime cost of operating a vehicle, encompassing all capital, operational, and overhead expenses minus residual value. TCO provides a *holistic* financial view that goes beyond the simple purchase price, enabling fleet managers to make rational long-term decisions.

**Major Components of TCO:**

1. **Capital Cost (CAPEX):** Purchase price, loan interest, depreciation.
2. **Fuel and Lubricant Cost:** Largest variable cost component (~30–40% of operating cost).
3. **Maintenance and Repair Cost:** Routine servicing, parts replacement, breakdown costs.
4. **Driver Wages and Overheads:** Salary, benefits, training.
5. **Insurance, Taxes, and Permits:** Mandatory statutory costs.
6. **Tyre and Battery Cost:** Wear-based consumables.
7. **Downtime / Opportunity Cost:** Revenue lost during vehicle unavailability.
8. **Salvage Value (Recovery):** Resale or scrap value at end of life.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
TCO = C_{\text{CAPEX}} + C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{driver}} + C_{\text{insurance}} + C_{\text{tyre}} + C_{\text{down}} - S_{\text{salvage}}
$$

**Role in Fleet Optimization:**
- Supports **make-vs-buy** and **lease-vs-buy** decisions.
- Enables **vehicle class selection** (e.g., 10-tonne vs. 16-tonne tipper).
- Identifies **hidden cost drivers** (e.g., high-maintenance models).
- Drives **replacement policy** decisions (replace vs. retain).
- Provides a **standardized metric** to compare heterogeneous fleet options.

**Part (b) — Numerical Replacement Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Average Cost Formula:**

$$
\bar{C}(n) = \frac{\text{Purchase Price} - S(n) + C_{ac}(n)}{n}
$$

**Step 2: Compute for Each Year:**

**Year 1:**
$$
\bar{C}(1) = \frac{15 - 12.0 + 1.8}{1} = \frac{4.8}{1} = 4.80 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
\bar{C}(2) = \frac{15 - 9.5 + 4.0}{2} = \frac{9.5}{2} = 4.75 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
\bar{C}(3) = \frac{15 - 7.0 + 7.0}{3} = \frac{15.0}{3} = 5.00 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 4:**
$$
\bar{C}(4) = \frac{15 - 4.5 + 11.0}{4} = \frac{21.5}{4} = 5.375 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Year 5:**
$$
\bar{C}(5) = \frac{15 - 2.0 + 16.5}{5} = \frac{29.5}{5} = 5.90 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Step 3: Tabulate Results:**

| Year ($n$) | Average Cost $\bar{C}(n)$ (₹ lakh/year) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 4.800 |
| 2 | **4.750 (Minimum)** |
| 3 | 5.000 |
| 4 | 5.375 |
| 5 | 5.900 |

**Step 4: Decision:**

The minimum average cost of ₹4.75 lakh/year occurs at **$n^\* = 2$ years**. The truck should be replaced after **2 years** of operation.

**Explanation:** Beyond year 2, the marginal increase in operating cost (driven by wear, increased breakdowns, higher fuel consumption, and falling reliability) exceeds the depreciation savings. Continuing to operate the truck past its economic life incurs avoidable cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Fleet Optimization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in fleet sizing — students often give fractional answers like 121.76 trucks. **Always round UP** because a fractional truck cannot be purchased.
>
> 2. **Confusing Load Factor with Capacity Utilization Ratio** — they are related but not identical. LF is based on *weight/volume*, while CUR includes time dimensions too.
>
> 3. **Skipping the geometric feasibility check** in Clarke-Wright — students often merge customers who are *inside* a route (not at end nodes), violating the algorithm's logic.
>
> 4. **Confusing depreciation with operating cost** in the replacement model. Depreciation belongs in the numerator; do NOT double-count.
>
> 5. **Not stating units** in final answers — KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units (e.g., writing "63" instead of "63 km").
>
> 6. **Ignoring constraints** — real VRP has capacity, time-window, and driver-rest constraints. A solution that violates these is mathematically invalid for KTU.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam:**
>
> - **Fleet Optimization** = Right size + Right route + Right load + Right lifecycle.
> - **Four Pillars:** Sizing, Routing, Loading, Replacement.
> - **Fleet Sizing Equation:** $N_{\text{req}} = \lceil T_{\text{demand}} \cdot S_f / (C_v \cdot H_{\text{avail}} \cdot U_r) \rceil$.
> - **Clarke-Wright Savings:** $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$; merge highest savings first; check end-node + capacity.
> - **Load Factor:** $LF = (W_{\text{actual}} / W_{\text{rated}}) \times 100$; target **> 80%** for efficiency.
> - **Empty Running %:** $ERP = (D_{\text{empty}} / D_{\text{total}}) \times 100$; reduce via **backhauling**.
> - **TCO** = CAPEX + Fuel + Maintenance + Driver + Insurance + Tyres + Downtime − Salvage.
> - **Minimum Average Cost Method:** Optimal replacement age = year with minimum $\bar{C}(n)$.
> - **Cost per Km (CPK):** $CPK = (C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}}) / D_{\text{total}}$.
> - **VRP vs TSP:** TSP visits all nodes once; VRP adds vehicle capacity constraints.
> - **KPIs to memorize:** LF, ERP, VUR, CPK, MTBF, TCO, FE.
> - **Heuristics taught in KTU:** Clarke-Wright Savings (most common), Nearest Neighbour, Sweep Algorithm.
> - **Common KTU Mistakes:** Forgetting units, skipping ceiling function, missing constraints, misapplying savings formula.
> - **Industrial Context:** Kerala-specific examples (Kochi port logistics, KSRTC bus operations, seafood/coconut transport) earn contextual appreciation marks.
> - **Integration Theme:** Combine optimization techniques for compounding effect (e.g., route + load + backhaul optimization together yield > 30% cost reduction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Route Optimization and Load Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Route Optimization and Load Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Route Optimization — Formal Definition

**Route Optimization** is the process of determining the most cost-effective (in terms of distance, time, fuel, or a combination thereof) route or set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to travel from a central depot to deliver goods or provide services at multiple geographically dispersed customer locations, while satisfying a set of operational constraints (vehicle capacity, time windows, driver hours, etc.).

In the formal operations research language, route optimization solves variants of the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**, originally proposed by Dantzig and Ramser (1959) and later formalized by Clarke and Wright (1964) through the **Savings Algorithm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Route optimization is classified under *strategic and tactical fleet management*. It directly impacts **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** which typically constitutes **6 %–12 %** of the GDP of a nation, of which **40 %–60 %** is attributable to transportation alone.

### 1.2 Load Planning — Formal Definition

**Load Planning** (also called **Cargo Loading Optimization** or **Payload Distribution**) is the engineering process of arranging, sequencing, and placing heterogeneous cargo items (parcels, pallets, containers, or loose cargo) inside the bounded volume of a transport vehicle (truck, trailer, container, or aircraft ULD) such that:
1. The **center of gravity (CG)** remains within safe lateral/longitudinal limits,
2. The **gross vehicle weight (GVW)** and **axle load limits** are not exceeded,
3. The **cube utilization** (% of filled volume) and **weight utilization** are maximized,
4. Cargo stability, accessibility (LIFO/FIFO), and damage prevention constraints are satisfied.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

**Route Optimization Analogy — "The Milkman Problem":**
Imagine you are a milkman in Kerala delivering to 30 houses scattered across Kottayam town. You start from the dairy at 6 AM, must visit every house, and return by 9 AM. You can carry only **100 litres** in your bike. Which houses do you serve on Trip 1, which on Trip 2? In which order? The *route* on each trip should be the **shortest path that visits each chosen customer once and returns to the dairy**. This is the essence of the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**, the simplest form of VRP.

**Load Planning Analogy — "The House-Move Puzzle":**
Picture packing a tempo traveller for a family of 4 moving from Kochi to Bengaluru. The sofa must go on the left (heavy items low and along the axle to keep the CG low), the fragile TV must be on top of the mattress (cushioning), the gas cylinder must be vertical and outside the cabin (safety regulation), and bags must be accessible without unloading the fridge (LIFO access). The goal: **fit the most in the safest way**. That is load planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metrics (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Payload utilization (weight)** = (Loaded Weight / Rated Payload) × 100
> - **Volume utilization (cube)** = (Used Volume / Container Volume) × 100
> - **Drop density** = Number of drops per route (target: 8–18 for LTL, 1–3 for FTL)
> - **Empty running (dead-head) %** = (Non-revenue km / Total km) × 100 (industry benchmark: **<15 %**)

---

### 1.4 Why These Two Topics are Co-Taught

Route optimization and load planning are **two sides of the same coin**. Even the *shortest* route is wasteful if the truck returns half-empty. Even a *perfectly packed* truck is a money-loser if the route zig-zags across the state. Together, they form the **tactical planning layer** of fleet management, sitting between strategic decisions (fleet size) and operational decisions (driver assignment).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP Family)

The VRP family is a hierarchy of increasingly constrained routing models. A KTU student must know each variant and its real-world trigger.

| # | VRP Variant | Full Form | Real-World Trigger | KTU Weight |
|---|-------------|-----------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | **TSP** | Travelling Salesman Problem | One salesman, one vehicle, visit all cities once | Foundational |
| 2 | **VRP** | Vehicle Routing Problem | Multiple vehicles, capacity limits | Core |
| 3 | **VRPTW** | VRP with Time Windows | Customers specify "deliver between 10 AM–12 PM" | Very common |
| 4 | **VRPPD** | VRP with Pickup and Delivery | Each customer has both a pickup AND a drop | Reverse logistics |
| 5 | **OVRP** | Open Vehicle Routing Problem | Vehicles do NOT return to depot | Last-mile delivery to remote hubs |
| 6 | **MDVRP** | Multi-Depot VRP | Multiple depots (e.g., Amazon FCs) | Multi-warehouse ops |
| 7 | **DVRP** | Dynamic VRP | New orders arrive during the day | E-commerce / on-demand |
| 8 | **EVRP** | Electric VRP | Battery range + charging station visits | Future fleet planning |

### 2.2 Core Route Optimization Objectives and Constraints

A route optimization model always has two components: an **objective function** (what to minimize) and a **set of constraints** (what must be satisfied).

**Objective Function (typical for KTU):**

$$\min Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$$

where:
- $K$ = set of vehicles in the fleet,
- $A$ = set of arcs (roads) connecting nodes $i$ and $j$,
- $c_{ij}$ = cost (distance, time, or ₹) of traversing arc $(i,j)$,
- $x_{ijk}$ = binary decision variable = **1** if vehicle $k$ travels arc $(i,j)$, else **0**.

**Constraints (standard VRP formulation):**

1. **Each customer visited exactly once:**

$$\sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} = 1, \quad \forall i \in C$$

2. **Vehicle capacity constraint:**

$$\sum_{i \in C} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q_k, \quad \forall k \in K$$

where $q_i$ = demand of customer $i$, $Q_k$ = capacity of vehicle $k$, $y_{ik}$ = 1 if customer $i$ served by vehicle $k$.

3. **Flow conservation** (what enters a node must leave):

$$\sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in V} x_{jik}, \quad \forall i \in C, \; \forall k \in K$$

4. **Subtour elimination** (Miller-Tucker-Zemlin formulation):

$$u_i - u_j + Q_k \cdot x_{ijk} \leq Q_k - q_j, \quad \forall i,j \in C, \; k \in K$$

### 2.3 The Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm (Most-Tested Heuristic in KTU)

This is the **classic exam favourite** for the 14-mark KTU question. The procedure:

> **Step 1:** Start with the trivial solution where each of $n$ customers is served by a dedicated vehicle making a round trip Depot $\rightarrow$ Customer $i$ $\rightarrow$ Depot.

> **Step 2:** Compute the **savings** $S_{ij}$ for every pair of customers $(i, j)$:

$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

where $d_{0i}$ = depot-to-customer $i$ distance, $d_{ij}$ = customer $i$ to customer $j$ distance.

> **Step 3:** Sort savings in descending order.

> **Step 4:** Merge routes link by link, but only if:
> - Customer $i$ is currently an *end* (either first or last stop) of one route,
> - Customer $j$ is currently an *end* of another (or the same) route,
> - Merging does NOT violate vehicle capacity,
> - Merging does NOT create a cycle (sub-tour).

> **Step 5:** Stop when no more profitable (positive savings) merges are possible.

### 2.4 Load Planning: Mathematical Model

**Bin Packing Formulation (3D Load Planning):**

Load planning is mathematically equivalent to the **3D Bin Packing Problem**, an **NP-hard** combinatorial optimization problem.

**Decision Variables:**
- $x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if item $i$ is placed in position $k$ of vehicle $j$
- $y_j \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if vehicle $j$ is used

**Constraints:**
1. Each item placed in at most one position:

$$\sum_{j} \sum_{k} x_{ijk} \leq 1, \quad \forall i$$

2. At most one item per position (no overlap):

$$\sum_{i} x_{ijk} \leq 1, \quad \forall j, k$$

3. Total weight $\leq$ vehicle payload:

$$\sum_{i} w_i \cdot \sum_{k} x_{ijk} \leq P_j \cdot y_j, \quad \forall j$$

4. Total volume $\leq$ vehicle volume:

$$\sum_{i} v_i \cdot \sum_{k} x_{ijk} \leq V_j \cdot y_j, \quad \forall j$$

5. **Center of Gravity (CG) constraint** — lateral:

$$\left| \sum_{i} w_i \cdot x_{i} \cdot l_{ix} \Big/ \sum_{i} w_i \right| \leq \delta_x \cdot L_j / 2$$

where $\delta_x$ = allowable CG offset fraction (typically **5 %–10 %** of wheelbase).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — these are the **direct-solve formulas** examiners expect you to reproduce.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Clarke-Wright Savings | $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | km or ₹ | Route merging decision |
| 2 | Total Route Cost | $C_R = c_d \cdot D + c_t \cdot T + c_f \cdot F$ | ₹ | Compare two routes |
| 3 | Payload Utilization | $\eta_w = W_{load} / W_{payload}$ × **100** | % | Loading efficiency |
| 4 | Cube (Volume) Utilization | $\eta_v = V_{load} / V_{container}$ × **100** | % | Truck fill rate |
| 5 | Dead-Head % | $\eta_d = D_{empty} / D_{total}$ × **100** | % | Backhaul waste |
| 6 | Cost per Tonne-Km | $C_{tk} = C_{total} / (W \cdot D)$ | ₹/t·km | Carrier comparison |
| 7 | Load Density Threshold | $\rho_{crit} = W_{payload} / V_{container}$ | t/m³ | Weight-vs-volume limit |
| 8 | CG Offset (lateral) | $e_y = \sum w_i \cdot y_i / \sum w_i$ | m | Stability check |
| 9 | Vehicle Turnover | $VT = n_{trips} / T_{period}$ | trips/day | Fleet productivity |
| 10 | Routing Density | $R_d = n_{drops} / D_{route}$ | drops/km | Stop spacing |

**Standard Practical Constants (industrial):**

- Light commercial vehicle payload utilization benchmark = **80 %–85 %**
- Heavy commercial vehicle (HCV) volume utilization benchmark = **90 %–95 %**
- Diesel truck fuel consumption = **0.25 L/km to 0.45 L/km** (depending on GVW)
- Average speed in Indian urban context = **20–25 km/h**; highway = **50–60 km/h**

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Load Sequencing — Centre of Gravity Logic

In practice, load planners follow the **HEAVY-AT-BOTTOM, LIGHT-ON-TOP, AGAINST-THE-CAB** rule. The reasoning:
- A **high CG** increases the **rollover risk**. A truck rolling over at 60 km/h is 3× more likely if the CG is 1.2 m versus 0.8 m above ground.
- A **rear-loaded** truck (CG behind rear axle) reduces traction on the drive axle on slippery roads, especially relevant during Kerala monsoon.
- The **front bulkhead** is the strongest wall; heaviest items go behind it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1: Clarke-Wright Savings (Full Solved Example)

> **Problem Statement:** A depot (node 0) serves 4 customers (A, B, C, D). Distance matrix (km) is given below. Use the **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm** to compute the optimal routing for a single vehicle of capacity **40 units**. Customer demands: A=10, B=15, C=20, D=10.

| | **0** | **A** | **B** | **C** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 8 | 14 | 18 | 10 |
| **A** | 8 | 0 | 6 | 12 | 9 |
| **B** | 14 | 6 | 0 | 7 | 11 |
| **C** | 18 | 12 | 7 | 0 | 5 |
| **D** | 10 | 9 | 11 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 1: Compute all pairwise savings $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$**

$$S_{AB} = d_{0A} + d_{0B} - d_{AB} = 8 + 14 - 6 = 16 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{AC} = d_{0A} + d_{0C} - d_{AC} = 8 + 18 - 12 = 14 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{AD} = d_{0A} + d_{0D} - d_{AD} = 8 + 10 - 9 = 9 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{BC} = d_{0B} + d_{0C} - d_{BC} = 14 + 18 - 7 = 25 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{BD} = d_{0B} + d_{0D} - d_{BD} = 14 + 10 - 11 = 13 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{CD} = d_{0C} + d_{0D} - d_{CD} = 18 + 10 - 5 = 23 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2: Sort savings in descending order**

| Rank | Pair | $S_{ij}$ (km) |
|------|------|----------------|
| 1 | B–C | **25** |
| 2 | C–D | **23** |
| 3 | A–B | **16** |
| 4 | A–C | **14** |
| 5 | B–D | **13** |
| 6 | A–D | 9 |

**Step 3: Apply route-merging rules**

Total customer demand = 10 + 15 + 20 + 10 = **55 units**. Since 55 > 40 (vehicle capacity), we **cannot** combine all four into one route. We need at least 2 vehicles.

> **Iteration 1:** Merge B–C (savings = 25). New route: **0 → B → C → 0**. Load = 15+20 = 35 ≤ 40 ✓

> **Iteration 2:** Merge C–D (savings = 23). C is an *end* of route 0–B–C–0. Try to attach D. New route: **0 → B → C → D → 0**. Load = 15+20+10 = **45 > 40** ✗ Reject.

> **Iteration 3:** Merge A–B (savings = 16). A is a separate route (0–A–0). B is an end of route 0–B–C–0. New route: **0 → A → B → C → 0**. Load = 10+15+20 = 45 > 40 ✗ Reject.

> **Iteration 4:** Merge A–C (savings = 14). A is a route (0–A–0). C is an end of route 0–B–C–0. New route: **0 → A → B → C → 0**. Load = 45 > 40 ✗ Reject.

> **Iteration 5:** Merge B–D (savings = 13). B is an end of 0–B–C–0. D is a separate route. New route: **0 → B → C → D → 0**. Load = 15+20+10 = 45 > 40 ✗ Reject.

> **Iteration 6:** Merge A–D (savings = 9). A is a separate route (0–A–0). D is a separate route (0–D–0). Both are ends. New route: **0 → A → D → 0**. Load = 10+10 = 20 ≤ 40 ✓

**Step 4: Final route set**

- **Route 1:** 0 → B → C → 0 (load 35, distance = 14+7+18 = **39 km**)
- **Route 2:** 0 → A → D → 0 (load 20, distance = 8+9+10 = **27 km**)

**Total distance = 39 + 27 = 66 km**

**Compare with trivial solution** (4 dedicated trips): 2(8) + 2(14) + 2(18) + 2(10) = 100 km.
**Savings achieved = (100 − 66) / 100 = 34 %.**

> [!NOTE]
> This 34 % savings on distance is consistent with **industry averages of 5 %–30 %** improvement from route optimization in real fleets.

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2: Load Planning (CG and Utilization)

> **Problem Statement:** A mini-truck has payload = **2.0 tonnes**, cargo body length = **3.0 m**, width = **1.8 m**, height = **1.5 m**. Three pallets are to be loaded: P1 (mass 800 kg, position 0.5 m from front), P2 (mass 700 kg, position 1.5 m from front), P3 (mass 500 kg, position 2.5 m from front). Find payload utilization and CG position. The rear axle is at **2.2 m** from the front. The maximum allowable CG offset from rear axle is **±0.15 m**.

**Step 1: Payload Utilization**

$$\eta_w = \frac{W_{load}}{W_{payload}} = \frac{800 + 700 + 500}{2000} = \frac{2000}{2000} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 2: Volume Utilization**

$$V_{container} = 3.0 \times 1.8 \times 1.5 = 8.1 \text{ m}^3$$

Assuming each pallet occupies 1.2 × 1.0 × 1.0 m:

$$V_{load} = 3 \times (1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0) = 3.6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{3.6}{8.1} \times 100 = 44.4\%$$

(Note the *mismatch* between 100 % weight util and 44 % volume util — characteristic of dense cargo.)

**Step 3: Centre of Gravity (longitudinal, measured from front)**

$$x_{CG} = \frac{\sum m_i \cdot x_i}{\sum m_i} = \frac{(800 \times 0.5) + (700 \times 1.5) + (500 \times 2.5)}{2000}$$

$$x_{CG} = \frac{400 + 1050 + 1250}{2000} = \frac{2700}{2000} = 1.35 \text{ m from front}$$

**Step 4: Offset from rear axle**

$$e = x_{CG} - x_{axle} = 1.35 - 2.2 = -0.85 \text{ m}$$

Wait — the CG is *0.85 m in front of* the rear axle. The limit is ±0.15 m. **This violates the constraint.** The truck would be nose-heavy; risk of front-axle overload and loss of steering traction.

**Step 5: Replan** — shift P3 (500 kg) to the rear. Place P1 at 2.0 m, P2 at 1.5 m, P3 at 0.5 m:

$$x_{CG} = \frac{(800 \times 2.0) + (700 \times 1.5) + (500 \times 0.5)}{2000} = \frac{1600 + 1050 + 250}{2000} = \frac{2900}{2000} = 1.45 \text{ m}$$

Still not safe. Place P1 at 2.5 m, P2 at 1.5 m, P3 at 0.5 m:

$$x_{CG} = \frac{(800 \times 2.5) + (700 \times 1.5) + (500 \times 0.5)}{2000} = \frac{2000 + 1050 + 250}{2000} = \frac{3300}{2000} = 1.65 \text{ m}$$

Offset = 1.65 − 2.2 = **−0.55 m** (still in front of axle). Not acceptable. The cargo is fundamentally too *front-heavy* for the axle position; in practice, the planner must **either shift the cargo bay** (relocate the rear axle) or **load lighter items in the rear**. This shows the **interactive nature** of route planning and load planning.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Heuristic vs. Exact Route Optimization

| Method | Type | Problem Size | Optimality Gap | Computation Time | KTU Use |
|--------|------|--------------|----------------|------------------|---------|
| **Clarke-Wright Savings** | Heuristic | Up to 100+ customers | 5 %–15 % | Seconds | Theory exam |
| **Nearest Neighbour** | Heuristic | Up to 50 customers | 10 %–25 % | Trivial | Quick demo |
| **Sweep Algorithm** | Heuristic | Geometric clusters | 5 %–20 % | Seconds | Clustered zones |
| **2-opt / 3-opt** | Local search | Up to 500 customers | 1 %–5 % | Minutes | Refinement step |
| **Tabu Search** | Meta-heuristic | Up to 1000 customers | <2 % | Minutes-hours | Industry standard |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | Meta-heuristic | 1000+ customers | <2 % | Hours | Dynamic VRP |
| **Branch-and-Cut (exact)** | Exact MIP | Up to 30–50 customers | 0 % (optimal) | Hours-days | Benchmark only |

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

*Define the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). List any FOUR real-world constraints that make the simple VRP harder to solve in practice.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimization problem that seeks to determine the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to service a set of customers from a central depot, such that each customer is visited exactly once (or in some variants, at least once) and total route cost is minimized.

Four real-world constraints that complicate VRP:
1. **Vehicle capacity limit** — sum of customer demands on a route cannot exceed payload.
2. **Time windows (VRPTW)** — each customer must be served within a specified time interval (e.g., 9 AM–11 AM).
3. **Driver working hours (HOS regulations)** — total route time limited to ~8 hours/day per Indian Motor Transport Workers rules.
4. **Heterogeneous fleet** — different vehicle types with different capacities and costs.

*(Examiner expects: definition + 4 constraints, written in ≤ 1 page. 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for constraints.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**

*Differentiate between **payload utilization** and **volume (cube) utilization** in load planning. Why is it important to monitor both?*

**Model Answer:**

**Payload Utilization (Weight Utilization):**

$$\eta_w = \frac{W_{loaded}}{W_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

It measures how much of the vehicle's *legal weight capacity* is used. It is a regulatory metric — exceeding it is illegal and unsafe.

**Volume (Cube) Utilization:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{cargo}}{V_{container}} \times 100\%$$

It measures how much of the vehicle's *physical space* is filled. It is an economic metric — empty volume is wasted asset utilization.

**Why monitor both:**
- Cargo may be **dense** (e.g., iron rods): 100 % weight util but only 30 % volume util.
- Cargo may be **light & bulky** (e.g., thermocol): 30 % weight util but 100 % volume util.
- Either way, the truck is operating *inefficiently*. A balanced target is ≥80 % on both metrics.
- In the first case, the planner must add a **backhaul** or **LTL consolidation**; in the second, the planner must add **ballast or stackable items**.

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for the imbalance explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Solved**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 4 Internal Choice Option A]**

A logistics company operates from a single depot (node 0) and serves 5 customers (A, B, C, D, E). The distance matrix (km) is given below. Each customer has a demand and a service time as listed.

| | **0** | **A** | **B** | **C** | **D** | **E** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 | 15 | 20 |
| **A** | 12 | 0 | 8 | 14 | 10 | 16 |
| **B** | 18 | 8 | 0 | 9 | 13 | 11 |
| **C** | 22 | 14 | 9 | 0 | 12 | 7 |
| **D** | 15 | 10 | 13 | 12 | 0 | 18 |
| **E** | 20 | 16 | 11 | 7 | 18 | 0 |

- Demands: A=8, B=12, C=10, D=9, E=11 (units)
- Vehicle capacity = **20 units per vehicle**
- Number of available vehicles = **3**

**(a)** Apply the **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm** to obtain the optimal route set. Show all savings calculations and the merging logic step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the final route set obtained in (a), compute: (i) total distance traveled, (ii) total demand carried per route, (iii) payload utilization per route, and (iv) suggest ONE operational improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Clarke-Wright Savings Calculation**

**Step 1:** Compute all pairwise savings $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$:

$$S_{AB} = 12 + 18 - 8 = 22$$

$$S_{AC} = 12 + 22 - 14 = 20$$

$$S_{AD} = 12 + 15 - 10 = 17$$

$$S_{AE} = 12 + 20 - 16 = 16$$

$$S_{BC} = 18 + 22 - 9 = 31$$

$$S_{BD} = 18 + 15 - 13 = 20$$

$$S_{BE} = 18 + 20 - 11 = 27$$

$$S_{CD} = 22 + 15 - 12 = 25$$

$$S_{CE} = 22 + 20 - 7 = 35$$

$$S_{DE} = 15 + 20 - 18 = 17$$

**Step 2: Sort descending**

| Rank | Pair | Savings (km) |
|------|------|--------------|
| 1 | C–E | **35** |
| 2 | B–C | **31** |
| 3 | B–E | **27** |
| 4 | C–D | **25** |
| 5 | A–B | **22** |
| 6 | A–C | **20** |
| 7 | B–D | **20** |
| 8 | A–D | **17** |
| 9 | A–E | **16** |
| 10 | D–E | **17** |

**Step 3: Merge Iterations (Capacity Q = 20)**

- **Iteration 1 — Pair C–E (35):** Both endpoints C and E are separate route ends. Merge → Route R1: **0 → C → E → 0**. Load = 10 + 11 = 21. ❌ **Exceeds 20. Reject.**

  *Correction:* In capacity violation, we may try shorter merge. Alternative: insert between existing arcs is not possible since both are isolated. So this pair cannot be used in this form.

- **Iteration 2 — Pair B–C (31):** Both are isolated routes. Merge → **0 → B → C → 0**. Load = 12 + 10 = 22 ❌ Reject.

- **Iteration 3 — Pair B–E (27):** Both isolated. Merge → **0 → B → E → 0**. Load = 12 + 11 = 23 ❌ Reject.

- **Iteration 4 — Pair C–D (25):** Both isolated. Merge → **0 → C → D → 0**. Load = 10 + 9 = 19 ✅ **Accepted**. Route R1: 0–C–D–0.

- **Iteration 5 — Pair A–B (22):** A isolated. B isolated. Merge → **0 → A → B → 0**. Load = 8 + 12 = 20 ✅ **Accepted**. Route R2: 0–A–B–0.

- **Iteration 6 — Pair A–C (20):** A is end of R2 (0–A–B–0). C is end of R1 (0–C–D–0). Merge → **0 → A → B → 0 ∪ 0 → C → D → 0 ⇒ 0 → A → B → ... → C → D → 0** with appropriate link. New route: **0 → A → B → C → D → 0**. Load = 8+12+10+9 = 39 ❌ Reject.

- **Iteration 7 — Pair B–D (20):** B is end of R2. D is end of R1. Merge → **0 → A → B → D → C → 0** (since linking B to D connects R2 and R1). Load = 8+12+9+10 = 39 ❌ Reject.

- **Iteration 8 — Pair A–D (17):** A is end of R2. D is end of R1. Load = 8+9 = 17 added to existing route... Actually merge attempts to combine ends. Connecting A and D forms cycle issue. Reject.

- **Iteration 9 — Pair D–E (17):** D is end of R1. E is isolated. Merge → **0 → C → D → E → 0**. Load = 10+9+11 = 30 ❌ Reject.

- **Iteration 10 — Pair A–E (16):** A is end of R2. E is isolated. Merge → **0 → A → B → E → 0**? No — E is *separate* isolated route, so merge gives: Route R2 extended: **0 → A → B → E → 0**. Load = 8+12+11 = 31 ❌ Reject. Or **0 → A → E → 0** and **0 → B → 0** etc. — still need to maintain linear chain. Not feasible.

**Final route set (since no more feasible merges):**

- **Route R1:** 0 → C → D → 0
- **Route R2:** 0 → A → B → 0
- **Route R3:** 0 → E → 0

Customer E alone justifies a third vehicle (3 vehicles were given).

**Part (b) — Computations**

**(i) Total distance:**

$$D_{R1} = d_{0C} + d_{CD} + d_{D0} = 22 + 12 + 15 = 49 \text{ km}$$

$$D_{R2} = d_{0A} + d_{AB} + d_{B0} = 12 + 8 + 18 = 38 \text{ km}$$

$$D_{R3} = 2 \times d_{0E} = 2 \times 20 = 40 \text{ km}$$

$$D_{total} = 49 + 38 + 40 = 127 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Demand per route:**

- R1: 10 + 9 = **19 units**
- R2: 8 + 12 = **20 units**
- R3: **11 units**

**(iii) Payload utilization:**

- R1: 19/20 × 100 = **95 %**
- R2: 20/20 × 100 = **100 %**
- R3: 11/20 × 100 = **55 %** (low — underutilized)

**(iv) Operational improvement:** Route R3 is significantly underutilized. Suggest either:
- **Add consolidation** — include one more small customer pickup on R3, OR
- **Assign R3 to a smaller vehicle** (e.g., LCV instead of HCV) to reduce cost-per-km by 30 %–40 %, OR
- **Use R3 as a backhaul** — bring return-load from a customer at node E to the depot (close the return loop with cargo).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 4 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Explain the **3D Bin Packing formulation** of load planning. List and mathematically state FIVE key constraints that govern cargo placement in a truck container. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A delivery truck has payload **5 tonnes**, container dimensions **5.0 m × 2.2 m × 2.0 m (L × W × H)**. Four cargo items must be loaded with the following data:

| Item | Mass (kg) | Length (m) | Width (m) | Height (m) | Fragile? |
|------|-----------|------------|-----------|------------|----------|
| P1 | 1500 | 1.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 | No |
| P2 | 800 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.8 | No |
| P3 | 600 | 1.2 | 1.0 | 1.0 | Yes |
| P4 | 1200 | 1.5 | 1.2 | 1.0 | No |

Compute: (i) total mass loaded and payload utilization, (ii) total cargo volume and cube utilization, (iii) draw a top-view stacking layout assuming P3 is placed on top of P1 (because P3 is fragile and needs cushioning), (iv) state TWO specific precautions during transit. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 3D Bin Packing Formulation**

The 3D Bin Packing Problem (3D-BPP) places rectangular 3D boxes (cargo items) into a larger rectangular container (truck body) such that:
- Every item is fully inside the container,
- No two items overlap,
- All items are axis-aligned (faces parallel to container walls),
- The volume of items placed is maximized (or number of bins minimized).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Let $I$ = set of items, $J$ = set of vehicle positions (discretized grid), $K$ = set of vehicles.

**Decision Variables:**
- $x_{ijk} = 1$ if item $i$ is placed at position $j$ in vehicle $k$, else 0
- $y_k = 1$ if vehicle $k$ is used

**Five Key Constraints:**

1. **Each item in at most one position:**

$$\sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in J} x_{ijk} \leq 1, \quad \forall i \in I$$

2. **At most one item per position (no overlap):**

$$\sum_{i \in I} x_{ijk} \leq 1, \quad \forall j \in J, \; \forall k \in K$$

3. **Mass constraint:**

$$\sum_{i \in I} m_i \cdot x_{ijk} \leq M_k \cdot y_k, \quad \forall k \in K$$

where $M_k$ is the rated payload of vehicle $k$.

4. **Volume constraint (simplified):**

$$\sum_{i \in I} v_i \cdot x_{ijk} \leq V_k \cdot y_k, \quad \forall k \in K$$

5. **Center of gravity (CG) constraint — lateral:**

$$\left| \frac{\sum_{i} m_i \cdot (c_{ix} - X_k)}{\sum_{i} m_i} \right| \leq \epsilon \cdot \frac{W_k}{2}$$

where $c_{ix}$ = lateral centroid of item $i$, $X_k$ = vehicle centerline, $W_k$ = vehicle width, $\epsilon$ = allowable offset ratio (5 %–10 %).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**(i) Total mass and payload utilization:**

$$M_{total} = 1500 + 800 + 600 + 1200 = 4100 \text{ kg}$$

$$\eta_w = \frac{4100}{5000} \times 100 = 82\%$$

**(ii) Volume and cube utilization:**

$$V_{P1} = 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.50 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{P2} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 = 0.80 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{P3} = 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.20 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{P4} = 1.5 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.80 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{load} = 1.50 + 0.80 + 1.20 + 1.80 = 5.30 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{container} = 5.0 \times 2.2 \times 2.0 = 22.0 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{5.30}{22.0} \times 100 = 24.1\%$$

The very low volume utilization (24 %) despite 82 % weight util indicates the truck is **size-constrained upward** — the operator should consider a **smaller vehicle (LCV) to reduce cost-per-km by ~30 %** for this typical load.

**(iii) Top-view stacking layout (P3 on top of P1, others on floor):**

| Container (top view, length 5.0 m × width 2.2 m) |
|:---:|
| FRONT OF TRUCK (cab side) ← → REAR (door) |
| Row 1 (y = 0 to 1.2 m): **P4** (1.5×1.2) occupies x = 0 to 1.5 m |
| Row 2 (y = 1.2 to 2.2 m): **P1** (1.5×1.0) at x = 0 to 1.5 m; **P3** stacked on P1 |
| Row 3 (y = 0 to 1.0 m, x = 1.5 to 2.5 m): **P2** (1.0×1.0) |

Front-to-back: P1+P3 (heaviest, near bulkhead) → P4 (next heaviest) → P2 (lightest, near door for LIFO access).

**(iv) Two specific transit precautions:**

1. **P3 (fragile) is stacked on P1** — secure with **edge protectors and shrink-wrap**; place a **vibration-dampening foam mat** between P1 and P3; mark with "FRAGILE — THIS SIDE UP" arrow labels on the top face.
2. **Lateral CG check** — combined CG of P1 (centroid x = 0.75 m) and P3 (centroid x = 0.6 m) is offset to the left; with P2 and P4 on the right, verify the lateral CG offset is within 5 % of half-width. Add **ratchet straps** to all pallets to prevent sliding on Kerala's monsoon-slick braking events.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Formula without units** — examiners deduct ½ mark if you write $S_{ij} = 22$ without "km" or "%".
> 2. **Capacity check forgotten** — when merging routes, ALWAYS show the cumulative demand ≤ Q. The single most-missed step.
> 3. **Confusing "savings" with "distance"** — $S_{ij}$ is the *saving* in km, not the route distance.
> 4. **Load planning = "just put it in"** — the question expects CG check, volume vs. weight tradeoff, and accessibility. A "real-world engineering" answer is required, not a kitchen-recipe.
> 5. **Not drawing the layout** — in 14-mark load planning questions, ALWAYS provide a top-view (or side-view) sketch; 1–2 marks reserved for it.
> 6. **Mixing TSP and VRP** — TSP is single-vehicle, VRP is multi-vehicle. Examiners catch this every year.
> 7. **Ignoring time windows** — if the question mentions "deliver by 11 AM", it is VRPTW, not plain VRP. Add a time constraint.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- [x] **VRP** = multi-vehicle TSP with capacity; **TSP** = single vehicle, visit all cities once.
- [x] **Clarke-Wright Savings Formula:** $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$. Higher = better to merge.
- [x] **Three pre-conditions to merge a route pair in Clarke-Wright:** (a) both are route ends, (b) capacity not violated, (c) no cycle formed.
- [x] **VRP variants** to know: VRPTW, VRPPD, OVRP, MDVRP, DVRP, EVRP.
- [x] **Payload utilization** = (Loaded weight / Rated payload) × 100. Benchmark: 80 %–85 %.
- [x] **Cube (volume) utilization** = (Loaded volume / Container volume) × 100. Benchmark: 90 %–95 % for HCV.
- [x] **Mismatched utilization** → either add a backhaul (low cube) or switch to a smaller truck (low weight).
- [x] **Load sequencing rule:** Heavy at bottom, light on top, heaviest against the front bulkhead, LIFO access via the rear door.
- [x] **CG constraint:** lateral offset ≤ **5 %–10 %** of half-width. Exceeding this increases rollover risk and is illegal under CMV rules.
- [x] **Cost per tonne-km** is the universal metric for comparing carriers: $C_{tk} = C_{total} / (W \cdot D)$.
- [x] **Dead-head %** (empty running) is the inverse measure of backhaul success; target **< 15 %**.
- [x] **Heuristics vs Exact:** Clarke-Wright is a heuristic (fast, near-optimal); Branch-and-Cut is exact (slow, optimal). Industry uses heuristics.
- [x] **Saving 30 % distance** is typical Clarke-Wright gain over the trivial 1-customer-per-vehicle solution.
- [x] **NP-hard:** both VRP and 3D-BPP are NP-hard. There is no polynomial-time exact algorithm.
- [x] **Time windows** add a real-time component; service must start within $[e_i, l_i]$.
- [x] **Heterogeneous fleet VRP** (HFVRP) is the realistic case — different trucks have different costs and capacities.
- [x] **Load density threshold** $\rho_{crit} = W/V$ decides whether the truck is weight-limited or volume-limited.
- [x] **Stability** of stacked cargo: heavier items below, items with larger base area below, items with higher CG above.
- [x] **Door-side accessibility** — LIFO for milk/medical, FIFO for FMCG goods.
- [x] **Indian regulation reference:** Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR) 1989 governs axle loads; max GVW for 2-axle truck = 16,200 kg, for 3-axle = 25,000 kg (with trailer up to 49,000 kg for multi-axle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Management in Fleet Operations – Cost Components of Fleet Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Management in Fleet Operations – Cost Components of Fleet Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fleet Cost Management** is the systematic process of identifying, classifying, monitoring, controlling, and optimizing all financial expenditures incurred throughout the entire life cycle of a fleet of vehicles — from acquisition, operation, maintenance, and compliance, to eventual disposal. It encompasses the strategic allocation of monetary resources to ensure maximum **operational efficiency**, **vehicle availability**, and **return on investment (ROI)** while minimizing total cost of ownership (TCO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cost Management in Fleet Operations refers to the analytical and procedural framework used by transport managers to break down fleet expenditure into **Fixed Costs (Standing Costs)**, **Variable Costs (Running Costs)**, and **Overhead/Administrative Costs**, and to use this data for budgeting, pricing, vehicle replacement decisions, and profitability analysis.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a fleet as a **large joint family** running a fleet of autorickshaws or buses. Each vehicle is like one family member who has to be **fed (fuel)**, **clothed (maintenance & tires)**, **educated (licensing & permits)**, **insured (insurance)**, and **housed (garage/depot rent)**. The "head of the family" — the fleet manager — must keep a meticulous account book to ensure the family doesn't overspend. If one auto costs ₹15/km to run and the competitor runs at ₹10/km, that family will soon go broke. **Cost management is simply the art of knowing where every rupee goes, so you can decide where to cut, where to invest, and when to replace an old vehicle with a new one.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Cost per Kilometre (CPK)** — the most critical single indicator
> - **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — life-cycle cost
> - **Break-Even Point (BEP)** — profitability threshold
> - **Cost Recovery Ratio** — revenue vs. cost ratio

### 1.3 Why Cost Management Matters in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, this topic directly addresses **CO4: Apply cost-control techniques in fleet operations to optimize resource utilization.** It is essential for careers in **transport logistics, supply chain, fleet consulting, and public transport management**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fleet Cost Components

Fleet costs are broadly classified into **three primary categories** for analytical purposes. The KTU board expects students to be able to enumerate at least 4-5 components under each head, and to know which costs are **fixed** and which are **variable**.

#### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (Standing Costs)
These costs are **time-dependent** — they remain relatively constant regardless of whether the vehicle runs 100 km or 10,000 km in a given period. They are incurred as long as the vehicle **exists** in the fleet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Fixed Costs — "SILDA"**
> - **S** — Salary of driver & permanent staff
> - **I** — Insurance premium
> - **L** — Licensing, permits, road tax
> - **D** — Depreciation
> - **A** — Administration, garage rent, office overheads

**Sub-components in detail:**

1. **Depreciation (D):** Loss in book value of the vehicle over its useful life. The most mathematically significant fixed cost.
2. **Insurance:** Annual premium for comprehensive, third-party, and goods-in-transit coverage. Mandatory under the Motor Vehicles Act.
3. **Road Tax & Registration:** One-time or annual state-level levies.
4. **Permits & Licensing:** National permit, state permit, fitness certificate, etc.
5. **Driver Wages (Fixed Component):** Basic pay, DA, and statutory benefits — independent of km run.
6. **Garage / Depot Rent:** Fixed rental of the parking and maintenance facility.
7. **Administrative Overheads:** Office salaries, audit, accountancy, software (GPS, TMS).
8. **Interest on Capital:** Opportunity cost of money locked up in vehicles (if not financed) or actual interest paid on loans.

#### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (Running / Operating Costs)
These costs are **distance-dependent** — they vary directly (or nearly directly) with the kilometres run. The more the vehicle runs, the higher these costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Variable Costs — "FOMTW"**
> - **F** — Fuel (Diesel / Petrol / CNG / EV electricity)
> - **O** — Oil, lubricants, grease
> - **M** — Maintenance & Repairs (spares, labour)
> - **T** — Tyres and tubes
> - **W** — Washing, cleaning, minor consumables

**Sub-components in detail:**

1. **Fuel Cost:** Typically **40–55%** of total variable cost — the single largest operating expense.
2. **Lubricants:** Engine oil, gear oil, grease, brake fluid, coolant.
3. **Tyre Cost:** Including re-treading, puncture repairs, balancing, alignment.
4. **Maintenance & Repairs:** Preventive (scheduled) + Corrective (breakdown) maintenance.
5. **Washing & Cleaning:** Daily/weekly washing, interior cleaning.
6. **Consumables:** Filters, wipers, bulbs, fuses, small parts.

#### 2.1.3 Overhead / Indirect Costs
These are **fleet-level** costs not directly attributable to a single vehicle but spread across the entire fleet.

- Workshop overheads, supervisory staff
- Communication costs (mobile, GPS data)
- Training and certification
- Miscellaneous / contingency provision (~2-5% of total cost)

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Straight-Line Depreciation (per year) | $D_{SL} = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | ₹/year; C = cost, S = salvage, n = life in years |
| 2 | Diminishing Balance Depreciation | $D_{DB} = BV \times d$ | ₹/year; BV = book value, d = rate (decimal) |
| 3 | Cost per Kilometre (CPK) | $CPK = \dfrac{FC + VC \cdot km + \text{Overhead}}{km}$ | ₹/km; the master metric |
| 4 | Variable Cost per km | $VC_{km} = \dfrac{\text{Total Variable Cost}}{km\ \text{run}}$ | ₹/km |
| 5 | Fixed Cost per km | $FC_{km} = \dfrac{\text{Total Fixed Cost}}{km\ \text{run}}$ | ₹/km; falls as km rises |
| 6 | Break-Even Point (units) | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{p - v}$ | Units; p = price/unit, v = variable cost/unit |
| 7 | Break-Even Revenue | $BEP_{rev} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{v}{p}}$ | ₹ |
| 8 | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) | $TCO = C_{acq} + \sum_{t=1}^{n}(O_t + M_t) - S$ | ₹; full life cycle cost |
| 9 | Present Worth of Fleet | $PW = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹; i = discount rate |
| 10 | Tyre Life Cost (per km) | $C_{tyre/km} = \dfrac{C_{tyre} \times (N_{new}+N_{retread})}{L_{total}}$ | ₹/km; N = number, L = life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CPK is the **decision-making heartbeat** of a transport company. Quoting a contract below CPK = loss. Replacing a vehicle is justified when rising maintenance makes its marginal CPK exceed the CPK of a new vehicle.

### 2.3 The Relationship Between Fixed, Variable, and Total Cost

The total cost of operating a vehicle is expressed as:

$$
TC = FC + (VC_{per\ km} \times km)
$$

Where:
- $TC$ = Total annual cost of the vehicle
- $FC$ = Annual fixed cost (₹)
- $VC_{per\ km}$ = Variable cost per kilometre (₹/km)
- $km$ = Annual kilometres run

This is a **linear equation** where the y-intercept is $FC$ and the slope is $VC_{per\ km}$. This linear model is the foundation of break-even and replacement analysis in the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Straight-Line Depreciation (Annual & Per-km)

The straight-line method assumes the vehicle loses value **uniformly** every year over its useful life.

**Given:**
- Purchase Cost $C$ = ₹15,00,000
- Salvage Value $S$ = ₹3,00,000
- Useful Life $n$ = 10 years
- Annual Kilometres $km$ = 60,000 km/year

**Step 1 — Apply the straight-line depreciation formula:**

$$
D_{SL} = \frac{C - S}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
D_{SL} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
D_{SL} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10}
$$

**Step 4 — Final annual depreciation in rupees:**

$$
D_{SL} = \text{₹}\,1{,}20{,}000\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-kilometre depreciation:**

$$
D_{per\ km} = \frac{D_{SL}}{km\ \text{per year}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
D_{per\ km} = \text{₹}\,2.00\ \text{per km}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost per Kilometre (CPK) — Complete Worked Example

**Given a truck with the following annual data:**

| Cost Head | Annual Amount (₹) | Type |
|---|---:|---|
| Depreciation | 1,20,000 | Fixed |
| Insurance | 45,000 | Fixed |
| Road Tax + Permits | 18,000 | Fixed |
| Driver Salary (Fixed part) | 2,40,000 | Fixed |
| Garage Rent (share) | 30,000 | Fixed |
| Interest on Loan (12% of 10L) | 1,20,000 | Fixed |
| **Total Fixed Cost (FC)** | **5,73,000** | — |
| Fuel (6,00,000) | 6,00,000 | Variable |
| Tyres (re-tread + new) | 80,000 | Variable |
| Lubricants | 25,000 | Variable |
| Maintenance & Repairs | 1,20,000 | Variable |
| Washing & Consumables | 15,000 | Variable |
| **Total Variable Cost (VC)** | **8,40,000** | — |
| **Annual km Run** | **60,000** | — |

**Step 1 — Compute Variable Cost per km:**

$$
VC_{per\ km} = \frac{Total\ Variable\ Cost}{Annual\ km} = \frac{8{,}40{,}000}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
VC_{per\ km} = \text{₹}\,14.00\ \text{per km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Fixed Cost per km:**

$$
FC_{per\ km} = \frac{Total\ Fixed\ Cost}{Annual\ km} = \frac{5{,}73{,}000}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
FC_{per\ km} = \text{₹}\,9.55\ \text{per km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Cost per Kilometre (CPK):**

$$
CPK = FC_{per\ km} + VC_{per\ km}
$$

$$
CPK = 9.55 + 14.00
$$

$$
\boxed{CPK = \text{₹}\,23.55\ \text{per km}}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Overhead Provision (typical 5%):**

$$
CPK_{final} = 23.55 \times 1.05 = \text{₹}\,24.73\ \text{per km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** The KTU board **always** expects students to show this three-tier separation — Fixed, Variable, and Overhead. Mixing the three is the single most common reason for losing 2-3 marks in Part B.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Break-Even Point for Fleet Operations
