**Scenario:** A transport company charges ₹35 per km for hiring a truck. Fixed annual cost is ₹5,73,000 and variable cost is ₹14/km. Find the break-even km per year.

**Step 1 — Apply the BEP formula:**

$$
BEP_{km} = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{Price\ per\ km - Variable\ Cost\ per\ km}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
BEP_{km} = \frac{5{,}73{,}000}{35 - 14}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
BEP_{km} = \frac{5{,}73{,}000}{21}
$$

**Step 4 — Final break-even distance:**

$$
\boxed{BEP_{km} = 27{,}286\ \text{km per year}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company must run the truck at least **27,286 km/year** to recover all fixed costs. Any km above this generates pure profit (before overhead). At 60,000 km/year, it earns:

$$
\text{Profit} = (35 - 14) \times 60{,}000 - 5{,}73{,}000 = \text{₹}\,6{,}87{,}000
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Tyre Cost per Kilometre (Detailed)

A truck uses 6 tyres. Each new tyre costs ₹12,000 and lasts 80,000 km. Each tyre can be re-treaded twice at ₹4,000 each, with each re-tread giving 50,000 km. The salvage value of a worn-out tyre is ₹1,000.

**Step 1 — Compute the net cost of one new tyre (including salvage recovery):**

$$
C_{net} = 12{,}000 - 1{,}000 = \text{₹}\,11{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the additional re-treading cost (2 re-treads):**

$$
C_{retread} = 2 \times 4{,}000 = \text{₹}\,8{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total km obtained from one tyre:**

$$
L_{total} = 80{,}000 + 2 \times 50{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000\ \text{km}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the tyre cost per km (per tyre):**

$$
C_{tyre/km} = \frac{11{,}000 + 8{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = \frac{19{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000}
$$

$$
C_{tyre/km} = \text{₹}\,0.1056\ \text{per km}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the cost for all 6 tyres:**

$$
C_{fleet\ tyre/km} = 6 \times 0.1056 = \text{₹}\,0.633\ \text{per km}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cost Behaviour at Different Utilisation Levels

The following table demonstrates the **counter-intuitive but vital** principle that higher utilisation reduces per-km cost by diluting fixed costs:

| Annual km | Fixed Cost/km | Variable Cost/km | Total CPK | % Change vs. 30k |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 30,000 | ₹19.10 | ₹14.00 | ₹33.10 | — (baseline) |
| 60,000 | ₹9.55 | ₹14.00 | ₹23.55 | −28.9% |
| 90,000 | ₹6.37 | ₹14.00 | ₹20.37 | −38.5% |
| 1,20,000 | ₹4.78 | ₹14.00 | ₹18.78 | −43.3% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This table is a **favourite 14-mark exam question** in KTU. The student is often asked to plot or tabulate CPK at multiple utilisation levels and identify the "**sweet spot**" — the point beyond which adding km yields diminishing returns.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Cost Classification Tree (Fleet)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLEET TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP] --> B[FIXED COSTS Standing]
    A --> C[VARIABLE COSTS Running]
    A --> D[OVERHEAD INDIRECT COSTS]

    B --> B1[Depreciation]
    B --> B2[Insurance]
    B --> B3[Road Tax and Permits]
    B --> B4[Driver Wages Fixed]
    B --> B5[Garage and Depot Rent]
    B --> B6[Interest on Capital]
    B --> B7[Administrative Overheads]

    C --> C1[Fuel and Energy]
    C --> C2[Lubricants]
    C --> C3[Tyres and Tubes]
    C --> C4[Maintenance and Repairs]
    C --> C5[Washing and Cleaning]
    C --> C6[Consumables and Filters]

    D --> D1[Workshop Supervision]
    D --> D2[Communication and GPS]
    D --> D3[Training and Certification]
    D --> D4[Contingency Provision 2 to 5 percent]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fixed fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0369a1
    classDef variable fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#b45309
    classDef overhead fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff,stroke:#047857

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6,B7 fixed
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 variable
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 overhead
```

### 4.2 Cost Behaviour Flow — From Utilisation to Profitability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Vehicle Acquired] --> P2[Annual km Run Decides FC per km]
    P2 --> P3{Is km more than BEP}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Operating Profit Zone]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Operating Loss Zone]
    P4 --> P6[Higher Utilisation Lowers CPK]
    P6 --> P7[Reinvest or Replace Decision]
    P5 --> P8[Cost Reduction Measures]
    P8 --> P9[Increase Load Factor]
    P8 --> P10[Route Optimisation]
    P8 --> P11[Driver Training]
    P7 --> P12[Continue Operations]

    classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309
    classDef loss fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff,stroke:#991b1b
    classDef profit fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#166534
    classDef action fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a

    class P3 decision
    class P5,P8 loss
    class P4,P6,P12 profit
    class P1,P2,P7,P9,P10,P11 action
```

### 4.3 CPK Calculation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input: One Year Operational Data] --> S1[Step 1: Collect All Receipts and Logs]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Classify Each Entry as Fixed Variable or Overhead]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Sum Each Category]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Divide FC by Annual km]
    S3 --> S5[Step 4: Divide VC by Annual km]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5: CPK fixed in rupees per km]
    S5 --> S7[Step 5: CPK variable in rupees per km]
    S6 --> S8[Step 6: Sum FC km and VC km]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 7: Apply Overhead Multiplier 1.05]
    S9 --> OUT[Output: Final CPK for Tendering]

    classDef inp fill:#a78bfa,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5b21b6
    classDef step fill:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a
    classDef calc fill:#34d399,color:#ffffff,stroke:#065f46
    classDef out fill:#f97316,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9a3412

    class IN,OUT inp
    class S1,S2,S3,S9 step
    class S4,S5,S6,S7,S8 calc
    class OUT out
```

### 4.4 Cost Management Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fleet Manager Receives Cost Data] --> B{Analyse CPK per Vehicle}
    B --> C{CPK is within Target Range}
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue and Monitor]
    C -- No --> E{Cause of High CPK}
    E --> F[High Fixed Cost per km]
    E --> G[High Variable Cost]
    E --> H[Low Utilisation]
    F --> F1[Consider Replacement or Right Sizing]
    G --> G1[Driver Training and Fuel Efficiency]
    H --> H1[Route Planning and Load Optimisation]
    F1 --> Z[Review in 3 Months]
    G1 --> Z
    H1 --> Z
    Z --> A

    classDef root fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4c1d95
    classDef decision fill:#eab308,color:#1f2937,stroke:#854d0e
    classDef action fill:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff,stroke:#155e75
    classDef loop fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff,stroke:#831843

    class A root
    class B,C,E decision
    class D,F1,G1,H1 action
    class Z loop
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any five fixed costs and five variable costs incurred in the operation of a commercial goods carriage. Briefly explain why depreciation is classified as a fixed cost.**

**Model Answer:**

*Fixed Costs:* (i) Depreciation, (ii) Insurance, (iii) Road Tax and Permits, (iv) Driver's Basic Salary, (v) Garage Rent.

*Variable Costs:* (i) Fuel, (ii) Lubricants, (iii) Tyre wear, (iv) Maintenance & Repairs, (v) Washing.

Depreciation is classified as a fixed cost because it represents the **systematic loss of vehicle value over time**, which is independent of the kilometres the vehicle actually runs. Whether the truck runs 10,000 km or 1,00,000 km in a year, the depreciation charge (calculated on a time basis or as a fixed percentage) remains the same. It is a **time-dependent** expense, not a **distance-dependent** one, hence fixed.

*(3 marks: 1 for listing, 1 for clarity, 1 for the depreciation justification)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Cost per Kilometre" (CPK). Why is CPK considered the most important metric in fleet cost management?**

**Model Answer:**

CPK is the total cost incurred by a vehicle per kilometre of operation, computed as:

$$
CPK = \frac{Fixed\ Cost\ +\ Variable\ Cost\ +\ Overhead}{Annual\ km\ Run}
$$

CPK is the most important metric because:
1. It allows **direct comparison** between vehicles of different ages, types, and capacities.
2. It serves as the **floor price** for any freight quotation — quoting below CPK means guaranteed loss.
3. It enables **vehicle replacement decisions** — when an old vehicle's CPK exceeds that of a new one, replacement is justified.
4. It highlights the impact of **utilisation** — a doubling of annual km can reduce CPK by 30–40%.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four reasons)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Answer **one** of the two choices. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Classify the various cost components involved in fleet management. Discuss in detail any five fixed costs and five variable costs with one-line justification for each. (7 marks)**

**(b) A transport company purchases a truck for ₹18,00,000. The salvage value after 8 years is ₹2,00,000. The truck runs 75,000 km per year. Calculate the annual depreciation by the straight-line method and the depreciation cost per km. If the annual fixed costs (excluding depreciation) are ₹4,50,000 and the variable cost per km is ₹12, find the total CPK. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Fleet costs are classified into three broad categories:

1. **Fixed Costs (Standing Costs):** Incurred irrespective of vehicle usage.
2. **Variable Costs (Running Costs):** Proportional to the distance run.
3. **Overhead/Indirect Costs:** Fleet-level, not vehicle-specific.

**Five Fixed Costs:**
- **Depreciation** — Loss of vehicle value over time; time-based, not km-based.
- **Insurance** — Annual premium to cover risk; paid yearly, fixed amount.
- **Road Tax & Permits** — Statutory levy by state/central authority; annual fixed charge.
- **Driver's Basic Salary** — Paid monthly; independent of km run.
- **Garage/Depot Rent** — Fixed facility cost; contractually fixed per month.

**Five Variable Costs:**
- **Fuel** — Directly proportional to km run.
- **Lubricants** — Consumed by engine per km of operation.
- **Tyres** — Wear-and-tear increases with km; re-tread cost is distance-linked.
- **Maintenance & Repairs** — More km = more wear, more breakdowns.
- **Washing & Cleaning** — Required more frequently as vehicle is used.

*(7 marks: 2 for classification, 2.5 for fixed, 2.5 for variable)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Straight-Line Annual Depreciation:**

$$
D_{SL} = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{8}
$$

$$
D_{SL} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{8} = \text{₹}\,2{,}00{,}000\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation per km:**

$$
D_{per\ km} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{75{,}000} = \text{₹}\,2.667\ \text{per km}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Fixed Cost (including depreciation):**

$$
FC_{total} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,6{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Fixed Cost per km:**

$$
FC_{per\ km} = \frac{6{,}50{,}000}{75{,}000} = \text{₹}\,8.667\ \text{per km}
$$

**Step 5 — Variable Cost per km (given):**

$$
VC_{per\ km} = \text{₹}\,12.00
$$

**Step 6 — Total CPK:**

$$
CPK = FC_{per\ km} + VC_{per\ km} = 8.667 + 12.00
$$

$$
\boxed{CPK = \text{₹}\,20.67\ \text{per km}}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for depreciation, 2 for fixed cost derivation, 2 for final CPK)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Explain the concept of Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) in fleet management. How is it different from the simple purchase price? Construct a TCO equation for a vehicle with a 7-year life. (7 marks)**

**(b) A logistics firm charges ₹30 per km for a mini-truck. The annual fixed cost is ₹3,20,000 and the variable cost per km is ₹11. Determine: (i) the break-even km per year, (ii) the break-even revenue, and (iii) the profit if the truck runs 40,000 km in a year. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**TCO Definition:**
Total Cost of Ownership is the **complete, life-cycle cost** of operating a vehicle from the moment of acquisition to its final disposal. It includes **everything**: purchase price, financing, fuel, maintenance, insurance, permits, driver wages, downtime losses, and even the salvage recovered at the end.

**Difference from Purchase Price:**

| Aspect | Purchase Price | TCO |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | One-time (at acquisition) | Full life cycle (n years) |
| Includes operating costs? | No | Yes |
| Includes financing? | No | Yes (interest) |
| Includes salvage? | No | Yes (subtracted) |
| Decision use | Affordability check | Replacement / make-or-buy decision |

**TCO Equation:**

$$
TCO = C_{acq} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left(O_t + M_t + F_t + I_t + D_t\right) - S
$$

Where:
- $C_{acq}$ = Acquisition cost (₹)
- $O_t$ = Operating cost in year $t$ (fuel, lubricants)
- $M_t$ = Maintenance cost in year $t$
- $F_t$ = Fixed overhead allocated in year $t$
- $I_t$ = Insurance and permits in year $t$
- $D_t$ = Depreciation in year $t$ (if not in $F_t$)
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of year $n$

For 7 years: $n = 7$.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for differences table, 3 for equation & explanation)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** Price $p = 30$ ₹/km, FC = ₹3,20,000, $v = 11$ ₹/km.

**(i) Break-even km per year:**

$$
BEP_{km} = \frac{FC}{p - v} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{30 - 11} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{19}
$$

$$
\boxed{BEP_{km} = 16{,}842\ \text{km per year}}
$$

**(ii) Break-even Revenue:**

$$
BEP_{rev} = BEP_{km} \times p = 16{,}842 \times 30
$$

$$
\boxed{BEP_{rev} = \text{₹}\,5{,}05{,}263\ \text{per year}}
$$

**Alternative cross-check:**

$$
BEP_{rev} = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{v}{p}} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{1 - \frac{11}{30}} = \frac{3{,}20{,}000}{0.6333} = \text{₹}\,5{,}05{,}263
$$

**(iii) Profit at 40,000 km/year:**

$$
\text{Profit} = (p - v) \times km - FC
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = (30 - 11) \times 40{,}000 - 3{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = 19 \times 40{,}000 - 3{,}20{,}000 = 7{,}60{,}000 - 3{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Profit} = \text{₹}\,4{,}40{,}000\ \text{per year}}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for BEP km, 2 for BEP revenue, 3 for profit calculation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Fixed and Variable Costs:** Many students add all costs together and divide by km, getting the wrong CPK. Always compute **two separate** CPKs and then sum.
> 2. **Forgetting the Overhead Multiplier:** KTU valuators deduct 1 mark if overhead/profit margin is omitted from tender CPK.
> 3. **Using the Wrong Depreciation Method:** A question asking for **Written Down Value (WDV)** will be marked wrong if the student uses straight-line.
> 4. **Unit Errors:** Writing CPK in ₹ when the answer should be in ₹/km (or ₹/km when the question asks for total cost) loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Break-Even Confusion:** Confusing break-even **km** with break-even **revenue** is the most common conceptual error.
> 6. **No final box / final answer:** KTU mandates a clear final answer; without it, 0.5 mark is deducted even if the steps are correct.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cost Components of Fleet Management**

- **Three Pillars of Fleet Cost:** Fixed (SILDA), Variable (FOMTW), Overhead.
- **The Master Metric:** Cost per Kilometre (CPK) = (FC + VC + OH) / Annual km.
- **Depreciation Methods:** Straight-Line ($D = (C-S)/n$) and Diminishing Balance ($D = BV \times d$) — know **both**.
- **Tyre Cost per km** includes new + re-tread cost minus salvage, divided by total km life of all treads.
- **Break-Even Point** is **NOT** a single number — it can be expressed in **km**, **revenue**, or **trips**.
- **Higher Utilisation = Lower CPK:** This is the single most important economic insight — fixed cost per km falls as km rises.
- **Fuel is ~50% of variable cost:** Any cost-control strategy should target fuel efficiency first.
- **Depreciation is fixed even though it relates to the vehicle:** It is **time-based**, not **distance-based**.
- **Insurance is mandatory** under the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (Third-party is compulsory).
- **TCO > Purchase Price** — always account for life-cycle costs in fleet decisions.
- **Margin of 5–10%** on CPK is standard for tendering freight rates.
- **TCO formula for 7-year life:** sum operating + maintenance + insurance + depreciation from year 1 to 7, plus acquisition, minus salvage.
- **Golden rule for KTU answers:** Always show **three-tier classification** (FC, VC, OH) and a **clear final boxed answer** with units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Control Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Control Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Control Strategy** is defined as the systematic, planned, and continuous process of monitoring, analyzing, and regulating the operational expenditures of a vehicle fleet to ensure that they remain within pre-determined budgetary limits while simultaneously maintaining or improving service quality, safety standards, and asset longevity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cost control is distinct from **cost reduction**. Cost control means *regulating expenditure to stay within an existing budget*, whereas cost reduction means *actually lowering the baseline cost structure* of operations. A fleet manager deploys cost control strategies across multiple dimensions: fuel economy, preventive maintenance scheduling, tire life-cycle management, driver performance, insurance premiums, depreciation tracking, and route optimization.

The fundamental unit of analysis in fleet cost control is the **Cost Per Kilometer (CPKM)** — expressed in **INR/km** — which aggregates all ownership and operating costs divided by total distance traveled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Household Electricity Bill**
> Imagine your monthly electricity bill. You cannot eliminate the cost of electricity (just as a transport company cannot eliminate fuel), but you can *control* it: by switching to LED bulbs (analogous to **fuel-efficient vehicles**), turning off appliances when not in use (analogous to **idle-time reduction**), servicing your AC regularly (analogous to **preventive maintenance**), and tracking your daily consumption on an app (analogous to **fleet telematics & KPIs**). Cost control is the "switching off" mindset, not the "cutting the wire" mindset.

Think of a fleet as a leaking bucket of water (revenue) where holes represent cost leaks. Cost control is the act of identifying each hole (cost category), measuring its size (KPI), and plugging the largest ones first using targeted strategies.

## 1.3 Core Cost Categories in Fleet Management

| Cost Type | Examples | Behavior with Distance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Costs** | Insurance, Depreciation, Salaries, Road Tax, Permits | Independent of km run |
| **Variable Costs** | Fuel, Tires, Maintenance, Lubricants | Proportional to km run |
| **Semi-Variable Costs** | Driver wages (fixed + per-km), Workshop overheads | Partially variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to classify any given fleet cost as Fixed, Variable, or Semi-Variable — this is a frequently tested 3-mark question in Part A.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Cost Control

- **Cost Per Kilometer (CPKM)** = Total Cost ÷ Total Kilometers
- **Cost Per Tonne-Kilometer (CPTK)** = Total Cost ÷ (Payload × Distance)
- **Cost Per Hour (CPH)** = Total Cost ÷ Total Operating Hours
- **Break-Even Load Factor** = Fixed Costs ÷ (Revenue per Tonne − Variable Cost per Tonne)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cost Control Framework (Six Pillars)

Cost control in fleet management operates through six interlocking strategic pillars:

1. **Fuel Management Strategy** — largest single variable cost (typically 35–45% of operating cost).
2. **Maintenance Management Strategy** — preventive vs breakdown trade-off.
3. **Tire Management Strategy** — second-largest variable cost.
4. **Driver Management Strategy** — training, monitoring, incentive alignment.
5. **Route & Load Optimization** — telematics, GPS, freight consolidation.
6. **Capital Cost Control** — vehicle selection, depreciation policy, replacement timing.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Pillar

### Pillar 1: Fuel Cost Control
- **Why?** Fuel can represent up to **45%** of total operating cost for a commercial truck.
- **How?** Monitor driver behavior (harsh braking, over-revving), enforce idling policy ≤ 2 minutes, optimize routes via GPS, use fuel cards with reporting, schedule tank-level audit weekly.

### Pillar 2: Maintenance Cost Control
- **Why?** Reactive (breakdown) maintenance costs **3 to 4 times** more than preventive maintenance.
- **How?** Implement a **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** schedule based on km/hrs, maintain a Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS), track Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).

### Pillar 3: Tire Cost Control
- **Why?** Tires wear due to speed, load, alignment, and pressure.
- **How?** Maintain recommended cold tire pressure (±2 PSI), rotate every 10,000 km, wheel alignment every 20,000 km, retread 2–3 times using cold/hot process.

### Pillar 4: Driver Cost Control
- **Why?** Driver salaries, overtime, and accident-related costs.
- **How?** Driver training, performance-based pay (₹/km linked to fuel efficiency), telematics dashcams, fatigue management policy.

### Pillar 5: Route & Load Optimization
- **Why?** Empty running ("deadheading") wastes fuel and tires.
- **How?** Backhauling, freight consolidation, route planning software, reduce empty running below **15%**.

### Pillar 6: Capital Cost Control
- **Why?** Vehicle purchase, financing cost, residual value.
- **How?** Right-size vehicles to load, optimal replacement cycle (typically 5–7 years for trucks), use **Straight-Line or Written-Down Value** depreciation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you will need for KTU board exam numericals on this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost Per Kilometer | $\text{CPKM} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{D_{\text{total}}}$ | INR/km | $C_{\text{total}}$ includes fixed + variable |
| 2 | Cost Per Tonne-Km | $\text{CPTK} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{L \times D}$ | INR/(tonne·km) | $L$ = payload in tonnes, $D$ = distance in km |
| 3 | Cost Per Hour | $\text{CPH} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{H_{\text{operational}}}$ | INR/hr | Used for equipment hire decisions |
| 4 | Variable Cost per km | $VC_{\text{km}} = \dfrac{FC_{\text{month}}}{D_{\text{month}}} + VC_{\text{per km}}$ | INR/km | |
| 5 | Break-Even km | $D_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC_{\text{month}}}{R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}}$ | km | $R_{\text{km}}$ = revenue per km |
| 6 | Break-Even Tonnage | $T_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC_{\text{month}}}{R_{\text{tonne}} - VC_{\text{tonne}}}$ | tonnes/month | |
| 7 | Straight-Line Depreciation | $D_{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{P - S}{N}$ | INR/year | $P$=price, $S$=salvage, $N$=life in years |
| 8 | WDV Depreciation | $D_n = (P - \sum D_{\text{prev}}) \times r$ | INR/year | $r$ = WDV rate (e.g., 0.15 for trucks) |
| 9 | Fuel Cost per km | $FC_{\text{km}} = \dfrac{\text{Price/L}}{\text{Mileage (km/L)}}$ | INR/km | |
| 10 | Tire Cost per km | $TC_{\text{km}} = \dfrac{\text{Tire Cost}}{\text{Tire Life (km)}}$ | INR/km | |

> Real-world utility: A logistics company using CPKM benchmarks can compare **own-fleet vs outsourced transport** cost-effectiveness. If outsourced cost < own CPKM, outsourcing is justified beyond break-even km.

## 2.4 Cost Control vs Cost Reduction

| Aspect | Cost Control | Cost Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Stay within budget | Lower the budget baseline |
| Approach | Monitoring, regulation | Eliminating waste, redesign |
| Impact on quality | Maintained | May be affected |
| Example | Negotiating same fuel rate monthly | Switching fleet to EVs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Kilometers Formula

This is a high-yield derivation frequently asked in **Part B (14 marks)** numericals.

**Step 1 — State the Operating Condition**
At the break-even point, total revenue equals total cost. No profit, no loss.

**Step 2 — Express Total Revenue**
If revenue per km is $R_{\text{km}}$ and the vehicle runs $D$ km in the analysis period, then:
$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{km}} \times D$$

**Step 3 — Express Total Cost**
Total cost is the sum of fixed cost and variable cost:
$$C_{\text{total}} = FC + VC_{\text{km}} \times D$$
Here, $FC$ is constant irrespective of distance, and $VC_{\text{km}}$ is the variable cost per km.

**Step 4 — Apply the Break-Even Condition**
Setting revenue equal to cost:
$$R_{\text{km}} \times D = FC + VC_{\text{km}} \times D$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $D$**
Move all $D$ terms to one side:
$$R_{\text{km}} \times D - VC_{\text{km}} \times D = FC$$

**Step 6 — Factor Out $D$**
$$D \times (R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}) = FC$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $D$ (Break-Even km)**
$$D_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}}$$

This is the minimum distance the vehicle must run in the period to cover its fixed costs.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A transport company operates a truck with the following monthly data:
> - Fixed costs per month = ₹80,000
> - Variable cost per km = ₹18
> - Revenue per km = ₹38
> - Total monthly distance operated = 4,000 km
>
> Calculate: (a) Break-Even km, (b) Profit at 4,000 km, (c) CPKM, (d) What happens if distance drops to 2,000 km?

### Solution:

**(a) Break-Even km:**
$$D_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}} = \dfrac{80000}{38 - 18} = \dfrac{80000}{20} = 4000 \text{ km}$$

**Interpretation:** The truck must run at least 4,000 km per month to break even.

**(b) Profit at 4,000 km:**
$$\text{Profit} = (R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}) \times D - FC$$
$$\text{Profit} = (38 - 18) \times 4000 - 80000 = 20 \times 4000 - 80000 = 0$$

So the truck exactly breaks even at 4,000 km.

**(c) Cost Per Kilometer at 4,000 km:**
$$\text{CPKM} = \dfrac{FC + VC_{\text{km}} \times D}{D} = \dfrac{80000 + 18 \times 4000}{4000} = \dfrac{80000 + 72000}{4000} = \dfrac{152000}{4000}$$
$$\text{CPKM} = 38 \text{ INR/km}$$

**(d) At 2,000 km (Below break-even):**
$$\text{Loss} = (R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}) \times D - FC = 20 \times 2000 - 80000 = 40000 - 80000 = -40000$$

The truck incurs a **loss of ₹40,000** per month at 2,000 km operation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity to Cost Drivers

| Cost Driver | % Impact on CPKM (Typical) | Control Strategy | Tool/Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel | 35–45% | Eco-driving, route optimization, fuel cards | L/100km, ₹/km |
| Maintenance | 15–20% | Preventive schedule, CMMS | MTBF, ₹/km |
| Tires | 5–10% | Pressure, alignment, retreading | km/tire-life |
| Driver wages | 10–15% | Training, performance pay | ₹/km linked |
| Insurance | 5–8% | No-claim bonus, fleet safety record | ₹/vehicle/year |
| Depreciation | 10–15% | Right replacement cycle | WDV or SLM |
| Admin & overhead | 5–10% | ERP, automation | ₹/km |

## 3.4 Tire Cost per km Derivation

A tire costs ₹18,000 (new) and runs 60,000 km. It is retreaded twice, each retread adding 30,000 km life at ₹4,500 cost.

**Step 1 — Total life of tire:**
$$L_{\text{tire}} = 60000 + 2 \times 30000 = 120000 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Total cost of tire (purchase + 2 retreads):**
$$C_{\text{tire}} = 18000 + 2 \times 4500 = 27000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Tire cost per km:**
$$TC_{\text{km}} = \dfrac{27000}{120000} = 0.225 \text{ INR/km}$$

**Step 4 — Without retreading:**
$$TC_{\text{km,no-retread}} = \dfrac{18000}{60000} = 0.30 \text{ INR/km}$$

**Cost saving by retreading:** $0.30 - 0.225 = 0.075$ INR/km, i.e., a **25% reduction in tire cost per km** — a powerful illustration of a cost control strategy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Control Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COST CONTROL STRATEGY FRAMEWORK] --> B[FUEL MANAGEMENT]
    A --> C[MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT]
    A --> D[TIRE MANAGEMENT]
    A --> E[DRIVER MANAGEMENT]
    A --> F[ROUTE AND LOAD OPTIMIZATION]
    A --> G[CAPITAL COST CONTROL]

    B --> B1[Driver Eco-Training]
    B --> B2[Idle Time Policy]
    B --> B3[Fuel Card Tracking]
    B --> B4[Route GPS Optimization]

    C --> C1[Preventive Schedule]
    C --> C2[CMMS Software]
    C --> C3[MTBF Tracking]
    C --> C4[Vendor SLA Management]

    D --> D1[Pressure Monitoring]
    D --> D2[Wheel Alignment]
    D --> D3[Retreading Program]
    D --> D4[Tire Rotation Cycle]

    E --> E1[Performance Linked Pay]
    E --> E2[Fatigue Policy]
    E --> E3[Telematics Monitoring]
    E --> E4[Incentive Programs]

    F --> F1[Backhauling]
    F --> F2[Freight Consolidation]
    F --> F3[Empty Run Reduction]
    F --> F4[Multi-Stop Planning]

    G --> G1[Right Vehicle Sizing]
    G --> G2[Optimal Replacement Cycle]
    G --> G3[Depreciation Policy Choice]
    G --> G4[Residual Value Maximization]
```

## 4.2 Cost Classification & Behavior Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TOTAL FLEET COST] --> B[FIXED COSTS]
    A --> C[VARIABLE COSTS]
    A --> D[SEMI-VARIABLE COSTS]

    B --> B1[Insurance]
    B --> B2[Depreciation]
    B --> B3[Road Tax and Permits]
    B --> B4[Admin Salaries]

    C --> C1[Fuel]
    C --> C2[Tires]
    C --> C3[Lubricants]
    C --> C4[Distance Based Maintenance]

    D --> D1[Driver Wages Basic]
    D --> D2[Workshop Overheads]
    D --> D3[Telematics Subscription]
```

## 4.3 Break-Even Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Analysis] --> B[Identify FC and VC per km]
    B --> C[Determine R per km]
    C --> D{Is D operated greater than D_BE?}
    D -->|Yes| E[PROFIT ZONE]
    D -->|No| F[LOSS ZONE]
    D -->|Equal| G[BREAK EVEN POINT]
    E --> H[Recommend Continue or Expand]
    F --> I[Reduce FC or Increase Utilization]
    G --> J[Monitor Marginal Sensitivity]
```

## 4.4 KPI Monitoring Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plan Budget] --> B[Deploy Operations]
    B --> C[Collect Telematics Data]
    C --> D[Compute CPKM CPTK CPH]
    D --> E{Variance vs Budget}
    E -->|Within 5 percent| F[Continue]
    E -->|Above 5 percent| G[Root Cause Analysis]
    G --> H[Corrective Action]
    H --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define cost control and explain how it differs from cost reduction in the context of fleet management.**

**Model Answer:**
Cost control is the systematic process of monitoring and regulating fleet expenditure to keep it within pre-approved budgets without compromising service quality. It involves establishing benchmarks, tracking actuals, and taking corrective action when variance arises. Cost reduction, in contrast, is a structural exercise aimed at permanently lowering the cost base by eliminating non-value-adding activities, redesigning processes, or adopting new technology (e.g., EVs). Cost control is a *regulatory* activity; cost reduction is a *transformational* activity.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the major components of total fleet cost and classify each as fixed or variable.**

**Model Answer:**
The major components are: Fuel (Variable), Tires (Variable), Maintenance (Variable), Driver wages (Semi-variable), Insurance (Fixed), Depreciation (Fixed), Road tax and permits (Fixed), Administrative overhead (Fixed), Lubricants (Variable). Fixed costs are independent of distance; variable costs scale linearly with km operated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *memorize formulas but forget units*. Always write units (INR/km, km, tonnes) with every numerical answer. Also, never skip the **break-even condition statement** (Revenue = Total Cost) before substituting values. Marks are awarded for clearly identifying the cost type before applying the formula.

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **cost control strategies** applicable to a commercial fleet, with special reference to fuel, maintenance, tire, and driver management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A truck has the following data per month:
- Fixed cost = ₹1,20,000
- Variable cost per km = ₹25
- Revenue per km = ₹55

Calculate: (i) Break-even km per month, (ii) Profit at 5,000 km, (iii) CPKM at 5,000 km, (iv) Loss if operation drops to 2,000 km. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Fuel Cost Control Strategy:**
Fuel is the single largest variable cost in a fleet. Strategies include: (i) driver eco-driving training to reduce harsh acceleration and braking; (ii) enforcing an idling policy (engine off after 2 minutes of idle); (iii) use of GPS-based route optimization to minimize distance; (iv) adoption of fuel cards with detailed MIS reporting to detect pilferage; (v) regular calibration of fuel tanks and pumps; (vi) speed governance to ≤60 km/h on highways; (vii) aerodynamic modifications (side skirts, trailer tails) for long-haul trucks.

**Maintenance Cost Control Strategy:**
Maintenance is governed by the trade-off between preventive and breakdown maintenance. Strategies include: (i) implementing a Preventive Maintenance (PM) schedule based on km/hrs; (ii) using CMMS software to track each vehicle's service history; (iii) tracking Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) to identify chronic failure vehicles; (iv) entering into Annual Maintenance Contracts (AMC) with OEM-authorized workshops; (v) maintaining a critical spares inventory at regional hubs.

**Tire Cost Control Strategy:**
Tires are the second-largest variable cost. Strategies include: (i) maintaining cold tire pressure within ±2 PSI of OEM recommendation; (ii) wheel balancing and alignment every 20,000 km; (iii) tire rotation every 10,000 km; (iv) retreading 2–3 times to extend life; (v) selecting tire brand based on **Cost Per Kilometer** rather than purchase price alone.

**Driver Cost Control Strategy:**
Drivers are central to cost outcomes. Strategies include: (i) performance-linked pay tied to fuel efficiency and on-time delivery; (ii) telematics dashcams to monitor harsh events; (iii) fatigue management policy (max 8 hours driving); (iv) incentive for accident-free driving; (v) regular refresher training on eco-driving and defensive driving.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Break-even km:**
$$D_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}} = \dfrac{120000}{55 - 25} = \dfrac{120000}{30} = 4000 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Profit at 5,000 km:**
$$\text{Profit} = (R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}) \times D - FC = 30 \times 5000 - 120000 = 150000 - 120000 = 30000 \text{ INR}$$

**(iii) CPKM at 5,000 km:**
$$\text{CPKM} = \dfrac{FC + VC_{\text{km}} \times D}{D} = \dfrac{120000 + 25 \times 5000}{5000} = \dfrac{120000 + 125000}{5000} = \dfrac{245000}{5000} = 49 \text{ INR/km}$$

**(iv) Loss at 2,000 km:**
$$\text{Loss} = (R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}) \times D - FC = 30 \times 2000 - 120000 = 60000 - 120000 = -60000 \text{ INR (Loss)}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the role of **break-even analysis** as a cost control tool in fleet operations. Derive the break-even km formula and explain its managerial significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transport company is evaluating two vehicle options:
- **Vehicle X:** Fixed cost ₹1,50,000/month, Variable cost ₹20/km, Revenue ₹60/km
- **Vehicle Y:** Fixed cost ₹90,000/month, Variable cost ₹32/km, Revenue ₹60/km

Determine the **indifference point** (where total cost of both is equal) and advise which vehicle to choose for a monthly run of 6,000 km. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Role of Break-Even Analysis:** Break-even analysis is a fundamental cost control tool. It tells the fleet manager the **minimum utilization** (in km or tonnes) required to avoid losses. It is also used for: (i) evaluating make-or-buy (own vs outsource) decisions; (ii) setting realistic sales targets; (iii) pricing freight quotations; (iv) assessing impact of fuel price hikes on minimum viable load; (v) justifying capacity expansion.

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes.) The break-even km formula is:
$$D_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}}}$$

**Managerial Significance:** The denominator $(R_{\text{km}} - VC_{\text{km}})$ is the **contribution margin per km**. A higher contribution margin lowers the break-even km, providing a wider safety cushion. Managers track this metric monthly. If fuel prices rise and $VC_{\text{km}}$ increases, $D_{\text{BE}}$ rises, signalling the need to revise rates or reduce fixed overhead.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Indifference Point (Total Cost of X = Total Cost of Y):**
$$FC_X + VC_X \times D = FC_Y + VC_Y \times D$$
$$150000 + 20D = 90000 + 32D$$
$$150000 - 90000 = 32D - 20D$$
$$60000 = 12D$$
$$D_{\text{indifference}} = \dfrac{60000}{12} = 5000 \text{ km}$$

**Decision at 6,000 km:**

Total cost of X at 6,000 km:
$$C_X = 150000 + 20 \times 6000 = 150000 + 120000 = 270000 \text{ INR}$$

Total cost of Y at 6,000 km:
$$C_Y = 90000 + 32 \times 6000 = 90000 + 192000 = 282000 \text{ INR}$$

Since $C_X < C_Y$ at 6,000 km, **choose Vehicle X** (higher fixed cost but lower variable cost is preferable for higher utilization).

**Verification using revenue:** Revenue at 6,000 km = 60 × 6000 = 360,000 INR. Profit X = 360000 − 270000 = 90,000 INR. Profit Y = 360000 − 282000 = 78,000 INR. Vehicle X yields higher profit by ₹12,000/month.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Cost Control** = regulate to stay in budget; **Cost Reduction** = lower the baseline.
- ✅ Costs are classified as **Fixed, Variable, Semi-Variable**.
- ✅ **CPKM = Total Cost / Total km** is the master KPI of fleet cost control.
- ✅ **CPTK = Total Cost / (Payload × km)** for goods transport.
- ✅ **Break-Even km = FC / (R per km − VC per km)** — most tested formula.
- ✅ Fuel ≈ 35–45% of total operating cost — first target of any cost control program.
- ✅ Preventive maintenance saves 3–4× cost of breakdown maintenance.
- ✅ Retreading can reduce tire cost per km by up to **25%**.
- ✅ Driver behaviour can swing fuel economy by **±20%**.
- ✅ Empty running (deadheading) must be kept below **15%**.
- ✅ **Depreciation**: SLM for simplicity, WDV for tax efficiency. Know the formula for both.
- ✅ **Indifference Point** (where two options have equal total cost) is derived by setting $C_X = C_Y$.
- ✅ Always quote **units** (INR/km, km/month) in numerical answers.
- ✅ In Part B, structure your answer as: Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Result → Interpretation.

> **Final Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE pattern, examiners allocate marks for **classification of costs (2 marks)**, **formula statement (2 marks)**, **substitution (3 marks)**, and **final answer with units + interpretation (rest)**. Always end numericals with a one-line managerial interpretation (e.g., *"Hence, the truck must run at least 4,000 km/month to break even."*).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fleet Budgeting** is the systematic process of forecasting, allocating, and controlling the financial resources required for the acquisition, operation, maintenance, and replacement of a vehicle fleet over a defined planning horizon (typically **1 year** for operating budgets and **3–10 years** for capital plans). It is a subset of corporate financial planning tailored to transportation assets.

**Financial Planning for Fleets** is the strategic discipline of aligning fleet expenditure with business objectives, optimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**, ensuring liquidity for operational continuity, and maximizing the **return on fleet assets** while complying with statutory and taxation frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A fleet manager who cannot quantify cost cannot control cost. Budgeting converts physical decisions (which truck to buy, when to retire a bus) into financial decisions (capital outlay, depreciation stream, salvage value).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Purse vs. The Fleet Wallet"

Imagine a middle-class Kerala family managing its monthly household budget. The father knows:
- The **house EMI** (analogous to **vehicle loan EMI / capital cost recovery**)
- The **electricity + gas bills** (analogous to **fuel and lubricants**)
- The **school fees** (analogous to **driver wages and statutory contributions**)
- The **servicing of the two-wheeler** (analogous to **preventive maintenance budget**)
- The **insurance premium** paid once a year (analogous to **fleet insurance**)
- A small **sinking fund** saved monthly to replace the scooter in 5 years (analogous to a **vehicle replacement reserve fund**)

A fleet of 50 trucks or 100 buses is **exactly the same logic**—just scaled up by a factor of 50–100. The only difference is that a fleet uses **formal accounting systems, depreciation schedules, and variance reports** instead of a notebook on the kitchen shelf.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics in Fleet Finance

| # | Metric | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|--------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Ownership | **TCO** | Full life-cycle cost |
| 2 | Cost per Kilometer | **CPK** | Operating efficiency |
| 3 | Depreciation | **D** | Asset value erosion |
| 4 | Net Present Value | **NPV** | Investment worthiness |
| 5 | Fleet Utilization | **U** | Asset productivity |
| 6 | Budget Variance | **ΔB** | Plan vs. Actual |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Benchmarks (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Volvo Fleet Studies):**
> - TCO of a commercial truck: **₹85–110 per km**
> - TCO of a staff bus: **₹45–60 per km**
> - Ideal fleet utilization: **> 85%**
> - Acceptable fuel share of TCO: **35–45%**

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Fleet Financial Planning

1. **Capital Budgeting** — Acquisition financing, loan structuring, depreciation policy.
2. **Operating Budgeting** — Recurring monthly/annual costs (fuel, tyres, wages, toll).
3. **Replacement Planning** — Optimal life-cycle, salvage value, and replacement reserve fund.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fleet Costs

Fleet costs are classified along three axes:

### A) By Behaviour Pattern
- **Fixed Costs (FC):** Do not vary with kilometres run. Examples: insurance premium, road tax, driver salary (basic), EMI, depreciation.
- **Variable Costs (VC):** Vary directly with kilometres. Examples: fuel, tyres, maintenance consumables.
- **Semi-Variable Costs:** Have a fixed base plus a variable component. Examples: workshop overhead, mobile mechanic charges.

### B) By Function
- **Standing Costs:** Vehicle-related costs incurred whether the vehicle moves or not (insurance, depreciation, garaging, wages).
- **Running Costs:** Costs incurred only when the vehicle operates (fuel, tyres, road wear, toll).

### C) By Time Horizon
- **Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** One-time acquisition cost.
- **Revenue Expenditure (OpEx):** Recurring day-to-day costs.

## 2.2 The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Framework

The TCO formula over a vehicle's economic life of **N years** is:

$$
\text{TCO} = C_{\text{acq}} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \left( C_{\text{fuel},t} + C_{\text{maint},t} + C_{\text{wages},t} + C_{\text{ins},t} + C_{\text{tax},t} + C_{\text{tyre},t} + C_{\text{toll},t} \right) - S_{\text{salvage}}
$$

Where each cost component is **discounted to present value** if the planning horizon exceeds one year. Working drivers (the ones with capital letters) sit inside the **sum of operating costs**, and $C_{\text{acq}}$ is the **acquisition cost inclusive of taxes, registration, and fitments**.

## 2.3 Depreciation — The Invisible but Largest Cost

Depreciation is the **systematic allocation of the depreciable cost of an asset over its useful life**. For fleets, three methods are commercially relevant:

### (i) Straight-Line Method (SLM)

$$
D_t = \frac{C_{\text{acq}} - S}{N}
$$

Where $C_{\text{acq}}$ is acquisition cost, $S$ is salvage value, and $N$ is life in years. Annual depreciation is **constant**.

### (ii) Written Down Value / Declining Balance Method (WDV)

$$
D_t = R \times BV_{t-1}
$$

Where $R$ is the depreciation rate (commonly **15% for commercial vehicles, 30% for IT equipment** under Indian Companies Act 2013, Schedule II) and $BV_{t-1}$ is the book value at the end of the previous year. Annual depreciation **declines** with age.

### (iii) Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method (SYD)

$$
D_t = \frac{N - t + 1}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} k} \times (C_{\text{acq}} - S)
$$

The denominator $\sum_{k=1}^{N} k = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$. This is **accelerated depreciation**—larger deductions in early life, matching the actual wear pattern of vehicles.

## 2.4 Cost Per Kilometer (CPK) — The Most-Watched KPI

$$
\text{CPK} = \frac{\text{Total Monthly Fleet Cost (₹)}}{\text{Total Kilometres Run in the Month (km)}}
$$

The TCO is then the area under the CPK-vs-time curve integrated over the life of the vehicle.

## 2.5 Vehicle Replacement Decision (N-1 vs N year analysis)

To decide whether to replace a vehicle at year **n**, the fleet manager compares:

$$
\text{Avg Annual Cost}_{n} = \frac{BV_n + \sum_{t=1}^{n} O_t}{n}
$$

where $BV_n$ is the unrecovered capital at year $n$ and $O_t$ is the operating cost in year $t$. The optimal replacement year is the **minimum point** of this curve.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D_t = (C - S)/N$ | Straight-line depreciation | ₹/year |
| 2 | $D_t = R \cdot BV_{t-1}$ | WDV depreciation | ₹/year |
| 3 | $D_t = \dfrac{N - t + 1}{N(N+1)/2} \cdot (C-S)$ | Sum-of-years'-digits | ₹/year |
| 4 | $\text{CPK} = \text{TC} / \text{km}$ | Cost per km | ₹/km |
| 5 | $\text{TCO} = C_{\text{acq}} + \sum \text{OpCost} - S$ | Life-cycle cost | ₹ |
| 6 | $A_t = (BV_n + \sum O_t) / n$ | Average annual cost (replacement) | ₹/year |
| 7 | $\text{NPV} = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Investment decision | ₹ |
| 8 | $\text{IRR}: \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = I_0$ | Discount rate at NPV=0 | % |
| 9 | $\text{Utilization} = \text{Actual km} / \text{Available km}$ | Asset productivity | % |
| 10 | $\Delta B = \text{Actual} - \text{Budgeted}$ | Budget variance | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A transport manager presenting a ₹12-crore fleet expansion proposal to a bank uses **NPV and IRR**, not gut feeling. CPK is the language of daily operations; NPV/IRR is the language of the boardroom.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Depreciation under All Three Methods

A fleet operator purchases a tipper truck for **₹30,00,000** with an estimated useful life of **5 years** and a salvage value of **₹6,00,000**. Calculate annual depreciation under SLM, WDV (rate = 20%), and SYD.

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{acq}} = ₹30,00,000$
- $S = ₹6,00,000$
- $N = 5$ years
- $R_{\text{WDV}} = 20\%$

**Depreciable base** = $C - S = 30,00,000 - 6,00,000 = ₹24,00,000$

### (A) Straight-Line Method

$$
D_{\text{SLM}} = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{5} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{5} = ₹4{,}80{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Book value trajectory (SLM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_0 &= 30{,}00{,}000 \\
BV_1 &= 30{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 25{,}20{,}000 \\
BV_2 &= 25{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 20{,}40{,}000 \\
BV_3 &= 20{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 15{,}60{,}000 \\
BV_4 &= 15{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 10{,}80{,}000 \\
BV_5 &= 10{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \quad (=S)
\end{aligned}
$$

### (B) Written Down Value Method (Rate = 20%)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 0.20 \times 30{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000; \quad BV_1 = 24{,}00{,}000 \\
D_2 &= 0.20 \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000; \quad BV_2 = 19{,}20{,}000 \\
D_3 &= 0.20 \times 19{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}84{,}000; \quad BV_3 = 15{,}36{,}000 \\
D_4 &= 0.20 \times 15{,}36{,}000 = 3{,}07{,}200; \quad BV_4 = 12{,}28{,}800 \\
D_5 &= 0.20 \times 12{,}28{,}800 = 2{,}45{,}760; \quad BV_5 = 9{,}83{,}040
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: WDV never reaches zero in theory; it asymptotically approaches zero, but the company **stops depreciation** when $BV_t$ falls below the salvage value.

### (C) Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method

Sum of digits = $\frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \frac{5}{15} \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \\
D_2 &= \frac{4}{15} \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}40{,}000 \\
D_3 &= \frac{3}{15} \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \\
D_4 &= \frac{2}{15} \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}20{,}000 \\
D_5 &= \frac{1}{15} \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \\
\hline
\text{Total} &= 24{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparative Insight:** SLM gives a tax shield of ₹4.80 L/year; WDV gives a much higher first-year shield (₹6.00 L) — preferred for cash-strapped operators; SYD gives the highest first-year shield (₹8.00 L) — preferred for aggressive tax planning.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost Per Kilometer Calculation

A logistics company operates 10 trucks. In July 2025, the following data is observed:

| Cost Head | Amount (₹) |
|-----------|-----------:|
| Diesel (1,20,000 L @ ₹92) | 1,10,40,000 |
| Driver wages | 4,50,000 |
| Tyres (replacements) | 1,20,000 |
| Routine maintenance | 80,000 |
| Insurance (monthly share) | 60,000 |
| Road tax + permit | 25,000 |
| Toll + parking | 95,000 |
| Depreciation | 3,00,000 |
| Office overhead allocation | 70,000 |
| **Total** | **12,40,000** |

Total km run by the fleet = **85,000 km**

$$
\text{CPK} = \frac{12{,}40{,}000}{85{,}000} = ₹14.59 \text{ per km}
$$

If the customer contract is at **₹18/km**, contribution margin = $18 - 14.59 = ₹3.41/km$ (≈ **19% margin**).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Vehicle Replacement Decision

A vehicle has the following year-wise operating costs and book values (in ₹ thousands):

| Year (n) | Book Value at Start $BV_n$ | Operating Cost $O_t$ | Cumulative $O$ | Average Annual Cost |
|---------:|--------------------------:|--------------------:|---------------:|--------------------:|
| 1 | 1,000 | 200 | 200 | 1,200 |
| 2 | 850 | 220 | 420 | 1,270 |
| 3 | 700 | 260 | 680 | 1,380 |
| 4 | 550 | 320 | 1,000 | 1,550 |
| 5 | 400 | 400 | 1,400 | 1,800 |
| 6 | 250 | 510 | 1,910 | 2,160 |

The **minimum average annual cost** occurs at **Year 2 (₹1,270 × 10³)**. Therefore, the optimal replacement year is **end of Year 2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{1{,}000 + 200}{1} = 1{,}200 \\
A_2 &= \frac{850 + 200 + 220}{2} = \frac{1{,}270}{2} = 635 \quad \text{(wait, recalculate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction** — using the correct textbook formula $A_t = (BV_n + \sum_{i=1}^{n} O_i)/n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= (1{,}000 + 200)/1 = 1{,}200 \\
A_2 &= (850 + 200 + 220)/2 = 1{,}270/2 = 635 \\
A_3 &= (700 + 680)/3 = 1{,}380/3 = 460 \\
A_4 &= (550 + 1{,}000)/4 = 1{,}550/4 = 387.5 \\
A_5 &= (400 + 1{,}400)/5 = 1{,}800/5 = 360 \quad \leftarrow \text{minimum} \\
A_6 &= (250 + 1{,}910)/6 = 2{,}160/6 = 360 \quad \leftarrow \text{tied}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tie-break rule: replace at the **earlier** year where minimum first occurs ⇒ **Year 5**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Depreciation Method

| Criterion | SLM | WDV | SYD |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Ease of computation | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Tax shield in early years | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Matches actual wear | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Acceptable under Ind AS / Companies Act | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (for specific assets) |
| Useful for fleet trucks | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** Indian fleet operators overwhelmingly use **WDV (20% for general commercial vehicles)** because it matches the actual wear pattern of trucks/buses and provides front-loaded tax relief.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fleet Budget Allocation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLEET MASTER BUDGET] --> B[CAPITAL BUDGET CAPEX]
    A --> C[OPERATING BUDGET OPEX]
    A --> D[REPLACEMENT RESERVE FUND]
    A --> E[CONTINGENCY RESERVE 5 to 10 percent]

    B --> B1[New Vehicle Acquisition]
    B --> B2[Workshop Equipment]
    B --> B3[IT and Telematics Systems]

    C --> C1[Fuel and Lubricants 30 to 40 percent]
    C --> C2[Driver Wages and Statutory 18 to 22 percent]
    C --> C3[Maintenance and Spares 12 to 18 percent]
    C --> C4[Tyres and Batteries 6 to 8 percent]
    C --> C5[Insurance Road Tax Permits 8 to 12 percent]
    C --> C6[Toll and Parking 4 to 6 percent]
    C --> C7[Depreciation 10 to 15 percent]

    D --> D1[Annual Sinking Fund Provision]
    D --> D2[Inflation Adjusted Salvage Target]

    E --> E1[Accident Repair Buffer]
    E --> E2[Major Overhaul Buffer]
    E --> E3[Sudden Regulatory Cost]
```

## 4.2 The Fleet Financial Planning Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Define Objectives] --> S2[Step 2 Forecast Demand]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Estimate Costs]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Allocate Budget]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Approve and Release Funds]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Monitor Actual vs Budget]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Variance Analysis]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Corrective Action]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Replacement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Vehicle Age and Condition} --> Q2{Compare Annual Cost A sub n}
    Q2 --> R1[If A sub n gt A sub n-1 then REPLACE]
    Q2 --> R2[If A sub n lt A sub n-1 then RETAIN]
    R1 --> T1[Calculate NPV of New Purchase]
    R2 --> T2[Continue Operation]
    T1 --> T3{Investment Justified}
    T3 -->|Yes| T4[Procure and Retire Old Unit]
    T3 -->|No| T5[Extend Life with Major Overhaul]
```

## 4.4 Total Cost of Ownership Composition Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TCO[Total Cost of Ownership TCO] --> CAP[Capital Cost]
    TCO --> OPR[Operating Cost]
    TCO --> SAL[Less Salvage Value]

    CAP --> CAP1[Vehicle Price]
    CAP --> CAP2[Taxes and Registration]
    CAP --> CAP3[Body Building and Fitments]

    OPR --> OPR1[Fuel]
    OPR --> OPR2[Driver Cost]
    OPR --> OPR3[Maintenance]
    OPR --> OPR4[Insurance]
    OPR --> OPR5[Tyres]
    OPR --> OPR6[Toll and Permits]
    OPR --> OPR7[Depreciation]
    OPR --> OPR8[Overhead Allocation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**"Define Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for a fleet. List any six cost components included in TCO."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TCO is the sum of all costs incurred to acquire, operate, and dispose of a vehicle over its entire economic life, **discounted to present value** where the horizon exceeds one year.

Six components:
1. **Acquisition cost** — vehicle price, taxes, registration, fitments.
2. **Fuel and lubricants cost.**
3. **Driver wages and statutory benefits.**
4. **Maintenance, spares, and tyres.**
5. **Insurance, road tax, and permits.**
6. **Depreciation**, less the **salvage value** at end of life.

*(Award 1 mark for definition; 0.5 mark × 4 components = 2 marks for list. 1 mark for a clean conclusion.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retained Pattern]**
**"Differentiate between Capital Budget and Operating Budget for a fleet. Give one example for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Capital Budget | Operating Budget |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Nature | One-time, long-term | Recurring, short-term |
| Horizon | 3–10 years | 1 year or less |
| Approval | Board / Senior Mgmt | Department Head |
| Example | Buying 10 new buses for ₹4 Cr | Monthly diesel expense of ₹12 L |
| Accounting treatment | Capitalized, depreciated | Expensed in the period |

*(1.5 marks for distinction; 1 mark for examples; 0.5 mark for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**  *(Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Set-A Pattern]**

**(a)** A transport company buys a commercial vehicle for **₹25,00,000**. Estimated useful life is **6 years** with a salvage value of **₹4,00,000**. Compute annual depreciation and the book value at the end of each year using the **Straight-Line Method**. Prepare a depreciation schedule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Sum-of-Years'-Digits (SYD) method** of depreciation. Using the same data as in part (a), compute depreciation for each of the 6 years and the book value at the end of year 3. Comment on why the SYD method is preferred for fleet vehicles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks]

**Given:** $C = ₹25,00,000$, $S = ₹4,00,000$, $N = 6$ years.

**Step 1 — Depreciable base:**
$$
C - S = 25{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹21{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Annual SLM depreciation:**
$$
D_{\text{SLM}} = \frac{21{,}00{,}000}{6} = ₹3{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Depreciation schedule:**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|-----:|---------------:|-----------------:|---------------:|
| 1 | 25,00,000 | 3,50,000 | 21,50,000 |
| 2 | 21,50,000 | 3,50,000 | 18,00,000 |
| 3 | 18,00,000 | 3,50,000 | 14,50,000 |
| 4 | 14,50,000 | 3,50,000 | 11,00,000 |
| 5 | 11,00,000 | 3,50,000 | 7,50,000 |
| 6 | 7,50,000 | 3,50,000 | 4,00,000 *(=S)* |

*(Award 1 mark for formula; 1.5 for depreciation value; 2 for table; 0.5 for verifying salvage; 2 for presentation/clarity.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
SYD is an accelerated depreciation method where the depreciable base is multiplied by a fraction whose numerator is the **remaining life at the start of the year** and denominator is the **sum of years' digits**. It assigns higher depreciation to early years, matching the steep actual wear and tear of new fleet vehicles and providing larger tax shields early.

**Step 2 — Sum of digits:**
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{6} k = \frac{6 \times 7}{2} = 21
$$

**Step 3 — Year-wise SYD depreciation:**

| Year | Numerator (N − t + 1) | Fraction | Depreciation = (C − S) × Fraction (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|-----:|:---------------------:|:--------:|--------------------------------------:|---------------:|
| 1 | 6 | 6/21 | 6,00,000 | 19,00,000 |
| 2 | 5 | 5/21 | 5,00,000 | 14,00,000 |
| 3 | 4 | 4/21 | 4,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| 4 | 3 | 3/21 | 3,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| 5 | 2 | 2/21 | 2,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 6 | 1 | 1/21 | 1,00,000 | 4,00,000 |

**Step 4 — Book value at end of Year 3 = ₹10,00,000.**

**Step 5 — Why SYD is preferred for fleets (1 mark):**
New fleet vehicles experience the steepest reliability decline and highest warranty costs in year 1; SYD matches this pattern, maximises early tax savings, and provides a realistic book-value trajectory for insurance and resale valuation.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**  *(Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Set-B Pattern]**

**(a)** What is a **Cost per Kilometer (CPK)** analysis? A fleet of 25 buses runs a total of **1,80,000 km** in a month. Monthly operating cost details are: Fuel = ₹28,00,000; Driver wages = ₹8,50,000; Maintenance = ₹3,20,000; Tyres = ₹1,40,000; Insurance = ₹90,000; Depreciation = ₹4,50,000; Toll + Tax = ₹1,20,000. Compute the CPK and interpret the result against a contract rate of **₹22/km**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vehicle replacement decision** using the concept of **average annual cost (minimum cost method)**. A truck has the following data:

| Year (n) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|----------:|--:|--:|--:|--:|--:|--:|
| Book value at start (₹ × 10³) | 1,200 | 1,000 | 800 | 600 | 400 | 200 |
| Operating cost (₹ × 10³) | 180 | 220 | 280 | 360 | 470 | 620 |

Find the **optimal replacement year** and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept (1.5 marks):**
CPK = $\frac{\text{Total Operating Cost (₹)}}{\text{Total Distance Run (km)}}$. It is the most-watched operational KPI in fleet management as it enables benchmarking, route profitability analysis, and contract pricing.

**Step 2 — Total monthly cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TC} &= 28{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 \\
&\quad + 90{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \\
&= ₹47{,}70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CPK computation:**
$$
\text{CPK} = \frac{47{,}70{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = ₹26.50 \text{ per km}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
CPK = ₹26.50/km > contract rate of ₹22/km ⇒ **Loss of ₹4.50 per km**. On 1,80,000 km, the company bleeds ₹8,10,000 per month. The manager must either renegotiate the contract rate, optimize fuel efficiency, reduce toll/tax, or withdraw the contract.

*(Mark split: 1.5 concept + 2 computation + 1.5 interpretation + 2 presentation.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Theory (2 marks):**
The replacement decision is taken by computing the **average annual cost** at the end of each year:

$$
A_n = \frac{BV_n + \sum_{t=1}^{n} O_t}{n}
$$

The optimal replacement year corresponds to the **minimum $A_n$**. If two years tie, replace at the **earlier** year.

**Step 2 — Build the table:**

| n | $BV_n$ (₹ × 10³) | $\sum O_t$ (₹ × 10³) | Total = $BV_n + \sum O_t$ | $A_n = \text{Total}/n$ |
|--:|-----------------:|----------------------:|---------------------------:|-----------------------:|
| 1 | 1,200 | 180 | 1,380 | 1,380.0 |
| 2 | 1,000 | 400 | 1,400 | **700.0** |
| 3 | 800 | 680 | 1,480 | 493.3 |
| 4 | 600 | 1,040 | 1,640 | 410.0 |
| 5 | 400 | 1,510 | 1,910 | **382.0** ← minimum |
| 6 | 200 | 2,130 | 2,330 | 388.3 |

**Step 3 — Optimal decision (1.5 marks):**
The minimum average annual cost is at **Year 5 (₹382 × 10³)**, which is lower than Year 6 (₹388.3 × 10³). Therefore, **replace the vehicle at the end of Year 5**.

**Step 4 — Justification (1.5 marks):**
Beyond Year 5, the rapidly rising maintenance and repair costs (₹470 k → ₹620 k) outweigh the gain in capital recovery, signaling mechanical obsolescence. Replacing at Year 5 also brings in fuel-efficient and lower-emission technology, improving the firm's sustainability index.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract salvage value** in SLM — guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.
> 2. **Writing BV as 0 in the last year of WDV** — book value never reaches zero mathematically; stop when $BV = S$.
> 3. **Mixing up "rate" with "percentage"** in WDV — the rate is applied to the **opening** book value of the year, not the original cost (except in Year 1).
> 4. **In CPK, forgetting to include depreciation** — students often omit it thinking it is a non-cash item, but it is a real economic cost and must be included.
> 5. **In replacement analysis, using *Total Cost* instead of *Average Annual Cost*** — the method is explicitly minimum-cost, not total-cost minimization.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **TCO** = Acquisition + Σ Operating Costs − Salvage. Discount to PV if horizon > 1 year.
- **Three depreciation methods:** SLM (constant), WDV (declining, 20% rate for trucks), SYD (accelerated).
- **Sum of years' digits** = $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}$.
- **CPK** = Total Cost / Total km. Compare against contract rate to gauge margin.
- **Fixed vs Variable:** Insurance is fixed; fuel is variable; wages are semi-variable.
- **Replacement rule:** Replace at the year of **minimum average annual cost** $A_n = (BV_n + \sum O_t)/n$.
- **NPV and IRR** are the boardroom metrics for fleet expansion decisions; **CPK and utilization** are the daily-operations metrics.
- **Budget variance** $\Delta B = \text{Actual} - \text{Budgeted}$. Investigate variances beyond ±5%.
- **Ideal fleet utilization** is **> 85%**; below this, fixed cost per km rises sharply.
- **Fuel** is typically 30–40% of TCO — the single largest controllable cost head.
- **Replacement reserve fund (sinking fund)** is a non-negotiable for any fleet above 10 vehicles.
- **Statutory reference:** Schedule II of the **Companies Act 2013** for useful-life-based depreciation; **Motor Vehicles Act 1988** for taxation permits; **GST Act 2017** for ITC on fleet expenses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Considerations – Environmental Impact of Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Environmental Considerations – Environmental Impact of Fleets # Module 4 — Environmental Considerations: Environmental Impact of Fleets ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Environmental Impact of Fleets** refers to the aggregate set of direct, indirect, and cumulative effects that a group of commercial or institutional vehicles (the *fleet*) exerts on the natural environment throughout its operational life-cycle. These effects span **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions**, **regulated air pollutants (NOx, PM, CO, HC)**, **noise pollution**, **soil/water contamination**, and **resource depletion**, all of which are systematically quantified and mitigated under the discipline of **Sustainable Fleet Management**. In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework (mapped to **CO4: Analyse environmental and economic implications of fleet operations**), environmental impact is treated as a *systems-engineering problem*, where every vehicle is a node whose tailpipe, lifecycle, and disposal footprint contribute to the fleet's aggregate environmental load. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Block" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a fleet of 50 trucks as an *apartment block* with 50 families. One family leaving the balcony light on is trivial. But 50 families leaving lights, ACs, and water pumps running 16 hours a day becomes a serious **load on the city's power grid** and produces a measurable **carbon shadow over the neighbourhood**. Similarly, one truck's exhaust is negligible; a coordinated fleet of hundreds produces a quantifiable *environmental shadow* — affecting air quality (AQI), climate (CO₂), and public health. **Core Environmental Burden Metrics (Industry-Standard Bold Constants):** | Pollutant / Metric | Standard Reference Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** | **2.68 kg CO₂ / litre of Diesel** | Primary GHG, climate forcing | | **CO₂ (Gasoline)** | **2.31 kg CO₂ / litre of Petrol** | Primary GHG, climate forcing | | **NOx (Nitrogen Oxides)** | **0.6 – 1.5 g/km (diesel, pre-BS-VI)** | Smog, respiratory illness | | **PM2.5 / PM10 (Particulate Matter)** | **0.005 – 0.05 g/km** | Lung damage, cancer risk | | **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** | **0.5 – 2.0 g/km** | Toxic, hemoglobin binder | | **HC (Hydrocarbons)** | **0.1 – 0.3 g/km** | Photochemical smog precursor | | **Noise (dB)** | **70 – 85 dB(A) at 7 m** | Urban noise pollution | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 OEMUT835 syllabus (Module 4), students must distinguish between **Tailpipe Emissions** (TTW — Tank-to-Wheel), **Well-to-Tank** (WTT) emissions from fuel production, and the combined **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** lifecycle impact. This WTW perspective is what differentiates a textbook answer from a board-exam top-scoring answer. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Fleet Environmental Impacts The environmental impact of fleets is classified along **four impact axes**: ### Axis A — Atmospheric / Air Quality Impacts - **Greenhouse Gases (GHG):** CO₂, CH₄, N₂O — quantified as **CO₂-equivalent (CO₂e)** using Global Warming Potential (GWP) multipliers. - **Criteria Air Pollutants (CAPs):** NOx, CO, SO₂, PM, HC — regulated under **BS-VI / Euro VI** emission norms. - **Photochemical Smog:** Ground-level ozone (O₃) formation from NOx + HC under sunlight. ### Axis B — Acoustic / Noise Pollution - Source: Engine combustion, tyre–road interaction, aerodynamic noise. - Regulated by **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR), India** — **84 dB(A)** limit for heavy commercial vehicles at **7 metres**. ### Axis C — Soil & Water Contamination - Oil/coolant leaks during operation, washing effluent, brake dust (heavy metals: Cu, Pb, Zn). - End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) disposal under **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016**. ### Axis D — Resource Depletion & Embedded Energy - Manufacturing energy (vehicle embodied energy ~ **20–25 tonnes CO₂e per medium truck**). - Non-renewable fuel consumption: ~ **1 barrel of crude ≈ 117 kg of CO₂e** (WTT). ## 2.2 Why These Impacts Occur — The "How" Behind Each - **CO₂** → direct stoichiometric product of hydrocarbon fuel combustion in the engine. Proportional to fuel burned and carbon content of fuel. - **NOx** → formed at peak cylinder temperatures (>**1500 °C**) when N₂ + O₂ combine via the Zeldovich mechanism. - **PM** → originates from incomplete combustion (soot) and lube oil combustion; exacerbated in diesel due to high C/H ratio. - **Noise** → mechanical vibration + combustion pressure waves radiate as acoustic energy. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions asked on Environmental Impact of Fleets in KTU ESE.** | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{CO_2} = F_c \times EF_{fuel}$ | CO₂ emissions from fuel burned | kg CO₂ | | 2 | $F_c = \dfrac{D}{FE}$ | Fuel consumed = Distance / Fuel Efficiency | litres | | 3 | $CO_2e = \sum_i \big(m_i \times GWP_i\big)$ | CO₂-equivalent via GWP weighting | kg CO₂e | | 4 | $NO_x = VKT \times EF_{NO_x}$ | Distance-based NOx emission | kg NOx | | 5 | $\eta_{fleet} = \dfrac{\sum V_{actual}}{\sum V_{ideal}} \times 100\%$ | Fleet utilization efficiency | % | | 6 | $E_{saved} = N \times VKT \times (EF_{old} - EF_{new})$ | Emission savings on technology upgrade | kg/year | | 7 | $AQI = \max_i \big(\dfrac{C_i}{B_i}\big) \times 100$ | Sub-index based AQI (Indian CPCB-MOEF formula) | index | | 8 | $C_{fleet} = \dfrac{TCO}{VKT \times L_{avg}}$ | Cost-per-km (linked to environmental efficiency) | ₹/km | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Table Syntax Note:** All conditional / absolute bars in the formula sheet above are rendered using $\max(\cdot)$ and $\big(\cdot\big)$ to strictly avoid breaking the markdown table pipe-delimiter structure. Students writing exams should use parentheses (not vertical bars) for clarity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Logistics Companies (e.g., DHL, FedEx):** Use CO₂e accounting for *carbon-neutral shipping* commitments aligned with **Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi)**. - **Indian Public Sector:** State Transport Undertakings (STUs) like KSRTC must report annual fleet emissions under **MoRTH** guidelines. - **Smart City Missions:** Fleet electrification is tracked via **EV penetration %** as a Key Performance Indicator (KPI) under NEP 2020 sustainability mandates. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — Aggregate Fleet CO₂ Emissions **Problem Setup:** A logistics fleet operates **N = 25** diesel trucks. Each truck covers **D = 60,000 km/year** at an average fuel efficiency of **FE = 4.5 km/litre**. Compute the annual aggregate CO₂ emitted by the fleet. ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation The fundamental mass-balance relation linking fuel consumption to CO₂ emission is: $$ E_{CO_2} \;=\; F_c \times EF_{fuel} $$ where $E_{CO_2}$ is the mass of CO₂ emitted (kg), $F_c$ is the fuel consumed (litres), and $EF_{fuel}$ is the emission factor (kg CO₂ per litre). ### Step 2 — Compute per-vehicle fuel consumption For a single truck, fuel consumed in one year is: $$ F_{c,\,single} \;=\; \frac{D}{FE} \;=\; \frac{60{,}000 \text{ km}}{4.5 \text{ km/litre}} $$ $$ F_{c,\,single} \;=\; 13{,}333.33 \text{ litres} $$ **Textual interpretation:** Each truck burns roughly **13,333 litres** of diesel per year — equivalent to filling an average petrol-tank (**50 L**) about **267 times** per truck per year. ### Step 3 — Apply the CO₂ emission factor For diesel, the standard emission factor is $EF_{diesel} = 2.68$ kg CO₂ / litre. $$ E_{CO_2,\,single} \;=\; 13{,}333.33 \times 2.68 $$ $$ E_{CO_2,\,single} \;=\; 35{,}733.33 \text{ kg CO}_2 \;=\; 35.73 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 $$ ### Step 4 — Scale to the entire fleet Multiply the per-vehicle emissions by the fleet size $N = 25$: $$ E_{CO_2,\,fleet} \;=\; N \times E_{CO_2,\,single} \;=\; 25 \times 35.73 $$ $$ E_{CO_2,\,fleet} \;=\; 893.33 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 \text{ per year} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** The fleet emits **≈ 893.3 tonnes of CO₂ annually**. To offset this via mature forestry, one would need approximately **14,900 trees** growing for 10 years (since one tree absorbs ~ **6 kg CO₂/year** on average). --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — Emission Reduction from Technology Upgrade (EV Substitution) **Problem Setup:** Replace **15 of the 25 trucks** with equivalent electric vehicles (eVans). EV electricity consumption = **0.20 kWh/km**; grid emission factor for Kerala ≈ **0.65 kg CO₂ / kWh** (CEA, 2023). Compute annual CO₂ saved. ### Step 1 — Establish the saving equation $$ E_{saved} \;=\; N_{upgraded} \times VKT \times \big(EF_{old} - EF_{new}\big) $$ where $VKT$ is vehicle-km travelled by the upgraded subset. ### Step 2 — Compute $EF_{new}$ for EV Per km, the EV emits: $$ EF_{new} \;=\; 0.20 \text{ kWh/km} \times 0.65 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh} $$ $$ EF_{new} \;=\; 0.13 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km} $$ ### Step 3 — Express diesel $EF_{old}$ in kg/km terms $$ EF_{old} \;=\; \frac{2.68 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{L}}{4.5 \text{ km/L}} \;=\; 0.5956 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and compute saving $$ E_{saved} \;=\; 15 \times 60{,}000 \times (0.5956 - 0.13) $$ $$ E_{saved} \;=\; 15 \times 60{,}000 \times 0.4656 $$ $$ E_{saved} \;=\; 419{,}040 \text{ kg CO}_2 \;=\; 419.04 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 \text{ saved per year} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Upgrading just **60%** of the fleet to EVs eliminates **~47% of total fleet CO₂** (419 / 893 ≈ 46.9%). This single calculation is a *board-favourite* answer for showcasing engineering economics + environmental impact linkage. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — BS-VI vs BS-IV Fleet Emission Comparison (Diesel Trucks, g/km) | Pollutant | BS-IV Limit | BS-VI Limit | Reduction (%) | Health Impact Addressed | |---|---|---|---|---| | NOx | 0.46 | 0.046 | **~90 %** | Smog, asthma | | PM (mass) | 0.025 | 0.005 | **~80 %** | Lung cancer, CVD | | CO | 1.50 | 0.50 | **~67 %** | Hemoglobin poisoning | | HC + NOx (combined) | 0.55 | 0.17 | **~69 %** | Ground-level ozone | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fleet Environmental Impact Pathway — Functional Architecture The diagram below maps the **cause → pathway → effect → mitigation** chain for fleet environmental impact, replacing an unrenderable physical sketch with a clean, board-examinable logical flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[FleetOperation
VehicleKilometresTravelled] --> B1[DirectTailpipeEmissions
CO2 NOx PM CO HC] A1 --> B2[IndirectUpstreamEmissions
FuelProduction WTT] A1 --> B3[NonExhaustEmissions
TyreBrakeRoadWear] A1 --> B4[NoisePollution
EngineTyreAerodynamic] B1 --> C1[AirQualityDegradation
AQI Rise Smog] B2 --> C2[LifecycleCarbonFootprint
CO2e Inventory] B3 --> C3[ParticulateandMicroplasticLoad
PM2.5 Soil and Water] B4 --> C4[UrbanNoisePollution
dBA Beyond Limit] C1 --> D1[PublicHealthImpact
RespiratoryCVD Mortality] C2 --> D2[ClimateChangeForcing
GlobalWarming] C3 --> D3[EcosystemDamage
HeavyMetalBioaccumulation] C4 --> D4[QualityofLifeLoss
SleepDisorder Stress] D1 --> E1[MitigationStrategyA
BSVI Vehicles and DPF SCR] D2 --> E2[MitigationStrategyB
Electrification and Biodiesel] D3 --> E3[MitigationStrategyC
EcoDriving and Routing] D4 --> E4[MitigationStrategyD
NightDelivery and ElectricFleets] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The four **green impact streams** (B1–B4) converge into **three health/environmental endpoints** (C1–C3) plus **noise impact** (C4), which then trigger **four mitigation levers** (E1–E4). In a 14-mark answer, students should reference at least **2 of these mitigation levers** with quantitative justification to secure full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Define Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions and explain why it is preferred over Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) analysis in fleet environmental assessments.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions quantify the total CO₂-equivalent released across the *entire fuel lifecycle* — from crude oil extraction, refining, and transport (**Well-to-Tank, WTT**) to combustion in the engine (**Tank-to-Wheel, TTW**). WTW is preferred because it captures **upstream carbon** in fuel production (often **~20–25%** of total lifecycle CO₂ for petroleum fuels), providing a *true* environmental footprint. For example, an EV powered by coal-based electricity may show high TTW emissions, but its WTW emissions are still lower than a petrol ICE vehicle — a nuance only the WTW lens reveals. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **List any THREE major categories of environmental impact generated by commercial vehicle fleets.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Air Quality Impacts** — Tailpipe emissions of CO₂, NOx, PM, CO, and HC contributing to smog and urban AQI degradation. **(1 mark)** 2. **Noise Pollution** — Engine, tyre-road, and aerodynamic noise typically ranging **70–85 dB(A)**, exceeding WHO urban guidelines. **(1 mark)** 3. **Soil and Water Contamination** — Oil leaks, brake dust heavy metals, washing effluent, and ELV disposal residue. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **A logistics company operates 30 diesel trucks. Each truck covers 80,000 km/year with fuel efficiency of 5 km/litre. The company plans to upgrade to BS-VI vehicles with SCR/DPF retrofits, which reduce NOx emissions by 90%.
