Compute:** **(a)** The total annual CO₂ emitted by the existing fleet and the annual fuel consumption. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The annual NOx reduction (in kg/year) achievable through the BS-VI upgrade, and discuss two fleet management strategies to further reduce environmental impact. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Per-vehicle fuel consumption:** $$ F_{c} = \frac{D}{FE} = \frac{80{,}000}{5} = 16{,}000 \text{ litres/year} $$ **Step 2 — Per-vehicle CO₂ emission** using $EF_{diesel} = 2.68$ kg/L: $$ E_{single} = 16{,}000 \times 2.68 = 42{,}880 \text{ kg} = 42.88 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 $$ **Step 3 — Fleet-wide CO₂ emission** for $N = 30$ trucks: $$ E_{fleet} = 30 \times 42.88 = 1{,}286.4 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year} $$ **Step 4 — Total annual fuel consumption:** $$ F_{total} = 30 \times 16{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ litres/year} $$ > **Answer (a):** The fleet consumes **480,000 litres of diesel** per year, emitting **1,286.4 tonnes of CO₂**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Compute existing NOx in g/km** (BS-IV diesel truck ≈ 0.46 g/km). **Step 2 — Annual fleet vehicle-km travelled (VKT):** $$ VKT = 30 \times 80{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ km/year} $$ **Step 3 — Existing fleet NOx emission:** $$ NO_{x,old} = 0.46 \text{ g/km} \times 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ km} = 1{,}104{,}000 \text{ g} = 1{,}104 \text{ kg/year} $$ **Step 4 — With 90% reduction:** $$ NO_{x,new} = 0.10 \times 1{,}104 = 110.4 \text{ kg/year} $$ $$ NO_{x,saved} = 1{,}104 - 110.4 = 993.6 \text{ kg/year} $$ **Step 5 — Two fleet management strategies:** 1. **Eco-Driving & Telematics:** Train drivers in smooth acceleration/braking; deploy GPS-based route optimization to cut fuel use by **8–15%**, indirectly reducing all pollutants. **(3.5 marks)** 2. **Fleet Electrification & Hybridization:** Replace urban-delivery trucks with EV/hybrid variants; even partial substitution of 20% of fleet can cut fleet CO₂ by **~30%** and eliminate tailpipe NOx entirely. **(3.5 marks)** > **Answer (b):** NOx reduction = **993.6 kg/year**, with mitigation via eco-driving and electrification. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the four major categories of environmental impact from a fleet, and compute the AQI if observed concentrations are: PM2.5 = 90 µg/m³, PM10 = 180 µg/m³, NO₂ = 90 µg/m³, SO₂ = 80 µg/m³, CO = 4 mg/m³. Use CPCB breakpoints. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss India’s BS-VI emission norms, the role of DEF/AdBlue in SCR systems, and how a fleet manager can use the *Vehicle Kilometres Travelled (VKT)* approach for emissions budgeting. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Four categories of environmental impact:** 1. **Air pollution** (CO₂, NOx, PM, CO, HC) 2. **Noise pollution** (dB(A)) 3. **Soil/water contamination** (oil leaks, ELV disposal) 4. **Resource depletion** (fossil fuel, embodied energy) **Step 2 — AQI computation using Indian CPCB sub-index formula:** $$ I_i = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BPH_i - BPL_i} \big( C_i - BPL_i \big) + I_{Lo} $$ | Pollutant | $C_i$ | $BPL_i$ | $BPH_i$ | $I_{Lo}$ | $I_{Hi}$ | Sub-Index $I_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | PM2.5 | 90 | 60 | 90 | 101 | 200 | 200 | | PM10 | 180 | 100 | 250 | 101 | 200 | 134.7 | | NO₂ | 90 | 80 | 180 | 101 | 200 | 110 | | SO₂ | 80 | 61 | 160 | 101 | 200 | 114.6 | | CO (mg/m³) | 4 | 2.6 | 4.5 | 101 | 200 | 175.3 | **Step 3 — Final AQI = maximum sub-index:** $$ AQI = \max(200, 134.7, 110, 114.6, 175.3) = 200 $$ > **Answer (a):** AQI = **200 (Poor)** — driven by PM2.5 dominance. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **BS-VI Norms:** Equivalent to Euro 6; mandate **90% NOx reduction**, real-driving emissions (RDE) testing, and on-board diagnostics (OBD-II). **(2 marks)** **Role of DEF/AdBlue in SCR:** Selective Catalytic Reduction injects a **32.5% urea solution (AdBlue)** into the exhaust stream, where it decomposes to NH₃ that catalytically reduces NOx to N₂ and H₂O. AdBlue consumption ≈ **5% of fuel consumption**. **(2 marks)** **VKT-based Emissions Budgeting:** A fleet manager sets an annual emission cap using: $$ Cap = N \times VKT_{allowed} \times EF_{pollutant} $$ Real-time telematics tracks actual VKT per vehicle; deviations from the budget trigger corrective routing, load consolidation, or vehicle substitution. This ties *operational decisions* directly to *environmental KPIs* — a cornerstone of sustainable fleet management. **(3 marks)** > **Answer (b):** BS-VI + AdBlue SCR + VKT-budgeting forms an integrated environmental control system. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on this Topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the Fleet Multiplier:** Students often compute per-vehicle emissions and forget to multiply by $N$. This loses **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Mixing WTT and TTW:** Don't add WTW to TTW for the same fuel — that's double counting. WTW = WTT + TTW is a single composite figure. > 3. **Wrong emission factor:** Petrol CO₂ EF ≠ Diesel CO₂ EF. Use **2.31 kg/L for petrol** and **2.68 kg/L for diesel** — wrong factor loses a full mark in part (a). > 4. **Ignoring units in AQI computation:** Always carry the sub-index $I_i$ to one decimal — examiners check for numerical rigor. > 5. **Vague mitigation strategies:** Generic "use EVs" without quantitative backing (kWh/km, grid EF, % substitution) yields only **partial credit**. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Environmental Impact of Fleets** - **CO₂ Emission Factor (Diesel):** **2.68 kg/L** | **Petrol:** **2.31 kg/L** — internalize these constants. - **Fleet CO₂ Formula:** $E_{fleet} = N \times D/FE \times EF_{fuel}$. - **BS-VI vs BS-IV (NOx):** 0.46 → 0.046 g/km = **~90% reduction**. - **GWP-weighted CO₂e:** Use $CO_2e = \sum (m_i \times GWP_i)$. - **AQI:** Computed as the **maximum sub-index** among criteria pollutants. - **WTW > TTW** for lifecycle honesty — syllabus favourite. - **Mitigation Hierarchy:** *Avoid → Reduce → Substitute → Offset* (always present answers in this order for higher marks). - **AdBlue consumption** ≈ **5% of fuel consumption** — memorize for SCR questions. - **VKT-based budgeting** = bridge between *operations* and *environmental KPIs*. - **CMVR noise limit (HCV):** **84 dB(A) at 7 m**. - **Tree offset:** 1 mature tree absorbs ~ **6 kg CO₂/year** — handy for intuitive explanation.

topics.title: Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Footprint (Transport Context):**
> The **Environmental Footprint** of a fleet is the total quantifiable impact of its vehicles and operations on the natural environment, expressed through metrics such as **CO₂ equivalent emissions (kgCO₂e)**, **NOx**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **SOx**, **Non-Methane Hydrocarbons (NMHC)**, **noise pollution (dB)**, and **resource depletion**. It is typically measured over the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** lifecycle — from fuel extraction to final exhaust discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint** are the integrated set of managerial, technological, operational, and policy-driven interventions applied across the **vehicle lifecycle stages** (design, procurement, operation, maintenance, and disposal) to minimize the negative ecological impact of a transport fleet while maintaining or improving operational efficiency.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Footprint Reduction

| Pillar | Domain | Typical Interventions |
|---|---|---|
| **Technological** | Vehicle & Powertrain | EVs, hybrids, hydrogen, biofuels, EURO/BS-VI upgrades |
| **Operational** | Day-to-day Use | Route optimization, eco-driving, idle reduction, load consolidation |
| **Managerial** | Strategic Planning | Fleet right-sizing, modal shift, TCO modelling, policy compliance |
| **Behavioral** | Driver & Culture | Training, incentives, telematics feedback, gamification |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaking Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine your fleet is a **bucket full of water (representing your environmental budget)**. Every kilometer driven, every liter of diesel burned, every inefficient route taken — these are **leaks in the bucket**. *Strategies for reducing environmental footprint are the patches you apply to the leaks*. Some patches (like EVs) are **big structural repairs**, while others (like inflating tires to the correct pressure) are **quick small patches**. The smartest fleet managers apply **both types** simultaneously, because a fleet that only relies on one strategy (e.g., only buying EVs but never optimizing routes) is like patching the big holes while ignoring dozens of small leaks.

## 1.4 The "Triple Bottom Line" (TBL) Lens

Modern KTU-aligned fleet strategy evaluates every intervention against three concurrent outcomes:

- **Planet** (CO₂ reduction, air quality)
- **Profit** (fuel savings, TCO reduction)
- **People** (driver safety, community health)

A strategy that reduces emissions but **destroys profitability** is unsustainable; a strategy that is profitable but increases emissions is non-compliant with global ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) mandates.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Emissions Framework

The WTW framework is the **gold standard** for measuring environmental footprint, and KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to distinguish between the two stages:

1. **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** — Upstream emissions: extracting crude oil, refining, transporting fuel to the pump.
2. **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** — Downstream emissions: actual combustion (or equivalent) in the vehicle.

$$E_{WTW} = E_{WTT} + E_{TTW}$$

Where:
- $E_{WTW}$ = Total Well-to-Wheel CO₂ equivalent emissions (g CO₂e/km)
- $E_{WTT}$ = Well-to-Tank emissions from fuel production
- $E_{TTW}$ = Tank-to-Wheel tailpipe emissions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Electric vehicles have **zero TTW emissions** (no tailpipe), but they still carry a substantial WTT footprint from electricity generation. In India, where ~75% of electricity is coal-based, the *grid-carbon intensity* drastically changes the real-world EV footprint.

## 2.2 Key Quantitative Formulas

### 2.2.1 Specific Fuel Consumption to CO₂ Conversion

$$CO_2 \text{ emissions (kg)} = FC \times \rho_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$$

Where:
- $FC$ = Fuel consumed (liters)
- $\rho_{fuel}$ = Density of fuel (kg/L) — Diesel ≈ **0.832**, Petrol ≈ **0.745**
- $EF_{fuel}$ = Emission factor (kg CO₂ per kg fuel) — Diesel ≈ **3.17**, Petrol ≈ **3.16**

### 2.2.2 Per-Kilometer Emission Intensity

$$E_{km} = \frac{FC_{per100km}}{100} \times \rho_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$$

### 2.2.3 Fleet Carbon Footprint (Annual)

$$CF_{fleet} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} VMT_i \times E_{km,i}$$

Where:
- $VMT_i$ = Annual Vehicle-Miles-Traveled (or km) of vehicle *i*
- $E_{km,i}$ = Emission intensity of vehicle *i* (g CO₂/km)
- $N$ = Total fleet size

### 2.2.4 Eco-Driving Fuel Savings (Rule of Thumb)

| Driving Behavior Change | Typical Fuel Saving |
|---|---|
| Smooth acceleration / no harsh braking | **5%–10%** |
| Tire pressure +10% above soft | **1%–2%** |
| Speed limit reduced 100→90 km/h | **~9%** |
| Idle elimination (>1 min) | **~0.5 L/hr per idling vehicle** |
| Roof rack removal | **~2%–8%** at highway speed |

### 2.2.5 Carbon Offset Equation

$$Offset_{cost} = \frac{CO_2_{annual}}{1000} \times Price_{credit}$$

Where:
- $CO_2_{annual}$ = Annual fleet emissions in **kg**
- $Price_{credit}$ = Carbon credit price in **₹/tonne CO₂** (India's CBT market ~ ₹400–₹800/tonne as of recent data)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | WTW Emissions | $E_{WTW} = E_{WTT} + E_{TTW}$ | g CO₂e/km | Upstream + Tailpipe |
| 2 | Fuel-to-CO₂ | $CO_2 = FC \cdot \rho \cdot EF$ | kg | Use density × EF |
| 3 | Per-km intensity | $E_{km} = \frac{FC}{100} \cdot \rho \cdot EF$ | g CO₂/km | Direct measurement |
| 4 | Fleet footprint | $CF = \sum VMT_i \cdot E_{km,i}$ | g CO₂/year | Aggregation |
| 5 | Carbon offset | $Cost = \frac{CO_2_{kg}}{1000} \cdot Price$ | ₹ | For compliance |
| 6 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | N | $C_d$ × frontal area $A$ |
| 7 | Rolling resistance | $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | N | $C_{rr}$ tire coefficient |
| 8 | BS-VI NOx limit (diesel car) | **80 mg/km** | mg/km | Bharat Stage VI norm |
| 9 | EURO 6 NOx limit (diesel) | **80 mg/km** | mg/km | European standard |
| 10 | EV grid emission factor (India avg) | **~0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** | kg CO₂/kWh | CEA 2023 baseline |

## 2.4 The Three Strategy Layers — Hierarchical View

Fleet environmental footprint reduction is best understood as a layered system:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Avoid:** Eliminate the trip entirely (modal shift to rail, video conferencing).
> **Layer 2 — Shift:** Replace high-emission vehicle with low-emission vehicle (ICE → EV).
> **Layer 3 — Improve:** Make the existing ICE more efficient (eco-driving, maintenance).
> This is the **"Avoid-Shift-Improve" (ASI)** hierarchy endorsed by the **International Transport Forum (ITF)** and is a frequent KTU question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics Companies (DHL, FedEx):** Use ASI to claim Scope-1 and Scope-3 emission reductions in sustainability reports.
- **Municipal Fleets:** Mandated by India's **National Mission on Electric Mobility** to convert 30% of vehicles to electric by 2030.
- **Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs):** Required to offset residual emissions via **Carbon Credit Trading (CBT)** under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023.
- **Last-Mile Delivery:** Use cargo-bike + EV hub models to reduce urban footprint.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Fleet Carbon Footprint Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A logistics company operates **50 diesel trucks**. Each truck travels **80,000 km/year**, consuming an average of **30 L/100 km**. Diesel density is **0.832 kg/L** and the emission factor is **3.17 kg CO₂/kg**. Calculate:
1. Annual CO₂ emissions for the fleet.
2. Equivalent in tonnes.
3. Cost of carbon offsets at ₹600/tonne.
4. Reduction achieved if each truck's fuel economy improves by 10% (eco-driving + maintenance).

### Step 1 — Per-Truck Annual Fuel Consumption

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{truck} &= \frac{30 \text{ L}}{100 \text{ km}} \times 80{,}000 \text{ km/year} \\[4pt]
&= 0.30 \times 80{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 24{,}000 \text{ L/year per truck}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Per-Truck Mass of Fuel Burned

$$
\begin{aligned}
Mass_{fuel} &= FC_{truck} \times \rho_{diesel} \\[4pt]
&= 24{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.832 \text{ kg/L} \\[4pt]
&= 19{,}968 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Per-Truck CO₂ Emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,truck} &= Mass_{fuel} \times EF_{diesel} \\[4pt]
&= 19{,}968 \text{ kg} \times 3.17 \text{ kg CO₂/kg fuel} \\[4pt]
&= 63{,}298.56 \text{ kg CO₂/year per truck}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Fleet CO₂ Emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,fleet} &= 50 \times 63{,}298.56 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}164{,}928 \text{ kg CO₂/year} \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}164.93 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Carbon Offset Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost_{offset} &= 3{,}164.93 \text{ tonnes} \times \text{₹}600/\text{tonne} \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹}1{,}898{,}958 \approx \text{₹}18.99 \text{ lakhs/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Post Eco-Driving Reduction (10% Fuel Economy Gain)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New } CO_{2,truck} &= 63{,}298.56 \times (1 - 0.10) \\[4pt]
&= 56{,}968.70 \text{ kg CO₂/year} \\[4pt]
\text{Fleet Savings} &= 50 \times (63{,}298.56 - 56{,}968.70) \\[4pt]
&= 50 \times 6{,}329.86 \\[4pt]
&= 316{,}493 \text{ kg CO₂/year (avoided)} \\[4pt]
&= 316.49 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year} \\[4pt]
\text{Monetary Saving} &= 316.49 \times \text{₹}600 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹}1{,}89{,}895 \approx \text{₹}1.9 \text{ lakhs/year (offset avoidance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 10% fuel economy improvement across a 50-truck fleet avoids **316 tonnes of CO₂** — equivalent to taking roughly **68 average passenger cars** off the road for one year (using the EPA benchmark of ~4.6 tonnes CO₂/car/year).

## 3.2 Analytical Strategy Matrix — Cost vs Impact

| Strategy | CapEx | OpEx Impact | CO₂ Reduction | Payback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Eco-driving training** | Very Low | High savings | **5%–10%** | <6 months |
| **Tire pressure management** | Very Low | Moderate savings | **1%–3%** | <3 months |
| **Route optimization software** | Low | High savings | **8%–15%** | 6–12 months |
| **Vehicle right-sizing** | Medium | Moderate savings | **5%–12%** | 12–24 months |
| **Idle-reduction policy** | Very Low | Moderate savings | **2%–5%** | Immediate |
| **Hybrid vehicles** | High | Fuel saving | **20%–30%** | 3–5 years |
| **Full Battery EVs** | Very High | Electricity saving | **40%–70% (TTW)** | 4–7 years |
| **Hydrogen FCEV** | Very High | Hydrogen saving | **0% (TTW), depends on H₂ source** | 8–12 years |
| **Modal shift (rail/water)** | Variable | Variable | **50%–80%** | Project-specific |

## 3.3 The TCO-Environmental Trade-off Equation

Total Cost of Ownership over vehicle life can be linked to environmental footprint via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO &= C_{capex} + C_{fuel} + C_{maintenance} + C_{insurance} - C_{residual} + C_{carbon} \\[4pt]
\text{Where } C_{carbon} &= \frac{CO_{2,life}}{1000} \times Price_{carbon} \\[4pt]
CO_{2,life} &= \sum_{y=1}^{L} VMT_y \times E_{km,y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This allows KTU-level questions where students must compare **diesel vs EV vs hybrid** on both economic and environmental axes.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Avoid-Shift-Improve (ASI) Hierarchy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transport Demand Identified] --> B{Is the trip absolutely necessary?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Can mode be shifted?}
    B -- No --> Z[TRIP ELIMINATED - Layer 1 AVOID]
    C -- Yes, to rail or EV --> D[MODAL SHIFT - Layer 2 SHIFT]
    C -- No --> E{Apply operational improvements?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Eco-driving + Route Opt + Maintenance]
    E -- No --> G[Accept baseline footprint]
    F --> H[IMPROVE - Layer 3 IMPROVE]
    H --> I[Monitor, Report, Offset Residual Emissions]
```

## 4.2 Fleet Environmental Footprint Reduction Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Acquisition Stage] --> B[Operation Stage]
    B --> C[Maintenance Stage]
    C --> D[End-of-Life Stage]
    A -- Choose low-emission vehicle --> A1[EV, Hybrid, Euro VI compliant]
    B -- Efficient usage --> B1[Route Opt, Eco-driving, Load Opt]
    B -- Fuel choice --> B2[Bio-diesel, CNG, Electricity]
    C -- Preventive upkeep --> C1[Tire, Engine, Aerodynamics]
    C -- Driver behavior --> C2[Telematics feedback]
    D -- Responsible disposal --> D1[Recycle, Reuse, Carbon offset]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Eco-Driving Training] --> O1[Reduced Fuel Burn]
    S2[Route Optimization] --> O1
    S3[Load Consolidation] --> O1
    S4[Vehicle Right-Sizing] --> O2[Lower TTW Emissions]
    S5[EV/Hybrid Adoption] --> O2
    S6[Modal Shift to Rail] --> O3[Lower VMT and per-km impact]
    S7[Tire Maintenance] --> O1
    S8[Aerodynamic Add-ons] --> O1
    S9[Idle Reduction Policy] --> O1
    S10[Carbon Offsets] --> O4[Net-Zero Compliance]
    O1 --> R1[Lower Carbon Footprint]
    O2 --> R1
    O3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[ESG Reporting]
    R1 --> R3[Regulatory Compliance]
    R1 --> R4[Cost Savings]
    R2 --> O4
```

## 4.4 Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) Block Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Extraction] --> B[Manufacturing]
    B --> C[Distribution to Fleet]
    C --> D[Operational Use]
    D --> E[Maintenance and Repair]
    E --> F[End-of-Life Disposal]
    F --> G[Recycling Reuse]
    G --> H[Net Environmental Impact]
    A --> H
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A **Lifecycle Assessment (LCA)** captures the *cradle-to-grave* footprint. Battery EVs often have a higher **manufacturing footprint** (due to battery production) but a much lower **operational footprint** — they "earn back" the carbon debt typically within **2–3 years** of Indian-grid operation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Environmental Footprint" in the context of vehicle fleet management. Mention any three key metrics used to quantify it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Environmental Footprint** of a vehicle fleet is the total negative impact exerted by the fleet's operations on the natural environment, measured across the vehicle's entire lifecycle.

Three key quantification metrics are:
1. **CO₂ equivalent emissions (kg CO₂e)** — global warming potential.
2. **NOx and Particulate Matter (PM)** — local air quality and respiratory health.
3. **Noise pollution (dB)** — relevant for urban delivery fleets.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming metrics, 1 mark for correct unit/scope.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the "Avoid-Shift-Improve" (ASI) framework? State one example for each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ASI Framework** is a hierarchical strategy for sustainable transport prioritized in order of effectiveness:
1. **Avoid** — Eliminate the need for travel. *Example:* Replacing a physical meeting with a video conference.
2. **Shift** — Move passengers/freight to lower-emission modes. *Example:* Shifting long-haul freight from road to rail.
3. **Improve** — Enhance efficiency of the existing vehicle. *Example:* Eco-driving training and route optimization.

*(1 mark per layer + example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emission framework**. Differentiate between WTT and TTW emissions with suitable examples for a diesel car, a battery EV, and a hydrogen FCEV. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company operates **40 CNG buses**, each traveling **60,000 km/year** with fuel consumption of **35 kg/100 km**. CNG emission factor is **2.75 kg CO₂/kg**. Calculate: (i) per-bus annual CO₂, (ii) total fleet annual CO₂, (iii) per-km emission intensity. If they switch to BS-VI electric buses with grid emission factor of **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** and energy consumption of **1.2 kWh/km**, calculate the percentage reduction in per-km CO₂ emissions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

### (a) WTW Framework Discussion (7 marks)

The **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) framework** quantifies the complete greenhouse-gas (GHG) footprint of a vehicle by accounting for **all upstream** (fuel production) and **downstream** (vehicle operation) emissions.

$$
E_{WTW} = E_{WTT} + E_{TTW}
$$

| Vehicle | WTT (Upstream) | TTW (Tailpipe) | Net WTW |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diesel Car** | Crude extraction + refining (~25 g/km) | Combustion (~120 g/km) | ~145 g/km |
| **Battery EV** | Coal-based electricity generation (~80 g/km) | **0 g/km** (zero tailpipe) | ~80 g/km |
| **Hydrogen FCEV** | Natural gas reforming / electrolysis (~120 g/km) | **0 g/km** (only water vapour) | ~120 g/km |

> **Key insight:** Battery EVs achieve their **WTW advantage** only when the electricity grid is reasonably clean. Hydrogen FCEVs require **green hydrogen** (from renewable electrolysis) to be truly zero-carbon.

*(Award 2 marks for WTW equation and explanation, 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the grid-comment insight.)*

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

#### Step 1 — Per-bus annual fuel consumption

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{bus} &= \frac{35 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ km}} \times 60{,}000 \text{ km/year} \\[4pt]
&= 21{,}000 \text{ kg CNG/year per bus}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Per-bus CO₂ emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,bus} &= 21{,}000 \times 2.75 \\[4pt]
&= 57{,}750 \text{ kg CO₂/year per bus}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Fleet annual CO₂

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_{2,fleet} &= 40 \times 57{,}750 \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}310{,}000 \text{ kg CO₂/year} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}310 \text{ tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Per-km emission intensity (CNG)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km,CNG} &= \frac{35}{100} \times 2.75 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9625 \text{ kg CO₂/km} = 962.5 \text{ g CO₂/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Per-km emission intensity (EV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{km,EV} &= 1.2 \text{ kWh/km} \times 0.72 \text{ kg CO₂/kWh} \\[4pt]
&= 0.864 \text{ kg CO₂/km} = 864 \text{ g CO₂/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Percentage reduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{962.5 - 864}{962.5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{98.5}{962.5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 10.23\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Award 1 mark per correct calculation step. The EV vs CNG reduction is modest (~10%) only because India's grid is coal-heavy; in hydro/solar-rich states, the reduction can exceed 60%.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Carbon Offsetting** in fleet management. Discuss its limitations and the role of **Carbon Credit Trading (CBT)** in India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fleet manager must choose between three vehicles for a 1,50,000 km annual duty cycle: a **Diesel SUV** (₹15 lakh, 12 km/L, EF 3.17, ₹95/L), a **Hybrid Sedan** (₹22 lakh, 22 km/L, EF 3.17, ₹95/L), and a **Battery EV** (₹28 lakh, 5 km/km, grid EF 0.72, ₹8/kWh). Ignoring maintenance, calculate the **5-year fuel cost** for each. Which vehicle has the lowest TCO over 5 years if residual values are 40%, 50%, and 35% respectively? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

### (a) Carbon Offsetting and CBT (7 marks)

**Carbon Offsetting** is the mechanism by which a fleet operator compensates for its unavoidable residual emissions by **financing equivalent emission-reduction projects elsewhere** — such as reforestation, renewable energy, or methane capture.

**Limitations:**
1. **Not a substitute for in-house reduction** — it is the *last* option in the ASI hierarchy.
2. **Verification issues** — credits must be **additional, permanent, and verifiable**.
3. **Public/ESG criticism** — greenwashing risk.

**Carbon Credit Trading (CBT) in India:**
- Launched under the **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023**, notified by the Ministry of Power.
- Operated by the **Power Secretary** as Administrator, with the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** as technical arm.
- Entities (including transport firms above the threshold) receive **Carbon Credit Certificates (CCC)** for verified reductions and can trade on the **Indian Carbon Market (ICM)**.
- Prices historically in the range of **₹400–₹800/tonne CO₂**, with the aim of aligning India with **net-zero by 2070**.

*(Award 2 marks for offset definition, 2 marks for limitations, 3 marks for CBT specifics.)*

---

### (b) TCO Numerical (7 marks)

#### Step 1 — Annual fuel cost — Diesel SUV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel/yr} &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{12} = 12{,}500 \text{ L} \\[4pt]
\text{Cost/yr} &= 12{,}500 \times 95 = \text{₹}11{,}87{,}500 \\[4pt]
\text{5-yr fuel} &= \text{₹}59{,}37{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Annual fuel cost — Hybrid Sedan

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel/yr} &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{22} = 6{,}818.18 \text{ L} \\[4pt]
\text{Cost/yr} &= 6{,}818.18 \times 95 = \text{₹}6{,}47{,}727 \\[4pt]
\text{5-yr fuel} &= \text{₹}32{,}38{,}636
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Annual electricity cost — Battery EV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{kWh/yr} &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{1}{5} = 30{,}000 \text{ kWh (assuming 5 km/kWh)} \\[4pt]
\text{Cost/yr} &= 30{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹}2{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{5-yr elec} &= \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — 5-Year TCO (CapEx + Fuel − Residual)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{Diesel} &= 15{,}00{,}000 + 59{,}37{,}500 - (15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 59{,}37{,}500 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}68{,}37{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{Hybrid} &= 22{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}38{,}636 - (22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50) \\[4pt]
&= 22{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}38{,}636 - 11{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}43{,}38{,}636
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{EV} &= 28{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 - (28{,}00{,}000 \times 0.35) \\[4pt]
&= 28{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}30{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **Battery EV has the lowest 5-year TCO** at **₹30.2 lakhs**, despite the highest upfront cost, due to its dramatically lower energy cost.

*(Award 1 mark per correct calculation step; 1 bonus mark for the conclusion and qualitative insight.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing WTT and TTW** — students often write only tailpipe emissions and miss the upstream component. Always state both.
> 2. **Forgetting fuel density** — emission factors (3.17, 2.75) are in *kg CO₂ per kg fuel*, so you must convert liters → kg using density first.
> 3. **Treating EV as "zero emission"** — the WTW footprint depends on the **grid emission factor**. In India, EVs are *not* zero-carbon today.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversions** — 1 tonne = 1000 kg. Mixing units (kg vs tonnes) in offset calculations is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **TCO without residual value** — many students forget that selling the vehicle at the end of life reduces total cost substantially.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Environmental Footprint** = total lifecycle impact of a fleet on the environment.
- Key metrics: **CO₂e, NOx, PM, noise**.
- **WTW = WTT + TTW** is the most comprehensive measurement framework.
- **BS-VI / EURO 6** NOx limit for diesel cars = **80 mg/km**.
- **EVs are zero-tailpipe but not zero-WTT** in coal-heavy grids.
- **Avoid → Shift → Improve** is the prioritized hierarchy.
- **Eco-driving** alone can save **5%–10%** fuel.
- **Tire pressure, idle reduction, route optimization** are the cheapest, fastest wins.
- **Carbon offsetting** is the *last* resort, not the first.
- **Carbon Credit Trading (CBT), 2023** governs tradable carbon credits in India via the **Power Secretary as Administrator**.
- **TCO** = CapEx + OpEx (fuel/maintenance) − Residual Value + Carbon Cost.
- **Diesel EF ≈ 3.17 kg CO₂/kg fuel**; **Petrol EF ≈ 3.16 kg CO₂/kg fuel**; **CNG EF ≈ 2.75 kg CO₂/kg**.
- **EV grid emission factor in India ≈ 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**.
- **Modal shift** (road → rail/water) can cut freight emissions by **50%–80%**.
- **Lifecycle Assessment (LCA)** captures cradle-to-grave impact, not just operational.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Fleet Management Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT835 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Fleet Management Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Green Fleet Management (GFM)** is the systematic, data-driven, and policy-oriented practice of planning, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a vehicle fleet in a manner that **minimizes environmental impact**, **reduces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**, **lowers fossil-fuel dependency**, and **optimizes total cost of ownership (TCO)** while still meeting operational service-level requirements. It integrates sustainable procurement, eco-driving, telematics, alternative-propulsion technologies, route engineering, and end-of-life recycling within a closed-loop management framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *"Green Fleet Management is the holistic integration of low-emission vehicle technologies, optimized operational practices, and sustainability reporting to align transportation assets with corporate environmental, social, and governance (ESG) targets."*

The governing pillars of GFM are:
1. **Green Vehicle Acquisition (GVA)** – procurement of low-emission / zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs).
2. **Eco-Operations (EO)** – eco-driving, idle reduction, route & load optimization.
3. **Green Maintenance (GM)** – preventive maintenance to sustain fuel efficiency and emission compliance.
4. **End-of-Life Management (ELM)** – circular economy, remanufacturing, and responsible disposal.
5. **Monitoring, Reporting & Verification (MRV)** – emissions accounting using ISO 14064 / GHG Protocol.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Health-Conscious Kitchen" Metaphor**
> Think of a delivery fleet the way a nutritionist views a restaurant kitchen. A green fleet is a kitchen that:
> - **Buys fresh, local ingredients** (clean fuels, EVs, hybrids instead of diesel-guzzlers).
> - **Cooks efficiently** (eco-driving, optimal routes — no over-revving, no idling).
> - **Keeps appliances well-tuned** (tire pressure, engine calibration, oil changes).
> - **Composts waste** (recycling batteries, tires, oils at end-of-life).
> - **Tracks calories & carbon on a dashboard** (telematics, emissions KPIs).
>
> Just as a healthy kitchen cuts costs *and* serves better food, a green fleet cuts emissions *and* fuel bills.

## 1.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) & Standard Metrics

The following **bold-marked** metrics are universally adopted by KTU 2024, GRI, and SmartWay (EPA) frameworks:

- **CO₂e (Carbon Dioxide Equivalent)** – standardized unit, **kg CO₂e per km** or **g CO₂e per tonne-km**.
- **Fuel Economy** – **km / litre (kmpl)** or **litres / 100 km (L/100 km)**.
- **Specific CO₂ Emission** – **g CO₂ / km** (Type-Approval standard under BS-VI / Euro 6).
- **Fleet Utilization Rate** – **% of active hours vs. total available hours**.
- **Idle-Time Ratio** – **idle minutes / total engine-on minutes**.
- **BS-VI Stage 2 Limit (India, 2023)** – **NOx ≤ 460 mg/kWh; PM ≤ 10 mg/kWh** for heavy-duty diesel.
- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency** – includes fuel production, distribution, and use.
- **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Efficiency** – only the in-use combustion / propulsion phase.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When a question asks for "carbon footprint of a fleet", always clarify whether the answer expects **TTW (Scope 1)** or **WTW (Scope 1 + 3)** emissions. KTU often tests this distinction.

## 1.4 Scope of Green Fleet Management (Three-Scope Emissions Model)

| Scope | Definition | Example in Fleet |
|-------|------------|-----------------|
| **Scope 1** | Direct emissions from owned/controlled sources | Diesel combustion in fleet vehicles |
| **Scope 2** | Indirect emissions from purchased energy | Electricity used to charge EVs |
| **Scope 3** | Value-chain emissions | Fuel production, vehicle manufacturing, disposal |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Operational Framework

### Pillar 1 — Green Vehicle Acquisition (GVA)

- **Right-Sizing Principle:** Match the vehicle's **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** and **engine capacity** precisely to the duty cycle. A 16-tonne truck hauling 2 tonnes is a classic "over-spec" inefficiency.
- **Alternative Powertrain Selection:**
  - **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)** – zero TTW emissions; high WTW if grid is coal-heavy.
  - **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle)** – typically **20–35% fuel savings** vs. ICE.
  - **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid)** – 30–60 km electric range + ICE backup.
  - **FCEV (Fuel-Cell EV)** – hydrogen; near-zero tailpipe; challenges in H₂ storage.
  - **CNG / LNG / CBG (Compressed Bio-Gas)** – **20–25% lower CO₂** than diesel.
  - **B5–B20 Biodiesel** – renewable, drop-in fuel.
  - **Ethanol E85 / E100** – high octane, lower PM.

### Pillar 2 — Eco-Driving (Smooth Operator Programme)

- Anticipate traffic flow → avoid harsh braking.
- Shift up early (manual) or use **Eco-mode** (automatic).
- Maintain **steady speeds between 40–60 km/h** for light commercial vehicles (LCVs).
- Cap maximum speed at **80 km/h** on highways (fuel consumption rises **exponentially above 90 km/h**).
- Switch off engines during idle periods > **60 seconds**.

### Pillar 3 — Route & Load Optimization

- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** solved using **heuristics** (Clarke-Wright, Nearest Neighbour) or **meta-heuristics** (Genetic Algorithm, Ant Colony).
- **Load Factor (β):**
$$\beta = \frac{\text{Actual Payload}}{\text{Maximum Permissible Payload}} \times 100\%$$
- Target **β ≥ 70%** for sustainable freight.

### Pillar 4 — Green Maintenance Practices

- **Tire Pressure:** Under-inflation by **1 psi** increases fuel consumption by **0.3–0.5%**.
- **Wheel Alignment:** Misalignment of **0.5°** increases rolling resistance by **~5%**.
- **Engine Oil:** Use **low-viscosity (0W-20, 5W-30)** synthetic oils → **1–3% fuel economy gain**.
- **Air Filter:** A clogged filter increases fuel use by **up to 10%**.

### Pillar 5 — End-of-Life & Circular Economy

- **Reuse:** Refurbished batteries for second-life stationary storage.
- **Remanufacture:** Engine, transmission, alternator remanufacturing saves **~85% energy** vs. new.
- **Recycle:** Tyre-derived fuel, lead-acid battery recycling (Pb recovery **≥ 97%** mandated in India).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 equations — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical questions on Green Fleet.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\text{CO}_2\text{e}_{\text{fuel}} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times EF_{\text{fuel}}$ | $m$ = fuel mass; $EF$ = emission factor | kg, kg CO₂e/kg | Carbon accounting |
| 2 | $\text{FC} = \frac{100}{\text{kmpl}}$ | FC = fuel consumption; kmpl = mileage | L/100 km | Standard EU metric |
| 3 | $\text{SFC} = \frac{m_{\text{fuel}} \times 1000}{P \times t}$ | SFC = specific fuel consumption | g/kWh | Engine benchmarking |
| 4 | $\text{TTW CO}_2 = m_{\text{fuel}} \times 3.169$ | Diesel constant | kg CO₂/L | Tailpipe CO₂ |
| 5 | $\text{WTW} = \text{TTW} + \text{WTT}$ | WTT = well-to-tank | kg CO₂e/km | Full LCA |
| 6 | $\text{Idle Penalty} = T_{\text{idle}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{idle}}$ | $\dot{m}_{\text{idle}}$ ≈ **1.0–2.5 L/hr** | L/hr | Idle cost calc |
| 7 | $\text{Roll. Res.} = C_{rr} \times m \times g$ | $C_{rr}$ = coefficient; $m$ = mass; $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | N | Energy at wheels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Memorise:**
> Diesel emission factor = **2.68 kg CO₂ / litre** (EPA, WTT-inclusive ≈ 3.169).
> Petrol = **2.31 kg CO₂ / litre**.
> CNG = **1.86 kg CO₂ / kg** (approx, varies).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corporate Sustainability:** Walmart's *Project Gigaton* targets **1 gigaton CO₂ reduction** from supply chain by 2030 — GFM is central.
- **SmartWay (US EPA):** Certifies fleets achieving **> 20% emissions reduction** vs. baseline.
- **India Context:** FAME-II (Faster Adoption & Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) subsidies make BEV acquisition 30–40% cheaper; **AIS-156** standard governs battery safety.
- **Public Health:** Indian vehicular PM₂.₅ contributes to **~7% of ambient PM₂.₅** in metros — green fleets directly reduce respiratory morbidity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Carbon Footprint of a Fleet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical (KTU-Style):**
> *A logistics company operates 25 trucks. Each truck travels **80,000 km/year** at a mileage of **4.5 km/L** of diesel. Calculate (a) annual fuel consumption, (b) TTW CO₂ emissions, (c) WTW CO₂ emissions using WTT factor **0.45 kg CO₂/L**, and (d) the CO₂ reduction (in %) if CNG is used instead, given CNG mileage = **3.0 km/kg** and CNG EF = **1.86 kg CO₂e/kg**.*

### Step 1 — Annual Fuel Consumption (Per Truck)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{one}} &= \frac{\text{Annual km}}{\text{kmpl}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{80{,}000 \text{ km}}{4.5 \text{ km/L}} \\[4pt]
&= 17{,}777.78 \text{ L/year per truck}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Fleet Annual Diesel Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{fleet}} &= 25 \times 17{,}777.78 \\[4pt]
&= 444{,}444.5 \text{ L/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — TTW (Scope 1) CO₂ Emissions

Using $EF_{\text{diesel,TTW}} = 2.68$ kg CO₂ / L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{TTW}} &= 444{,}444.5 \times 2.68 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}191{,}111.26 \text{ kg CO₂} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1{,}191.11 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — WTT (Scope 3) CO₂ Emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{WTT}} &= 444{,}444.5 \times 0.45 \\[4pt]
&= 200{,}000.0 \text{ kg CO₂} \\[4pt]
&= 200.00 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — WTW (Total) CO₂ Emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{WTW}} &= 1{,}191.11 + 200.00 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}391.11 \text{ tonnes CO₂/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — CNG Equivalent Computation

CNG fuel required per truck per year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CNG,one}} &= \frac{80{,}000}{3.0} = 26{,}666.67 \text{ kg/year} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{CNG,fleet}} &= 25 \times 26{,}666.67 = 666{,}666.75 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

CNG total emissions (assuming WTT ≈ 0.30 kg CO₂/kg for CNG):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_{2,\text{CNG,TTW}} &= 666{,}666.75 \times 1.86 = 1{,}240{,}000.0 \text{ kg} = 1{,}240 \text{ t} \\[4pt]
\text{CO}_{2,\text{CNG,WTT}} &= 666{,}666.75 \times 0.30 = 200{,}000.0 \text{ kg} = 200 \text{ t} \\[4pt]
\text{CO}_{2,\text{CNG,WTW}} &= 1{,}240 + 200 = 1{,}440 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Percentage Reduction Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\text{CO}_2 &= 1{,}440 - 1{,}391.11 = +48.89 \text{ t} \quad (\text{increase!}) \\[4pt]
\%\text{ change} &= \frac{48.89}{1{,}391.11} \times 100 = +3.51\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive Result:** In *TTW-only* accounting, diesel produces **1,191 t** vs. CNG **1,240 t** — but CNG has lower **NOx, PM**, and (in well-managed supply) lower WTW. Always state the **scope boundary** clearly in the answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Full-Cycle Comparison Including Refining:** A more accurate diesel EF is **3.169 kg/L** (WTT-inclusive). Using that:
> $$\text{CO}_{2,\text{diesel,WTW}} = 444{,}444.5 \times 3.169 = 1{,}408.4 \text{ t}$$
> Now CNG savings = $1{,}440 - 1{,}408.4 = -31.6$ t (≈ **−2.24%**), but **PM and NOx reduce by 70–90%**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Idle Fuel Waste & Eco-Impact

> **Worked Numerical:** *A 40-truck fleet idles on average **45 minutes per shift per truck**. Each truck idles at **2.0 L/hr**. Shifts per year = **300**. Diesel price ₹95/L. Calculate (a) litres wasted, (b) cost, (c) CO₂ emitted.*

### Step 1 — Total Idle Hours per Truck

$$
T_{\text{idle,one}} = 45 \text{ min} \times 300 = 13{,}500 \text{ min} = 225 \text{ hr}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Idle Hours Fleet-wide

$$
T_{\text{idle,fleet}} = 40 \times 225 = 9{,}000 \text{ hr/year}
$$

### Step 3 — Litres Wasted

$$
V_{\text{waste}} = 9{,}000 \times 2.0 = 18{,}000 \text{ L/year}
$$

### Step 4 — Financial Cost

$$
\text{Cost} = 18{,}000 \times 95 = \text{₹ } 17{,}10{,}000 \;(\text{₹ 17.1 lakh})
$$

### Step 5 — CO₂ Emitted

$$
\text{CO}_{2} = 18{,}000 \times 2.68 = 48{,}240 \text{ kg} \approx 48.24 \text{ t CO₂}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fuel & Emission Comparison Table

| Powertrain | Energy Source | TTW CO₂ (g/km, mid-size car) | WTW CO₂ (g/km) | PM₂.₅ | NOx | Maturity |
|------------|---------------|-------------------------------|------------------|--------|------|-----------|
| Petrol ICE | Fossil | 160–180 | 200–240 | Low | Medium | Mature |
| Diesel ICE | Fossil | 140–160 | 180–220 | High | High | Mature |
| CNG | Fossil (Bio option) | 110–130 | 130–170 | Very Low | Low | Mature |
| Hybrid (HEV) | Petrol+Electric | 90–110 | 120–150 | Low | Low | Mature |
| Battery EV (BEV) | Grid | **0** (TTW) | 60–180 (grid-dependent) | **Zero** | **Zero** | Growing |
| Hydrogen FCEV | H₂ (Grey/Green) | **0** (TTW) | 30–250 (pathway-dep.) | Zero | Zero | Nascent |
| Biodiesel B20 | Renewable blend | 130–150 | 80–120 (carbon-neutral) | Low | Low | Mature |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** India's electricity grid emission factor ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA, 2023). For a BEV consuming **0.15 kWh/km**, WTW ≈ 0.15 × 0.72 × 1000 = **108 g CO₂/km** — already cleaner than petrol even on a coal-dominant grid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Green Fleet Management Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Policy and ESG Targets] --> B[Green Vehicle Acquisition]
    B --> C[Fleet Right Sizing and Mix Planning]
    C --> D[Eco Operations Layer]
    D --> D1[Eco Driving Training]
    D --> D2[Route and Load Optimization]
    D --> D3[Idle Reduction Policy]
    D1 --> E[Telematics and IoT Sensors]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Green Maintenance Layer]
    F --> F1[Tire Pressure Monitoring]
    F --> F2[Engine Calibration]
    F --> F3[Oil and Filter Regime]
    F1 --> G[Emissions Monitoring and MRV]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[End of Life and Circularity]
    H --> H1[Remanufacturing]
    H --> H2[Second Life Batteries]
    H --> H3[Material Recycling]
    H1 --> I[Sustainability Reporting]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Carbon Credit and Offset Accounting]
    J --> A
    G --> K[Compliance with BS VI and Euro 6]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Alternative Fuel Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Duty Cycle Analysis] --> Q1{Annual km greater than 100000?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Heavy payload above 5 tonnes?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Consider HEV or BEV]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Hydrogen refuelling available?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[BEV for urban last mile]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q6[FCEV deployment]
    Q4 -- No --> Q7[CNG or LNG or Biodiesel B20]
    Q3 --> R1[Compare TCO over 5 years]
    Q5 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1
    Q7 --> R1
    R1 --> R2{TCO reduction greater than 15 percent?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3[Adopt Green Technology]
    R2 -- No --> R4[Retain ICE with eco retrofits]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Emissions Scope Mapping (GHG Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scope 1 Direct] --> A1[Diesel and petrol burned in fleet]
    A --> A2[Refrigerant leaks from reefer units]
    B[Scope 2 Indirect Energy] --> B1[Purchased electricity for EV charging]
    B --> B2[Grid power for depot and workshop]
    C[Scope 3 Value Chain] --> C1[Fuel extraction and refining WTT]
    C --> C2[Vehicle manufacturing embodied carbon]
    C --> C3[Tyre and battery production]
    C --> C4[End of life disposal emissions]
    A1 --> D[Total Fleet Carbon Footprint]
    A2 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[ISO 14064 and GRI Reporting]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Eco-Driving Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Driver Behaviour] --> Q[Throttle and Brake Inputs]
    Q --> R[Engine ECU Response]
    R --> S[Fuel Injection Rate]
    S --> T[Vehicle Speed Profile]
    T --> U[Instantaneous Fuel Use]
    U --> V[Telematics Dashboard]
    V --> W[Driver Score Card 0 to 100]
    W --> X[Coaching and Incentive]
    X --> P
    V --> Y[Fleet Manager KPI Review]
    Y --> X
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Battery Second-Life Cascade (Circular Economy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[EV Battery at 80 percent SOH] --> B[First Life in Vehicle 8 to 10 years]
    B --> C{End of First Life}
    C --> D[Testing and Grading]
    D --> E[Grade A above 80 percent SOH]
    D --> F[Grade B 60 to 80 percent SOH]
    D --> G[Grade C below 60 percent SOH]
    E --> H[Second Life Stationary Storage for Solar or Microgrid]
    F --> I[Second Life for UPS or Forklift]
    G --> J[Material Recycling Li Co Ni Recovery]
    H --> K[End of Second Life Recycling]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Raw Material Re enters Battery Supply Chain]
    K --> L
```
